topics.title: Characteristics of Non-Linear Systems
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PERAT634 - NON-LINEAR CONTROL
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Characteristics of Non-Linear Systems

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

A **non-linear control system** is a dynamical system whose mathematical model contains one or more non-linear operators, meaning the governing differential (or difference) equation fails to satisfy the **principle of superposition** (homogeneity and additivity).

Mathematically, a system described by the operator $\mathcal{N}$ acting on input $u(t)$ to produce output $y(t)$ is non-linear if:

$$\mathcal{N}\{\alpha u_1(t) + \beta u_2(t)\} \neq \alpha \mathcal{N}\{u_1(t)\} + \beta \mathcal{N}\{u_2(t)\}$$

where $\alpha$ and $\beta$ are arbitrary scalars and $u_1(t), u_2(t)$ are arbitrary inputs.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight:** The hallmark of a non-linear system is the **failure of the superposition principle**. If superposition holds for ALL inputs, the system is linear. A system may *appear* linear over a limited operating range (small-signal linearization), but is globally non-linear.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Imagine a **bicycle pump**:

- When you push the handle **gently** (small input force), air flows out almost proportionally — this is the **linear region**.
- When you push **hard** (large input force), the valve's spring bottoms out, and additional force produces **no extra airflow** — this is **saturation**.
- If you push **backwards** lightly, nothing happens (dead zone). If you then push forwards, the valve suddenly **snaps open** (hysteresis / backlash).

The pump behaves *piecewise* — different rules in different regions — and a single linear equation simply cannot describe it across its full operating range.

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering Takeaway:** Real physical systems (motors, valves, amplifiers, hydraulic actuators) are **inherently non-linear**. Linear models are merely **local approximations** near a chosen operating point.

## 1.3 Distinguishing Features at a Glance

- Non-linear systems can produce outputs whose **frequency content is entirely different** from the input.
- They can have **multiple isolated equilibrium points**, finite-escape trajectories, limit cycles, sub-harmonics, and even chaos.
- They lack a single, universal analytical solution method — techniques must be chosen **case by case**.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Eight Defining Characteristics of Non-Linear Systems

### Characteristic 1 — Violation of Superposition
The response to a sum of inputs is **not** the sum of responses to each input taken separately. This is the *defining* mathematical fingerprint.

### Characteristic 2 — Multiple Isolated Equilibrium Points
Unlike linear systems (which have a *single* equilibrium, or a *continuous family* of equilibria), non-linear systems can possess **two or more isolated equilibrium states**.

Example — Van der Pol oscillator:

$$\ddot{x} - \mu(1-x^2)\dot{x} + x = 0$$

This system has a **single equilibrium point at the origin**, but exhibits a **limit cycle** (an isolated closed orbit), which is itself a "quasi-equilibrium" of oscillatory behaviour.

A simpler multi-equilibrium example:

$$\dot{x} = x - x^3$$

Equilibria occur where $\dot{x}=0$:

$$x - x^3 = x(1-x^2) = 0 \implies x = 0,\; x = +1,\; x = -1$$

Three isolated equilibrium points — impossible for a linear $\dot{x} = ax + b$.

### Characteristic 3 — Generation of New Frequencies (Harmonics)
When a sinusoidal input of frequency $\omega$ is applied, the **steady-state output contains harmonics** $2\omega, 3\omega, \dots, n\omega$.

For a pure square-law element $y = x^2$, with input $x = A\sin\omega t$:

$$y = A^2 \sin^2\omega t = \frac{A^2}{2}(1 - \cos 2\omega t)$$

A new frequency $2\omega$ appears where none existed in the input.

### Characteristic 4 — Limit Cycles (Self-Sustained Oscillations)
A **limit cycle** is an isolated closed trajectory in the phase plane such that **all nearby trajectories spiral toward it** (stable) or away from it (unstable).

The amplitude and frequency of a limit cycle are **independent of initial conditions**, which is fundamentally different from linear oscillations whose amplitude depends on initial conditions.

### Characteristic 5 — Jump Resonance (Frequency-Response Hysteresis)
In some non-linear systems, as input frequency is **slowly increased**, the output amplitude suddenly **jumps** at a critical frequency; as frequency is **slowly decreased**, the jump occurs at a **different frequency**. This produces a **hysteresis loop** in the frequency response — never seen in linear systems.

### Characteristic 6 — Sub-harmonic and Almost-Periodic Oscillations
- **Sub-harmonics**: Output oscillates at $\omega/n$ when input is at frequency $\omega$.
- **Almost periodic**: Output contains incommensurate frequencies (ratio is irrational).

### Characteristic 7 — Finite Escape Time
Some non-linear systems reach infinity in **finite time**, e.g.:

$$\dot{x} = x^2, \quad x(0) = x_0 > 0 \implies x(t) = \frac{x_0}{1 - x_0 t}$$

Blows up at $t = 1/x_0$ — a phenomenon no linear system exhibits.

### Characteristic 8 — Sensitivity to Initial Conditions (Chaos)
Two arbitrarily close initial conditions lead to **exponentially diverging trajectories**. Quantified by a **positive Lyapunov exponent**. Deterministic yet unpredictable in the long term.

## 2.2 Common Physical Non-Linearities

| Non-linearity | Mathematical Model | Engineering Example |
|---|---|---|
| **Saturation** | $y = \text{sat}(u) = \begin{cases} k u, & \vert u \vert \leq u_{\max} \\ k u_{\max}\,\text{sgn}(u), & \vert u \vert > u_{\max} \end{cases}$ | Amplifier output, magnetic saturation of motor |
| **Dead Zone** | $y = \begin{cases} 0, & \vert u \vert \leq d \\ k(u - d\,\text{sgn}(u)), & \vert u \vert > d \end{cases}$ | DC motor with stiction, valve stiction |
| **Relay (On-Off)** | $y = M\,\text{sgn}(u)$ | Thermostat, bang-bang controller |
| **Relay with Hysteresis** | $y = \begin{cases} +M, & u > +h \\ -M, & u < -h \\ \text{previous value}, & \vert u \vert \leq h \end{cases}$ | Schmitt trigger, two-position valve |
| **Backlash** | Two-segment piecewise mapping with memory | Gear trains, mechanical linkages |
| **Coulomb Friction** | $F_f = -F_c\,\text{sgn}(\dot{x})$ | Sliding friction in bearings |
| **Square Law** | $y = k u^2$ | Fluid flow through orifice, $q \propto \sqrt{\Delta p}$ |

## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!NOTE]
> **Critical Exam Reference Table — memorize the units and boundary conditions.**

| Concept | Formula / Condition | Key Property |
|---|---|---|
| Superposition Test | $\mathcal{N}\{\alpha u_1 + \beta u_2\} \neq \alpha \mathcal{N}\{u_1\} + \beta \mathcal{N}\{u_2\}$ | Failure ⇒ non-linear |
| Equilibrium Points | $\dot{x} = f(x) = 0$ roots | Multiple isolated roots possible |
| Limit Cycle | Closed isolated trajectory in phase plane | Amplitude independent of IC |
| Square Law Harmonic | $A\sin\omega t \to \frac{A^2}{2}(1-\cos 2\omega t)$ | Generates $2\omega$ |
| Cubic Harmonic | $A\sin\omega t \to \frac{3A^3}{4}\sin\omega t - \frac{A^3}{4}\sin 3\omega t$ | Fundamental + $3\omega$ |
| Finite Escape | $\dot{x} = x^2 \Rightarrow t_{esc} = 1/x_0$ | Blow-up in finite time |
| Saturation Limit | $\vert u \vert \leq u_{\max}$ ⇒ linear; else constant | Piecewise linear |
| Dead Zone Width | $2d$ (total) | Zero output for small input |
| Relay Output | $y \in \{-M,\, +M\}$ only | Discontinuous |
| Lyapunov Stability | $\dot{V}(x) < 0$ for stability | Used for non-linear analysis |

## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Robotics**: Joint friction (Coulomb + viscous) and actuator saturation are dominant non-linearities.
- **Aerospace**: Aerodynamic lift $L \propto v^2$ — inherently non-linear.
- **Power Electronics**: Switched-mode converters are *piecewise-linear* non-linear systems.
- **Process Control**: Control valves exhibit both dead-zone and equal-percentage characteristics.
- **Biomedical**: Pupil response to light, neural firing (Hodgkin–Huxley equations) are highly non-linear.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Worked Derivation 1 — Proving Superposition Violation

**Problem:** Show that the system $y(t) = x^2(t)$ is non-linear using two input signals.

**Step 1 — Define the test inputs**

Choose two arbitrary inputs:

$$x_1(t) = \sin(\omega t), \quad x_2(t) = \cos(\omega t)$$

**Step 2 — Define the combined input**

The linear combination with arbitrary scalars $\alpha=1, \beta=1$:

$$x_{12}(t) = x_1(t) + x_2(t) = \sin(\omega t) + \cos(\omega t)$$

**Step 3 — Compute the response to the combined input**

$$y_{12}(t) = [x_1(t) + x_2(t)]^2$$

Expand the square:

$$y_{12}(t) = x_1^2(t) + 2 x_1(t) x_2(t) + x_2^2(t)$$

**Step 4 — Compute the responses to individual inputs**

$$y_1(t) = \sin^2(\omega t), \quad y_2(t) = \cos^2(\omega t)$$

Sum them:

$$y_1(t) + y_2(t) = \sin^2(\omega t) + \cos^2(\omega t) = 1$$

**Step 5 — Compare**

$$y_{12}(t) = 1 + 2\sin(\omega t)\cos(\omega t) = 1 + \sin(2\omega t)$$

$$y_1(t) + y_2(t) = 1$$

Therefore:

$$y_{12}(t) \neq y_1(t) + y_2(t)$$

The cross-term $2\sin(\omega t)\cos(\omega t)$ is the *extra* contribution that violates additivity.

**Conclusion:** The system $y = x^2$ is **non-linear**, and the cross-term $2x_1x_2$ is the proof.

---

## 3.2 Worked Derivation 2 — Finding Multiple Equilibrium Points

**Problem:** Find all equilibrium points of $\dot{x} = x - x^3$ and classify their stability.

**Step 1 — Set $\dot{x} = 0$**

$$x - x^3 = 0 \implies x(1 - x^2) = 0$$

Factor further:

$$x(1-x)(1+x) = 0$$

**Step 2 — Identify the equilibria**

$$x^*_1 = -1, \quad x^*_2 = 0, \quad x^*_3 = +1$$

**Step 3 — Linearize around each equilibrium** using $f'(x) = 1 - 3x^2$

$$\begin{aligned}
\text{At } x^*_1 = -1: \quad & f'(-1) = 1 - 3(1) = -2 < 0 \quad \text{(Stable)} \\
\text{At } x^*_2 = 0: \quad & f'(0) = 1 > 0 \quad \text{(Unstable)} \\
\text{At } x^*_3 = +1: \quad & f'(+1) = 1 - 3(1) = -2 < 0 \quad \text{(Stable)} \\
\end{aligned}$$

**Step 4 — Physical interpretation**

The system has **two stable equilibria** (the "wells" at $\pm 1$) separated by an **unstable equilibrium** at the origin. Depending on the initial condition, the system will settle at $+1$ or $-1$. A linear system of form $\dot{x} = ax + b$ can only ever have **one** equilibrium.

---

## 3.3 Worked Derivation 3 — Frequency Generation in Square-Law Element

**Problem:** Apply $u(t) = A\sin(\omega t)$ to a square-law element and identify all output frequency components.

**Step 1 — Apply the non-linearity**

$$y(t) = u^2(t) = A^2 \sin^2(\omega t)$$

**Step 2 — Use the trigonometric identity**

$$\sin^2\theta = \frac{1 - \cos(2\theta)}{2}$$

**Step 3 — Substitute**

$$y(t) = \frac{A^2}{2}\bigl(1 - \cos(2\omega t)\bigr)$$

**Step 4 — Identify components**

$$\begin{aligned}
\text{DC component:} \quad & \frac{A^2}{2} \\
\text{AC component:} \quad & -\frac{A^2}{2}\cos(2\omega t) \text{ at frequency } 2\omega \\
\end{aligned}$$

The output has **zero energy at the input frequency $\omega$** but contains a **DC bias** and a **second harmonic $2\omega$** — a signature of non-linear behaviour.

---

## 3.4 Analytical Matrix — Linear vs Non-Linear System Comparison

| Property | Linear System | Non-Linear System |
|---|---|---|
| Superposition | Holds | Fails |
| Number of Equilibria | Single (or family) | Multiple isolated |
| Frequency Response | Input $\omega$ → Output $\omega$ only | Generates harmonics $2\omega, 3\omega, \ldots$ |
| Steady-state error to step | Constant | Can vary with step size |
| Response to IC | Predictable, scales linearly | Highly sensitive (chaos possible) |
| Stability | Global, one analysis suffices | Local — must be checked region by region |
| Solutions | Closed-form (often) | Rarely closed-form |
| Analytical tools | Laplace, Fourier, root locus | Phase plane, Lyapunov, describing function, Popov |
| Numerical methods | Well-behaved | Can be stiff / divergent |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1 `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023, Model Paper]`

**Differentiate between linear and non-linear control systems. List any four characteristics of non-linear systems.**

**Model Answer:**

A linear control system obeys the principle of superposition and homogeneity, while a non-linear system violates these principles.

**Four characteristics of non-linear systems:**

1. They do not obey the superposition principle.
2. They can have multiple isolated equilibrium points.
3. They can exhibit limit cycles — self-sustained oscillations of fixed amplitude and frequency.
4. They generate new frequencies (harmonics, sub-harmonics) not present in the input signal.

Additional valid characteristics: jump resonance, finite escape time, sensitivity to initial conditions (chaos).

---

### Question 2 `[KTU University Exam - May 2024, Model Paper]`

**What is a limit cycle? Why is it impossible in a linear time-invariant system?**

**Model Answer:**

A **limit cycle** is an isolated closed trajectory in the phase plane of a second-order system such that all neighbouring trajectories spiral toward it (stable limit cycle) or away from it (unstable limit cycle).

**Why it cannot occur in an LTI system:** In an LTI second-order system, the trajectory is a unique solution for given initial conditions. If the trajectory is closed for one initial condition, it is closed for *all* initial conditions — meaning there is a *continuous family* of closed orbits, not an *isolated* one. A limit cycle, by definition, must be **isolated**. Hence LTI systems can never exhibit limit cycles.

---

## 5.2 Part B — Descriptive Questions (14 Marks Each)

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning:** In Module 1, students frequently lose marks by (a) confusing **saturation** with **dead zone**, (b) forgetting that non-linear systems can have **multiple equilibrium points**, and (c) drawing phase portraits without **arrow directions** indicating trajectory flow. Always label axes, mark equilibrium points clearly with $\bullet$, and indicate flow direction with arrows.

---

### Question A `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2022, Adapted]` — **14 Marks**

**(a)** Explain with mathematical justification **why a system described by** $y = x^2$ **is non-linear**. Show that superposition fails using suitable test signals. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** For the system $\dot{x} = x - x^3$, find **all equilibrium points**, classify their stability, and sketch the phase portrait. Comment on the number of equilibria as evidence of non-linearity. **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution — Part (a)

**Step 1 — State the superposition test**

A system $y = f(x)$ is linear **iff** for all scalars $\alpha, \beta$ and inputs $x_1, x_2$:

$$f(\alpha x_1 + \beta x_2) = \alpha f(x_1) + \beta f(x_2)$$

**Step 2 — Apply to $f(x) = x^2$**

Let $x_1 = A\sin\omega t$, $x_2 = B\cos\omega t$, and $\alpha = \beta = 1$.

**Step 3 — Compute LHS**

$$f(x_1 + x_2) = (x_1 + x_2)^2 = x_1^2 + 2x_1 x_2 + x_2^2$$

**Step 4 — Compute RHS**

$$f(x_1) + f(x_2) = x_1^2 + x_2^2$$

**Step 5 — Compare**

$$\text{LHS} - \text{RHS} = 2 x_1 x_2 \neq 0 \quad \text{(in general)}$$

Since the cross-term $2x_1 x_2$ does not vanish, **superposition fails**, and the system $y = x^2$ is non-linear. **∎**

#### Model Solution — Part (b)

**Step 1 — Find equilibria**

$$\dot{x} = x - x^3 = x(1-x)(1+x) = 0$$

$$\implies x^*_1 = -1, \quad x^*_2 = 0, \quad x^*_3 = +1$$

**Step 2 — Linearize around each**

$$f'(x) = 1 - 3x^2$$

$$\begin{aligned}
f'(-1) &= 1 - 3 = -2 < 0 \implies \text{stable} \\
f'(0) &= 1 > 0 \implies \text{unstable} \\
f'(+1) &= -2 < 0 \implies \text{stable} \\
\end{aligned}$$

**Step 3 — Phase portrait description**

- Between $-1 < x < 0$: $\dot{x} > 0$ (unstable region — flow moves right)
- Between $0 < x < 1$: $\dot{x} > 0$ (stable region — flow moves right toward $+1$)
- For $x > 1$: $\dot{x} < 0$ (flow moves left toward $+1$)
- For $x < -1$: $\dot{x} < 0$ (flow moves left away from $-1$? — verify: $\dot{x} = x - x^3$; at $x=-2$, $\dot{x}=-2+8=+6 > 0$, so flow moves right toward $-1$)

**Phase portrait sketch (1-D flow):**

```
   <──────|──────────|───────>──────>  x
          -1    ↑    0    ↑    +1
       (stable)  ↑  (unstable)  ↑ (stable)
                 ↑              ↑
          arrows point right    arrows point right
```

**Step 4 — Conclusion on non-linearity**

A linear system $\dot{x} = ax + b$ has **exactly one** equilibrium $x^* = -b/a$. Here we have **three** equilibria, which conclusively demonstrates the non-linear nature of the system. **∎**

---

### Question B `[KTU University Exam - May 2023, Adapted]` — **14 Marks**

**(a)** Define the following non-linearities with neat sketches and write their input-output equations: **(i) Saturation, (ii) Dead Zone, (iii) Relay with Hysteresis**. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A sinusoidal input $u(t) = 5\sin(10t)$ is applied to a square-law non-linearity $y = u^2$. Determine the **DC component**, the **amplitude**, and the **frequency** of the output. Comment on frequency generation. **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution — Part (a)

**(i) Saturation**

$$y(t) = \begin{cases} k\,u(t), & \vert u(t) \vert \leq u_{\max} \\ k\,u_{\max}\,\text{sgn}\bigl(u(t)\bigr), & \vert u(t) \vert > u_{\max} \end{cases}$$

Sketch: Linear slope $k$ in the central region, flat horizontal lines at $\pm k\,u_{\max}$ beyond saturation limits.

**(ii) Dead Zone**

$$y(t) = \begin{cases} 0, & \vert u(t) \vert \leq d \\ k\bigl(u(t) - d\,\text{sgn}(u(t))\bigr), & \vert u(t) \vert > d \end{cases}$$

Sketch: Zero output in the central region $-d \leq u \leq d$; linear slope $k$ outside, but shifted by $\pm d$ (so the line doesn't pass through origin).

**(iii) Relay with Hysteresis**

$$y(t) = \begin{cases} +M, & u(t) > +h \text{ or } u(t) \geq -h \text{ and previous output was } +M \\ -M, & u(t) < -h \text{ or } u(t) \leq +h \text{ and previous output was } -M \end{cases}$$

Sketch: Output jumps from $-M$ to $+M$ at $u = +h$ (rising input), and from $+M$ to $-M$ at $u = -h$ (falling input). This forms a rectangular hysteresis loop.

#### Model Solution — Part (b)

**Given:** $u(t) = 5\sin(10t)$, $y = u^2$

**Step 1 — Substitute**

$$y(t) = 25 \sin^2(10t)$$

**Step 2 — Apply identity**

$$\sin^2\theta = \frac{1 - \cos(2\theta)}{2}$$

**Step 3 — Expand**

$$y(t) = \frac{25}{2}\bigl(1 - \cos(20t)\bigr)$$

**Step 4 — Identify components**

$$\begin{aligned}
\text{DC component:} \quad & Y_{DC} = \frac{25}{2} = 12.5 \\
\text{AC amplitude:} \quad & Y_{AC} = \frac{25}{2} = 12.5 \\
\text{AC frequency:} \quad & \omega_{out} = 20 \text{ rad/s} \quad (\text{i.e., } 2\omega_{in}) \\
\end{aligned}$$

**Step 5 — Comment on frequency generation**

The input had frequency $\omega_{in} = 10$ rad/s with zero DC component. The output contains:

- A **DC bias** of $12.5$ (rectification effect — characteristic of even-powered non-linearities).
- An **AC component at $2\omega_{in} = 20$ rad/s** — the **second harmonic**.

No energy exists at the original input frequency $10$ rad/s. This frequency-doubling effect is **impossible in any linear system** and is a definitive signature of non-linear behaviour. **∎**

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid-Revision Checklist for KTU Module 1:**

- **Definition:** A non-linear system is one that **violates the superposition principle** — i.e., $\mathcal{N}\{\alpha x_1 + \beta x_2\} \neq \alpha \mathcal{N}\{x_1\} + \beta \mathcal{N}\{x_2\}$.
- **Multiple Equilibria:** Non-linear systems can have **isolated** equilibrium points (e.g., $x^3 - x = 0$ gives three equilibria).
- **Limit Cycle:** Isolated closed orbit; **amplitude independent of initial conditions**; does NOT exist in LTI systems.
- **Frequency Generation:** $x^2 \to$ second harmonic + DC bias; $x^3 \to$ fundamental + third harmonic.
- **Jump Resonance:** Sudden amplitude jump at critical frequency with **hysteresis** during upward vs downward frequency sweep.
- **Finite Escape Time:** $\dot{x} = x^2$ blows up in finite time $t = 1/x_0$.
- **Common Non-linearities:** Saturation, Dead Zone, Relay, Hysteresis, Backlash, Coulomb Friction, Square Law.
- **Phase Portrait Tools:** Stable node, unstable node, saddle, stable/unstable focus, limit cycle.
- **Practical Examples:** Amplifier saturation, valve dead-zone, thermostat relay, gear backlash, motor Coulomb friction.
- **Linearization Caveat:** A non-linear system may be approximated as linear **only** within a small operating range; this is *small-signal* or *local* linearization.

> [!NOTE]
> **Final Exam Tip (KTU 2024):** Always draw **neat sketches** of non-linearities with clearly marked slopes, breakpoints, and saturation limits. Examiners award marks for labelled diagrams even when equations are partially incorrect. Also, in Part B answers, briefly justify *why* the answer demonstrates non-linear behaviour — this earns the conceptual marks that differentiate an A-grade answer from a B-grade one.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Methods of Analysis
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PERAT634 - NON-LINEAR CONTROL
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Methods of Analysis

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 What are "Methods of Analysis" in Non-Linear Control?

In the context of non-linear control systems, **Methods of Analysis** refers to the rigorous set of mathematical and graphical techniques used to investigate, characterize, and predict the behavior of systems governed by non-linear differential equations, for which the principle of superposition (a cornerstone of linear analysis) does **not** hold.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology):**
> *Methods of Analysis are systematic analytical procedures employed to determine the stability, transient response, steady-state behavior, and dynamic performance of non-linear control systems where the system dynamics are described by one or more non-linear differential equations of the form:*
> $$\dot{x}(t) = f(x(t), u(t), t)$$
> *where $f(\cdot)$ is a non-linear vector function of the state $x(t)$, input $u(t)$, and time $t$.*

### 1.2 Why Can't We Use Linear Methods Directly?

Linear control theory offers a mature, elegant toolkit: Laplace transforms, transfer functions, root locus, Bode plots, and Nyquist criterion. However, applying these tools directly to non-linear systems yields **incorrect or misleading results** because:

1. **Superposition fails:** If $y_1(t)$ is the response to $x_1(t)$ and $y_2(t)$ to $x_2(t)$, then the response to $x_1(t) + x_2(t)$ is **NOT** $y_1(t) + y_2(t)$.
2. **Frequency content changes:** A pure sinusoidal input to a non-linear system produces harmonics — output contains frequencies not present in input.
3. **Multiple equilibrium points:** Non-linear systems can have multiple isolated equilibrium states.
4. **Limit cycles:** Self-sustained oscillations of fixed amplitude and frequency (a phenomenon unique to non-linear systems).

### 1.3 Intuitive Analogy

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-World Analogy — The River and the Rapids:**
> Imagine analyzing a **gentle, slow river** (linear system). The current is predictable, the flow obeys simple rules, and small disturbances decay smoothly. Now consider a **mountain stream with rapids, whirlpools, and sudden drops** (non-linear system). You cannot use a river-flow formula to predict behavior at the rapids. Instead, you need specialized tools — a topographic map, water velocity probes, and turbulence analysis — to predict what happens at each distinct feature.
> Likewise, **Methods of Analysis** are the specialized "topographic maps and turbulence probes" we use to understand the "rapids" of non-linear control systems.

### 1.4 Classification of Methods of Analysis

| Category | Method | Typical Application |
|----------|--------|---------------------|
| **Approximate Analytical** | Linearization (Taylor / Jacobian) | Small-signal analysis near operating point |
| **Exact Analytical** | Feedback Linearization, Lie Algebra | Exact transformation to linear form |
| **Graphical/Geometric** | Phase Plane Method | Second-order systems (analytical & graphical) |
| **Harmonic Balance** | Describing Function Method | Higher-order systems with mild non-linearity |
| **Energy-Based** | Lyapunov's Direct Method | Stability of any order system |
| **Frequency-Domain** | Popov Criterion, Circle Criterion | Absolute stability of Lur'e systems |
| **Singular Perturbation** | Two-Time-Scale Analysis | Systems with fast and slow dynamics |

> [!TIP]
> **Syllabus Highlight:** In **Module 1** of KTU PERAT634, the primary focus is on **Linearization Methods** and the **Phase Plane Method**, with foundational exposure to the **Describing Function** approach. Higher-order energy and frequency criteria appear in later modules.

## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Method I — Linearization Techniques

Linearization is the process of approximating a non-linear system by an equivalent linear model valid within a small neighborhood of an operating point. This is the **most commonly employed** first step in non-linear analysis.

#### 2.1.1 Taylor Series Linearization (Small-Signal Approximation)

For a scalar non-linear function $y = f(x)$, expanding about an operating point $(x_0, y_0)$ using Taylor's theorem:

$$y = f(x_0) + \left.\frac{df}{dx}\right|_{x_0} (x - x_0) + \left.\frac{1}{2!}\frac{d^2f}{dx^2}\right|_{x_0} (x - x_0)^2 + \cdots$$

Retaining only the first-order (linear) term:

$$y \approx f(x_0) + \left.\frac{df}{dx}\right|_{x_0} \cdot \Delta x$$

**Why this works:** Near $x_0$, the higher-order terms $(x - x_0)^2, (x - x_0)^3, \ldots$ are negligibly small if the deviation $\Delta x = (x - x_0)$ is small.

#### 2.1.2 Jacobian Linearization (Vector Form)

For a non-linear state-space system:

$$\dot{x} = f(x, u), \quad y = h(x, u)$$

The **Jacobian matrices** evaluated at the equilibrium point $(x_0, u_0)$ are:

$$A = \left.\frac{\partial f}{\partial x}\right|_{(x_0, u_0)}, \quad B = \left.\frac{\partial f}{\partial u}\right|_{(x_0, u_0)}$$

The linearized model becomes:

$$\Delta \dot{x} = A \cdot \Delta x + B \cdot \Delta u$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Significance in Engineering:** This is the **mathematical foundation of every small-signal AC analysis** in electronics (BJT, FET amplifiers), aerodynamic stability derivatives, robotic manipulator control, and chemical process control. Every SPICE simulation of a transistor circuit begins with Jacobian linearization.

#### 2.1.3 Exact Linearization (Input-State Linearization)

Unlike approximate methods, exact linearization uses a non-linear state transformation $z = \phi(x)$ and a non-linear feedback law $u = \alpha(x) + \beta(x) v$ to convert the system into a **completely equivalent linear system** in the new coordinates.

The result:

$$\dot{z} = Az + Bv$$

> [!NOTE]
> This method is covered in detail in **higher modules** of the syllabus. In Module 1, the focus is on understanding the concept and recognizing that non-linear systems can be transformed into linear ones under specific conditions.

### 2.2 Method II — Phase Plane Method

The phase plane method is a **graphical technique** for analyzing second-order non-linear systems. It plots the system's trajectory in a 2D state space $(x_1, x_2)$ where $x_2 = \dot{x}_1$.

For a system:

$$\ddot{x} + f(x, \dot{x}) = 0$$

The phase trajectory equation is:

$$\frac{dx_2}{dx_1} = \frac{f(x_1, x_2)}{-x_2}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Key Geometric Features in the Phase Plane:**
> - **Equilibrium point (Singular point):** Where $\dot{x}_1 = 0$ AND $\dot{x}_2 = 0$ simultaneously.
> - **Limit Cycle:** An isolated closed trajectory — represents sustained oscillation.
> - **Trajectories never cross** at non-singular points (Uniqueness Theorem for ODEs).
> - **Symmetry about the $x_1$-axis** if the system is odd-symmetric (no $x$ term, only $\dot{x}$).

### 2.3 Method III — Describing Function Method

The describing function (DF) is a **quasi-linear approximation** that extends sinusoidal-input describing-function analysis to non-linear systems. It replaces the actual non-linear element with an equivalent "gain" that is **amplitude-dependent**.

For a non-linearity with input $x(t) = X \sin(\omega t)$:

$$N(X) = \frac{Y_1}{X} \angle \phi_1 = \frac{\sqrt{B_1^2 + A_1^2}}{X} \angle \tan^{-1}\left(\frac{A_1}{B_1}\right)$$

Where:

$$B_1 = \frac{1}{\pi} \int_{0}^{2\pi} y(t) \sin(\omega t) \, d(\omega t), \quad A_1 = \frac{1}{\pi} \int_{0}^{2\pi} y(t) \cos(\omega t) \, d(\omega t)$$

For memoryless, odd non-linearities (most common), $A_1 = 0$, so the DF is **real**:

$$N(X) = \frac{B_1}{X}$$

**Prediction of Limit Cycle (using DF):**

$$1 + N(X) G(j\omega) = 0 \implies G(j\omega) = -\frac{1}{N(X)}$$

The intersection of the Nyquist plot of $G(j\omega)$ with the locus of $-1/N(X)$ gives the predicted amplitude $X$ and frequency $\omega$ of the limit cycle.

### 2.4 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Formula / Concept | Expression | Application |
|---|-------------------|------------|-------------|
| 1 | General non-linear state equation | $\dot{x} = f(x, u, t)$ | System representation |
| 2 | Taylor series linearization (scalar) | $y \approx f(x_0) + f'(x_0) \cdot \Delta x$ | Approximate linearization |
| 3 | Jacobian matrix $A$ | $A = \partial f / \partial x \vert_{x_0, u_0}$ | State matrix of linearized system |
| 4 | Jacobian matrix $B$ | $B = \partial f / \partial u \vert_{x_0, u_0}$ | Input matrix of linearized system |
| 5 | Linearized state equation | $\Delta \dot{x} = A \Delta x + B \Delta u$ | Small-signal model |
| 6 | Phase plane slope equation | $dx_2 / dx_1 = f(x_1, x_2) / (-x_2)$ | Trajectory construction |
| 7 | Isocline equation | $f(x_1, x_2) + m \cdot x_2 = 0$ | Graphical method with slope $m$ |
| 8 | Describing function (memoryless, odd) | $N(X) = B_1 / X$ | Quasi-linear equivalent gain |
| 9 | Limit cycle condition | $1 + N(X) G(j\omega) = 0$ | Predict sustained oscillation |
| 10 | Characteristic equation (linearized) | $\det(\lambda I - A) = 0$ | Eigenvalue-based stability |
| 11 | Equilibrium condition | $f(x_0) = 0$ | Find singular points |
| 12 | Sign-definiteness (Lyapunov context) | $V(x) > 0$ and $\dot{V}(x) < 0$ | Stability check (Module 2 preview) |

> [!WARNING]
> **Unit Consistency Note:** The Describing Function $N(X)$ is **dimensionless** only when $X$ and $Y$ share the same physical units. For example, with a relay output in Volts and input in Volts, $N(X)$ is dimensionless. However, with a torque-non-linearity (input: angle, output: torque), one must carefully track units.

### 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

| Field | Application of Methods of Analysis |
|-------|-----------------------------------|
| **Aerospace** | Jacobian linearization of aircraft dynamics (stability derivatives: $C_{L_\alpha}$, $C_{m_q}$) |
| **Robotics** | Phase plane design of joint trajectory controllers with saturation |
| **Power Electronics** | Describing function analysis of switching converters (SMPS stability) |
| **Automotive** | Anti-lock braking systems (ABS) — non-linear friction models |
| **Chemical Process** | pH neutralization, CSTR dynamics — strongly non-linear |
| **Biomedical** | Drug dose-response modeling, neural firing dynamics |
| **Structural** | Building response under seismic (hysteretic) loading |

## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Exhaustive Derivation: Jacobian Linearization of a Non-Linear System

**Problem Setup:** Consider the non-linear system:

$$\dot{x}_1 = x_2$$

$$\dot{x}_2 = -x_1^3 - x_2 + u$$

**Step 1 — Identify the operating point (equilibrium).**
At equilibrium, $\dot{x}_1 = 0$ and $\dot{x}_2 = 0$. Taking $u_0 = 0$ (free response):

$$x_{2,0} = 0$$

$$-x_{1,0}^3 = 0 \implies x_{1,0} = 0$$

So the equilibrium point is $x_0 = [0, \; 0]^T$.

**Step 2 — Define deviation variables.**

$$\Delta x_1 = x_1 - x_{1,0} = x_1, \quad \Delta x_2 = x_2 - x_{2,0} = x_2$$

**Step 3 — Write the system in standard form $f(x, u)$.**

$$f(x, u) = \begin{bmatrix} f_1 \\ f_2 \end{bmatrix} = \begin{bmatrix} x_2 \\ -x_1^3 - x_2 + u \end{bmatrix}$$

**Step 4 — Compute the Jacobian $A = \partial f / \partial x$.**

$$A = \begin{bmatrix} \dfrac{\partial f_1}{\partial x_1} & \dfrac{\partial f_1}{\partial x_2} \\[10pt] \dfrac{\partial f_2}{\partial x_1} & \dfrac{\partial f_2}{\partial x_2} \end{bmatrix} = \begin{bmatrix} 0 & 1 \\ -3x_1^2 & -1 \end{bmatrix}$$

**Step 5 — Evaluate $A$ at the operating point $x_0 = [0, 0]^T$.**

$$A \big|_{x_0} = \begin{bmatrix} 0 & 1 \\ 0 & -1 \end{bmatrix}$$

**Step 6 — Compute the Jacobian $B = \partial f / \partial u$.**

$$B = \begin{bmatrix} \dfrac{\partial f_1}{\partial u} \\[10pt] \dfrac{\partial f_2}{\partial u} \end{bmatrix} = \begin{bmatrix} 0 \\ 1 \end{bmatrix}$$

**Step 7 — Evaluate $B$ at the operating point.**

$$B \big|_{x_0} = \begin{bmatrix} 0 \\ 1 \end{bmatrix}$$

**Step 8 — Write the linearized system in deviation form.**

$$\begin{bmatrix} \Delta \dot{x}_1 \\ \Delta \dot{x}_2 \end{bmatrix} = \begin{bmatrix} 0 & 1 \\ 0 & -1 \end{bmatrix} \begin{bmatrix} \Delta x_1 \\ \Delta x_2 \end{bmatrix} + \begin{bmatrix} 0 \\ 1 \end{bmatrix} \Delta u$$

**Step 9 — Stability check via eigenvalues of $A$.**

$$\det(\lambda I - A) = \det \begin{bmatrix} \lambda & -1 \\ 0 & \lambda + 1 \end{bmatrix} = \lambda(\lambda + 1) = 0$$

Eigenvalues: $\lambda_1 = 0$, $\lambda_2 = -1$.

**Step 10 — Interpret the result.**
Since $\lambda_1 = 0$ lies on the imaginary axis, the linearized system is **marginally stable (borderline)**. This means the linearization is **inconclusive** about the true non-linear stability — a textbook example of where linearization fails. The non-linear term $-x_1^3$ eventually stabilizes the system (it's a hardening spring), but the linear model cannot capture this.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Critical Insight:** The presence of eigenvalues on the $j\omega$-axis after linearization is a **signal to apply non-linear stability methods** (e.g., Lyapunov, Phase Plane) rather than trusting the linear prediction.

### 3.2 Exhaustive Derivation: Describing Function of an Ideal Relay (with Hysteresis)

**Problem Setup:** Find the describing function of a relay with output levels $\pm M$ and hysteresis half-width $h$ (so the relay switches at $x = +h$ on the way up and $x = -h$ on the way down).

**Step 1 — Input Assumption.** Assume a sinusoidal input:

$$x(t) = X \sin(\omega t), \quad X > h$$

**Step 2 — Output Description.** Due to hysteresis, the output switches states **later** than the input zero-crossings. Let $\theta_1$ be the switch-on angle and $\theta_2$ the switch-off angle. By symmetry of the hysteresis loop:

$$\theta_1 = \sin^{-1}(h/X)$$

The output $y(t)$ is:
- $y = +M$ for $\theta_1 < \omega t < \pi - \theta_1$ (a half-period later)
- $y = -M$ for $\pi + \theta_1 < \omega t < 2\pi - \theta_1$

By the symmetry of the relay with hysteresis, $y(t)$ is **odd**, so $A_1 = 0$.

**Step 3 — Compute $B_1$.**

$$B_1 = \frac{1}{\pi} \int_{0}^{2\pi} y(t) \sin(\omega t) \, d(\omega t)$$

By the half-wave symmetry $y(t + \pi) = -y(t)$:

$$B_1 = \frac{2}{\pi} \int_{\theta_1}^{\pi - \theta_1} M \sin(\omega t) \, d(\omega t)$$

**Step 4 — Evaluate the integral.**

$$B_1 = \frac{2M}{\pi} \Big[ -\cos(\omega t) \Big]_{\theta_1}^{\pi - \theta_1} = \frac{2M}{\pi} \Big[ -\cos(\pi - \theta_1) + \cos(\theta_1) \Big]$$

Using $\cos(\pi - \theta_1) = -\cos(\theta_1)$:

$$B_1 = \frac{2M}{\pi} \Big[ \cos(\theta_1) + \cos(\theta_1) \Big] = \frac{4M \cos(\theta_1)}{\pi}$$

**Step 5 — Substitute $\cos(\theta_1) = \sqrt{1 - h^2 / X^2}$.**

$$B_1 = \frac{4M}{\pi} \sqrt{1 - \frac{h^2}{X^2}}$$

**Step 6 — Form the describing function.**

$$N(X) = \frac{B_1}{X} = \frac{4M}{\pi X} \sqrt{1 - \frac{h^2}{X^2}}$$

**Step 7 — Special case check.** When $h = 0$ (ideal relay with no hysteresis):

$$N(X) = \frac{4M}{\pi X}$$

This matches the well-known result for an ideal relay (memoryless, odd) and is **independent of $X$** for $h = 0$ — it's a constant gain. For $h > 0$, $N(X)$ is **amplitude-dependent**.

**Step 8 — Physical interpretation.** As $X \to \infty$, $N(X) \to 0$ (the relay effectively acts like a small-signal amplifier when severely overdriven). As $X \to h^+$, $N(X) \to 0$ (boundary of operation). $N(X)$ attains a maximum at some intermediate $X$, which determines the most likely limit cycle amplitude.

### 3.3 Step-by-Step Phase Plane Analysis (Isocline Method)

**Problem Setup:** Construct the phase trajectory for:

$$\ddot{x} + 0.5 \dot{x} + \sin(x) = 0$$

**Step 1 — Convert to state variables.** Let $x_1 = x$, $x_2 = \dot{x}_1$. Then:

$$\dot{x}_1 = x_2$$

$$\dot{x}_2 = -\sin(x_1) - 0.5 x_2$$

**Step 2 — Find equilibrium points.** Setting $\dot{x}_1 = 0$ and $\dot{x}_2 = 0$:

$$x_2 = 0, \quad \sin(x_1) = 0 \implies x_1 = n\pi, \quad n \in \mathbb{Z}$$

Equilibria: $(0, 0)$, $(\pi, 0)$, $(-\pi, 0)$, $(2\pi, 0)$, $\ldots$

**Step 3 — Derive the slope equation.**

$$\frac{dx_2}{dx_1} = \frac{-\sin(x_1) - 0.5 x_2}{x_2} = m \text{ (constant, the isocline condition)}$$

**Step 4 — Isocline equation.** Setting $dx_2/dx_1 = m$:

$$x_2 = \frac{-\sin(x_1)}{m + 0.5}$$

For each chosen slope $m$, this equation gives the locus of points in the phase plane where trajectories cross with that slope.

**Step 5 — Draw isoclines for selected values of $m$.**
- For $m = 0$ (horizontal crossings): $x_2 = -2\sin(x_1)$
- For $m = \infty$ (vertical crossings): $x_2 = 0$ (the $x_1$-axis)
- For $m = -0.5$ (a special isocline): $x_2 = $ undefined (denominator = 0) — asymptote.

**Step 6 — Sketch trajectories.** At each point on each isocline, draw a short line with slope $m$. Connect these line segments smoothly to form the trajectories. Closed trajectories (limit cycles) may emerge around unstable equilibria like $(\pi, 0)$.

**Step 7 — Classify equilibria.** Using Jacobian linearization at each equilibrium:

- At $(0, 0)$: $A = \begin{bmatrix} 0 & 1 \\ -\cos(0) & -0.5 \end{bmatrix} = \begin{bmatrix} 0 & 1 \\ -1 & -0.5 \end{bmatrix}$ — Eigenvalues are complex with negative real part → **Stable Focus** (spiral sink).
- At $(\pi, 0)$: $A = \begin{bmatrix} 0 & 1 \\ -\cos(\pi) & -0.5 \end{bmatrix} = \begin{bmatrix} 0 & 1 \\ 1 & -0.5 \end{bmatrix}$ — Eigenvalues are real, one positive → **Unstable Saddle**.

> [!TIP]
> **Exam Tip:** In the KTU exam, when asked to draw phase portraits, you are typically required to **(a)** find equilibria, **(b)** classify them, and **(c)** sketch 2-3 trajectories. A complete analysis with isoclines is preferred for full marks.
