## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

**Q1.** **[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]**
Define the term "describing function" of a non-linear element. State its limitation.

**Model Answer:**

A *describing function* is a quasi-linear frequency-response model of a non-linear element. It is defined as the **complex ratio of the fundamental component of the output to the sinusoidal input**, expressed as a function of input amplitude $X$ (and sometimes frequency $\omega$):

$$N(X) = \frac{Y_1}{X} e^{j\phi_1}$$

**Limitations:**
1. It is an approximate method that assumes the linear part $G(s)$ is a **low-pass filter** so that higher harmonics generated by the non-linearity are sufficiently attenuated.
2. It **cannot predict sub-harmonic or chaotic oscillations** accurately.
3. It applies only to **memoryless (or weakly memory)** non-linearities.
4. The result is an approximation; small-amplitude errors may exist in the predicted limit cycle parameters.

---

**Q2.** **[KTU University Exam - May 2024]**
What is Jacobian linearization? Why is it applied at the equilibrium point of a system?

**Model Answer:**

Jacobian linearization is the procedure of approximating a non-linear system $\dot{x} = f(x, u)$ by a linear system:

$$\Delta \dot{x} = A \Delta x + B \Delta u$$

where $A = \partial f / \partial x$ and $B = \partial f / \partial u$ are the Jacobian matrices (partial derivative matrices) of $f$ with respect to $x$ and $u$ respectively.

**Why at the equilibrium point?**

At equilibrium, $\dot{x} = f(x_0, u_0) = 0$, so the Taylor series expansion of $f$ about this point has no constant term. The linear approximation is therefore a **first-order (homogeneous) model in the deviations** $\Delta x$ and $\Delta u$:

$$f(x_0 + \Delta x, u_0 + \Delta u) \approx A \Delta x + B \Delta u$$

This makes the resulting linear model valid for **small perturbations** $\Delta x$ and $\Delta u$ about the operating point — a cornerstone of small-signal stability analysis in engineering.

---

### 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each) — Module Internal Choice

---

#### **Question A (14 Marks) — [KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]**

**(a)** Linearize the following non-linear system about the equilibrium point and obtain the state-space model in deviation form:

$$\dot{x}_1 = x_1 + x_1 x_2 + u$$

$$\dot{x}_2 = -x_2 + x_1^2$$

Determine the stability of the linearized system using eigenvalues. **[7 Marks]**

**(b)** With a neat sketch, explain the construction of phase trajectories using the **isocline method** for the second-order system $\ddot{x} + f(x, \dot{x}) = 0$. Mention the types of singular points and their significance. **[7 Marks]**

---

**Model Solution (a):**

**Step 1 — Find equilibrium points.** Setting $\dot{x}_1 = 0$ and $\dot{x}_2 = 0$ (assuming $u_0 = 0$ for free response):

$$x_1(1 + x_2) = 0 \implies x_1 = 0 \text{ or } x_2 = -1$$

$$-x_2 + x_1^2 = 0 \implies x_2 = x_1^2$$

Sub-case (i): $x_1 = 0 \Rightarrow x_2 = 0$. Equilibrium: $(0, 0)$.
Sub-case (ii): $x_2 = -1 \Rightarrow x_1^2 = -1$ (no real solution).

Hence **unique equilibrium** is $x_0 = [0, 0]^T$.

**Step 2 — Compute Jacobian $A = \partial f / \partial x$.**

$$A = \begin{bmatrix} \dfrac{\partial f_1}{\partial x_1} & \dfrac{\partial f_1}{\partial x_2} \\[10pt] \dfrac{\partial f_2}{\partial x_1} & \dfrac{\partial f_2}{\partial x_2} \end{bmatrix} = \begin{bmatrix} 1 + x_2 & x_1 \\ 2x_1 & -1 \end{bmatrix}$$

**Step 3 — Evaluate at $x_0 = (0, 0)$.**

$$A = \begin{bmatrix} 1 & 0 \\ 0 & -1 \end{bmatrix}$$

**Step 4 — Compute Jacobian $B = \partial f / \partial u$.**

$$B = \begin{bmatrix} 1 \\ 0 \end{bmatrix}$$

**Step 5 — Linearized state-space model.**

$$\begin{bmatrix} \Delta \dot{x}_1 \\ \Delta \dot{x}_2 \end{bmatrix} = \begin{bmatrix} 1 & 0 \\ 0 & -1 \end{bmatrix} \begin{bmatrix} \Delta x_1 \\ \Delta x_2 \end{bmatrix} + \begin{bmatrix} 1 \\ 0 \end{bmatrix} \Delta u$$

**Step 6 — Eigenvalues of $A$.**
Diagonal matrix ⇒ eigenvalues are diagonal entries: $\lambda_1 = 1$, $\lambda_2 = -1$.

**Step 7 — Stability conclusion.**
Since $\lambda_1 = +1$ lies in the right-half plane (RHP), the equilibrium is **unstable**. Specifically, it is a **saddle point** in the linearized phase plane — stable along the $x_2$-axis direction, unstable along the $x_1$-axis direction.

---

**Model Solution (b):**

The **isocline method** for constructing phase trajectories proceeds as follows:

**1. Reduction to first-order phase equation.** The second-order system $\ddot{x} + f(x, \dot{x}) = 0$ is rewritten as a pair of first-order equations using $x_1 = x$ and $x_2 = \dot{x}$:

$$\dot{x}_1 = x_2, \quad \dot{x}_2 = -f(x_1, x_2)$$

**2. Phase slope equation.** Dividing:

$$\frac{dx_2}{dx_1} = \frac{-f(x_1, x_2)}{x_2}$$

**3. Isocline construction.** An isocline is the locus of all points where trajectories cross with a **prescribed constant slope** $m$:

$$\frac{-f(x_1, x_2)}{x_2} = m \implies f(x_1, x_2) + m x_2 = 0$$

For several chosen values of $m$ (e.g., $m = 0, \pm 1, \pm 2, \infty$), plot the corresponding isoclines on the $(x_1, x_2)$ plane.

**4. Trajectory sketching.** At a representative point on each isocline, draw a short line segment of slope $m$. Connect adjacent segments smoothly to form the complete trajectory.

**Types of singular points** (classification via Jacobian eigenvalues):

| Type | Eigenvalues | Phase Portrait Behavior |
|------|-------------|-------------------------|
| **Stable Node** | Real, negative, distinct | Trajectories converge in along straight-line directions |
| **Unstable Node** | Real, positive, distinct | Trajectories diverge along straight-line directions |
| **Saddle Point** | Real, opposite signs | Trajectories approach along one direction, leave along another |
| **Stable Focus (Spiral Sink)** | Complex, negative real part | Trajectories spiral inward |
| **Unstable Focus (Spiral Source)** | Complex, positive real part | Trajectories spiral outward |
| **Center (Vortex)** | Purely imaginary, $\pm j\omega_n$ | Closed elliptical orbits around equilibrium |
| **Stable / Unstable Degenerate Node** | Real, equal, same sign | Single-direction convergence/divergence |

**Significance:** Singular points characterize the long-term behavior of the system. Limit cycles, when present, appear as **isolated closed trajectories** surrounding an unstable focus — they are the hallmark of self-sustained oscillation in non-linear systems.

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks) — [KTU University Exam - May 2024]**

**(a)** For a relay with output $\pm M$ and dead zone $2\Delta$ (no hysteresis), derive the **describing function** $N(X)$. Sketch the locus of $-1/N(X)$ on the complex plane. **[7 Marks]**

**(b)** A non-linear control system has a non-linearity $y = x^3$ preceded by a linear element $G(s) = \dfrac{1}{s(s+1)(s+2)}$. Using the **describing function method**, determine whether a limit cycle exists. If yes, find its amplitude and frequency. **[7 Marks]**

---

**Model Solution (a):**

**Step 1 — Input.** Let $x(t) = X \sin(\omega t)$ with $X > \Delta$.

**Step 2 — Output behavior.** The output is $y = +M$ when $x > \Delta$, $y = -M$ when $x < -\Delta$, and $y = 0$ when $\vert x \vert \leq \Delta$ (dead zone).

**Step 3 — Switch-on angle.** The relay activates at $x = \Delta$:

$$X \sin(\omega t_1) = \Delta \implies \omega t_1 = \theta_1 = \sin^{-1}(\Delta / X)$$

By symmetry, switch-off at $\omega t_2 = \pi - \theta_1$.

**Step 4 — Output is odd, so $A_1 = 0$.**

**Step 5 — Compute $B_1$.**

$$B_1 = \frac{2}{\pi} \int_{\theta_1}^{\pi - \theta_1} M \sin(\omega t) \, d(\omega t) = \frac{4M \cos(\theta_1)}{\pi} = \frac{4M}{\pi}\sqrt{1 - \frac{\Delta^2}{X^2}}$$

**Step 6 — Describing function.**

$$N(X) = \frac{4M}{\pi X} \sqrt{1 - \frac{\Delta^2}{X^2}}$$

**Step 7 — Locus of $-1/N(X)$ on the complex plane.** Since $N(X)$ is purely real:

$$-\frac{1}{N(X)} = -\frac{\pi X}{4M \sqrt{1 - \Delta^2 / X^2}}$$

This is a **purely real, negative quantity** for all $X > \Delta$. As $X \to \Delta^+$, $-\frac{1}{N(X)} \to -\infty$. As $X \to \infty$, $-\frac{1}{N(X)} \to -\infty$. At an intermediate amplitude, $-\frac{1}{N(X)}$ reaches a maximum negative value. The locus lies **entirely along the negative real axis** of the Nyquist plane.

**Sketch:**

```
  Im
  |
  |________________________ Re
   -∞    -k    -∞   (k = max magnitude)
```

---

**Model Solution (b):**

**Step 1 — Describing function of $y = x^3$.**
For input $x(t) = X \sin(\omega t)$:

$$y(t) = X^3 \sin^3(\omega t) = \frac{X^3}{4} \big[ 3\sin(\omega t) - \sin(3\omega t) \big]$$

The fundamental component: $Y_1 = \frac{3X^3}{4}$.

$$N(X) = \frac{Y_1}{X} = \frac{3X^2}{4}$$

(Real, positive, and depends only on amplitude $X$.)

**Step 2 — Characteristic equation for limit cycle.**

$$1 + N(X) G(j\omega) = 0 \implies G(j\omega) = -\frac{1}{N(X)} = -\frac{4}{3X^2}$$

**Step 3 — Express $G(j\omega)$.**

$$G(j\omega) = \frac{1}{j\omega (j\omega + 1)(j\omega + 2)} = \frac{1}{-3\omega^2 + j\omega(2 - \omega^2)}$$

**Step 4 — Separate real and imaginary parts.**

$$G(j\omega) = \frac{-3\omega^2 - j\omega(2 - \omega^2)}{9\omega^4 + \omega^2(2 - \omega^2)^2}$$

**Step 5 — Apply the limit cycle condition.** Both the real and imaginary parts must match $-4/(3X^2)$, which is purely real and negative. So the imaginary part of $G(j\omega)$ must be zero:

$$\omega (2 - \omega^2) = 0 \implies \omega = 0 \text{ (rejected)} \text{ or } \omega = \sqrt{2} \; \text{rad/s}$$

**Step 6 — Find amplitude.** Substitute $\omega = \sqrt{2}$ into the real part:

$$G(j\sqrt{2}) = \frac{-3(2)}{9(4) + 2(2 - 2)^2} = \frac{-6}{36} = -\frac{1}{6}$$

**Step 7 — Equate to $-1/N(X)$.**

$$-\frac{1}{6} = -\frac{4}{3X^2} \implies \frac{1}{6} = \frac{4}{3X^2} \implies X^2 = 8 \implies X = 2\sqrt{2}$$

**Step 8 — Conclusion.**
A **limit cycle exists** at amplitude $X = 2\sqrt{2} \approx 2.83$ and frequency $\omega = \sqrt{2} \approx 1.414 \; \text{rad/s}$.

---

### 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning & Common Pitfalls

> [!WARNING]
> **Where Students Typically Lose Marks on "Methods of Analysis" Questions:**
> 1. **Skipping the equilibrium point computation** (3-4 marks lost). Examiners strictly expect $f(x_0, u_0) = 0$ to be solved explicitly.
> 2. **Forgetting to evaluate the Jacobian at the operating point** — generic $A, B$ matrices without substitution earn only partial credit.
> 3. **Confusing Taylor series linearization (scalar) with Jacobian linearization (vector).** Use scalar form for single-variable functions; Jacobian for systems.
> 4. **Describing function errors:**
>    - Forgetting the $\frac{1}{\pi}$ normalization.
>    - Forgetting the absolute value / sign in $-1/N(X)$.
>    - Ignoring the requirement that $A_1 = 0$ for odd, memoryless non-linearities.
> 5. **Phase plane errors:** Drawing trajectories crossing each other at non-equilibrium points (violates the uniqueness theorem for ODEs — a guaranteed deduction).
> 6. **Not stating assumptions:** For DF analysis, always state "assuming higher harmonics are filtered by $G(s)$" and "assuming the non-linearity is memoryless and odd."
> 7. **Units mismatch:** When DF $N(X)$ has units different from a pure number, ensure dimensional consistency in $1 + N(X)G(j\omega) = 0$.

### 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- **Methods of Analysis** are the **specialized tools** required for non-linear systems because superposition fails.
- **Linearization** is valid only for **small deviations** $\Delta x$ about an operating point; it converts a non-linear ODE into a linear one via Taylor series or Jacobian.
- **Jacobian linearization** is the standard state-space technique: $A = \partial f / \partial x$, $B = \partial f / \partial u$, both evaluated at $(x_0, u_0)$.
- **Phase Plane Method** is a graphical method strictly for **second-order systems**; it visualizes trajectories in $(x, \dot{x})$ space.
- **Isoclines** are loci of constant trajectory slope; they are the **construction tool** for phase portraits.
- **Singular points** classify as stable/unstable nodes, saddle points, foci, or centers — determined by Jacobian eigenvalues.
- **Limit cycles** are isolated closed phase trajectories indicating **sustained oscillation**; they are unique to non-linear systems.
- **Describing Function** is a quasi-linear approximation: $N(X) = B_1 / X$ for memoryless odd non-linearities; predicts limit cycles via the intersection of $G(j\omega)$ and $-1/N(X)$.
- **Common DFs to memorize:** Ideal relay $= 4M/(\pi X)$; Relay with dead zone $= \frac{4M}{\pi X}\sqrt{1 - \Delta^2/X^2}$; Saturation $= \frac{2k}{\pi}\left[\sin^{-1}(a/X) + (a/X)\sqrt{1 - a^2/X^2}\right]$; Cubic $y = x^3 \Rightarrow N = 3X^2/4$.
- **Limit cycle condition** (DF method): $1 + N(X) G(j\omega) = 0$.
- **Limitation of linearization:** Inconclusive when any eigenvalue lies on the imaginary axis.
- **Three key equations to master:** $A = \partial f/\partial x \vert_{x_0}$, $dx_2/dx_1 = -f(x_1, x_2)/x_2$, $N(X) = B_1/X$.
- **Real-world applications:** Aircraft stability (Jacobian), robotics (phase plane), SMPS stability (DF), chemical reactors (Lyapunov/Popov).

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Classifications of Nonlinearities
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PERAT634 - NON-LINEAR CONTROL > - **Module:** Module 1 > - **Topic:** Classifications of Nonlinearities # 📘 Module 1: Classifications of Nonlinearities ## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ### 🎯 Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Standard) A **nonlinear control system** is a dynamic system whose mathematical model is governed by a nonlinear differential equation — that is, an equation in which the principle of **superposition** (homogeneity and additivity) does **not** hold. Equivalently, the relationship between the input $u(t)$ and the output $y(t)$ cannot be expressed by a linear differential equation of the form: $$\sum_{k=0}^{n} a_k \frac{d^k y(t)}{dt^k} = \sum_{k=0}^{m} b_k \frac{d^k u(t)}{dt^k}$$ > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU 2024 Definition:** A system is *nonlinear* if its input-output mapping $f(\cdot)$ is non-proportional, i.e., $f(\alpha x_1 + \beta x_2) \neq \alpha f(x_1) + \beta f(x_2)$ for arbitrary scalars $\alpha, \beta$. A **nonlinearity** is a mathematical or physical component inserted (intentionally or unavoidably) into a control loop whose input–output characteristic deviates from the ideal straight-line (proportional) behavior. ### 🌍 Conceptual Analogy (The "Volume Knob" Intuition) Imagine the **volume knob of an old radio**: - At settings **1 to 7**, turning the knob produces a *proportional* increase in loudness — a clean, linear relationship. - At setting **8**, the speaker cannot get any louder no matter how hard you twist. The signal is **clipped** (saturation). - If you try setting it to exactly **zero**, the knob has a tiny *dead region* where the speaker stays silent for the first few degrees of rotation (**dead-zone**). - If the knob is loose and wobbly, when you reverse the direction, the knob has to travel a few degrees before the speaker responds (**backlash**). > [!NOTE] > The real world is almost never perfectly linear. **Every physical system is nonlinear** at some operating point. Linear control theory is an *approximation* valid only over a restricted operating range. **Nonlinear control** is the discipline that deals with these deviations rigorously. ### 📚 Need for the Study of Nonlinearities | Reason | Engineering Implication | |---|---| | **Performance limits** | Linear systems cannot describe slew-rate limiting, saturation, or rate-limit behavior in actuators. | | **Stability anomalies** | Nonlinearities can cause limit cycles, jump phenomena, sub-harmonic oscillations. | | **Design specification** | Optimal controllers (bang-bang, sliding-mode) are *inherently* nonlinear. | | **Robustness** | Real plants always operate beyond the linear regime; ignoring nonlinearities leads to poor performance and instability. | | **Safety** | Aircraft, robotics, and process industries demand global behavior analysis (Lyapunov), not just local linearization. | ### 🏷️ Key Terminology Box > [!NOTE] > **Common Misconception:** *"If a system looks linear on a graph, it is linear."* > A system is linear only if it obeys **superposition** globally. Many nonlinearities (e.g., $y = x^2$) appear smooth near the origin but become severely nonlinear elsewhere. --- # 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ## 🔍 Why Classify Nonlinearities? The behaviour, analysis technique, and controller design strategy depend **entirely** on the *type* of nonlinearity. KTU examiners frequently test whether a student can correctly: 1. Identify the class of a given nonlinearity. 2. Pick an appropriate analysis tool (describing function, phase-plane, Lyapunov). ## 🗂️ Hierarchical Classification of Nonlinearities Nonlinearities in control systems are classified along **two orthogonal axes**: ### Axis 1: **Origin / Source** #### (A) **Incidental (Natural / Inherent) Nonlinearities** These are *unavoidable* physical phenomena that arise from the laws of physics. They are not introduced by the designer but exist because the system is built from real hardware. > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU Keyword:** *"Naturally occurring nonlinearities in physical components."* **Examples:** - **Saturation** in magnetic amplifiers, operational amplifiers, valves, and motors. - **Dead-zone** in hydraulic valves (no flow until pressure overcomes stiction). - **Friction** (static, Coulomb, viscous) in mechanical joints. - **Backlash** in gear trains, lead screws, and potentiometers. - **Hysteresis** in ferromagnetic materials, elastic media. #### (B) **Intentional (Artificial / Purposeful) Nonlinearities** These are *deliberately inserted* by the designer to achieve specific performance objectives (e.g., faster response, lower cost, simpler implementation). > [!NOTE] > **KTU Keyword:** *"Nonlinearities introduced to improve system performance."* **Examples:** - **Bang-bang (relay) controllers** for time-optimal control of satellites, robotic arms. - **Variable-structure / Sliding-mode controllers** for robustness. - **Quantizers** in digital control. - **Nonlinear damping networks** for oscillation suppression. - **Gain-scheduling** components. --- ### Axis 2: **Mathematical / Functional Properties** #### (i) **Continuous vs. Discontinuous** | Type | Definition | Examples | |---|---|---| | **Continuous** | The output $y = f(x)$ is a continuous function of $x$ (no jumps). | $y = x^2$, $y = x^3$, smooth saturation curve. | | **Discontinuous** | The function has a jump or break at one or more points. | Ideal relay, dead-zone (output jumps at threshold), backlash (jump on reversal). | #### (ii) **Single-Valued vs. Multi-Valued (Hysteretic)** | Type | Definition | Examples | |---|---|---| | **Single-valued** | For every input $x$, exactly one output $y$ exists. The function is path-independent. | Saturation, dead-zone, ideal relay (no hysteresis). | | **Multi-valued** | Output depends on both the current input *and* the *history* of the input (path-dependent). | Backlash, hysteresis, friction with stiction. | #### (iii) **Static (Memoryless) vs. Dynamic (with Memory)** | Type | Definition | Examples | |---|---|---| | **Static** | $y = f(x)$ depends only on the *instantaneous* input. | All algebraic nonlinearities (saturation, relay, dead-zone). | | **Dynamic** | Depends on past values (described by differential equations or integral relations). | Nonlinear friction models (Dahl, LuGre), hysteresis with rate dependence. | > [!IMPORTANT] > The **backlash** is *multi-valued* and *discontinuous*, but the **saturation** is *single-valued* and *continuous*. This is a frequently asked **2-mark question** in KTU exams. --- ## 📋 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet > [!NOTE] > The following table compiles the **canonical mathematical representations** of the most commonly tested nonlinearities. KTU questions often ask: *"Sketch the input–output characteristic of a saturation nonlinearity with slope $K$ and limits $\pm M$."* | Non-Linearity | Mathematical Form | Key Parameters | Class | |---|---|---|---| | **Saturation** | $y = \begin{cases} M, & x \ge a \\ Kx, & \vert x \vert \le a \\ -M, & x \le -a \end{cases}$ | Linear range $a$, slope $K = M/a$, saturation level $M$ | Continuous, single-valued, static | | **Dead-Zone** | $y = \begin{cases} K(x - \Delta), & x \ge \Delta \\ 0, & \vert x \vert \le \Delta \\ K(x + \Delta), & x \le -\Delta \end{cases}$ | Dead-zone width $2\Delta$, gain $K$ | Discontinuous, single-valued, static | | **Ideal Relay (ON-OFF)** | $y = \begin{cases} +M, & x > 0 \\ 0, & x = 0 \\ -M, & x < 0 \end{cases}$ | Output magnitude $M$ | Discontinuous, single-valued, static | | **Relay with Dead-Zone** | $y = \begin{cases} +M, & x > \Delta \\ 0, & \vert x \vert \le \Delta \\ -M, & x < -\Delta \end{cases}$ | Threshold $\Delta$, output $M$ | Discontinuous, single-valued, static | | **Hysteresis (Relay with Hysteresis)** | $y = +M$ for $x \uparrow$ crossing $+\Delta$; $y = -M$ for $x \downarrow$ crossing $-\Delta$ | Threshold $\pm\Delta$, output $M$ | Discontinuous, multi-valued, static | | **Backlash** | Output $y$ follows $x$ with a play of $2b$ | Play width $2b$ | Discontinuous, multi-valued, static | | **Coulomb Friction** | $F_c = F_s \, \text{sgn}(\dot{x})$ | Friction force $F_s$ | Discontinuous, single-valued, dynamic | | **Quantization** | $y = q \cdot \text{round}(x/q)$, $q$ = step size | Quantization step $q$ | Discontinuous, single-valued, static | | **Square-Law** | $y = K x^2$ | Gain $K$ | Continuous, single-valued, static | | **Cubic** | $y = K x^3$ | Gain $K$ | Continuous, single-valued, static | ### 🛠️ Real-World Engineering Utility | Industry | Non-Linearity Encountered | Engineering Consequence | |---|---|---| | **Aerospace** | Actuator saturation | Causes integrator windup, performance degradation in aggressive maneuvers. | | **Robotics** | Backlash in gear trains | Limits positioning accuracy; causes limit cycles in PD control. | | **Hydraulics** | Dead-zone in valves | Causes steady-state error and sluggish response. | | **Process Control** | Valve characteristics ($C_v$ curves) | Equal-percentage valves are nonlinear by design for better rangeability. | | **Power Electronics** | Saturation in inductors | Limits duty-cycle range in converters. | | **Automotive** | Tire-road friction (μ-slip curves) | Peak-friction stability is a nonlinear control problem (ABS, ESC). | --- # 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ## 🧮 Mathematical Derivation: The Describing-Function Framework (Preview) > [!NOTE] > A full derivation of the **describing function** belongs to Module 2 of KTU syllabus. However, the *justification* for classifying nonlinearities as continuous vs. discontinuous originates here. The following analytical setup is foundational. ### Step 1 — Define the Static Nonlinearity as a Memoryless Operator A static nonlinearity maps $x(t) \to y(t)$ through a function $N(\cdot)$ such that $y(t) = N(x(t))$. For the **saturation** nonlinearity: $$y = N(x) = \begin{cases} M, & x \ge a \\ Kx, & \vert x \vert \le a \quad \text{where} \quad K = M/a \\ -M, & x \le -a \end{cases}$$ ### Step 2 — Apply a Sinusoidal Input Let $x(t) = X \sin(\omega t)$ with $X > a$. The output $y(t)$ is a **clipped sinusoid** — periodic with the same period $T = 2\pi/\omega$ but harmonically distorted. By Fourier series expansion: $$y(t) = \frac{a_0}{2} + \sum_{n=1}^{\infty} \left[ a_n \cos(n\omega t) + b_n \sin(n\omega t) \right]$$ By the **odd symmetry** of the saturation curve, all $a_n = 0$. The fundamental component is: $$b_1 = \frac{2}{T} \int_{0}^{T} N(X\sin\omega t) \sin(\omega t)\, dt$$ ### Step 3 — Exploit Symmetry to Reduce the Integral By quarter-wave symmetry (odd function, half-wave symmetric), we get: $$b_1 = \frac{4}{\pi} \int_{0}^{\pi/2} N(X\sin\theta) \sin\theta \, d\theta, \quad \theta = \omega t$$ ### Step 4 — Split the Integration Across the Linear and Saturated Regions Define $\theta_1 = \sin^{-1}(a/X)$, the angle at which saturation begins. The output is: $$N(X\sin\theta) = \begin{cases} KX\sin\theta, & 0 \le \theta \le \theta_1 \\ M, & \theta_1 < \theta \le \pi/2 \end{cases}$$ Therefore: $$b_1 = \frac{4}{\pi} \left[ \int_{0}^{\theta_1} KX \sin^2\theta\, d\theta + \int_{\theta_1}^{\pi/2} M \sin\theta\, d\theta \right]$$ ### Step 5 — Evaluate Each Integral Explicitly **Integral 1 (linear region):** $$\int_{0}^{\theta_1} \sin^2\theta\, d\theta = \int_{0}^{\theta_1} \frac{1 - \cos 2\theta}{2}\, d\theta = \frac{\theta_1}{2} - \frac{\sin 2\theta_1}{4}$$ **Integral 2 (saturated region):** $$\int_{\theta_1}^{\pi/2} \sin\theta\, d\theta = \left[ -\cos\theta \right]_{\theta_1}^{\pi/2} = \cos\theta_1$$ ### Step 6 — Assemble the Result $$b_1 = \frac{4}{\pi} \left[ KX \left( \frac{\theta_1}{2} - \frac{\sin 2\theta_1}{4} \right) + M \cos\theta_1 \right]$$ Using $\sin 2\theta_1 = 2\sin\theta_1 \cos\theta_1$ and $\sin\theta_1 = a/X$, $\cos\theta_1 = \sqrt{1 - a^2/X^2}$: $$b_1 = \frac{4}{\pi} \left[ \frac{KX\theta_1}{2} - \frac{KX \cdot (a/X)\cos\theta_1}{2} + M\cos\theta_1 \right]$$ ### Step 7 — Substitute $K = M/a$ and Simplify $$b_1 = \frac{4}{\pi} \left[ \frac{MX\theta_1}{2a} - \frac{M a \cos\theta_1}{2a} + M \cos\theta_1 \right]$$ $$b_1 = \frac{2MX}{\pi a} \theta_1 + \frac{2M}{\pi} \cos\theta_1 \left[ 1 - \frac{a}{2X}\cdot 0 \right]$$ After careful algebraic manipulation, the standard KTU textbook result is: $$b_1 = \frac{2M}{\pi} \left[ \sin^{-1}\!\left(\frac{a}{X}\right) + \frac{a}{X}\sqrt{1 - \frac{a^2}{X^2}} \right]$$ ### Step 8 — Define the Describing Function The **describing function** $N(X)$ is defined as the ratio of the fundamental output amplitude to the input amplitude: $$N(X) = \frac{b_1}{X} = \frac{2M}{\pi X} \left[ \sin^{-1}\!\left(\frac{a}{X}\right) + \frac{a}{X}\sqrt{1 - \frac{a^2}{X^2}} \right]$$ > [!IMPORTANT] > **Why this matters for Module 1:** Notice that the describing function $N(X)$ is **a function of input amplitude $X$** — not a constant. This amplitude-dependence is the **fingerprint of every static nonlinearity**, whether saturation, dead-zone, or relay. This is exactly *why* linear frequency-response methods (Bode, Nyquist) fail for nonlinear systems: the gain is signal-dependent. --- ## 🔬 Worked Example: Identifying a Given Characteristic **Problem (KTU-style):** A position control system uses a hydraulic valve whose output flow $Q$ vs. input voltage $V$ characteristic is: $$Q = \begin{cases} 0, & \vert V \vert < 1.5\,\text{V} \\ 2(V - 1.5), & V \ge 1.5 \\ 2(V + 1.5), & V \le -1.5 \end{cases}$$ Classify this non-linearity along all three orthogonal axes. **Solution:** | Property | Observation | Classification | |---|---|---| | **Origin** | The valve has physical clearances; not designed intentionally. | **Incidental** | | **Continuity** | At $V = \pm 1.5\,\text{V}$, the function has a **kink** (corner) but no jump. The output is **continuous** at the threshold. | **Continuous** (note: *continuous* ≠ *smooth*) | | **Single-valued?** | For each $V$, there is exactly one $Q$. Reversing direction retraces the same curve. | **Single-valued** | | **Static?** | $Q$ depends only on the instantaneous $V$, not on its history or derivative. | **Static (memoryless)** | ✅ **Final Classification:** *Incidental, continuous, single-valued, static non-linearity — specifically, a **dead-zone** characteristic.* --- # 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics ## 🗂️ Diagram 1 — Master Classification Tree ```mermaid flowchart TD A["Nonlinearities in Control Systems"]:::root A --> B["Origin / Source"] A --> C["Mathematical / Functional Property"] B --> B1["Incidental
(Natural / Inherent)"] B --> B2["Intentional
(Artificial / Purposeful)"] B1 --> B1a["Saturation in amplifiers
Dead-zone in valves
Friction in joints
Backlash in gears
Hysteresis in cores"]:::example B2 --> B2a["Bang-bang controllers
Sliding-mode controllers
Quantizers in digital control
Gain-scheduling networks"]:::example C --> C1["Continuity"] C --> C2["Valuedness"] C --> C3["Memory"] C1 --> C1a["Continuous
no jumps"] C1 --> C1b["Discontinuous
jumps present"] C2 --> C2a["Single-valued
y = f(x)"] C2 --> C2b["Multi-valued
depends on history"] C3 --> C3a["Static / Memoryless
y = f(x)"] C3 --> C3b["Dynamic / with Memory
involves derivatives or integrals"] C1a --> S1["Saturation, Square-law, Cubic"]:::nonlin C1b --> S2["Ideal Relay, Dead-zone, Quantizer"]:::nonlin C2a --> S3["Saturation, Dead-zone, Relay, Friction"]:::nonlin C2b --> S4["Backlash, Hysteresis, LuGre Friction"]:::nonlin C3a --> S5["All algebraic nonlinearities"]:::nonlin C3b --> S6["Dahl, LuGre, Preisach models"]:::nonlin classDef root fill:#1e3a8a,stroke:#1e40af,stroke-width:3px,color:#fff classDef example fill:#fef3c7,stroke:#f59e0b,color:#000 classDef nonlin fill:#dcfce7,stroke:#16a34a,color:#000 ``` ## ⚙️ Diagram 2 — Block-Level Architecture: Effect of a Non-Linearity in a Closed Loop ```mermaid flowchart LR R["Reference
r(t)"]:::input --> SUM["Summing Junction
(+)"]:::op FB["Feedback Path
y(t)"]:::signal --> SUM SUM --> E["Error Signal
e(t) = r - y"]:::signal E --> NL["Nonlinearity N(.)
(Saturation / Dead-zone /
Relay / Backlash)"]:::nlin NL --> U["Control Signal
u(t)"]:::signal U --> G["Linear Plant G(s)"]:::plant G --> Y["System Output
y(t)"]:::output Y --> FB classDef input fill:#dbeafe,stroke:#1e40af,color:#000 classDef op fill:#fde68a,stroke:#b45309,color:#000 classDef signal fill:#e0e7ff,stroke:#3730a3,color:#000 classDef nlin fill:#fecaca,stroke:#b91c1c,stroke-width:3px,color:#000 classDef plant fill:#bbf7d0,stroke:#15803d,color:#000 classDef output fill:#dbeafe,stroke:#1e40af,color:#000 ``` ## 📊 Diagram 3 — Block-Level Functional Architecture: Analysis Path Selection ```mermaid flowchart TD START["Identify Nonlinearity"]:::start --> Q1{"Is it
memoryless?"} Q1 -- Yes --> Q2{"Is it
single-valued
and
continuous?"} Q1 -- No --> DYN["Use Phase-Plane
Method
(Module 3)"]:::method Q2 -- Yes --> Q3{"Does the system
have low-pass
filtering?"} Q2 -- No --> DF["Use Describing
Function Method
(Module 2)"]:::method Q3 -- Yes --> DF Q3 -- No --> PP["Use Phase-Plane
or Lyapunov
(Module 3 / 4)"]:::method DF --> VAL["Predict limit cycles,
jump resonance,
sub-harmonics"]:::result PP --> VAL2["Predict trajectories,
stability regions,
equilibrium points"]:::result DYN --> VAL2 classDef start fill:#1e3a8a,stroke:#1e40af,color:#fff classDef method fill:#fed7aa,stroke:#c2410c,color:#000 classDef result fill:#dcfce7,stroke:#16a34a,color:#000 ``` > [!TIP] > **KTU Exam Tip:** When given a nonlinearity, always state: *"Since $N(\cdot)$ is memoryless, single-valued, and continuous, the **describing function** is applicable provided the linear part has sufficient low-pass filtering."* This single sentence scores 2 marks in a 14-mark question. --- # 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap --- ## 📝 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each) ### **Q1. [KTU University Exam – May 2024]** **"Differentiate between inherent (incidental) and intentional nonlinearities. Give two examples of each."** **Model Answer (3 marks):** | Aspect | Inherent (Incidental) | Intentional (Artificial) | |---|---|---| | **Origin** | Arise naturally from physical laws and component imperfections. | Deliberately introduced by the designer. | | **Purpose** | Unavoidable; degrade performance if unaccounted for. | To improve system response or reduce cost. | | **Example 1** | Saturation in op-amp / magnetic amplifier. | Bang-bang (relay) controller in satellite attitude control. | | **Example 2** | Backlash in a gear train. | Sliding-mode controller in variable-structure systems. | *(1 mark for clear distinction, 1 mark each for valid examples — total 3 marks.)* --- ### **Q2. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]** **"Classify the following nonlinearities as continuous/discontinuous and single-valued/multi-valued: (a) Saturation, (b) Backlash, (c) Dead-zone, (d) Relay with hysteresis."** **Model Answer (3 marks):** | Non-linearity | Continuity | Valuedness | |---|---|---| | (a) Saturation | Continuous | Single-valued | | (b) Backlash | Discontinuous | Multi-valued | | (c) Dead-zone | Continuous (kink at threshold) | Single-valued | | (d) Relay with hysteresis | Discontinuous | Multi-valued | *(½ mark per correct entry × 6 entries = 3 marks.)* --- ## 📝 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each) > **KTU 2024 ESE Pattern:** Each Part B question carries **14 marks** with internal choice (Or option). Sub-parts typically split as **7 + 7 marks**. --- ### **Question A (14 Marks)** [KTU University Exam – May 2024] **(a)** Define a static nonlinearity. With neat sketches, explain the input-output characteristics of **saturation**, **dead-zone**, and **ideal relay** nonlinearities. State the mathematical equations governing each. **(7 marks)** **(b)** What is meant by **multi-valued (hysteretic) nonlinearity**? Sketch and derive the input-output relation of a **backlash** nonlinearity with play width $2b$. Mention two engineering systems where backlash is dominant. **(7 marks)** --- #### ✅ Model Solution for Q.A(a) — 7 marks **Definition (2 marks):** A static (memoryless) nonlinearity is a device whose output at any instant depends *only* on the input at that instant: $y(t) = N(x(t))$. It is described by an algebraic (not differential) relation. **Sketches and equations (5 marks, split 2 + 2 + 1):** **1. Saturation (2 marks):** For input $x$ and output $y$ with linear range $[-a, +a]$ and saturation level $M = Ka$: $$y = \begin{cases} +M, & x \ge +a \\ Kx, & -a \le x \le +a \\ -M, & x \le -a \end{cases}$$ The characteristic is a straight line of slope $K$ inside $[-a, a]$ and horizontal lines at $\pm M$ outside. Occurs in op-amps, magnetic amplifiers, DC motors. **2. Dead-Zone (2 marks):** For threshold $\Delta$ and linear gain $K$: $$y = \begin{cases} K(x - \Delta), & x \ge +\Delta \\ 0, & \vert x \vert \le \Delta \\ K(x + \Delta), & x \le -\Delta \end{cases}$$ The output is zero for $\vert x \vert \le \Delta$, then linear outside. Occurs in hydraulic valves, electric motors under low voltage, diodes. **3. Ideal Relay (1 mark):** $$y = \begin{cases} +M, & x > 0 \\ -M, & x < 0 \end{cases}$$ Output snaps between $+M$ and $-M$. Used in bang-bang controllers. --- #### ✅ Model Solution for Q.A(b) — 7 marks **Definition (2 marks):** A multi-valued nonlinearity is one in which the output $y$ for a given input $x$ is **not unique**; it depends on the *direction* of motion of $x$ (i.e., on the past history of the input). Such systems are also called **hysteretic** or **non-unique** nonlinearities. **Backlash derivation (3 marks):** Consider two geared elements separated by a play of $2b$ (i.e., $\pm b$ clearance on each side). Let the input shaft angle be $x$ and the output shaft angle be $y$. - **For increasing $x$** (after reversal from decreasing): the input must travel a distance $b$ before contacting the driven gear. So $y$ remains constant at its previous value $y_0$ until $x = y_0 + b$. Then for $x > y_0 + b$: $y = x - b$. - **For decreasing $x$**: $y$ remains constant at $y_0$ until $x = y_0 - b$. Then $y = x + b$. The general input-output relation is: $$y = \begin{cases} x - b, & \text{if } \dot{x} > 0 \text{ and } x - y = b \text{ (right flank)} \\ y_{\text{const}}, & \text{if } \dot{x} = 0 \text{ or } \vert x - y \vert < b \text{ (mid-region)} \\ x + b, & \text{if } \dot{x} < 0 \text{ and } y - x = b \text{ (left flank)} \end{cases}$$ The characteristic is a **parallelogram-like loop** of width $2b$ when $x$ is swept back and forth. **Engineering examples (2 marks):** 1. **Gear trains** in robotic manipulators — causes positioning inaccuracy and limit cycles in closed-loop position control. 2. **Lead screws and potentiometers** in CNC machines and servo systems — causes dead-band and tracking error. --- ### **Question B (14 Marks — Alternative Choice)** [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023] **(a)** Explain the classification of nonlinearities based on **(i) continuity**, **(ii) valuedness**, and **(iii) origin**. For each class, provide one example and one engineering consequence. **(7 marks)** **(b)** Sketch the input-output characteristics of a **relay with dead-zone and hysteresis**. Derive its **describing function** and show that it is a real, amplitude-dependent gain. **(7 marks)** --- #### ✅ Model Solution for Q.B(a) — 7 marks **Classification chart (full 7 marks, split as 1 + 1 + 1 for the three classes, 1 + 1 + 1 + 1 for examples & consequences):** | Basis | Class | Example | Engineering Consequence | |---|---|---|---| | **Continuity** | **Continuous** (no jumps in $y$ vs. $x$) | Saturation in op-amp | Causes integrator windup; reduces effective gain at large signals. | | | **Discontinuous** (jumps present) | Ideal relay | Generates limit cycles and chattering. | | **Valuedness** | **Single-valued** (one $y$ per $x$) | Dead-zone in valve | Causes steady-state error and sluggish response. | | | **Multi-valued** (depends on history) | Backlash in gears | Causes positioning inaccuracy and limit cycles in PD controllers. | | **Origin** | **Incidental** (naturally arising) | Coulomb friction in joints | Causes stick-slip motion; destabilizes low-velocity tracking. | | | **Intentional** (designed in) | Sliding-mode controller | Provides robustness to matched disturbances. | **Conclusion (1 mark):** The proper classification guides the choice of analysis method — describing function for continuous, single-valued, memoryless nonlinearities with low-pass linear part; phase-plane for second-order systems with multi-valued nonlinearities; and Lyapunov methods for global stability. --- #### ✅ Model Solution for Q.B(b) — 7 marks **Sketch (2 marks):** A relay with dead-zone $\Delta$ and hysteresis width $2h$ has the following input-output characteristic: - For $x$ increasing through $+\Delta$: $y$ jumps from $0$ to $+M$. - For $x$ decreasing through $-(\Delta + h)$: $y$ jumps from $+M$ to $0$, then to $-M$ when $x$ decreases further to $-\Delta - h$. The output $y$ can take values in $\{-M, 0, +M\}$ depending on both the current value of $x$ and the *direction* of $x$'s motion. **Describing function derivation (5 marks):** Apply sinusoidal input $x(t) = X\sin(\omega t)$ with $X > \Delta + h$. The switching angles are: $$\alpha = \sin^{-1}\!\left(\frac{\Delta}{X}\right), \quad \beta = \sin^{-1}\!\left(\frac{\Delta + h}{X}\right)$$ The output is a square wave of amplitude $M$. The fundamental component is found from Fourier analysis. By odd symmetry of the hysteresis loop, the describing function is purely real: $$N(X) = \frac{4M}{\pi X} \left[ \cos\alpha - \cos\beta \right]$$ Substituting the switching angles: $$N(X) = \frac{4M}{\pi X} \left[ \sqrt{1 - \left(\frac{\Delta}{X}\right)^2} - \sqrt{1 - \left(\frac{\Delta + h}{X}\right)^2} \right]$$ **Key insight (1 mark):** $N(X)$ is a **real-valued function of the input amplitude $X$ only** — there is no frequency dependence (since the nonlinearity is static) and no imaginary part. As $X \to \infty$, $N(X) \to 0$ (the input grows but the output is capped at $M$). This amplitude-dependent gain is the **fingerprint of static nonlinearities** and the foundation of the describing-function method of Module 2. --- ## ⚠️ KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout > [!WARNING] > **Top 3 places where students LOSE MARKS in classification questions:** > > 1. **Confusing "continuous" with "smooth".** A dead-zone characteristic is *continuous* (no jump) but NOT *smooth* (it has a kink at $\pm \Delta$). Always specify which. > 2. **Forgetting the third axis (memory/static).** Many answers correctly identify continuity and valuedness but omit whether the nonlinearity is static or dynamic. KTU examiners award marks for *all three* orthogonal axes. > 3. **Calling a hysteresis loop "backlash" (or vice versa).** Hysteresis is the *general* multi-valued phenomenon (e.g., in magnetic B-H curves); backlash is a *specific* mechanical form of hysteresis with a parallelogram-shaped characteristic. > > **Bonus tip:** Always draw the input–output sketch on the answer sheet — it is worth at least **2 marks** even if the written description is incomplete. --- ## ✅ Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember > [!IMPORTANT] > **Rapid Revision Checklist — Classifications of Nonlinearities (Module 1)** > > - 🔹 A system is **nonlinear** iff it does not obey **superposition** (homogeneity + additivity). > - 🔹 Two orthogonal classification axes: **Origin** (Incidental vs. Intentional) and **Mathematical Properties** (Continuity, Valuedness, Memory). > - 🔹 **Incidental nonlinearities** are natural (saturation, dead-zone, friction, backlash, hysteresis). > - 🔹 **Intentional nonlinearities** are designed (bang-bang, sliding-mode, quantizers). > - 🔹 **Saturation** → continuous, single-valued, static; describes amplifier/motor limits. > - 🔹 **Dead-zone** → continuous (kink), single-valued, static; describes valve clearances. > - 🔹 **Ideal Relay** → discontinuous, single-valued, static; used in bang-bang control. > - 🔹 **Relay with Hysteresis** → discontinuous, **multi-valued**, static. > - 🔹 **Backlash** → discontinuous, **multi-valued**, static; parallelogram-shaped characteristic of width $2b$. > - 🔹 **Coulomb Friction** → discontinuous, single-valued, *dynamic* (depends on $\dot{x}$). > - 🔹 **Describing function** $N(X)$ is amplitude-dependent for static nonlinearities — this is *why* linear methods fail. > - 🔹 The classification chosen determines the **analysis tool**: Describing Function (DF) for single-valued, continuous, memoryless + low-pass linear part; Phase-Plane for second-order with multi-valued; Lyapunov for global stability. > - 🔹 KTU favourite definition: *"A system is nonlinear if the principle of superposition does not hold."* ---

topics.title: Common Physical Nonlinearities
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PERAT634 - NON-LINEAR CONTROL
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Common Physical Nonlinearities

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# Common Physical Nonlinearities — Core Definition & Intuitive Overview

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Anchor (PERAT634 — Module 1):** *Classification and characteristics of physical non-linearities. Saturation, dead-zone, relay, hysteresis, backlash, and friction. Static and dynamic non-linearities. Common input-output mappings used in control engineering practice.*

## 1.1 What is a "Physical Nonlinearity"?

In classical linear control theory, we assume that every block in the block diagram obeys the **principle of superposition** — output is directly proportional to input, and the response to the sum of two inputs equals the sum of individual responses. A **physical nonlinearity** is a real, hardware-imperfect behavior that **violates this linearity** in a measurable, repeatable, and often unavoidable way.

Formally, an element is nonlinear if its input-output relationship $y = f(x)$ cannot be written in the form:

$$y = a_1 x + a_0$$

For all admissible operating points. Instead, the relationship is described by some **nonlinear function $f(\cdot)$** that is either *static* (memoryless, e.g., saturation) or *dynamic* (state-dependent, e.g., friction with stiction).

> [!NOTE]
> **Memoryless vs. Dynamic Nonlinearity:**
> - **Static (Memoryless):** Output depends *only* on the current input. Example: a saturating amplifier.
> - **Dynamic (with memory):** Output depends on present *and* past values of input or internal state. Example: backlash in a gear train.

## 1.2 Intuitive Real-World Analogy — The "Driver and Car" Model

Imagine you are driving a car on a highway and you want to hold a steady speed of 80 km/h.

| Real Driving Phenomenon | Engineering Nonlinearity |
|---|---|
| You press the accelerator; after some pedal travel, the car *just starts* moving | **Dead-Zone** (small inputs produce zero output) |
| Floor the accelerator — the car speed simply *stops increasing* beyond a limit | **Saturation** (actuator has a hard physical limit) |
| The car's speed *oscillates* around setpoint because thermostat-like control kicks in | **Relay (On-Off) with Hysteresis** |
| When you change direction, the gear has a small "slop" before re-engaging | **Backlash** (multi-valued play in gears/leadscrews) |
| The car shakes a little at very low speed and you must "break free" | **Friction (Stiction + Coulomb + Viscous)** |

> [!TIP]
> **Why does KTU emphasize this topic?** Almost *every* industrial actuator you will design, model, or tune — from DC motor drivers to hydraulic valves, from furnace heaters to robotic gearheads — exhibits one or more of these nonlinearities. Recognizing them is the **first step toward choosing the right analysis tool** (describing function, phase-plane, Lyapunov, sliding mode, etc.).

## 1.3 Classification of Common Physical Nonlinearities

The KTU board conventionally classifies physical nonlinearities into the following six families, all of which are covered in this note:

1. **Saturation** — actuator or amplifier limit
2. **Dead-Zone** — insensitivity region near origin
3. **Ideal Relay (Signum)** — two-position switching
4. **Relay with Hysteresis / Dead-Zone Relay** — switching with memory
5. **Backlash** — multi-valued gap in mechanical linkages
6. **Friction (Static, Coulomb, Viscous, Stiction)** — resistive forces in motion

A seventh, **Quantization**, is also relevant in digital systems but is usually listed separately.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> All these nonlinearities are **time-invariant** (their characteristics do not change with time) and **continuous-valued** (except the relay). This allows us to apply the **Describing Function (DF)** method in the next stage of analysis, which is the principal analytical tool for Module 1 and Module 2 of PERAT634.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Saturation Nonlinearity

**Physical Origin:** Every physical amplifier, motor, valve, and transistor has a finite power supply. Beyond a certain input magnitude, the output simply cannot grow further.

**Input-Output Equation:**

$$y(t) = \begin{cases} M, & x(t) > a \\ K \cdot x(t), & \vert x(t) \vert \le a \\ -M, & x(t) < -a \end{cases}$$

where $K = M/a$ is the linear-region slope, $a$ is the linear threshold, and $M$ is the saturation level.

**Graphical Form:** A linear ramp of slope $K$ in the central region, capped by horizontal lines $\pm M$ outside.

**Engineering Implications:**
- Causes **integrator wind-up** in PI controllers.
- Reduces effective loop gain for large disturbances → **poor large-signal response**.
- Can introduce **limit cycles** when paired with energy-storing elements.

## 2.2 Dead-Zone Nonlinearity

**Physical Origin:** A small control effort is insufficient to overcome static friction, fluid pressure thresholds, or sensor noise floors. Until the input exceeds a threshold, the output is zero.

**Input-Output Equation:**

$$y(t) = \begin{cases} 0, & \vert x(t) \vert \le \Delta \\ K\bigl(x(t) - \Delta \cdot \text{sgn}(x)\bigr), & \vert x(t) \vert > \Delta \end{cases}$$

**Engineering Implications:**
- Introduces **steady-state error** that grows with the dead-zone width $\Delta$.
- Causes **threshold jitter / limit cycles** in servos, especially with integrator action.

## 2.3 Ideal Relay (Signum / Bang-Bang)

**Physical Origin:** Thermostats, solenoid valves, contactors, and ON/OFF solenoid drivers have only two stable output states.

**Input-Output Equation:**

$$y(t) = M \cdot \text{sgn}\bigl(x(t)\bigr)$$

> [!NOTE]
> The ideal relay is the **simplest and most aggressive** nonlinearity — the describing function is the largest in magnitude among all standard nonlinearities for a given amplitude, which is why relay-based **autotuning** (Åström–Hägglund) exploits this property.

## 2.4 Relay with Hysteresis

**Physical Origin:** Magnetic relays, Schmitt triggers, and thermostats with thermal lag switch ON at one input level and OFF at a different one, creating a *hysteresis loop*.

**Input-Output Equation:**

$$y(t) = \begin{cases} +M, & x(t) > +b, \text{ or } (x(t) > -b \text{ and } y \text{ was } +M) \\ -M, & x(t) < -b, \text{ or } (x(t) < +b \text{ and } y \text{ was } -M) \end{cases}$$

with $b$ as the half-width of the hysteresis band.

**Engineering Implications:**
- Causes **oscillatory limit cycles** even without any plant dynamics.
- The relay-with-hysteresis DF is **amplitude-dependent** in both real and imaginary parts.

## 2.5 Backlash Nonlinearity

**Physical Origin:** Gear trains, lead screws, and rack-and-pinion systems have a small clearance so that the driven member does not move until the driving member has traveled through the gap.

**Input-Output Equation (multi-valued):**

$$y(t) = \begin{cases} K\bigl(x(t) - b/2\bigr), & \dot{x} > 0, \; \text{or } \dot{x}=0 \text{ with } y=K(x-b/2) \\ K\bigl(x(t) + b/2\bigr), & \dot{x} < 0, \; \text{or } \dot{x}=0 \text{ with } y=K(x+b/2) \end{cases}$$

where $b$ is the total backlash width. The output is a **multi-valued** function of input — it depends on the *direction* of motion.

**Engineering Implications:**
- Principal cause of **limit cycles and poor settling** in position servos.
- Cannot be linearized by a single DF — requires *dual-input describing function* (DIDF) or phase-plane analysis.

## 2.6 Friction Nonlinearity

**Physical Origin:** All mechanical motion is opposed by friction, which is empirically a **combination** of four components:

| Component | Mathematical Form | Physical Cause |
|---|---|---|
| **Viscous Friction** | $F_v = B \cdot v$ | Lubricant shear |
| **Coulomb Friction** | $F_c = F_c \cdot \text{sgn}(v)$ | Surface contact |
| **Static Friction (Stiction)** | $F_s$ — must be overcome to initiate motion | Adhesion at rest |
| **Stribeck Effect** | $F_{stribeck} = F_s \cdot e^{-(v/v_s)^2} \cdot \text{sgn}(v)$ | Mixed-lubrication transition |

The **complete friction model** is:

$$F_{friction}(v) = \bigl[F_c + (F_s - F_c) e^{-(v/v_s)^2}\bigr] \cdot \text{sgn}(v) + B \cdot v$$

**Engineering Implications:**
- Causes **stick-slip** motion at low velocities.
- Generates **limit cycles** in position servos.
- Is *the* most-studied nonlinearity in precision motion control (wafer stages, telescopes, robotics).

## 2.7 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| Nonlinearity | Symbolic Form | DF $N(A)$ (single-tone $A\sin\omega t$) | Valid For |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Ideal Relay** | $y = M\,\text{sgn}(x)$ | $\dfrac{4M}{\pi A}$ | All $A$ |
| **Saturation** | $y = \text{sat}(x; a, M)$ | $\dfrac{2K}{\pi}\left[\arcsin\!\dfrac{M}{A} + \dfrac{M}{A}\sqrt{1-\left(\dfrac{M}{A}\right)^2}\right]$ | $A \ge M$ |
| **Dead-Zone** | $y = \text{dz}(x; \Delta, K)$ | $K - \dfrac{2K}{\pi}\left[\arcsin\!\dfrac{\Delta}{A} + \dfrac{\Delta}{A}\sqrt{1-\left(\dfrac{\Delta}{A}\right)^2}\right]$ | $A \ge \Delta$ |
| **Relay + Dead-Zone** | Combined | $\dfrac{4M}{\pi A}\sqrt{1-\left(\dfrac{\Delta}{A}\right)^2}$ | $A \ge \Delta$ |
| **Relay + Hysteresis** | $b$ band, $M$ output | $\dfrac{4M}{\pi A}\sqrt{1-\left(\dfrac{b}{A}\right)^2} - j\dfrac{4Mb}{\pi A^2}$ | $A \ge b$ |
| **Coulomb Friction** | $F_c\,\text{sgn}(v)$ | $\dfrac{4F_c}{\pi A}$ (real) | All $A$ |
| **Quantization** | $n$ levels over $\pm M$ | $\approx \dfrac{2M}{nA}\cdot\dfrac{\sin(n\pi A/2M)}{n\pi A/2M}$ | Step size $q = 2M/n$ |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Sign convention used by KTU board:** All DFs in the table above are evaluated for input $x(t) = A\sin\omega t$, output fundamental component $y_1(t) = N(A) A \sin\omega t$, where $N(A) = B_1/A + j C_1/A$ in the complex plane. Always express the **negative reciprocal** $-1/N(A)$ and superimpose it on the Nyquist plot of $G(j\omega)$ to find the predicted limit cycle.

## 2.8 Real-World Utility

| Field | Common Nonlinearity | Engineering Consequence |
|---|---|---|
| **Electric Motors** | Saturation, Coulomb friction | Torque limit, low-speed stick-slip |
| **Hydraulic Servos** | Dead-zone, saturation | Position error, valve saturation |
| **Process Control** | Relay with hysteresis | Thermostat-induced temperature oscillations |
| **Robotics** | Backlash, joint friction | Tracking error at low speeds, settling overshoot |
| **Power Electronics** | Saturation, dead-zone | Harmonics, cross-over distortion |
| **Aerospace Actuators** | Hysteresis, saturation | Limit cycles in autopilot loops |

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Derivation Mandate:** All steps of the Fourier-based Describing Function (DF) derivations are written out explicitly. No steps are skipped. The student is expected to reproduce these in the KTU End Semester Examination.

## 3.1 Derivation — Describing Function of the Ideal Relay

**Setup:** Input is a sinusoid $x(t) = A\sin\omega t$, with $A > 0$. The relay output is:

$$y(t) = \begin{cases} +M, & 0 < \omega t < \pi \\ -M, & \pi < \omega t < 2\pi \end{cases}$$

**Step 1 — Symmetry observation.** $y(t)$ is an *odd* square wave of period $T = 2\pi/\omega$. Therefore:
- The DC term $a_0 = 0$.
- All cosine Fourier coefficients $a_n = 0$.
- Only the sine terms survive.

**Step 2 — Compute the fundamental sine coefficient $b_1$:**

$$b_1 = \frac{2}{T}\int_{0}^{T} y(t)\sin(\omega t)\,dt$$

Substituting the piecewise output:

$$b_1 = \frac{2}{2\pi/\omega}\left[\int_{0}^{\pi/\omega}(+M)\sin(\omega t)\,dt + \int_{\pi/\omega}^{2\pi/\omega}(-M)\sin(\omega t)\,dt\right]$$

**Step 3 — Evaluate the first integral.** Let $\theta = \omega t$, $d\theta = \omega\,dt$:

$$\int_{0}^{\pi/\omega} M\sin(\omega t)\,dt = \frac{M}{\omega}\int_{0}^{\pi}\sin\theta\,d\theta = \frac{M}{\omega}\bigl[-\cos\theta\bigr]_{0}^{\pi} = \frac{M}{\omega}\bigl[1-(-1)\bigr] = \frac{2M}{\omega}$$

**Step 4 — Evaluate the second integral.**

$$\int_{\pi/\omega}^{2\pi/\omega}(-M)\sin(\omega t)\,dt = -\frac{M}{\omega}\int_{\pi}^{2\pi}\sin\theta\,d\theta = -\frac{M}{\omega}\bigl[-\cos\theta\bigr]_{\pi}^{2\pi} = -\frac{M}{\omega}\bigl[1-(-1)\bigr] = -\frac{2M}{\omega}$$

**Step 5 — Combine.**

$$b_1 = \frac{\omega}{\pi}\left[\frac{2M}{\omega} - \frac{2M}{\omega}\right] \text{ (the two integrals equal) }\Rightarrow \frac{\omega}{\pi}\cdot\frac{4M}{\omega} = \frac{4M}{\pi}$$

**Step 6 — Describing Function.** Since $b_1/A$ is the gain applied to the input sinusoid:

$$\boxed{\;N_{\text{relay}}(A) = \frac{b_1}{A} = \frac{4M}{\pi A}\;}$$

This is **real-valued and amplitude-dependent** — the relay behaves as a *variable-gain amplifier* that gets weaker as the input amplitude grows. This single fact is the cornerstone of relay-based **autotuning** at a critical point.

## 3.2 Derivation — Describing Function of Saturation

**Setup:** Saturation with linear region slope $K$, saturation level $M$, and linear-limit $\alpha = M/K$. The input is $x(t) = A\sin\omega t$ with $A \ge M$ (otherwise the system is in the linear region and $N(A) = K$).

**Step 1 — Identify the conduction angle.** Saturation activates when $\vert x(t) \vert = M$, i.e. when $A\sin\omega t = M$:

$$\omega t_1 = \arcsin\!\left(\frac{M}{A}\right) \equiv \psi$$

Beyond this angle, $y(t) = M$ (clamped). Within the linear zone, $y(t) = Kx(t) = KA\sin\omega t$.

**Step 2 — Output waveform on one quarter-period:**

$$y(t) = \begin{cases} K A \sin\omega t, & 0 \le \omega t \le \psi \\ M, & \psi \le \omega t \le \pi/2 \end{cases}$$

**Step 3 — Compute the fundamental sine coefficient.** The output is odd (no cosine components) and quarter-wave symmetric, so we can write:

$$b_1 = \frac{4}{T}\int_{0}^{T/4} y(t)\sin(\omega t)\,dt = \frac{4\omega}{2\pi}\int_{0}^{\pi/(2\omega)} y(t)\sin(\omega t)\,dt$$

$$b_1 = \frac{2\omega}{\pi}\left[\int_{0}^{\psi/\omega} KA\sin^2(\omega t)\,dt + \int_{\psi/\omega}^{\pi/(2\omega)} M\sin(\omega t)\,dt\right]$$

**Step 4 — Evaluate the first integral.**

$$\int_{0}^{\psi/\omega} KA\sin^2(\omega t)\,dt = KA\cdot\frac{1}{2}\int_{0}^{\psi/\omega}\bigl[1-\cos(2\omega t)\bigr]dt = \frac{KA}{2}\left[\frac{\psi}{\omega} - \frac{\sin(2\psi)}{2\omega}\right]$$

Using $2\sin\psi\cos\psi = \sin(2\psi)$:

$$= \frac{KA\psi}{2\omega} - \frac{KA\sin\psi\cos\psi}{2\omega}$$

**Step 5 — Evaluate the second integral.**

$$\int_{\psi/\omega}^{\pi/(2\omega)} M\sin(\omega t)\,dt = \frac{M}{\omega}\bigl[-\cos(\omega t)\bigr]_{\psi}^{\pi/2} = \frac{M}{\omega}\bigl[0 - (-\cos\psi)\bigr] = \frac{M\cos\psi}{\omega}$$

**Step 6 — Combine and substitute $\psi = \arcsin(M/A)$ and $\cos\psi = \sqrt{1-(M/A)^2}$.** After algebraic simplification:

$$b_1 = \frac{2KA}{\pi}\left[\arcsin\!\frac{M}{A} + \frac{M}{A}\sqrt{1-\left(\frac{M}{A}\right)^2}\right]$$

**Step 7 — Describing Function.**

$$\boxed{\;N_{\text{sat}}(A) = \frac{b_1}{A} = \frac{2K}{\pi}\left[\arcsin\!\frac{M}{A} + \frac{M}{A}\sqrt{1-\left(\frac{M}{A}\right)^2}\right]\;}$$

**Sanity checks:**
- As $A \to M$: $\arcsin(1) = \pi/2$ and the square-root term vanishes, giving $N = K$. ✓
- As $A \to \infty$: both terms $\to 0$, giving $N \to 0$. ✓ (The saturated relay effectively opens the loop at high amplitudes.)

## 3.3 Derivation — Describing Function of Dead-Zone

**Setup:** Dead-zone with threshold $\Delta$ and slope $K$. The input $x(t) = A\sin\omega t$ with $A \ge \Delta$.

**Step 1 — Output waveform.** Within the dead-zone ($\vert x \vert \le \Delta$), the output is *zero*. Outside it, the output follows a line of slope $K$ offset by $\Delta$:

$$y(t) = \begin{cases} 0, & 0 \le \omega t \le \phi \\ K\bigl(A\sin\omega t - \Delta\bigr), & \phi \le \omega t \le \pi - \phi \\ 0, & \pi - \phi \le \omega t \le \pi \end{cases}$$

where the half-dead-zone angle is $\phi = \arcsin(\Delta/A)$.

**Step 2 — Fundamental sine coefficient.** Using quarter-wave symmetry:

$$b_1 = \frac{4\omega}{\pi}\left[\int_{0}^{\phi/\omega} 0\cdot\sin(\omega t)\,dt + \int_{\phi/\omega}^{\pi/(2\omega)} K(A\sin\omega t - \Delta)\sin(\omega t)\,dt\right]$$

**Step 3 — Expand the second integral.**

$$\int_{\phi/\omega}^{\pi/(2\omega)} KA\sin^2(\omega t)\,dt - \int_{\phi/\omega}^{\pi/(2\omega)} K\Delta\sin(\omega t)\,dt$$

**First part** (using half-angle identity as in §3.2):

$$\frac{KA}{2}\left[\frac{\pi}{2\omega} - \frac{\phi}{\omega} + \frac{\sin(2\phi)}{2\omega}\right]$$

**Second part:**

$$K\Delta\cdot\frac{1}{\omega}\bigl[\cos(\omega t)\bigr]_{\phi}^{\pi/2} = \frac{K\Delta}{\omega}\bigl[0 - \cos\phi\bigr] = -\frac{K\Delta\cos\phi}{\omega}$$

**Step 4 — Assemble.**

$$b_1 = \frac{4\omega}{\pi}\left\{\frac{KA}{2\omega}\left[\frac{\pi}{2} - \phi + \frac{\sin(2\phi)}{2}\right] + \frac{K\Delta\cos\phi}{\omega}\right\}$$

After simplification and using $\sin(2\phi) = 2\sin\phi\cos\phi = 2(\Delta/A)\cos\phi$:

$$b_1 = \frac{2KA}{\pi}\left[\frac{\pi}{2} - \arcsin\!\frac{\Delta}{A} - \frac{\Delta}{A}\sqrt{1-\left(\frac{\Delta}{A}\right)^2}\right]$$

**Step 5 — Describing Function.**

$$\boxed{\;N_{\text{dz}}(A) = K - \frac{2K}{\pi}\left[\arcsin\!\frac{\Delta}{A} + \frac{\Delta}{A}\sqrt{1-\left(\frac{\Delta}{A}\right)^2}\right]\;}$$

**Sanity checks:**
- $A = \Delta$: $N = K - (2K/\pi)(\pi/2) = 0$. ✓
- $A \to \infty$: $N \to K$. ✓

## 3.4 Derivation — Describing Function of Relay with Hysteresis

**Setup:** The relay output is $+M$ when $x > +b$, $-M$ when $x < -b$, and *holds* its previous value in the band $\vert x \vert \le b$. Input: $x(t) = A\sin\omega t$, $A > b$.

**Step 1 — Switching angles.** The relay switches at $\omega t = \pi/2 - \psi$ (rising) and $\omega t = \pi/2 + \psi$ (falling), where:

$$\sin(\pi/2 - \psi) = \frac{b}{A} \;\Rightarrow\; \psi = \arccos\!\frac{b}{A}$$

**Step 2 — Fundamental Fourier coefficients (output is a phase-shifted square wave).** By direct integration over the positive half-cycle:

$$a_1 = \frac{2}{T}\int_{0}^{T} y(t)\cos(\omega t)\,dt = \frac{4M}{\pi A}\sqrt{1 - \frac{b^2}{A^2}}$$

$$b_1 = \frac{2}{T}\int_{0}^{T} y(t)\sin(\omega t)\,dt = -\frac{4Mb}{\pi A^2}$$

**Step 3 — Complex Describing Function.**

$$\boxed{\;N_{\text{rel,hyst}}(A) = \frac{a_1 - j b_1}{A} = \frac{4M}{\pi A}\sqrt{1 - \frac{b^2}{A^2}} - j\frac{4Mb}{\pi A^2}\;}$$

> [!NOTE]
> The **negative imaginary part** corresponds to a *phase lag* in the relay — the output fundamental lags the input by an angle that grows as the input amplitude shrinks. This phase lag is what makes the hysteresis relay generate self-sustained oscillations more readily than the ideal relay.

## 3.5 Limiting Case Check — Dead-Zone is "Saturation of the Opposite Sign"

A beautiful unifying identity:

$$N_{\text{dz}}(A) = K - N_{\text{sat}}(A)$$

This reflects the geometric fact that the dead-zone output is what remains of a linear response $Kx$ *after* subtracting the "missing" saturated piece. KTU examiners **love** this kind of cross-check — expect it as a 3-mark short answer.

# Structural Diagrams & Schematics

> [!NOTE]
> The following diagrams are functional/architectural Mermaid renderings that capture the *input-output structure* of each physical nonlinearity. They are rendered in lieu of freehand plots, which cannot be generated in plain text with the same precision. Each block shows the parameter set, characteristic equation, and the engineering interpretation.

## 4.1 Block-Level Classification of Physical Nonlinearities

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    classDef root fill:#1f2a44,stroke:#ffffff,stroke-width:2px,color:#ffffff
    classDef branch fill:#2e7d32,stroke:#ffffff,color:#ffffff
    classDef leaf fill:#455a64,stroke:#ffffff,color:#ffffff
    classDef warn fill:#c62828,stroke:#ffffff,color:#ffffff

    A["PHYSICAL NONLINEARITIES"]:::root
    A --> B["STATIC MEMORYLESS"]:::branch
    A --> C["DYNAMIC WITH MEMORY"]:::branch

    B --> B1["SATURATION"]:::leaf
    B --> B2["DEAD ZONE"]:::leaf
    B --> B3["IDEAL RELAY"]:::leaf
    B --> B4["QUANTIZATION"]:::leaf

    C --> C1["RELAY WITH HYSTERESIS"]:::leaf
    C --> C2["BACKLASH"]:::warn
    C --> C3["FRICTION STICTION COULOMB VISCOUS"]:::warn
    C --> C4["MAGNETIC HYSTERESIS"]:::leaf
```

## 4.2 Functional Block — Saturation Element

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    classDef in fill:#1565c0,stroke:#ffffff,color:#ffffff
    classDef mid fill:#ef6c00,stroke:#ffffff,color:#ffffff
    classDef out fill:#2e7d32,stroke:#ffffff,color:#ffffff

    IN["INPUT X OF T"]:::in --> M1["MULTIPLY BY K"]:::mid
    M1 --> CHK{"ABS X LESS THAN A"}:::mid
    CHK -- YES --> OUT1["OUTPUT Y EQUALS K X"]:::out
    CHK -- NO --> CAP["CLAMP TO PLUS OR MINUS M"]:::out
    CAP --> OUT2["OUTPUT Y EQUALS PLUS OR MINUS M"]:::out
    OUT1 --> JOIN["FINAL OUTPUT"]:::out
    OUT2 --> JOIN
```

**Parameter Set:** $\{K, a, M\}$, **Describing Function:** $N_{\text{sat}}(A) = \dfrac{2K}{\pi}\!\left[\arcsin\!\dfrac{M}{A} + \dfrac{M}{A}\sqrt{1-\left(\dfrac{M}{A}\right)^2}\right]$

## 4.3 Functional Block — Dead-Zone Element

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    classDef in fill:#1565c0,stroke:#ffffff,color:#ffffff
    classDef mid fill:#ef6c00,stroke:#ffffff,color:#ffffff
    classDef out fill:#2e7d32,stroke:#ffffff,color:#ffffff

    IN["INPUT X OF T"]:::in --> ABS["COMPUTE ABS X"]:::mid
    ABS --> CHK{"ABS X GREATER THAN DELTA"}:::mid
    CHK -- NO --> ZOUT["OUTPUT Y EQUALS ZERO"]:::out
    CHK -- YES --> LIN["OUTPUT Y EQUALS K X MINUS K DELTA SIGN X"]:::out
    ZOUT --> JOIN["FINAL OUTPUT"]:::out
    LIN --> JOIN
```

**Parameter Set:** $\{\Delta, K\}$, **Describing Function:** $N_{\text{dz}}(A) = K - \dfrac{2K}{\pi}\!\left[\arcsin\!\dfrac{\Delta}{A} + \dfrac{\Delta}{A}\sqrt{1-\left(\dfrac{\Delta}{A}\right)^2}\right]$

## 4.4 Functional Block — Relay with Hysteresis (Schematic)

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    classDef in fill:#1565c0,stroke:#ffffff,color:#ffffff
    classDef mid fill:#ef6c00,stroke:#ffffff,color:#ffffff
    classDef out fill:#2e7d32,stroke:#ffffff,color:#ffffff
    classDef mem fill:#6a1b9a,stroke:#ffffff,color:#ffffff

    IN["INPUT X OF T"]:::in --> SGN{"X GREATER THAN PLUS B"}:::mid
    SGN -- YES --> SETP["SET Y TO PLUS M"]:::out
    SGN -- NO --> SGN2{"X LESS THAN MINUS B"}:::mid
    SGN2 -- YES --> SETN["SET Y TO MINUS M"]:::out
    SGN2 -- NO --> HOLD["HOLD PREVIOUS VALUE OF Y"]:::mem
    SETP --> MEM["INTERNAL STATE MEMORY"]:::mem
    SETN --> MEM
    HOLD --> MEM
    MEM --> OUT["FINAL OUTPUT Y OF T"]:::out
```

**Parameter Set:** $\{M, b\}$ (output level, hysteresis half-band), **DF:** $N(A) = \dfrac{4M}{\pi A}\sqrt{1 - (b/A)^2} - j\dfrac{4Mb}{\pi A^2}$

## 4.5 Functional Block — Backlash (Two-Branch Selection)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    classDef in fill:#1565c0,stroke:#ffffff,color:#ffffff
    classDef mid fill:#ef6c00,stroke:#ffffff,color:#ffffff
    classDef out fill:#2e7d32,stroke:#ffffff,color:#ffffff
    classDef mem fill:#6a1b9a,stroke:#ffffff,color:#ffffff

    IN["INPUT X OF T"]:::in --> DIFF["COMPUTE TIME DERIVATIVE OF X"]:::mid
    DIFF --> DIR{"SIGN OF DX BY DT"}:::mid
    DIR -- POSITIVE --> BR1["BRANCH UPPER Y EQUALS K X MINUS K B BY 2"]:::out
    DIR -- NEGATIVE --> BR2["BRANCH LOWER Y EQUALS K X PLUS K B BY 2"]:::out
    DIR -- ZERO --> STAY["RETAIN PREVIOUS BRANCH FROM MEMORY"]:::mem
    BR1 --> MERGE["SELECTED OUTPUT"]:::out
    BR2 --> MERGE
    STAY --> MERGE
```

> [!WARNING]
> **Multi-valued output:** Backlash is the only nonlinearity in this set whose output is a **set-valued function of input alone** — knowledge of the *sign of the velocity* is required. This is why backlash is sometimes called a *dynamic nonlinearity* in disguise.

## 4.6 Functional Block — Combined Friction Model

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    classDef in fill:#1565c0,stroke:#ffffff,color:#ffffff
    classDef mid fill:#ef6c00,stroke:#ffffff,color:#ffffff
    classDef out fill:#2e7d32,stroke:#ffffff,color:#ffffff

    IN["VELOCITY V OF T"]:::in --> SGN["COMPUTE SIGN V"]:::mid
    IN --> STRIB["STRIBECK EXP TERM F_S TIMES EXP MINUS V BY V_S SQUARED"]:::mid
    IN --> VIS["VISCOUS TERM B TIMES V"]:::mid
    IN --> COUL["COULOMB TERM F_C TIMES SIGN V"]:::mid
    SGN --> STRIB
    SGN --> COUL
    STRIB --> SUM["SUM ALL FOUR TERMS"]:::out
    COUL --> SUM
    VIS --> SUM
    SUM --> OUT["TOTAL FRICTION FORCE"]:::out
```

**Combined Friction Equation:**

$$F(v) = \bigl[F_c + (F_s - F_c)\,e^{-(v/v_s)^2}\bigr]\cdot\text{sgn}(v) + B\cdot v$$

## 4.7 Closed-Loop Position of Each Nonlinearity in a Typical Control Loop

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    classDef plant fill:#1b5e20,stroke:#ffffff,color:#ffffff
    classDef fb fill:#b71c1c,stroke:#ffffff,color:#ffffff
    classDef nl fill:#0d47a1,stroke:#ffffff,color:#ffffff
    classDef ctl fill:#4a148c,stroke:#ffffff,color:#ffffff

    R["REFERENCE R"]:::ctl --> SUM["COMPARATOR PLUS MINUS"]:::ctl
    SUM --> CONT["CONTROLLER C S"]:::ctl
    CONT --> NL["NONLINEAR ELEMENT N X"]:::nl
    NL --> PLANT["PLANT G S"]:::plant
    PLANT --> Y["OUTPUT Y"]:::plant
    PLANT --> H["SENSOR H S"]:::fb
    H --> SUM
```

> [!TIP]
> The describing-function method replaces $N(x)$ with $N(A)$ in series with $G(s)H(s)$, then locates intersections of $G(j\omega)$ with the **critical locus** $-1/N(A)$ to predict **limit cycles**.

# KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Mark Distribution Reference (KTU 2024):** Part A = 3 marks each (short answer, two questions per module typically). Part B = 14 marks each (full ESE module-internal choice with sub-parts (a) 7 marks and (b) 7 marks).

---

## Part A — 3-Mark Short-Answer Questions

### Q1. `[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023, PERAT634]`
**List any three common physical nonlinearities that arise in control systems. For each, give one real engineering example.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
1. **Saturation** — any amplifier or motor reaching its supply voltage/current limit, e.g., DC motor armature saturation.
2. **Dead-Zone** — small input voltages that produce no motor rotation, e.g., DC servo around zero command.
3. **Backlash** — gear train clearance in a robotic joint.
4. *(Optional 4th)* **Relay with Hysteresis** — household thermostat switching heater on/off.

*Marking: 1 mark per correctly identified nonlinearity + example.*

### Q2. `[KTU University Exam — May 2024, PERAT634]`
**Distinguish between a static nonlinearity and a dynamic nonlinearity. Give one example of each.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

| Aspect | Static (Memoryless) | Dynamic (with Memory) |
|---|---|---|
| **Definition** | Output depends *only* on present input | Output depends on present *and* past inputs/states |
| **Math form** | $y = f(x)$ | $y = f(x, \dot x, \text{history})$ |
| **Example** | Saturation, dead-zone | Backlash, hysteresis, friction |

*Marking: 1 mark for definition, 1 mark for difference, 1 mark for examples.*

---

## Part B — 14-Mark Module-Internal-Choice Questions

### Question A (14 Marks) `[KTU University Exam — Dec 2022, PERAT634]`

**(a)** With a neat sketch, derive the **describing function of an ideal relay** with output magnitude $M$. (7 marks)

**(b)** A unity-feedback closed-loop system has linear plant $G(s) = \dfrac{10}{s(s+1)(s+2)}$ in series with an ideal relay of magnitude $M = 1.5$. Using the **describing function method**, predict the existence, amplitude, and frequency of any self-sustained oscillation. (7 marks)

#### Model Solution — Q.A(a)

**Step 1 — Plot the characteristic.** For $x(t) = A\sin\omega t$, the output is a square wave of amplitude $\pm M$ in phase with the sign of the input.

**Step 2 — Compute the fundamental Fourier coefficient.** Following §3.1:

$$b_1 = \frac{4M}{\pi}$$

**Step 3 — Describing Function:**

$$\boxed{N(A) = \frac{4M}{\pi A}}$$

The DF is **real, positive, and amplitude-dependent** — plotted in the complex plane it lies on the negative real axis, meaning $-1/N(A)$ lies on the **negative real axis** between $-\infty$ and $0$.

*(Full derivation as in §3.1: 6 marks for derivation, 1 mark for sketch + statement.)*

#### Model Solution — Q.A(b)

**Step 1 — Nyquist plot of $G(j\omega)$.** The polar plot of $G(j\omega) = \dfrac{10}{j\omega(j\omega+1)(j\omega+2)}$ crosses the **negative real axis** at some frequency. Setting $\text{Im}\{G(j\omega)\} = 0$ and solving gives $\omega \approx 1.414\ \text{rad/s}$, and at this frequency:

$$\vert G(j\omega)\vert = \frac{10}{\omega\sqrt{\omega^2+1}\sqrt{\omega^2+4}} = \frac{10}{1.414\cdot\sqrt{3}\cdot\sqrt{6}} = \frac{10}{6.0} \approx 1.667$$

So the polar plot crosses the negative real axis at $-1.667$.

**Step 2 — Critical locus of the relay.** Since $N(A) = 4M/(\pi A) = 6/(\pi A) \approx 1.909/A$, the critical locus is:

$$-\frac{1}{N(A)} = -\frac{\pi A}{6}$$

This is the entire negative real axis from $-\infty$ (at $A = 0$) to $0$ (at $A \to \infty$).

**Step 3 — Intersection.** Equating the crossing magnitudes:

$$1.667 = \frac{\pi A}{6} \;\Rightarrow\; A = \frac{6 \times 1.667}{\pi} \approx 3.184$$

**Step 4 — Conclusion.** A self-sustained oscillation (limit cycle) exists with:

$$\boxed{\omega_c \approx 1.414\ \text{rad/s}, \quad A_c \approx 3.18}$$

*Marking: 2 marks for Nyquist crossing, 2 marks for DF critical locus, 2 marks for intersection, 1 mark for conclusion.*

---

### Question B (14 Marks) `[KTU University Exam — May 2023, PERAT634]`

**(a)** With a neat sketch, derive the **describing function of a saturation nonlinearity** with linear slope $K$, linear-limit input $a$, and saturation output $M = Ka$. (7 marks)

**(b)** A temperature control loop has plant $G(s) = \dfrac{2}{s(s+1)(0.5s+1)}$ in series with a saturation element having $K = 4$, $M = 8$. Determine the **maximum sinusoidal input amplitude $A$** for which the loop behaves as if there is *no* saturation. Comment on the closed-loop behaviour when $A$ exceeds this value. (7 marks)

#### Model Solution — Q.B(a)

**Step 1 — Define parameters.** Let $\alpha = M/K = 8/4 = 2$ (linear threshold).

**Step 2 — Output waveform.** For input $x = A\sin\omega t$ with $A \ge M$:

$$y(t) = \begin{cases} K A \sin\omega t, & 0 \le \omega t \le \arcsin(M/A) \\ M, & \arcsin(M/A) \le \omega t \le \pi/2 \end{cases}$$

**Step 3 — Fundamental Fourier coefficient.** Following the derivation in §3.2:

$$b_1 = \frac{2KA}{\pi}\left[\arcsin\!\frac{M}{A} + \frac{M}{A}\sqrt{1-\left(\frac{M}{A}\right)^2}\right]$$

**Step 4 — Describing Function:**

$$\boxed{N_{\text{sat}}(A) = \frac{2K}{\pi}\left[\arcsin\!\frac{M}{A} + \frac{M}{A}\sqrt{1-\left(\frac{M}{A}\right)^2}\right]}$$

*Marking: 1 mark for waveform, 4 marks for integration steps, 1 mark for final DF, 1 mark for sketch.*

#### Model Solution — Q.B(b)

**Step 1 — Identify the boundary.** Saturation becomes *inactive* (i.e., the loop is purely linear) as long as the input amplitude at the saturation element never exceeds the linear threshold $a = M/K = 2$. Thus the boundary amplitude is:

$$A_{\text{boundary}} = a = 2$$

**Step 2 — Compute the loop gain for $A \le 2$.** $N(A) = K = 4$, and the open-loop transfer function is:

$$L(s) = K \cdot G(s) = \frac{8}{s(s+1)(0.5s+1)}$$

Crossover frequency: solve $\vert L(j\omega)\vert = 1$. Numerically, $\omega_c \approx 1.5\ \text{rad/s}$, phase margin $\approx 40°$ — stable.

**Step 3 — Behaviour when $A > 2$.** From §3.2, $N_{\text{sat}}(A)$ **decreases** with $A$ — the effective loop gain drops. Consequences:
- Crossover frequency **decreases** → slower response.
- Phase margin **increases** → more damped but sluggish.
- Steady-state error for step inputs **increases** because the integrator is preceded by a saturating element.
- Risk of **integrator wind-up** if the controller has integral action.

*Marking: 2 marks for boundary, 2 marks for nominal loop, 2 marks for $A > 2$ behaviour, 1 mark for engineering comment.*

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall — Where Students Commonly Lose Marks**
>
> 1. **Confusing $N(A)$ with $b_1$.** $N(A) = b_1/A$, *not* $b_1$ itself. Many students forget to divide by the input amplitude $A$ and end up reporting a "DF" that is independent of $A$ — a dead giveaway of incomplete understanding.
> 2. **Forgetting the units/scale of $\omega$.** The DF method assumes a *sinusoidal input at frequency $\omega$* and is a **first-harmonic** approximation. Reporting "$\omega$" without explicitly computing the imaginary part of the plant's frequency response leads to lost marks.
> 3. **Mixing up dead-zone and saturation DFs.** Remember the identity $N_{\text{dz}}(A) = K - N_{\text{sat}}(A)$ — it is a quick cross-check that examiners reward.
> 4. **Plotting $-1/N(A)$ on the wrong side.** For a *negative-real* $N(A)$ (rare — only in odd-symmetric systems with inversion), the critical locus can be on the *positive* real axis. Always verify the sign.
> 5. **Ignoring multi-valued nonlinearities (backlash).** Backlash needs a **Dual-Input Describing Function (DIDF)** or **phase-plane** method; using a single-tone DF is mathematically invalid here. Examiners will deduct for this.

---

## Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!TIP]
> **Rapid-Revision Checklist — must be at your fingertips for KTU ESE**

- [ ] **Six canonical physical nonlinearities:** Saturation, Dead-Zone, Ideal Relay, Relay with Hysteresis, Backlash, Friction.
- [ ] **Static vs. Dynamic:** Saturation, dead-zone, ideal relay are *static*; hysteresis, backlash, friction are *dynamic*.
- [ ] **Ideal Relay DF:** $N(A) = 4M/(\pi A)$ — pure real, positive, amplitude-dependent.
- [ ] **Saturation DF:** $N(A) = (2K/\pi)\bigl[\arcsin(M/A) + (M/A)\sqrt{1-(M/A)^2}\bigr]$, valid for $A \ge M$.
- [ ] **Dead-Zone DF:** $N(A) = K - (2K/\pi)\bigl[\arcsin(\Delta/A) + (\Delta/A)\sqrt{1-(\Delta/A)^2}\bigr]$, valid for $A \ge \Delta$.
- [ ] **Unifying identity:** $N_{\text{dz}}(A) + N_{\text{sat}}(A) = K$.
- [ ] **Relay + Hysteresis DF:** $N(A) = (4M/(\pi A))\sqrt{1-(b/A)^2} \;-\; j(4Mb/(\pi A^2))$ — has a *phase-lag* term.
- [ ] **Backlash** is *multi-valued* — cannot be DF-analyzed with a single sinusoid; use DIDF or phase-plane.
- [ ] **Friction model:** $F = \bigl[F_c + (F_s - F_c)e^{-(v/v_s)^2}\bigr]\text{sgn}(v) + B v$ — Stribeck + Coulomb + Viscous.
- [ ] **Limit cycle prediction:** Intersect $G(j\omega)$ (linear part) with $-1/N(A)$ (nonlinear critical locus).
- [ ] **Engineering "fingerprints":** Saturation → integrator wind-up; Dead-zone → steady-state error; Relay → bang-bang oscillation; Backlash → poor low-speed tracking; Friction → stick-slip at low velocity.
- [ ] **Describing Function Assumption (very important for KTU viva):** The plant $G(s)$ is a **low-pass filter** so that harmonics generated by the nonlinearity are sufficiently attenuated — the first-harmonic approximation is then valid.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Yield Mapping for KTU 2024:** This topic contributes directly to **CO1** of PERAT634: *"Identify and classify physical nonlinearities in control systems and apply describing function analysis to predict limit cycles."* Mastery of the six DFs in §2.7 and the four derivations in §3.1–3.4 is sufficient to secure **full marks** on any Module-1 ESE question.

topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PERAT634 - NON-LINEAR CONTROL
> - **Module:** Module 1
