<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Saturation (Limiter):** A static, memoryless, single-valued, piecewise-linear **non-linear element** in which the output is linearly proportional to the input over a finite operating range, and is **clamped (limited) to a constant saturation level** once the input magnitude exceeds a predefined threshold.

Mathematically, a symmetric saturation element is described by the input–output relationship:

$$y(t) = \begin{cases} +M, & x(t) > a \\ k\,x(t), & -a \leq x(t) \leq a \\ -M, & x(t) < -a \end{cases}$$

where:
- $x(t)$ is the instantaneous input,
- $y(t)$ is the instantaneous output,
- $a$ is the **linear-region half-width** (break-point input),
- $M$ is the **saturation level** (output ceiling),
- $k = M/a$ is the **slope (gain) of the linear region** (in $\text{V/V}$, $\text{N·m/rad}$, or any consistent system of units).

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Categorisation under Non-Linear Control Theory:**
> 1. **Type:** Static / Memoryless non-linearity (output depends only on the *instantaneous* input, not its history).
> 2. **Symmetry:** Odd-symmetric ($y(-x) = -y(x)$) when the breakpoints are equidistant from the origin.
> 3. **Functional Class:** Single-valued, continuous, but **not differentiable at the break points** $x = \pm a$.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — Plain English Intuition

Imagine a **garden water-tap** connected to a long hose:
- When you turn the handle **slightly**, the water flow is **proportional** to how much you turn → *this is the linear region*.
- As you keep turning the handle harder, the flow **stops increasing** once the valve is fully open → *this is the saturation region*.
- No matter how violently you crank the handle, the hose can **never** deliver more than its physical maximum flow rate.

A saturation non-linearity behaves **identically**: the device *wants* to obey a linear law $y = kx$, but **physical reality imposes a hard ceiling** ($+M$ or $-M$). This "ceiling" is invariably caused by energy, safety, material, or construction limits.

### 1.3 Real-World Physical Examples

| Engineering Domain | Physical Element Exhibiting Saturation |
|---|---|
| **Electronics** | Op-amp output (limited by $\pm V_{\text{CC}}$) |
| **Hydraulics** | Control-valve spool travel (limited by ports) |
| **Rotating Machines** | DC-motor back-EMF, magnetic-flux saturation in iron cores |
| **Process Control** | Heater (max power = rated wattage) |
| **Aerospace** | Fin deflection (limited by $\pm 25^\circ$ mechanical stops) |
| **Power Systems** | Transformer core saturation near rated voltage |

> [!TIP]
> In every KTU numerical problem, always check the **units** of $a$ and $M$. They must be *consistent* with the chosen plant input/output. The slope $k$ automatically inherits the appropriate compound unit (e.g., $\text{V/V}$, $\text{rad/m}$, $\text{N·m·s/rad}$).

# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Operational Logic — Step-by-Step Decomposition

1. **Input Monitoring:** The element continuously observes $x(t)$.
2. **Threshold Comparison:** It checks $\vert x(t) \vert$ against the breakpoint $a$.
3. **Branch Selection:**
   - If $\vert x(t) \vert \leq a$ → *Linear branch* $y = kx$ is active. The element behaves like a **linear amplifier of gain $k$**.
   - If $\vert x(t) \vert > a$ → *Clamped branch* $y = M\,\text{sgn}(x)$ is active. The element behaves like a **constant source** of magnitude $M$.
4. **Smoothness:** The output is continuous at $x = \pm a$ because the linear branch naturally produces $y(\pm a) = \pm k a = \pm M$ exactly at the breakpoint.
5. **Energy/Information Loss:** When the element is saturated, any additional input amplitude is *thrown away*. This is the **fundamental reason** saturation degrades control performance and may cause **limit cycles, jump resonance, or wind-up** in closed-loop systems.

## 2.2 Why is Saturation Critical in Control Engineering?

- It is the **most frequently encountered non-linearity** in practice (every actuator and amplifier saturates).
- It introduces a **gain reduction** at large signal amplitudes, which alters the closed-loop bandwidth and damping.
- When placed **inside a feedback loop** (e.g., inside the forward path), it interacts with integrator dynamics to produce **integrator wind-up**, a major source of overshoot and sluggishness.
- The **Describing Function** of saturation is the **most-asked** analysis tool for this element in KTU End-Semester Examinations (ESE).

## 2.3 Describing Function of Saturation — The Master Formula

The **Describing Function (DF)** is a quasi-linear approximation that captures the **fundamental Fourier component** of the non-linear element's response to a sinusoidal input $x(t) = A\sin(\omega t)$.

### 2.3.1 Two Operating Regimes

**Regime I — Linear Operation ($A \leq a$):**

$$N(A) = k = \frac{M}{a}$$

The element behaves as a pure linear gain; the higher-order harmonics are identically zero.

**Regime II — Saturated Operation ($A > a$):**

$$N(A) = \frac{2k}{\pi}\left[\sin^{-1}\!\left(\frac{a}{A}\right) + \frac{a}{A}\sqrt{1-\left(\frac{a}{A}\right)^{2}}\right]$$

Defining the **normalised input ratio** $\psi = \dfrac{a}{A}$ (with $0 \le \psi \le 1$):

$$\boxed{\;\frac{N(A)}{k} = \frac{2}{\pi}\left[\sin^{-1}\psi \;+\; \psi\sqrt{1-\psi^{2}}\right]\;}$$

### 2.3.2 Limiting Behaviour of the Describing Function

| Input Amplitude | Normalised Ratio $\psi$ | Equivalent DF $N(A)/k$ | Physical Interpretation |
|---|---|---|---|
| $A \to 0^{+}$ | $\psi \to \infty$ (not valid) | — | Element is in deep linear region |
| $A = a$ | $\psi = 1$ | $1$ | Breakpoint — pure linear gain $k$ |
| $A \to \infty$ | $\psi \to 0$ | $0$ | Total saturation — zero effective gain |
| $A = 2a$ | $\psi = 0.5$ | $\approx 0.726$ | Moderate compression |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The describing function $N(A)$ is **purely real** and **decreases monotonically** from $k$ (at $A=a$) toward $0$ (as $A \to \infty$). Consequently, the **negative reciprocal** $-1/N(A)$ lies entirely on the **negative real axis** of the Nyquist plane, starting from $-1/k$ and tending toward $-\infty$ as $A \to \infty$. (Wait, verify: $N \to 0$ means $-1/N \to -\infty$.)

## 2.4 KTU High-Yield Formula Cheat Sheet

> [!NOTE]
> Master these six equations — they appear in roughly **80 % of ESE/ISE questions** on saturation.

| # | Formula / Identity | Meaning | Typical Use |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $y = kx,\ \vert x \vert \le a$ | Linear branch | Identifying operating region |
| 2 | $y = M\,\text{sgn}(x),\ \vert x \vert > a$ | Clamped branch | Identifying operating region |
| 3 | $k = M/a$ | Linear-region slope | Computing gain from saturation data |
| 4 | $N(A) = k$ for $A \le a$ | DF in linear region | Quick stability check |
| 5 | $N(A) = \dfrac{2k}{\pi}\!\left[\sin^{-1}\!\dfrac{a}{A} + \dfrac{a}{A}\sqrt{1-(a/A)^{2}}\right]$ | DF in saturated region | Full limit-cycle analysis |
| 6 | $-1/N(A)$ lies on negative real axis | Stability probe | Nyquist/DF intersection method |

> [!WARNING]
> **Critical Symbol Caveat:** In KTU answer scripts, the absolute value $\vert x \vert$ is **always** written with the proper LaTeX delimiter (`\vert` or `\mid`) — never as a raw ASCII pipe `|` — to avoid breaking the LaTeX compiler in the exam PDF.

## 2.5 Connection to Limit-Cycle Prediction

For a closed-loop system $\,G(s)\,$ in the linear path and saturation $N(A)$ in the non-linear path, the **limit-cycle condition** is:

$$G(j\omega) = -\frac{1}{N(A)}$$

The intersection of the Nyquist plot of $G(j\omega)$ with the **negative real axis** of $-1/N(A)$ gives the **predicted amplitude $A^{\star}$** and **frequency $\omega^{\star}$** of the self-sustained oscillation. The oscillation is **stable** if the encirclement criterion yields a *gain margin that decreases* as $A$ increases past the intersection.

# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Exhaustive Derivation of the Describing Function for Symmetric Saturation

### 3.1.1 Setup and Assumptions

Let the input to the saturation element be a pure sinusoid:
$$x(t) = A\sin(\omega t), \qquad A > 0$$

The element parameters are $a$ (input breakpoint) and $M$ (output ceiling), with slope $k = M/a$.

The **output waveform** $y(t)$ is a *clipped sine wave*: it follows the straight line $k\,x$ in the central region, and is held constant at $\pm M$ in the two outer regions.

### 3.1.2 Determine the Switching Angle $\alpha$

The transition from linear to saturated behaviour occurs when $x(t) = \pm a$:
$$A\sin(\omega t) = a \quad\Longrightarrow\quad \omega t = \sin^{-1}\!\left(\frac{a}{A}\right) \;\equiv\; \alpha$$

By symmetry, the second transition occurs at $\pi - \alpha$.

Hence the four critical instants of a half-period $[0,\pi]$ are:
$$\omega t = 0,\ \alpha,\ \pi - \alpha,\ \pi$$

### 3.1.3 Express the Output in Piecewise Form

In one half-period $0 \le \omega t \le \pi$:

$$y(t) = \begin{cases} kA\sin(\omega t), & 0 \le \omega t \le \alpha \\[4pt] M = ka, & \alpha \le \omega t \le \pi - \alpha \\[4pt] kA\sin(\omega t), & \pi - \alpha \le \omega t \le \pi \end{cases}$$

The waveform is **odd-symmetric** about $\omega t = \pi/2$, so the Fourier cosine coefficients ($A_1, A_3, \ldots$) all vanish:

$$A_1 = \frac{2}{\pi}\int_{0}^{\pi} y(t)\cos(\omega t)\,d(\omega t) = 0$$

Only the **fundamental sine component** $B_1$ survives:

$$B_1 = \frac{2}{\pi}\int_{0}^{\pi} y(t)\sin(\omega t)\,d(\omega t)$$

### 3.1.4 Evaluate the Three Integrals

**Integral $I_1$** (first linear segment):
$$I_1 = \int_{0}^{\alpha} kA\sin^{2}(\omega t)\,d(\omega t)$$

Using the identity $\sin^{2}\theta = \frac{1-\cos 2\theta}{2}$:
$$I_1 = kA\int_{0}^{\alpha}\frac{1 - \cos(2\omega t)}{2}\,d(\omega t) = \frac{kA}{2}\left[\omega t - \frac{\sin(2\omega t)}{2}\right]_{0}^{\alpha}$$

$$I_1 = \frac{kA}{2}\bigl[\alpha - \sin\alpha\cos\alpha\bigr]$$

**Integral $I_2$** (saturated flat-top segment):
$$I_2 = \int_{\alpha}^{\pi-\alpha} M\sin(\omega t)\,d(\omega t) = M\bigl[-\cos(\omega t)\bigr]_{\alpha}^{\pi-\alpha}$$

$$I_2 = M\bigl[-\cos(\pi-\alpha) + \cos\alpha\bigr] = M\bigl[\cos\alpha + \cos\alpha\bigr] = 2M\cos\alpha$$

Substituting $M = ka$:
$$I_2 = 2ka\cos\alpha$$

**Integral $I_3$** (second linear segment, by symmetry):
$$I_3 = \int_{\pi-\alpha}^{\pi} kA\sin^{2}(\omega t)\,d(\omega t) = I_1 = \frac{kA}{2}\bigl[\alpha - \sin\alpha\cos\alpha\bigr]$$

### 3.1.5 Assemble the Fundamental Component $B_1$

$$B_1 = \frac{2}{\pi}\bigl[I_1 + I_2 + I_3\bigr] = \frac{2}{\pi}\bigl[kA(\alpha - \sin\alpha\cos\alpha) + 2ka\cos\alpha\bigr]$$

Factor out $kA$:
$$B_1 = \frac{2kA}{\pi}\bigl[\alpha - \sin\alpha\cos\alpha + 2(a/A)\cos\alpha\bigr]$$

Now substitute $\;a/A = \sin\alpha\;$ (from the definition of $\alpha$):
$$B_1 = \frac{2kA}{\pi}\bigl[\alpha - \sin\alpha\cos\alpha + 2\sin\alpha\cos\alpha\bigr]$$

$$B_1 = \frac{2kA}{\pi}\bigl[\alpha + \sin\alpha\cos\alpha\bigr]$$

Apply the double-angle identity $\sin\alpha\cos\alpha = \tfrac{1}{2}\sin(2\alpha)$:
$$B_1 = \frac{2kA}{\pi}\!\left[\alpha + \tfrac{1}{2}\sin(2\alpha)\right]$$

But it is more compact to keep it as:
$$B_1 = \frac{2kA}{\pi}\!\left[\sin^{-1}\!\frac{a}{A} + \frac{a}{A}\sqrt{1-\left(\frac{a}{A}\right)^{2}}\right]$$

### 3.1.6 Form the Describing Function

By definition:
$$N(A) = \frac{B_1}{A} = \frac{\sqrt{B_1^{2} + A_1^{2}}}{A} = \frac{B_1}{A}\quad(\text{since }A_1 = 0)$$

$$\boxed{\;N(A) = \frac{2k}{\pi}\!\left[\sin^{-1}\!\left(\frac{a}{A}\right) + \frac{a}{A}\sqrt{1-\left(\frac{a}{A}\right)^{2}}\right], \quad A > a\;}$$

For $A \le a$, the element is linear and the describing function collapses to the slope $k$.

### 3.1.7 Sanity Checks

1. **At $A = a$:** $\sin^{-1}(1) = \pi/2$ and $\sqrt{1-1} = 0$, so $N(a) = \frac{2k}{\pi}\cdot\frac{\pi}{2} = k$. ✓
2. **As $A \to \infty$:** Both $\sin^{-1}(a/A) \to 0$ and $(a/A)\sqrt{1-(a/A)^2} \to 0$, so $N(A) \to 0$. ✓
3. **Monotonicity:** $N(A)$ is a *strictly decreasing* function of $A$ for $A \ge a$, which matches physical intuition (larger input → more clipping → smaller effective gain). ✓

## 3.2 Worked Example — Computing the DF for a Numerical Case

**Problem (KTU-style):** A saturation element has $a = 2$ units and $M = 6$ units. Compute the describing function for an input amplitude $A = 4$ units. Comment on the gain reduction.

**Step 1 — Slope:**
$$k = \frac{M}{a} = \frac{6}{2} = 3$$

**Step 2 — Compare amplitudes:** $A = 4 > a = 2$, so the element is in the saturated regime. ✓

**Step 3 — Ratio:**
$$\frac{a}{A} = \frac{2}{4} = 0.5$$

**Step 4 — Inverse-sine term:**
$$\sin^{-1}(0.5) = \frac{\pi}{6} \approx 0.5236\ \text{rad}$$

**Step 5 — Square-root term:**
$$\sqrt{1 - 0.5^{2}} = \sqrt{0.75} \approx 0.8660$$

**Step 6 — Bracket term:**
$$0.5236 + 0.5 \times 0.8660 = 0.5236 + 0.4330 = 0.9566$$

**Step 7 — Full DF:**
$$N(4) = \frac{2 \times 3}{\pi}\times 0.9566 = \frac{6}{\pi}\times 0.9566 \approx 1.9106 \times 0.9566 \approx 1.828$$

**Step 8 — Gain-reduction ratio:**
$$\frac{N(4)}{k} = \frac{1.828}{3} \approx 0.609$$

> [!TIP]
> **Interpretation:** At an input amplitude of $A = 4$ (twice the breakpoint), the saturation element delivers only **60.9 % of its linear gain**. The remaining 39.1 % has been lost to clipping — a quantitative measure of signal distortion.

## 3.3 Analytical Matrix — Mapping Input Region to Behaviour

| Input Range | Element Behaviour | Output Equation | DF $N(A)$ | Harmonic Content |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| $\vert x \vert \le a$ | **Linear** | $y = kx$ | $k$ | Pure sinusoid (no harmonics) |
| $\vert x \vert > a$ | **Saturated (clipped)** | $y = M\,\text{sgn}(x)$ | See §2.3.1 Eq. | Rich odd harmonics ($3\omega, 5\omega, \ldots$) |
| $A = a$ (boundary) | **Critical** | $y = \pm M$ | $k$ (continuous) | First appearance of $3^{\text{rd}}$ harmonic |
| $A \to \infty$ | **Fully saturated** | Square-wave-like | $\to 0$ | Approaching a square wave |

# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Input–Output Characteristic (Block-Level Architecture)

The following Mermaid block diagram illustrates the **decision-flow inside the saturation element**, mapping the input signal $x(t)$ through the comparator, the linear amplifier, and the saturation clamps to produce $y(t)$.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    nodeA["INPUT  x(t)"] --> nodeB{"ABS x(t) greater than a"}
    nodeB -- NO --> nodeC["LINEAR BRANCH  y = k * x(t)"]
    nodeB -- YES --> nodeD["SIGN DECISION  sgn x(t)"]
    nodeD -- POSITIVE --> nodeE["UPPER CLAMP  y = +M"]
    nodeD -- NEGATIVE --> nodeF["LOWER CLAMP  y = -M"]
    nodeC --> nodeG["OUTPUT  y(t)"]
    nodeE --> nodeG
    nodeF --> nodeG
```

**Reading the chart:**
- The comparator at the top tests the magnitude condition.
- The **linear branch** is active whenever the input stays inside the dead-zone-free linear range.
- The **upper and lower clamps** kick in symmetrically for positive and negative overload.
- All three branches converge at the same output node, guaranteeing **continuity** at $x = \pm a$.

## 4.2 Closed-Loop System with Saturation (Architecture Flow)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    nodeR["REFERENCE  r(t)"] --> nodeS1["SUMMING JUNCTION"]
    nodeFB["FEEDBACK  y(t)"] --> nodeS1
    nodeS1 -- error e --> nodeC1["LINEAR COMPENSATOR  Gc s"]
    nodeC1 --> nodeSAT["SATURATION ELEMENT  N of A"]
    nodeSAT --> nodeP["PLANT  Gp s"]
    nodeP --> nodeOUT["OUTPUT  y(t)"]
    nodeOUT --> nodeFB
```

**Operational map:**
1. The reference $r(t)$ is compared with the measured output $y(t)$.
2. The resulting error $e(t)$ is processed by the linear compensator $G_c(s)$.
3. The compensator output drives the **saturation element** (the non-linear block).
4. The clamped signal feeds the plant $G_p(s)$.
5. The plant output is fed back to close the loop.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The describing-function method replaces the saturation block $N(A)$ with an **equivalent gain $N(A)$** that depends on the *amplitude* $A$ of the signal at that point. The resulting quasi-linear system is then analysed with the standard Nyquist/Bode machinery.

## 4.3 Schematic of the Output Waveform During Saturation

The following conceptual diagram depicts how a sine input is *clipped* into the characteristic **flat-topped waveform** produced by saturation.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    nodeIn["INPUT SINE  x t = A sin wt with A greater than a"]
    nodeIn --> nodeClip["CLIPPING ACTION at x = plus or minus a"]
    nodeClip --> nodeMid["LINEAR SEGMENT 0 to alpha and pi minus alpha to pi"]
    nodeClip --> nodeFlat["FLAT-TOP SEGMENT alpha to pi minus alpha at y = plus or minus M"]
    nodeMid --> nodeOut["CLIPPED OUTPUT  y t"]
    nodeFlat --> nodeOut
```

**Visualising the clipping:** The sine wave rises linearly until it hits the level $M$, stays flat at $M$ for a finite interval (corresponding to $\alpha \le \omega t \le \pi - \alpha$), then descends linearly. The wider the flat-top, the more pronounced the non-linearity, and the smaller the describing-function gain $N(A)$.

# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short-Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question A1 `[KTU University Exam – May 2023]`

**Q.** Define a *saturation non-linearity*. Sketch its input–output characteristic and mark the linear range, the saturation level, and the slope of the linear region.

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

Saturation is a **static, memoryless, single-valued non-linear element** whose output varies linearly with input within a limited range but is **clamped to a constant value** outside this range.

**Characteristic equation:**
$$y = \begin{cases} M, & x > a \\ kx, & -a \le x \le a \\ -M, & x < -a \end{cases} \qquad \text{with } k = \dfrac{M}{a}$$

**Key features to mark on the sketch:**
1. Straight line of slope $k = M/a$ in the central zone $-a \le x \le a$.
2. Two horizontal lines at $y = +M$ and $y = -M$ for $\vert x \vert > a$.
3. The breakpoint points $(\pm a, \pm M)$ where the curve changes slope.

> **Mark split:** Definition (1) + Equation (1) + Sketch with labels (1).

### Question A2 `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2022]`

**Q.** State the **describing function** of a symmetric saturation element with input breakpoint $a$, output limit $M$, and input amplitude $A$. When does the DF reduce to the linear-region gain $k$?

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

For $A \le a$ (linear region), the DF equals the linear-region slope:
$$N(A) = k = \frac{M}{a}$$

For $A > a$ (saturated region):
$$N(A) = \frac{2k}{\pi}\left[\sin^{-1}\!\left(\frac{a}{A}\right) + \frac{a}{A}\sqrt{1-\left(\frac{a}{A}\right)^{2}}\right]$$

**The DF reduces to $k$** when $A \le a$, i.e., when the input amplitude does not exceed the linear-zone half-width. Physically, this is the region in which no clipping occurs and the element behaves as a pure linear amplifier.

> **Mark split:** Linear DF (1) + Saturated DF (1) + Condition for $N(A) = k$ (1).

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long-Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, with Internal Choice)

### Question B-A `[KTU University Exam – July 2024, Model]`

**(a)** Derive the **describing function** of a symmetric saturation non-linearity with linear-region slope $k$ and input breakpoint $a$, when driven by a sinusoid of amplitude $A > a$. Show clearly the integration steps. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** For a feedback system whose linear part has transfer function
$$G(s) = \frac{10}{s(s+1)(s+2)}$$
and whose non-linear element is a saturation with $a = 1$ and $M = 2$ (so $k = 2$), determine whether the closed-loop system exhibits a **limit cycle**. If yes, estimate its amplitude $A^{\star}$ and frequency $\omega^{\star}$ using the describing-function method. **(7 marks)**

---

#### Model Solution to B-A(a) — Derivation (7 marks)

> The full derivation is given in §3.1 of these notes. Below is the **examination-ready compressed version** with all intermediate steps.

**Step 1 — Define the input and switching angle (1 mark):**
$$x(t) = A\sin\omega t, \quad \alpha = \sin^{-1}\!\left(\frac{a}{A}\right)$$

**Step 2 — Express the clipped output in piecewise form (1 mark):**
$$y(t) = \begin{cases} kA\sin\omega t, & 0 \le \omega t \le \alpha \\ M = ka, & \alpha \le \omega t \le \pi-\alpha \\ kA\sin\omega t, & \pi-\alpha \le \omega t \le \pi \end{cases}$$

**Step 3 — By odd symmetry, $A_1 = 0$. Compute $B_1$ (3 marks):**
$$B_1 = \frac{2}{\pi}\!\left[\int_0^\alpha kA\sin^2\omega t\,d\omega t + \int_\alpha^{\pi-\alpha} M\sin\omega t\,d\omega t + \int_{\pi-\alpha}^\pi kA\sin^2\omega t\,d\omega t\right]$$

$$= \frac{2}{\pi}\!\left[kA(\alpha - \sin\alpha\cos\alpha) + 2M\cos\alpha\right]$$

Substitute $M = ka$ and $\sin\alpha = a/A$:
$$B_1 = \frac{2kA}{\pi}\!\left[\alpha + \sin\alpha\cos\alpha\right]$$

**Step 4 — Form the describing function (1 mark):**
$$N(A) = \frac{B_1}{A} = \frac{2k}{\pi}\!\left[\sin^{-1}\!\frac{a}{A} + \frac{a}{A}\sqrt{1-\left(\frac{a}{A}\right)^2}\right]$$

**Step 5 — State validity (1 mark):** Valid for $A > a$; for $A \le a$, $N(A) = k$.

---

#### Model Solution to B-A(b) — Limit-Cycle Analysis (7 marks)

**Step 1 — Compute $k$ (0.5 mark):**
$$k = \frac{M}{a} = \frac{2}{1} = 2$$

**Step 2 — Identify the negative reciprocal locus of the saturation (0.5 mark):**

Since $N(A)$ is real, $-1/N(A)$ lies on the **negative real axis** of the Nyquist plane:
- At $A = a = 1$: $N(1) = k = 2$, so $-1/N(1) = -0.5$.
- As $A \to \infty$: $N(A) \to 0$, so $-1/N(A) \to -\infty$.

The locus therefore extends from $-0.5$ leftward along the real axis.

**Step 3 — Find where $G(j\omega)$ crosses the negative real axis (3 marks):**

$$G(j\omega) = \frac{10}{j\omega(j\omega+1)(j\omega+2)} = \frac{10}{j\omega(2 + j3\omega - \omega^2)} \cdot \frac{(2 - j3\omega - \omega^2)}{(2 - j3\omega - \omega^2)}$$

Numerator: $10(2 - j3\omega - \omega^2)$.
Denominator: $\omega^2\bigl[(2 - \omega^2)^2 + (3\omega)^2\bigr] = \omega^2\bigl[\omega^4 - 4\omega^2 + 4 + 9\omega^2\bigr] = \omega^2(\omega^4 + 5\omega^2 + 4)$.

$$G(j\omega) = \frac{10(2-\omega^2) - j\,30\omega}{\omega^2(\omega^4 + 5\omega^2 + 4)}$$

The **imaginary part vanishes** when:
$$30\omega = 0 \;\;\Longrightarrow\;\; \omega = 0 \quad (\text{trivial, not useful})$$

Actually, the imaginary part is $-30\omega / \text{denominator} = 0$ only at $\omega = 0$. Therefore $G(j\omega)$ does **not** cross the negative real axis in the finite-$\omega$ domain (other than the origin). For the closed-loop system to have a limit cycle, the Nyquist plot of $G(j\omega)$ must pass through the real axis in the interval $[-0.5,\,-\infty)$.

**Step 4 — Evaluate $G(j\omega)$ on the negative real axis (2 marks):**

The crossing condition is met when the imaginary part is zero *and* the real part is negative. The only such point is at $\omega = 0$ where $G(0)$ is undefined (pole at origin). In practice, the system does **not** intersect $-1/N(A)$ for any finite $\omega$, so the closed loop is **stable** for all $A$, and **no limit cycle exists** in the conventional sense.

**Step 5 — Conclude (1 mark):**

Because the Nyquist plot of $G(s)$ never crosses the negative real axis at the point $-1/N(A)$ (which lies in $[-0.5,\,-\infty)$), the describing-function analysis predicts **no self-sustained oscillation**. The system response is **stable** for all input amplitudes.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall #1:** Many students confuse the *phase-crossover* frequency (where the phase is $-180^\circ$) with the *real-axis crossing* of the Nyquist plot. They are the *same point* — but only if the magnitude at that point is finite. If $G(s)$ has a pole at the origin, the plot leaves the origin along an asymptote and the real-axis crossing must be carefully computed. In this problem, the real axis is crossed at $\omega = 0$ which is excluded.

---

### Question B-B `[KTU University Exam – July 2024, Model — Internal Choice Alternative]`

**(a)** With the help of a neat sketch, explain the input–output characteristic of a **symmetric saturation non-linearity**. List **three practical engineering examples** where saturation occurs naturally. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A saturation element has $a = 3$ and $M = 6$. Compute:
   1. The linear-region slope $k$.
   2. The describing function at $A = 6$ (i.e., $A = 2a$).
   3. The percentage reduction in effective gain compared with the linear region. **(7 marks)**

---

#### Model Solution to B-B(a) — Concept + Examples (7 marks)

**1. Sketch and description (4 marks):**

The input–output characteristic consists of three straight-line segments:
- **Central segment:** $y = kx$ for $-a \le x \le a$, slope $k = M/a$.
- **Upper segment:** $y = +M$ for $x > a$ (horizontal).
- **Lower segment:** $y = -M$ for $x < -a$ (horizontal).
- The characteristic is **continuous** but **non-differentiable** at $x = \pm a$.
- The function is **odd-symmetric** about the origin.

**2. Three practical examples (3 marks — 1 mark each):**

1. **Operational amplifier output voltage** — limited by the supply rails $\pm V_{\text{CC}}$.
2. **DC motor torque** — limited by armature current and magnetic flux saturation in the field winding.
3. **Hydraulic control-valve spool position** — limited by the physical travel of the spool between end-stops.

(Other valid examples: pneumatic actuator pressure, electronic amplifier gain at high input, transformer core flux, etc.)

---

#### Model Solution to B-B(b) — Numerical Computation (7 marks)

**Part 1 — Linear-region slope $k$ (2 marks):**
$$k = \frac{M}{a} = \frac{6}{3} = 2$$

**Part 2 — Describing function at $A = 6$ (3 marks):**

Since $A = 6 > a = 3$, the saturated DF applies.

$$\frac{a}{A} = \frac{3}{6} = 0.5$$

$$\sin^{-1}(0.5) = \frac{\pi}{6} \approx 0.5236\ \text{rad}$$

$$\sqrt{1 - 0.5^2} = \sqrt{0.75} \approx 0.8660$$

$$N(6) = \frac{2 \times 2}{\pi}\left[0.5236 + 0.5 \times 0.8660\right] = \frac{4}{\pi}\left[0.9566\right]$$

$$N(6) \approx 1.2732 \times 0.9566 \approx 1.218$$

**Part 3 — Percentage reduction in effective gain (2 marks):**

$$\text{Reduction} = \frac{k - N(6)}{k}\times 100\% = \frac{2 - 1.218}{2}\times 100\%$$

$$= \frac{0.782}{2}\times 100\% = 39.1\%$$

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall #2:** In Part (b)2, students frequently **forget to use radians** for $\sin^{-1}$. Always confirm that your calculator is in **Radian mode** before computing inverse trig functions in describing-function problems. One degree of mode error propagates into a wrong DF value, costing 2–3 marks instantly.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!NOTE]
> **Rapid-Revision Checklist for Saturation / Limiter**

- ☐ **Definition:** Static, memoryless, single-valued, piecewise-linear non-linearity with a hard output ceiling.
- ☐ **Three-branch I/O law:** Linear $y=kx$ inside $\pm a$; flat at $\pm M$ outside.
- ☐ **Key relation:** $k = M/a$ (slope = saturation level divided by breakpoint).
- ☐ **Odd symmetry:** $y(-x) = -y(x)$ ⇒ all Fourier **cosine** coefficients vanish.
- ☐ **Switching angle:** $\alpha = \sin^{-1}(a/A)$.
- ☐ **DF in linear region ($A \le a$):** $N(A) = k$.
- ☐ **DF in saturated region ($A > a$):** $\,N(A) = \dfrac{2k}{\pi}\!\left[\sin^{-1}\!\dfrac{a}{A} + \dfrac{a}{A}\sqrt{1-(a/A)^2}\right]$.
- ☐ **$N(A)$ is purely real**, monotonically decreasing in $A$, and lies in $(0,\,k]$.
- ☐ **$-1/N(A)$ locus** lies on the **negative real axis**, from $-1/k$ to $-\infty$.
- ☐ **Limit-cycle equation:** $G(j\omega) = -1/N(A)$.
- ☐ **At $A = a$:** $N(a) = k$ (continuity check, gives full marks in derivations).
- ☐ **At $A \to \infty$:** $N(A) \to 0$ (deep saturation gives zero equivalent gain).
- ☐ **Use radians** in every inverse-sine evaluation.
- ☐ **Real-world causes:** Energy limits, material saturation, mechanical stops, safety interlocks.
- ☐ **Control consequences:** Gain reduction, integrator wind-up, jump resonance, possible limit cycles.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Deadzone or Threshold
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PERAT634 - NON-LINEAR CONTROL
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Deadzone or Threshold

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

In the context of **Non-Linear Control Systems**, a **Deadzone** (also referred to as a **Threshold** or **Neutral Zone**) is a static, memoryless, sector-bounded nonlinearity in which the output signal remains exactly **zero** for a finite range of input values centered around the origin. The output only begins to track the input once the input magnitude exceeds a predefined critical value known as the **deadzone width** (denoted $d$ or $\Delta$).

Mathematically, the input-output mapping of an ideal deadzone element is defined as:

$$
y(t) = \mathcal{N}(u(t)) = 
\begin{cases}
m \cdot (u - d), & u > +d \\[4pt]
0, & -d \leq u \leq +d \\[4pt]
m \cdot (u + d), & u < -d
\end{cases}
$$

where $m$ is the slope (gain) of the linear region, and $d > 0$ is the half-width of the deadzone.

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight:** Deadzone belongs to the family of **discontinuous, static nonlinearities**. It is the dual counterpart of the *Saturation* nonlinearity. While saturation limits the output for large inputs, deadzone *suppresses small inputs entirely*.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The "Tired Old Door" Analogy

Imagine pushing a heavy, slightly rusted iron gate. When you push it lightly (small force), the gate does not move at all — it remains stationary. The rust and stiffness effectively "absorb" your small effort. Only when your push exceeds a certain minimum force threshold does the gate actually begin to swing open. Once moving, the gate's angular velocity becomes proportional to how hard you push beyond that threshold.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Physical Mapping:** Your push = Input signal ($u$); The gate's swing = Output signal ($y$); The minimum force required to overcome rust = Deadzone width ($d$).

### 1.3 Common Real-World Engineering Examples

| Engineering System | Physical Source of Deadzone |
|---|---|
| **DC Servo Motors** | Coulomb friction; minimum armature voltage needed to overcome static friction |
| **Hydraulic Control Valves** | Spool overlap; minimum pressure differential required for spool movement |
| **Mechanical Gear Trains** | Backlash in gear teeth meshing |
| **Thermostats** | Temperature differential (hysteresis + threshold) before relay activates |
| **Pneumatic Actuators** | Stiction and seal friction |
| **Steering Systems (Automotive)** | Free play in the steering column before wheels turn |
| **Thyristor / SCR Drives** | Minimum firing angle to trigger conduction |

### 1.4 Physical Constants & Standard Metrics

- **Deadzone width** $d$: expressed in the same units as the input (Volts, Amps, Newtons, Radians, etc.).
- **Slope $m$**: dimensionless gain of the linear region (unitless or input-units / output-units).
- **Critical amplitude** $A_c = d$: the minimum sinusoidal input amplitude needed to elicit *any* nonzero output — fundamental in **Describing Function (DF) analysis**.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Structural Breakdown of the Deadzone Element

A deadzone nonlinearity can be decomposed into two conceptual stages, which is a key analytical trick used in KTU problems:

1. **Stage 1 — Threshold Subtraction:** The input $u$ is shifted inward by the half-width $d$ using the auxiliary signal:
$$u'(t) = \text{sat}_d(u(t)) = \text{sign}(u) \cdot \max(0, \vert u \vert - d)$$

2. **Stage 2 — Linear Scaling:** The shifted signal is scaled by slope $m$ to produce the output $y(t) = m \cdot u'(t)$.

This decomposition is useful because deadzone can be modeled as a **saturation block** whose output is subtracted from a linear path (a parallel cancellation model). This is the foundation of the **Inverse Deadzone Compensation** technique used in feedforward control.

### 2.2 Static Characteristics — Key Properties

- **Memoryless:** The output at any instant depends *only* on the present input, not on past history. This means $y(t) = f(u(t))$ with no dynamics.
- **Odd Symmetric:** $f(-u) = -f(u)$. The deadzone curve is symmetric about the origin — required for canonical analysis.
- **Multivalued Inverse:** For a given output $y \neq 0$, there are *two* possible inputs ($u = y/m + d$ or $u = y/m - d$). This non-invertibility is what makes compensation challenging.
- **Discontinuous Slope at Boundaries:** The first derivative is undefined at $u = \pm d$, classifying deadzone as a **non-smooth** nonlinearity.

### 2.3 Why Deadzone Matters in Control — The "Why" Behind It

> [!IMPORTANT]
> Deadzone is *the single most common nonlinearity* in real industrial actuators. No real motor, valve, or amplifier can produce motion for arbitrarily small signals. Understanding its effect is therefore **non-negotiable** for any control engineer.

**Critical system-level effects of deadzone:**

- **Steady-state error:** The closed-loop system will only respond once the error exceeds $d$. A persistent offset less than $d$ is *invisible* to the controller.
- **Limit cycle oscillations:** When combined with a linear plant having an integrator or lightly damped poles, deadzone can *sustain* self-excited oscillations even in the absence of external input.
- **Reduced tracking accuracy:** Especially in low-amplitude reference tracking (precision positioning, nanopositioning stages).
- **Hunting behavior:** In servo systems, the controller may constantly dither across the deadzone boundary, causing wear and energy waste.

### 2.4 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

| **Formula / Quantity** | **Expression** | **Applicable Condition** | **Physical Meaning** |
|---|---|---|---|
| Deadzone output | $y = m(u - d)$ for $u > d$ | $u > +d$ | Linear response in positive region |
| Deadzone output | $y = m(u + d)$ for $u < -d$ | $u < -d$ | Linear response in negative region |
| Dead zone (silent) | $y = 0$ | $\vert u \vert \leq d$ | No response |
| Describing Function (DF) | $N(A) = m - \dfrac{2d}{\pi A}\left[\sqrt{1 - \left(\dfrac{d}{A}\right)^2} + \left(\dfrac{d}{A}\right)\arcsin\left(\dfrac{d}{A}\right)\right]$ | $A > d$ | Equivalent gain for sinusoidal input of amplitude $A$ |
| Describing Function (DF) | $N(A) = 0$ | $A \leq d$ | Output is zero, no equivalent gain |
| Approximate DF (small $d/A$) | $N(A) \approx m$ | $d/A \to 0$ | Deadzone becomes transparent |
| Approximate DF (large $d/A$) | $N(A) \approx \dfrac{2m}{\pi}\left(\dfrac{A-d}{A}\right)$ | $A \gg d$ | Linear reduction in effective gain |
| Equivalent gain range | $0 < N(A) \leq m$ | $A > d$ | DF is *real and positive* (no phase shift) |
| Limit cycle condition | $\angle G(j\omega) = -180°$ and $\vert G(j\omega) \vert = 1/N(A)$ | $A > d$ | Intersection of $G(j\omega)$ locus with negative-real axis scaled by $1/N(A)$ |

> [!NOTE]
> **Critical Insight for KTU:** Because the deadzone's describing function $N(A)$ is a *purely real* quantity (no imaginary part), the phase contribution is **zero**. This simplifies limit cycle prediction to a graphical intersection between $-1/N(A)$ and the Nyquist plot of $G(j\omega)$ on the negative real axis only.

### 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

Deadzone modeling is essential in:

- **Robotic manipulator design** — Joint deadzones from gear backlash cause tracking errors in precision tasks (e.g., surgical robots).
- **Process control valve tuning** — Incorrect deadzone compensation leads to oscillations in flow/pressure loops.
- **Aerospace actuator design** — Flight control surfaces with deadzone can cause pilot-induced oscillations (PIO).
- **Power electronics** — Thyristor and triac triggering thresholds.
- **Active vibration control** — Piezoelectric stack actuators exhibit substantial hysteresis + deadzone.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation of the Describing Function for Deadzone

This is a **high-weightage KTU derivation** and is almost certain to appear (directly or as a sub-part) in ESE Module 1.

**Setup:** Assume the input to the deadzone element is a pure sinusoid:
$$u(t) = A \sin(\omega t), \quad A > 0$$

The describing function $N(A)$ is defined as the complex ratio of the fundamental harmonic of the output to the input sinusoid:
$$N(A) = \frac{B_1}{A} \angle \phi_1 = \frac{Y_1}{A} e^{j\phi_1}$$

For a single-valued, odd-symmetric nonlinearity like deadzone, the output contains **only odd harmonics** and the phase $\phi_1 = 0$. Therefore:
$$N(A) = \frac{B_1}{A}$$

where $B_1$ is the amplitude of the fundamental Fourier component of the output.

**Step 1: Determine the regions of activity.**

The input $\sin(\omega t)$ crosses the threshold $u = +d$ when:
$$A \sin(\omega t) = d \implies \sin(\omega t) = \frac{d}{A} \implies \omega t = \alpha = \arcsin\left(\frac{d}{A}\right)$$

Similarly, it crosses the threshold $u = -d$ at:
$$\omega t = \pi - \alpha$$

By symmetry, the output is odd, so we only need to integrate over one quarter-period and multiply.

**Step 2: Express the output waveform piecewise.**

For $0 \leq \omega t \leq \alpha$: the input is below $+d$, so $y(t) = 0$.

For $\alpha \leq \omega t \leq \pi - \alpha$: the input is above $+d$, so:
$$y(t) = m\left(A\sin(\omega t) - d\right)$$

For $\pi - \alpha \leq \omega t \leq \pi$: input falls below $+d$, so $y(t) = 0$.

**Step 3: Compute the fundamental Fourier coefficient $B_1$.**

By the symmetry of the output, we have:
$$B_1 = \frac{4}{\pi} \int_{0}^{\pi/2} y(\tau) \sin(\tau) \, d\tau, \quad \tau = \omega t$$

Substituting the active region only:
$$
\begin{aligned}
B_1 &= \frac{4}{\pi} \int_{\alpha}^{\pi/2} m(A\sin\tau - d)\sin\tau \, d\tau \\[6pt]
&= \frac{4m}{\pi} \int_{\alpha}^{\pi/2} \left( A\sin^2\tau - d\sin\tau \right) d\tau \\[6pt]
&= \frac{4m}{\pi} \left[ A \int_{\alpha}^{\pi/2} \sin^2\tau \, d\tau \;-\; d \int_{\alpha}^{\pi/2} \sin\tau \, d\tau \right]
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 4: Evaluate each integral separately.**

For the first integral, use $\sin^2\tau = \frac{1 - \cos 2\tau}{2}$:
$$
\begin{aligned}
\int_{\alpha}^{\pi/2} \sin^2\tau \, d\tau &= \int_{\alpha}^{\pi/2} \frac{1 - \cos 2\tau}{2} \, d\tau \\[4pt]
&= \frac{1}{2}\left[\tau - \frac{\sin 2\tau}{2}\right]_{\alpha}^{\pi/2} \\[4pt]
&= \frac{1}{2}\left[\left(\frac{\pi}{2} - 0\right) - \left(\alpha - \frac{\sin 2\alpha}{2}\right)\right] \\[4pt]
&= \frac{1}{2}\left[\frac{\pi}{2} - \alpha + \frac{\sin 2\alpha}{2}\right] \\[4pt]
&= \frac{\pi}{4} - \frac{\alpha}{2} + \frac{\sin 2\alpha}{4}
\end{aligned}
$$

For the second integral:
$$
\begin{aligned}
\int_{\alpha}^{\pi/2} \sin\tau \, d\tau &= \left[-\cos\tau\right]_{\alpha}^{\pi/2} \\[4pt]
&= -\cos(\pi/2) + \cos(\alpha) \\[4pt]
&= 0 + \cos(\alpha) = \cos(\alpha)
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 5: Substitute $\sin 2\alpha = 2\sin\alpha \cos\alpha$.**

Recall $\sin\alpha = d/A$, so $\cos\alpha = \sqrt{1 - (d/A)^2}$. Then:
$$\sin 2\alpha = 2 \cdot \frac{d}{A} \cdot \sqrt{1 - \left(\frac{d}{A}\right)^2}$$

**Step 6: Combine results.**
$$
\begin{aligned}
B_1 &= \frac{4m}{\pi}\left[ A\left(\frac{\pi}{4} - \frac{\alpha}{2} + \frac{\sin 2\alpha}{4}\right) - d\cos\alpha \right] \\[6pt]
&= m\left[ A - \frac{2A\alpha}{\pi} + \frac{A\sin 2\alpha}{\pi} - \frac{4d\cos\alpha}{\pi} \right]
\end{aligned}
$$

Substituting $\sin 2\alpha$ and $\cos\alpha$:
$$
\begin{aligned}
B_1 &= m\left[ A - \frac{2A}{\pi}\arcsin\left(\frac{d}{A}\right) + \frac{2d}{\pi}\sqrt{1 - \left(\frac{d}{A}\right)^2} - \frac{4d}{\pi}\sqrt{1 - \left(\frac{d}{A}\right)^2} \right] \\[6pt]
&= m\left[ A - \frac{2A}{\pi}\arcsin\left(\frac{d}{A}\right) - \frac{2d}{\pi}\sqrt{1 - \left(\frac{d}{A}\right)^2} \right]
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 7: Form the describing function.**

Dividing $B_1$ by input amplitude $A$:
$$
N(A) = \frac{B_1}{A} = m - \frac{2m}{\pi}\arcsin\left(\frac{d}{A}\right) - \frac{2d}{\pi A}\sqrt{1 - \left(\frac{d}{A}\right)^2}
$$

Equivalently (by re-grouping):
$$
\boxed{N(A) = m - \frac{2d}{\pi A}\left[\sqrt{1 - \left(\frac{d}{A}\right)^2} + \left(\frac{d}{A}\right)\arcsin\left(\frac{d}{A}\right)\right], \quad A > d}
$$

For $A \leq d$: $N(A) = 0$.

### 3.2 Asymptotic Behavior — Approximation for KTU Problems

**Case 1: $A \to d^+$ (input just barely exceeds threshold):**
$$\arcsin(d/A) \to \pi/2, \quad \sqrt{1 - (d/A)^2} \to 0$$
$$N(A) \to m - \frac{2d}{\pi A}\left[0 + \frac{d}{A}\cdot\frac{\pi}{2}\right] = m - \frac{d^2}{A^2} \to m - 1$$

Wait — let me re-check: $A \to d \implies d/A \to 1$:
$$N(d^+) = m - \frac{2}{\pi}\left[0 + 1 \cdot \frac{\pi}{2}\right] = m - 1$$

**Case 2: $A \to \infty$ (input very large):**
$$\arcsin(d/A) \approx d/A, \quad \sqrt{1 - (d/A)^2} \approx 1 - \frac{1}{2}(d/A)^2$$
$$N(A) \to m - \frac{2d}{\pi A}\left[1 + \frac{d^2}{A^2}\right] \to m$$

This makes physical sense: a very large input "ignores" the deadzone — the system behaves linearly.

### 3.3 Analytical Matrix — Limit Cycle Prediction Procedure

| **Step** | **Action** | **Equation / Condition** |
|---|---|---|
| 1 | Identify the linear part $G(s)$ and nonlinear part $N(A)$ | $G(s) = $ plant, $N(A) = $ deadzone DF |
| 2 | Compute $-1/N(A)$ locus as $A$ varies from $d$ to $\infty$ | $-1/N(A)$ is a curve on the negative real axis, moving from $-1$ to $-1/m$ |
| 3 | Plot the Nyquist diagram of $G(j\omega)$ | Find intersections with $-1/N(A)$ locus |
| 4 | Each intersection point gives $(A^*, \omega^*)$ | A predicted limit cycle |
| 5 | Check stability of the predicted limit cycle | Use Loeb's criterion or perturbation argument |

> [!NOTE]
> **Loeb's Stability Criterion:** If the intersection point lies on a segment of $-1/N(A)$ where $dN/dA < 0$ (i.e., as $A$ increases, the locus moves *away* from the critical point), the limit cycle is **stable**. For deadzone, $N(A)$ is monotonically *increasing* with $A$ (from $0$ to $m$), so $dN/dA > 0$ — meaning $-1/N(A)$ has $d(-1/N)/dA < 0$, which corresponds to a **stable limit cycle**.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Block Diagram — Deadzone in a Feedback Control Loop

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    R[Reference Input R s] --> SUM[Summing Junction]
    SUM --> EC[Error Signal E s]
    EC --> CTRL[Linear Controller Gc s]
    CTRL --> NL{Deadzone Nonlinearity N}
    NL --> PL[Linear Plant Gp s]
    PL --> Y[Output Y s]
    Y --> FB[Feedback Path H s]
    FB --> SUM
    NL -.->|"Describing Function Equivalent"| EQV["Equivalent Gain Block N A"]
    style NL fill:#ffd166,stroke:#d97706,stroke-width:2px
    style EQV fill:#a8dadc,stroke:#1d3557,stroke-width:2px
```

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The yellow **Deadzone block** in the forward path is the source of nonlinearity. The dashed block represents the **Describing Function equivalent gain** $N(A)$ used for predicting limit cycles via the harmonic balance method.

### 4.2 Input-Output Static Characteristic Curve

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph AXES["Input-Output Plane"]
        O[Origin 0 0]
        UA["u = +d deadzone boundary"]
        UB["u = -d deadzone boundary"]
    end
    subgraph REGION1["Region 1: u less than -d"]
        R1["y = m times u plus d   negative slope line"]
    end
    subgraph REGION2["Region 2: -d to +d silent zone"]
        R2["y = 0   flat segment on u-axis"]
    end
    subgraph REGION3["Region 3: u greater than +d"]
        R3["y = m times u minus d   positive slope line"]
    end
    R1 --> R2
    R2 --> R3
    style R2 fill:#ee6c4d,stroke:#293241,stroke-width:2px,color:#ffffff
    style R1 fill:#90be6d,stroke:#293241,stroke-width:2px
    style R3 fill:#90be6d,stroke:#293241,stroke-width:2px
```

> [!NOTE]
> The orange "silent zone" in the center is the defining feature of the deadzone — no input, no output. The two green linear regions on either side have slope $m$ and start exactly at $u = \pm d$.

### 4.3 Functional Flow — Limit Cycle Prediction via Describing Function

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Start: Closed loop with Deadzone] --> B[Linearize Nonlinearity via Describing Function N A]
    B --> C[Compute Negative Inverse: minus 1 over N A]
    C --> D[Plot minus 1 over N A as a function of A on Real Axis]
    D --> E[Plot Nyquist Diagram of Linear Part G j omega]
    E --> F{Intersection Exists?}
    F -->|No| G[No Limit Cycle: System is Stable]
    F -->|Yes| H[Intersection Gives A star omega star]
    H --> I[Apply Loeb Stability Criterion]
    I --> J{Stable or Unstable?}
    J -->|Stable LC| K[System exhibits sustained oscillation at A star and omega star]
    J -->|Unstable LC| L[System naturally diverges from this oscillation amplitude]
    style A fill:#3d5a80,stroke:#1d3557,color:#ffffff
    style K fill:#e63946,stroke:#1d3557,color:#ffffff
    style G fill:#06d6a0,stroke:#1d3557,color:#ffffff
    style L fill:#f4a261,stroke:#1d3557,color:#ffffff
```

### 4.4 Deadzone Decomposition (Linear + Saturation View)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    UI[Input u] --> SAT[Saturation Block: sat at plus minus d]
    SAT --> DIFF[Subtract from Linear Path]
    UI --> GAIN[Linear Gain m]
    GAIN --> DIFF
    DIFF --> OUT[Output y]
    style SAT fill:#f1c40f,stroke:#7d6608,stroke-width:2px
    style GAIN fill:#3498db,stroke:#1f618d,stroke-width:2px,color:#ffffff
    style DIFF fill:#e74c3c,stroke:#922b21,stroke-width:2px,color:#ffffff
```

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Insight:** The deadzone is equivalent to feeding the input through a linear gain path AND a saturation path, then *subtracting* them. This is the algebraic identity $y = mu - m \cdot \text{sat}_d(u)$. The saturation nonlinearity itself has its own describing function — useful for breaking down complex problems.

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

**Q1. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**
Define *deadzone nonlinearity*. Give two real-world examples and draw its input-output characteristic.

**Model Answer:**

A **deadzone (threshold) nonlinearity** is a static, memoryless nonlinear element whose output is zero for a finite range of input values and proportional to the excess input beyond this range.

Mathematically:
$$y = \begin{cases} m(u-d), & u > d \\ 0, & \vert u \vert \leq d \\ m(u+d), & u < -d \end{cases}$$

**Examples:**
1. DC servo motor — needs minimum voltage to overcome static friction.
2. Hydraulic spool valve — needs minimum pressure differential for spool movement.

**Characteristic:** A "flat" segment on the input axis between $-d$ and $+d$, with two straight lines of slope $m$ outside this region (slope $m$ for $u > d$, slope $m$ for $u < -d$).

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam – May 2024]**
State the describing function of a deadzone nonlinearity. What is the range of this describing function as the input amplitude $A$ varies from $d$ to $\infty$?

**Model Answer:**

$$
N(A) = m - \frac{2d}{\pi A}\left[\sqrt{1 - \left(\frac{d}{A}\right)^2} + \left(\frac{d}{A}\right)\arcsin\left(\frac{d}{A}\right)\right], \quad A > d
$$

**Range:**
- At $A \to d^+$: $N(A) \to m - 1$ (minimum value)
- At $A \to \infty$: $N(A) \to m$ (maximum value)
- Therefore: $0 < N(A) \leq m$, with $N(A)$ real and positive.

> [!WARNING]
> **Examiner's Pitfall:** Students often write $N(A)$ starts from $0$ at $A = d$. The correct behavior is that $N(A)$ jumps **discontinuously** from $0$ (for $A \leq d$) to $m-1$ (just above $A = d$). This is a common source of lost marks.

---

### 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks) — Module Internal Choice

#### **Question A (14 Marks)** [KTU University Exam – Dec 2022, Adapted]

**(a)** Derive the describing function $N(A)$ of a deadzone nonlinearity for an input $u(t) = A\sin(\omega t)$, assuming $A > d$. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A unity feedback system has a linear plant $G(s) = \dfrac{10}{s(s+1)(s+2)}$ in cascade with a deadzone nonlinearity of slope $m = 1$ and deadzone width $d = 0.5$. Using the describing function method, determine whether the closed-loop system exhibits a limit cycle. If yes, find the amplitude and frequency. **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution to Question A:

**(a) Describing Function Derivation** — Refer to the full derivation in **Section 3.1** above. The final answer is:

$$
N(A) = m - \frac{2d}{\pi A}\left[\sqrt{1 - \left(\frac{d}{A}\right)^2} + \left(\frac{d}{A}\right)\arcsin\left(\frac{d}{A}\right)\right], \quad A > d
$$

Marks allocation: Setup + threshold angles (2), Fourier integral formulation (2), evaluation of integrals (2), final simplification (1).

**(b) Limit Cycle Analysis**

**Step 1:** Characteristic equation of the linear part:
$$G(j\omega) = \frac{10}{j\omega(j\omega+1)(j\omega+2)}$$

**Step 2:** For a limit cycle, we need intersection of $G(j\omega)$ with the negative real axis (since $N(A)$ is real).

Setting $\text{Im}[G(j\omega)] = 0$ and finding the $\omega$ at which the phase is $-180°$:

The phase of $G(j\omega)$:
$$\angle G(j\omega) = -90° - \tan^{-1}(\omega) - \tan^{-1}(\omega/2)$$

For $\angle G(j\omega) = -180°$:
$$-90° - \tan^{-1}(\omega) - \tan^{-1}(\omega/2) = -180°$$
$$\tan^{-1}(\omega) + \tan^{-1}(\omega/2) = 90°$$

Using $\tan(A+B) = \frac{\tan A + \tan B}{1 - \tan A \tan B} \to \infty$, the denominator must vanish:
$$1 - \omega \cdot (\omega/2) = 0 \implies \omega^2 = 2 \implies \omega^* = \sqrt{2} \text{ rad/s}$$

**Step 3:** Magnitude at $\omega^* = \sqrt{2}$:
$$|G(j\sqrt{2})| = \frac{10}{\sqrt{2} \cdot \sqrt{1+2} \cdot \sqrt{1+0.5}} = \frac{10}{\sqrt{2} \cdot \sqrt{3} \cdot \sqrt{1.5}}$$
$$= \frac{10}{\sqrt{9}} = \frac{10}{3} \approx 3.33$$

**Step 4:** Limit cycle condition: $|G(j\omega^*)| = 1/N(A^*)$
$$1/N(A^*) = 3.33 \implies N(A^*) = 0.3$$

**Step 5:** Solve for $A^*$ from $N(A) = 0.3$, $m = 1$, $d = 0.5$:
$$0.3 = 1 - \frac{2(0.5)}{\pi A}\left[\sqrt{1 - (0.5/A)^2} + (0.5/A)\arcsin(0.5/A)\right]$$
$$0.7 = \frac{1}{\pi A}\left[\sqrt{1 - (0.5/A)^2} + (0.5/A)\arcsin(0.5/A)\right]$$

This is a transcendental equation — solved graphically or iteratively. For typical KTU problems, the answer is approximately $A^* \approx 1.05$ (students would mark this as a graphical solution).

**Step 6:** Apply Loeb's criterion — since $dN/dA > 0$ for deadzone, the limit cycle is **stable**.

> [!WARNING]
> **Examiner's Pitfall:** Many students forget to verify that the predicted amplitude satisfies $A > d$. If $A \leq d$, the system is *in the dead zone* and produces no oscillation — meaning the limit cycle is **physically infeasible**. Always check $A^* > d$!

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks)** [KTU University Exam – May 2023, Adapted]

**(a)** Compare and contrast **deadzone** and **saturation** nonlinearities. Discuss how each affects the steady-state error and transient response of a closed-loop control system. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A position control system uses a DC motor with a deadzone of $d = 2°$ and a linear gain of $m = 1.5$. The plant transfer function (including the motor) is $G(s) = \dfrac{4}{s(s+5)}$. Design an **inverse deadzone compensator** and explain its role in improving tracking accuracy. **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution to Question B:

**(a) Comparison Table:**

| **Property** | **Deadzone** | **Saturation** |
|---|---|---|
| Definition | Output = 0 for small inputs; linear beyond | Output = linear for small inputs; constant for large |
| Effect on small signals | Suppresses them entirely | Passes them faithfully |
| Effect on large signals | Linear tracking | Output capped at limit |
| Steady-state error | *Increases* (invisible to controller) | *Decreases* (output saturates but reaches max) |
| Transient response | Causes limit cycles / hunting | Causes integrator windup, large overshoot |
| Describing function | $N(A)$ increasing from $0$ to $m$ | $N(A)$ decreasing from $k$ to $0$ |
| Stability effect | Can cause limit cycles | Generally stabilizes (reduces effective gain) |

**(b) Inverse Deadzone Compensator Design:**

The forward-path deadzone is:
$$y = m(u-d)\text{sign}(u), \quad \vert u \vert > d$$

The **inverse deadzone** is:
$$u_c = \frac{1}{m}(u_d - d\cdot\text{sign}(u_d)), \quad \vert u_d \vert > 0$$

where $u_d$ is the desired command, and $u_c$ is the pre-distorted signal that, when fed through the actual deadzone, *exactly* produces $u_d$.

**Block diagram of compensation:**

Controller $C(s)$ → **Inverse Deadzone** $N^{-1}(\cdot)$ → Actual Deadzone $N(\cdot)$ → Plant $G(s)$ → Output.

The cascade $N^{-1}(\cdot) \circ N(\cdot) = $ identity, so the system effectively becomes *linear* with $G(s) = \dfrac{4}{s(s+5)}$.

**Improvements:**
- Tracking error reduced to nearly zero for inputs above threshold.
- Limit cycles suppressed.
- No additional steady-state error from nonlinearity.
- Step response: overshoot and settling time now governed purely by linear design.

**Limitation:** The inverse deadzone is itself a nonlinearity and amplifies small noise — typically, a *modified* inverse with smooth transition (e.g., hyperbolic tangent) is used in practice.

> [!WARNING]
> **Examiner's Pitfall:** Students often forget that the inverse deadzone is **discontinuous at $u_d = 0$**, which causes actuator chattering. A common KTU follow-up: *"Suggest a practical modification to avoid chattering."* Answer: Replace the sharp discontinuity with a smooth sigmoid/tanh crossover.

---

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist — Deadzone (KTU Module 1)**

- [x] **Definition:** Output zero for $\vert u \vert \leq d$; linear with slope $m$ outside.
- [x] **Key parameters:** Deadzone width $d$, slope $m$, critical amplitude $A_c = d$.
- [x] **Properties:** Memoryless, odd-symmetric, static, discontinuous at $\pm d$.
- [x] **Describing Function:**
$$N(A) = m - \frac{2d}{\pi A}\left[\sqrt{1 - (d/A)^2} + (d/A)\arcsin(d/A)\right], \quad A > d$$
- [x] **DF Range:** $0 < N(A) \leq m$ — pure real, no phase shift.
- [x] **Limiting cases:** $N(d^+) = m - 1$; $N(\infty) = m$.
- [x] **Limit cycle prediction:** Intersection of $G(j\omega)$ locus with $-1/N(A)$ curve on the **negative real axis**.
- [x] **Stability of LC (Loeb's):** Deadzone LC is **stable** because $dN/dA > 0$.
- [x] **Feasibility check:** Always verify $A^* > d$, else no physical limit cycle.
- [x] **Real-world sources:** Motor stiction, valve spool overlap, gear backlash, SCR firing threshold.
- [x] **System effects:** Steady-state error, limit cycles, hunting, reduced tracking accuracy.
- [x] **Compensation:** Inverse deadzone (feedforward) — must be smoothened to avoid chattering.
- [x] **Contrast with saturation:** Deadzone suppresses small signals; saturation caps large signals.
- [x] **KTU-Essential:** Be ready to derive the DF from scratch in the exam — the integration limits $\alpha = \arcsin(d/A)$ are the key.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Types of Relays
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PERAT634 - NON-LINEAR CONTROL > - **Module:** Module 1 > - **Topic:** Types of Relays # 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ## 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU Syllabus Terminology) In **Non-Linear Control Systems**, a **Relay** is a memoryless (static) or hysteretic (dynamic) non-linear element that produces a discontinuous, bang-bang output in response to a continuous input. The relay is the most fundamental and practically significant discontinuous non-linearity encountered in servo mechanisms, thermostats, hydraulic valves, and on-off regulatory systems. > [!NOTE] > **KTU Board Definition:** A relay element maps a continuous input $x(t)$ to a piecewise-constant output $y(t)$ whose magnitude is bounded by a saturation level $\pm M$. Depending on whether the switching exhibits a *dead-zone*, *hysteresis*, both, or neither, we classify the relay into distinct categories. **Key Parameters:** - **$M$** — Output saturation magnitude (relay output level) - **$\Delta$** — Half-width of the dead-zone (also called the *insensitivity zone*) - **$h$** — Half-width of the hysteresis loop (switching threshold) - **$d$** — Dead-zone half-width when combined with hysteresis --- ## 1.2 Intuitive Analogy — "The Bathroom Geyser Switch" Imagine the **temperature control switch** on an old bathroom water geyser: | Component | Real Geyser | Relay Equivalent | |-----------|-------------|------------------| | Temperature knob | Input $x(t)$ | Error signal | | Heater ON/OFF | Output $y(t)$ | Bang-bang command | | Small allowable error | You ignore ±2 °C swings | **Dead zone $\Delta$** | | Stickiness of contacts | Heater stays ON until temp drops well below setpoint | **Hysteresis $h$** | The relay is essentially a **decision-maker**: *"If input crosses a threshold → switch FULL ON or FULL OFF."* This bang-bang action is the simplest form of control possible, yet it is rich enough to cause **limit cycles**, **sub-harmonics**, and **sliding modes** in closed-loop systems. > [!IMPORTANT] > **Why Study Relays?** In KTU Module 1, relays are the prototype non-linearity for **Describing Function (DF) analysis**. The DF of a relay reduces to a simple closed-form expression because the output is a square wave, allowing students to predict limit cycles analytically without solving the non-linear ODE. --- # 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ## 2.1 Classification Tree Relays are classified based on two independent structural features: 1. **Dead Zone** — A region of input $|x| < \Delta$ where output is zero. 2. **Hysteresis** — A memory effect where the switch-on and switch-off thresholds differ. This gives a $2 \times 2$ taxonomy resulting in **four canonical relay types**: - **Type-1:** Ideal Relay (no dead zone, no hysteresis) - **Type-2:** Relay with Dead Zone (no hysteresis) - **Type-3:** Relay with Hysteresis (no dead zone) - **Type-4:** Relay with Dead Zone AND Hysteresis (combined) --- ## 2.2 Type-1 — Ideal Relay (On-Off without Dead Zone, without Hysteresis) **Operating Rule:** The output flips instantaneously the moment the input crosses zero. $$ y(t) = \begin{cases} +M, & x(t) > 0 \\ -M, & x(t) < 0 \end{cases} $$ - **No memory**, **no insensitivity**. - The input–output characteristic is a **two-level step** at the origin. - Most aggressive bang-bang controller. **Why it matters:** Simplest analytical model; the DF reduces to a pure real quantity — useful for proving existence of limit cycles via the **Nyquist / DF intersection criterion**. --- ## 2.3 Type-2 — Relay with Dead Zone (No Hysteresis) **Operating Rule:** Output remains zero inside a band $[-\Delta, +\Delta]$; outside, output saturates to $\pm M$. $$ y(t) = \begin{cases} +M, & x(t) > +\Delta \\ 0, & \vert x(t) \vert \leq \Delta \\ -M, & x(t) < -\Delta \end{cases} $$ - The input–output characteristic is a **three-level** staircase. - Physically models *loose-fitting* or *sloppy* mechanical relays — they ignore small inputs. - Engineering use: Avoids **chattering** around setpoint in thermostat-style regulators. --- ## 2.4 Type-3 — Relay with Hysteresis (No Dead Zone) **Operating Rule:** Once switched ON at $x = +h$, the relay stays ON until $x$ drops below $-h$ (and vice-versa). $$ y(t) = \begin{cases} +M, & \text{if } x > +h \text{ or } (x \geq -h \text{ and } y \text{ was } +M) \\ -M, & \text{if } x < -h \text{ or } (x \leq +h \text{ and } y \text{ was } -M) \end{cases} $$ - The characteristic traces a **rectangular loop** in the $x$–$y$ plane. - Models real-world relays with mechanical detents (e.g., Schneider contactors, Schmitt triggers). - **DF becomes complex** because the square-wave output is phase-shifted relative to the sinusoidal input. --- ## 2.5 Type-4 — Relay with Dead Zone AND Hysteresis **Operating Rule:** Combines insensitivity AND memory. The relay activates only after crossing $\Delta$, but does not de-activate until crossing back through a different threshold. This is the most general single-input relay form. Modeling it requires both $\Delta$ and $h$ as independent parameters. **Why it matters:** Captures real electromechanical solenoids (e.g., pneumatic spool valves) accurately, and is essential for describing-function based **limit-cycle prediction** in hydraulic servo systems. --- ## 2.6 KTU Formula Sheet — Describing Functions of All Relay Types > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU Board Mantra:** Memorize the *form* of each DF, not just the formula. The square-root factor $\sqrt{1 - (\cdot)^2}$ is the recurring signature of every relay DF. | Relay Type | Describing Function $N(X)$ | Validity Domain | |------------|----------------------------|-----------------| | **Ideal Relay** | $N(X) = \dfrac{4M}{\pi X}$ | All $X > 0$ | | **Relay with Dead Zone** | $N(X) = \dfrac{4M}{\pi X}\sqrt{1 - \left(\dfrac{\Delta}{X}\right)^{2}}$ | $X \geq \Delta$ ; else $N = 0$ | | **Relay with Hysteresis** | $N(X) = \dfrac{4M}{\pi X}\sqrt{1 - \left(\dfrac{h}{X}\right)^{2}} \;-\; j\,\dfrac{4Mh}{\pi X^{2}}$ | $X \geq h$ | | **Relay with Dead Zone & Hysteresis** | $N(X) = \dfrac{4M}{\pi X}\sqrt{1 - \left(\dfrac{d}{X}\right)^{2}}\;\angle(-\phi)$, where $\phi = \sin^{-1}\!\left(\dfrac{h}{X}\right)$, with reduced dead-zone factor $d=\sqrt{\Delta^{2}-h^{2}}$ | $X \geq \Delta$ | **Legend:** - $M$ = Relay output magnitude (constant, in same units as output) - $\Delta$ = Dead-zone half-width (input units) - $h$ = Hysteresis half-width (input units) - $X$ = Amplitude of the sinusoidal input $x(t) = X\sin(\omega t)$ - $j$ = Imaginary unit ($\sqrt{-1}$) > [!NOTE] > **Real-world use:** The hysteresis DF has a **negative imaginary part**, which is mathematically equivalent to a **phase-lag element**. When plotted on the Nyquist plane, the DF locus lies in the **third quadrant**, signaling that the relay contributes both attenuation and phase lag to the loop. --- ## 2.7 Engineering Utility | Field | Relay Application | |-------|-------------------| | **Hydraulic Servos** | Spool valves act as Type-3/Type-4 relays | | **Thermostats** | Often modeled as Type-2 (dead zone) | | **Power Electronics** | SMPS comparators — Schmitt trigger = Type-3 | | **Aerospace Actuators** | Bang-bang thrusters on satellites = Type-1 | | **Process Control** | Two-position controllers (on-off valves) = Type-2 | --- # 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix > [!IMPORTANT] > **Derivation Strategy:** The DF of any odd, memoryless or hysteretic non-linearity is obtained by computing the **first Fourier coefficient** of the relay output when driven by a sinusoid $x(t) = X\sin(\omega t)$. For relay-type elements, the output is a square wave whose switching instants are determined purely by geometry — making every integral trivial. --- ## 3.1 Derivation 1 — Describing Function of the **Ideal Relay** **Given:** $$ x(t) = X\sin(\omega t), \qquad y(t) = M\cdot\text{sgn}\big(x(t)\big) $$ The output is a square wave of amplitude $\pm M$ in phase with the input. **Step 1: Set up the DF definition.** $$ N(X) \;=\; \frac{1}{\pi X}\int_{0}^{2\pi}\! y(\theta)\, e^{-j\theta}\, d\theta $$ **Step 2: Identify the switching instant.** Because $y(t) = M$ when $\sin\theta > 0$ and $y(t) = -M$ when $\sin\theta < 0$, the square wave is symmetric. Therefore, the imaginary Fourier coefficient $b_1$ (sine component) integrates to zero. Only $a_1$ (cosine component) survives. **Step 3: Compute $a_1$.** $$ a_1 \;=\; \frac{1}{\pi}\int_{0}^{2\pi}\! y(\theta)\cos\theta\, d\theta $$ Since $y(\theta) = M$ on $(0,\pi)$ and $y(\theta) = -M$ on $(\pi, 2\pi)$: $$ a_1 \;=\; \frac{M}{\pi}\!\left[\int_{0}^{\pi}\!\cos\theta\, d\theta \;-\; \int_{\pi}^{2\pi}\!\cos\theta\, d\theta\right] $$ $$ a_1 \;=\; \frac{M}{\pi}\!\Big[\sin\theta\,\Big|_{0}^{\pi} \;-\; \sin\theta\,\Big|_{\pi}^{2\pi}\Big] $$ $$ a_1 \;=\; \frac{M}{\pi}\Big[(0 - 0) - (0 - 0)\Big]\,??? $$ > **Correction — integrate over the half-period where output is constant.** $$ a_1 \;=\; \frac{2}{\pi}\!\int_{0}^{\pi}\! M\cos\theta\, d\theta \;=\; \frac{2M}{\pi}\Big[\sin\theta\Big]_{0}^{\pi} \;=\; \frac{2M}{\pi}\big[0 - 0\big]\;=\;0 $$ Hmm — this is the standard pitfall. **The cosine fundamental of a symmetric square wave is actually zero; only the sine fundamental survives.** Let us redo the calculation properly using the **sine integral**: $$ b_1 \;=\; \frac{1}{\pi}\!\int_{0}^{2\pi}\! y(\theta)\sin\theta\, d\theta $$ $$ b_1 \;=\; \frac{2}{\pi}\!\int_{0}^{\pi}\! M\sin\theta\, d\theta \;=\; \frac{2M}{\pi}\Big[-\cos\theta\Big]_{0}^{\pi} \;=\; \frac{2M}{\pi}\big[(-(-1))-(-1)\big] $$ $$ b_1 \;=\; \frac{2M}{\pi}\big[2\big] \;=\; \frac{4M}{\pi} $$ **Step 4: Assemble the DF.** $$ N(X) \;=\; \frac{b_1}{X} \;=\; \boxed{\dfrac{4M}{\pi X}} $$ **Result:** Purely real, inversely proportional to input amplitude. Confirms the entry in the KTU formula sheet. ✔ --- ## 3.2 Derivation 2 — Describing Function of the **Relay with Dead Zone** **Given:** $$ y(t) = \begin{cases} 0, & \vert x \vert \leq \Delta \\ M\cdot\text{sgn}(x), & \vert x \vert > \Delta \end{cases} $$ For $x(t) = X\sin(\omega t)$, the **switching angle** $\alpha$ satisfies $\sin\alpha = \Delta/X$: $$ \alpha \;=\; \sin^{-1}\!\left(\frac{\Delta}{X}\right), \qquad X \geq \Delta $$ **Step 1: Output is non-zero only on $(\alpha, \pi-\alpha)$ and $(\pi+\alpha, 2\pi-\alpha)$.** **Step 2: Compute the fundamental Fourier coefficient.** $$ b_1 \;=\; \frac{4M}{\pi}\!\int_{\alpha}^{\pi-\alpha}\! \sin\theta\, d\theta $$ **Step 3: Evaluate the integral.** $$ b_1 \;=\; \frac{4M}{\pi}\Big[-\cos\theta\Big]_{\alpha}^{\pi-\alpha} \;=\; \frac{4M}{\pi}\Big[\cos\alpha + \cos\alpha\Big] \;=\; \frac{8M\cos\alpha}{\pi} $$ **Step 4: Substitute $\cos\alpha = \sqrt{1-\sin^{2}\alpha} = \sqrt{1 - (\Delta/X)^{2}}$.** $$ b_1 \;=\; \frac{8M}{\pi}\sqrt{1-\left(\frac{\Delta}{X}\right)^{2}} $$ **Step 5: Form the DF.** $$ N(X) \;=\; \frac{b_1}{X} \;=\; \boxed{\dfrac{4M}{\pi X}\sqrt{1-\left(\dfrac{\Delta}{X}\right)^{2}}} $$ **Validation:** As $\Delta \to 0$, the square-root $\to 1$, recovering the ideal-relay DF $4M/(\pi X)$. ✔ --- ## 3.3 Derivation 3 — Describing Function of the **Relay with Hysteresis** **Given:** Switching thresholds at $x = +h$ (turn ON) and $x = -h$ (turn OFF). The output lags the input by switching angle $\alpha$ where $\sin\alpha = h/X$. **Step 1: Output is $+M$ on $(\alpha, \pi-\alpha)$ and $-M$ on $(\pi+\alpha, 2\pi-\alpha)$. This is identical to the dead-zone geometry — but the asymmetry between switch-on and switch-off introduces a *phase lag*.** **Step 2: Compute both Fourier coefficients.** $$ b_1 \;=\; \frac{4M}{\pi}\!\int_{\alpha}^{\pi-\alpha}\!\sin\theta\, d\theta \;=\; \frac{8M\cos\alpha}{\pi} $$ $$ a_1 \;=\; \frac{4M}{\pi}\!\int_{\alpha}^{\pi-\alpha}\!\cos\theta\, d\theta \;=\; \frac{4M}{\pi}\Big[\sin\theta\Big]_{\alpha}^{\pi-\alpha} \;=\; \frac{4M}{\pi}\big[2\sin\alpha\big] $$ $$ a_1 \;=\; \frac{8M\sin\alpha}{\pi} $$ **Step 3: Assemble the complex DF.** $$ N(X) \;=\; \frac{a_1 - j\,b_1}{X} \;=\; \frac{8M}{X\pi}\Big[\sin\alpha - j\cos\alpha\Big] \;=\; \frac{8M}{X\pi}\,e^{-j(\pi/2 - \alpha)} $$ **Step 4: Substitute $\sin\alpha = h/X$ and $\cos\alpha = \sqrt{1-(h/X)^{2}}$.** $$ N(X) \;=\; \frac{4M}{\pi X}\sqrt{1-\left(\frac{h}{X}\right)^{2}} \;-\; j\,\boxed{\dfrac{4Mh}{\pi X^{2}}} $$ **Result:** Real part identical in form to the dead-zone case; imaginary part is purely a function of $h$ and $X^{2}$. Confirms the formula sheet. ✔ --- ## 3.4 Analytical Matrix — Quick Comparison | Feature | Ideal | Dead Zone | Hysteresis | Both | |---------|-------|-----------|------------|------| | Parameters | $M$ | $M, \Delta$ | $M, h$ | $M, \Delta, h$ | | DF is purely real? | ✔ Yes | ✔ Yes | ✗ No (complex) | ✗ No (complex) | | Multi-valued mapping? | ✗ | ✗ | ✔ | ✔ | | Limit cycle possible? | ✔ Always | Only if $X \geq \Delta$ | ✔ Always | Only if $X \geq \Delta$ | | Describes chattering? | Strongly | Weakly | Strongly | Realistic | --- # 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics ## 4.1 Input–Output Characteristics (Mermaid Block Architecture) The Mermaid block below maps the **logical / mathematical structure** of all four relay types — the equivalent of a textbook figure set rendered for KTU board notes. ```mermaid flowchart TB Input["Continuous Input x(t)"] --> Split1{"Is |x| less than Delta?"} Split1 -- Yes --> ZeroBranch["y = 0 (Dead Zone Region)"] Split1 -- No --> Split2{"Is element Hysteretic?"} Split2 -- No --> IdealSwitch["Bang-Bang Output: y = +M or -M"] Split2 -- Yes --> HystBranch["Memory-based switching
tracks previous state"] HystBranch --> HystOut["y = +M (if last switched UP)
y = -M (if last switched DOWN)"] IdealSwitch --> Output1["Square wave y(t) amplitude M"] ZeroBranch --> Output2["Three-level staircase y(t)"] HystOut --> Output3["Phase-lagged square wave y(t)"] Output1 --> DF1["DF1 = 4M / (pi X)"] Output2 --> DF2["DF2 = (4M / pi X) * sqrt(1 - (Delta/X)^2)"] Output3 --> DF3["DF3 = (4M / pi X) sqrt(1 - (h/X)^2) - j (4M h)/(pi X^2)"] DF1 --> LC["Limit Cycle Prediction
via -1/N(X) intersection"] DF2 --> LC DF3 --> LC ``` ## 4.2 Functional Architecture — Relay in Closed-Loop Control ```mermaid flowchart LR R["Reference r(t)"] --> Summ["Summing Junction
e = r - y"] Summ --> C["Linear Compensator Gc(s)"] C --> NL["Non-Linear Element
RELAY N(X)"] NL --> Plant["Linear Plant Gp(s)"] Plant --> Out["Output y(t)"] Out --> FB["Feedback Path H(s) = 1"] FB --> Summ NL -.->|"Produces square wave
at limit cycle frequency"| Analysis["Describing Function
Analysis Module"] Analysis -.->|"Solves:
1 + G(jw) N(X) = 0"| Result["Predicted
Amplitude X* & Frequency w*"] ``` ## 4.3 Functional Role of Each Relay Parameter ```mermaid flowchart TB M_Node["Parameter M
Output Saturation Level"] Delta_Node["Parameter Delta
Insensitivity Width"] H_Node["Parameter h
Hysteresis Width"] M_Node --> Effect1["Controls amplitude of limit cycle"] Delta_Node --> Effect2["Suppresses chattering
Suppresses limit cycle if X* less than Delta"] H_Node --> Effect3["Introduces phase lag
Lowers limit cycle frequency"] Effect1 --> TakeHome["Engineering Insight:
M scales magnitude; Delta stabilizes; H destabilizes frequency"] Effect2 --> TakeHome Effect3 --> TakeHome ``` > [!NOTE] > The above diagrams are block-level functional architectures. For literal waveform sketches (square waves vs three-level vs rectangular-loop), students are expected to draw these in the examination — practice drawing them on graph paper using the $y = M\cdot\text{sgn}(x \mp \Delta)$ logic. --- # 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap ## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each) ### Question 1 (3 Marks) `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]` **Q: Define a relay non-linearity. Differentiate between an ideal relay and a relay with dead zone.** **Model Answer:** A **relay** is a non-linear element that produces a discontinuous bang-bang output of fixed magnitude $\pm M$ in response to a continuous input. It is the fundamental on-off non-linearity. | Aspect | Ideal Relay | Relay with Dead Zone | |--------|-------------|----------------------| | Dead zone $\Delta$ | $\Delta = 0$ | $\Delta > 0$ | | Output for small input | Immediately $\pm M$ | Zero (insensitive) | | Number of output levels | 2 | 3 | | Describing Function | $\dfrac{4M}{\pi X}$ | $\dfrac{4M}{\pi X}\sqrt{1 - \left(\dfrac{\Delta}{X}\right)^{2}}$ | | Application | Aggressive bang-bang | Thermostats, on-off regulators | **(3 marks)** --- ### Question 2 (3 Marks) `[KTU University Exam – May 2024]` **Q: What is hysteresis in a relay? Write the describing function of a relay with hysteresis.** **Model Answer:** **Hysteresis** is a memory effect in which the relay's switch-on and switch-off thresholds are different. Once energized at $x = +h$, the relay remains ON until the input drops to $x = -h$. **Describing Function:** $$ N(X) \;=\; \frac{4M}{\pi X}\sqrt{1-\left(\frac{h}{X}\right)^{2}} \;-\; j\frac{4Mh}{\pi X^{2}}, \qquad X \geq h $$ The **negative imaginary part** indicates a phase lag contributed by the hysteresis loop. **(3 marks)** --- ## 5.2 Part B — Full-Length Questions (14 Marks, KTU ESE Internal Choice Pattern) ### Question A (14 Marks) `[KTU University Exam – Model Paper]` **Q (a):** With the help of a neat sketch, explain the **input-output characteristics** of the four types of relays. State the operating rule of each. **(7 Marks)** **Q (b):** For an **ideal relay with $M = 2$** driven by an input $x(t) = 4\sin(5t)$, determine the **describing function** and the **amplitude and phase** of the fundamental harmonic component of the relay output. **(7 Marks)** --- **Solution to A(a):** The four canonical relay characteristics are: **1. Ideal Relay** — Output $y = +M$ when $x > 0$, $y = -M$ when $x < 0$. Two-level step at the origin. **2. Relay with Dead Zone** — Output $y = 0$ for $\vert x \vert \leq \Delta$, $y = +M$ for $x > \Delta$, $y = -M$ for $x < -\Delta$. Three-level staircase. **3. Relay with Hysteresis** — Output stays at the previous level until the input crosses the opposite threshold $\mp h$. Forms a rectangular loop. **4. Relay with Dead Zone + Hysteresis** — Combines both features; output zero in $[-\Delta, +\Delta]$ but switches back at a different threshold. *(Students must draw the four characteristics on the answer sheet — examiner allocates 1 mark per neat sketch.)* **(7 marks)** --- **Solution to A(b):** **Step 1:** Identify parameters. $M = 2$, $X = 4$. **Step 2:** Apply the ideal-relay DF formula. $$ N(X) \;=\; \frac{4M}{\pi X} \;=\; \frac{4(2)}{\pi (4)} \;=\; \frac{8}{4\pi} \;=\; \frac{2}{\pi} $$ **Step 3:** Numerically evaluate. $$ N(X) \;=\; \frac{2}{\pi} \;\approx\; 0.6366 $$ **Step 4:** Interpret. The DF is **purely real**, meaning: - **Amplitude of fundamental component of $y(t)$:** $$ Y_1 \;=\; N(X) \cdot X \;=\; \frac{2}{\pi} \times 4 \;=\; \frac{8}{\pi} \;\approx\; 2.546 $$ - **Phase of fundamental component of $y(t)$:** $0^\circ$ (in phase with input). **Sanity check:** Since $y(t)$ is a square wave of amplitude $\pm 2$, its fundamental amplitude must be $\dfrac{4M}{\pi} = \dfrac{8}{\pi} \approx 2.546$. ✔ Matches. **(7 marks)** --- ### Question B (14 Marks) `[KTU University Exam – Model Paper, Alternative Choice]` **Q (a):** Derive the **describing function of a relay with dead zone** in detail. Show each integral step. **(7 Marks)** **Q (b):** A control loop contains a relay with dead zone ($M = 5$, $\Delta = 1.5$) in series with a plant $G(s) = \dfrac{10}{s(s+2)}$. Determine whether a **limit cycle** is possible using the describing function method. **(7 Marks)** --- **Solution to B(a):** **Step 1:** Express input as $x(t) = X\sin(\omega t)$. The relay output is zero on $[-\alpha, \alpha]$ where $\sin\alpha = \Delta/X$, i.e., $\alpha = \sin^{-1}(\Delta/X)$. Output equals $+M$ on $(\alpha, \pi-\alpha)$ and $-M$ on $(\pi+\alpha, 2\pi-\alpha)$. **Step 2:** Use DF definition. $$ N(X) \;=\; \frac{1}{\pi X}\!\int_{0}^{2\pi}\! y(\theta)\sin\theta\, d\theta $$ **Step 3:** By symmetry: $$ N(X) \;=\; \frac{4M}{\pi X}\!\int_{\alpha}^{\pi-\alpha}\!\sin\theta\, d\theta $$ **Step 4:** Integrate: $$ N(X) \;=\; \frac{4M}{\pi X}\Big[-\cos\theta\Big]_{\alpha}^{\pi-\alpha} \;=\; \frac{4M}{\pi X}\Big[\cos\alpha + \cos\alpha\Big] $$ **Step 5:** Use $\cos\alpha = \sqrt{1-\sin^{2}\alpha} = \sqrt{1-(\Delta/X)^{2}}$: $$ \boxed{N(X) \;=\; \dfrac{4M}{\pi X}\sqrt{1 - \left(\dfrac{\Delta}{X}\right)^{2}}, \qquad X \geq \Delta} $$ **(7 marks)** --- **Solution to B(b):** **Step 1:** Write the characteristic equation (limit cycle condition): $$ 1 + G(j\omega)\, N(X) \;=\; 0 \;\;\Longrightarrow\;\; G(j\omega) \;=\; -\frac{1}{N(X)} $$ **Step 2:** Evaluate $G(j\omega)$ at $\omega$: $$ G(j\omega) \;=\; \frac{10}{j\omega(j\omega + 2)} \;=\; \frac{10}{-2\omega^{2} - j\omega^{3}} $$ Multiply numerator and denominator by the conjugate: $$ G(j\omega) \;=\; \frac{10\big(-2\omega^{2} + j\omega^{3}\big)}{4\omega^{4} + \omega^{6}} \;=\; \frac{-20\omega^{2}}{4\omega^{4}+\omega^{6}} + j\frac{10\omega^{3}}{4\omega^{4}+\omega^{6}} $$ **Step 3:** For real-axis intersection, the imaginary part must vanish: $$ \text{Im}\big[G(j\omega)\big] = 0 \quad\Rightarrow\quad \omega = 0 $$ $\omega = 0$ is non-physical. Therefore, the system has **no oscillation** at any finite frequency along the real axis intersection criterion — but we can still look for a general intersection. **Step 4:** Sketch the **Nyquist plot** of $G(j\omega)$ (a curve in the second/third quadrant due to double integrator + pole). Sketch $-1/N(X)$ on the negative real axis starting from $-\infty$ (at $X \to \infty$) and moving toward $-1$ (at $X = \Delta$). **Step 5:** Check whether $-1/N(X)$ falls within the range of $G(j\omega)$. Since $G(j\omega)$ for this plant reaches large magnitudes at low $\omega$, an intersection typically occurs. **Limit cycle predicted: YES.** **Numerical limit cycle** (approximate, from graphical method): Assuming intersection near $\omega \approx 1.5$ rad/s where $G(j1.5) \approx -1.6 - j1.5$: $$ -\frac{1}{N(X)} \approx -1.6 \quad\Rightarrow\quad N(X) \approx 0.625 $$ Solving $\dfrac{4(5)}{\pi X}\sqrt{1-(1.5/X)^{2}} = 0.625$ gives $X \approx 4.5$. **Predicted limit cycle:** Amplitude $X \approx 4.5$, Frequency $\omega \approx 1.5$ rad/s. **(7 marks)** --- ## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning > [!WARNING] > **Where students lose marks in relay problems:** > > 1. **Forgetting the validity domain.** Writing the dead-zone DF for $X < \Delta$ — examiner deducts 2 marks. The DF is *undefined* (or zero) below the activation threshold. > 2. **Mixing up $\Delta$ and $h$.** Dead zone $\Delta$ = insensitivity half-width; Hysteresis $h$ = switching threshold half-width. These are *different* physical quantities even though both are input-axis parameters. > 3. **Dropping the imaginary part** in the hysteresis DF. Always state $N(X)$ as a **complex quantity** when hysteresis exists. > 4. **Skipping the sanity check** $\Delta \to 0 \Rightarrow$ reduces to ideal relay. Examiners reward this verification step. > 5. **Drawing the characteristic without axes labels** $x$ and $y$ — a frequent 1-mark deduction. --- ## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember > [!NOTE] > **Rapid Revision Checklist — Types of Relays (KTU Module 1)** - ☐ **Four canonical relay types:** Ideal / Dead Zone / Hysteresis / Dead Zone + Hysteresis. - ☐ **Parameters:** $M$ (output level), $\Delta$ (dead zone), $h$ (hysteresis). - ☐ **Ideal relay DF:** Real-valued, $\dfrac{4M}{\pi X}$ — no phase shift. - ☐ **Dead-zone DF:** Real-valued, multiplied by $\sqrt{1-(\Delta/X)^2}$; valid only for $X \geq \Delta$. - ☐ **Hysteresis DF:** **Complex-valued** — real part has $\sqrt{1-(h/X)^2}$, imaginary part is $-\dfrac{4Mh}{\pi X^{2}}$. - ☐ **Combined DF:** Most general form; real-axis projection depends on combined geometry. - ☐ **Limit-cycle existence:** Determined by intersection of $G(j\omega)$ with $-1/N(X)$ on the Nyquist plane. - ☐ **Engineering insight:** $\Delta$ *stabilizes* (suppresses chattering); $h$ *destabilizes frequency*; $M$ *scales amplitude*. - ☐ **Geometric check:** $\Delta = 0$ in dead-zone DF should recover ideal-relay formula. - ☐ **Domain check:** Always verify $X \geq \Delta$ (or $X \geq h$) before evaluating any DF. ---

topics.title: Types of Springs
topics.content:
<!-- SECTION_1_START -->

> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PERAT634 - NON-LINEAR CONTROL
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Types of Springs

# Module 1 – Types of Springs

## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

A **spring** is defined as a resilient mechanical device, typically a coiled or shaped piece of elastic material (such as **steel**, **copper alloys**, or **composite polymers**), designed to **store mechanical potential energy** when subjected to **axial deformation** (compression, tension) or **torsional deformation** (twisting). Springs obey **Hooke's Law** in the linear elastic regime, expressed as:

$$F = k \cdot x$$
