# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question A.1 `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023 Model]`

**Q: Define a singular point of a nonlinear system $\dot{\mathbf{x}} = \mathbf{f}(\mathbf{x})$. How is it related to the Jacobian matrix?**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

A singular point $\mathbf{x}_e$ of the nonlinear autonomous system $\dot{\mathbf{x}} = \mathbf{f}(\mathbf{x})$ is an equilibrium state where the velocity vector vanishes, i.e., $\mathbf{f}(\mathbf{x}_e) = \mathbf{0}$. The Jacobian matrix $J = \partial \mathbf{f}/\partial \mathbf{x}$ evaluated at $\mathbf{x}_e$ governs the **local linearized dynamics** $\dot{\boldsymbol{\eta}} = J(\mathbf{x}_e)\boldsymbol{\eta}$ in a small neighbourhood of the singular point. The eigenvalues of $J(\mathbf{x}_e)$ determine the **type and stability classification** of the singular point, making the Jacobian the central tool in local qualitative analysis.

---

### Question A.2 `[KTU University Exam – May 2024 Model]`

**Q: Distinguish between a stable focus and a centre. How does the trace of the Jacobian help differentiate them?**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

| Feature | Stable Focus | Centre |
|---|---|---|
| Eigenvalues | $\sigma \pm j\omega,\ \sigma < 0$ | $0 \pm j\omega$ (pure imaginary) |
| Trace $T$ | $T = 2\sigma < 0$ | $T = 0$ |
| Determinant $\Delta$ | $\Delta = \sigma^2 + \omega^2 > 0$ | $\Delta = \omega^2 > 0$ |
| Behaviour | Spiralling inward, asymptotically stable | Closed orbits, Lyapunov stable but not asymptotically stable |
| Energy decay | Yes (dissipative system) | No (conservative system) |

The trace of the Jacobian is the deciding metric: a **negative non-zero trace** identifies a stable focus, while a **zero trace** (with positive determinant) identifies a centre.

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, with Internal Choice)

> [!NOTE]
> KTU 2024 Scheme mandates an **internal choice** for every Part B question. Two completely independent alternatives are provided below — you answer **either** Question B.1 **or** Question B.2.

### Question B.1 (14 Marks) `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2024 Model]`

**Q: For the nonlinear system**
$$\dot{x}_1 = x_2, \qquad \dot{x}_2 = -x_1 + x_1^2$$
**find all singular points, compute the Jacobian at each, and classify them using eigenvalues. Sketch the local phase portrait and comment on stability.**

#### Part (a) — Location of Singular Points and Jacobian (7 marks)

**Step 1 — Locate singular points:**

Setting $\dot{x}_1 = 0$ gives $x_2 = 0$. Setting $\dot{x}_2 = 0$ gives $-x_1 + x_1^2 = 0$, i.e., $x_1(x_1 - 1) = 0$.

$$\therefore\ \text{Singular points:}\quad \mathbf{x}_{e1} = (0, 0),\quad \mathbf{x}_{e2} = (1, 0)$$

**Step 2 — Form the Jacobian matrix:**

$$J(x_1, x_2) = \begin{bmatrix} 0 & 1 \\ -1 + 2x_1 & 0 \end{bmatrix}$$

**Step 3 — Evaluate Jacobian at each singular point:**

$$J(0,0) = \begin{bmatrix} 0 & 1 \\ -1 & 0 \end{bmatrix}, \qquad J(1,0) = \begin{bmatrix} 0 & 1 \\ 1 & 0 \end{bmatrix}$$

#### Part (b) — Eigenvalue Computation and Classification (7 marks)

**At $\mathbf{x}_{e1} = (0,0)$:**

Characteristic equation: $\lambda^2 - T\lambda + \Delta = 0$ with $T = 0$, $\Delta = 1$.

$$\lambda^2 + 1 = 0 \Rightarrow \lambda = \pm j$$

Pure imaginary eigenvalues $\Rightarrow$ **Centre** (Lyapunov stable, not asymptotically stable).

**At $\mathbf{x}_{e2} = (1,0)$:**

$T = 0$, $\Delta = -1$.

$$\lambda^2 - 1 = 0 \Rightarrow \lambda = \pm 1$$

Real eigenvalues of opposite sign $\Rightarrow$ **Saddle Point** (unstable).

**Local Phase Portrait Sketch Description:**

- At $(0,0)$: Closed elliptical orbits surrounding the origin (centre).
- At $(1,0)$: Hyperbolic X-pattern — stable manifold along the line $x_1 + x_2 = 0$ and unstable manifold along $x_1 - x_2 = 0$.
- A **separatrix** passes through the saddle $(1,0)$, separating the inner closed orbits from trajectories that escape to infinity.

**Stability Comment:** The equilibrium at $(0,0)$ is **Lyapunov stable but not asymptotically stable** (closed orbits preserve distance). The equilibrium at $(1,0)$ is **unstable** in the saddle sense — small perturbations along the unstable manifold diverge.

---

### Question B.2 (14 Marks) `[KTU University Exam – May 2024 Model — Alternative Choice]`

**Q: Consider the nonlinear system:**
$$\dot{x}_1 = -x_1 + x_1 x_2, \qquad \dot{x}_2 = -x_2 + x_1^2$$
**Determine all singular points, classify each by computing the trace, determinant, and discriminant of the Jacobian, and infer stability.**

#### Part (a) — Singular Points and Trace–Determinant Analysis (7 marks)

**Step 1 — Locate singular points:**

$$-x_1 + x_1 x_2 = x_1(x_2 - 1) = 0 \Rightarrow x_1 = 0 \text{ or } x_2 = 1$$
$$-x_2 + x_1^2 = 0 \Rightarrow x_2 = x_1^2$$

**Case 1:** $x_1 = 0 \Rightarrow x_2 = 0$. Singular point $\mathbf{x}_{e1} = (0,0)$.

**Case 2:** $x_2 = 1 \Rightarrow 1 = x_1^2 \Rightarrow x_1 = \pm 1$. Singular points $\mathbf{x}_{e2} = (1, 1)$ and $\mathbf{x}_{e3} = (-1, 1)$.

**Step 2 — Compute Jacobian:**

$$J = \begin{bmatrix} -1 + x_2 & x_1 \\ 2x_1 & -1 \end{bmatrix}$$

**Step 3 — Trace, determinant, discriminant at each point:**

| Singular Point | $J$ | $T$ | $\Delta$ | $D = T^2 - 4\Delta$ |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| $(0,0)$ | $\begin{bmatrix} -1 & 0 \\ 0 & -1 \end{bmatrix}$ | $-2$ | $1$ | $0$ |
| $(1,1)$ | $\begin{bmatrix} 0 & 1 \\ 2 & -1 \end{bmatrix}$ | $-1$ | $-2$ | $9$ |
| $(-1,1)$ | $\begin{bmatrix} 0 & -1 \\ -2 & -1 \end{bmatrix}$ | $-1$ | $-2$ | $9$ |

#### Part (b) — Classification and Stability Conclusion (7 marks)

**At $\mathbf{x}_{e1} = (0,0)$:**

$T = -2 < 0$, $\Delta = 1 > 0$, $D = 0$. Repeated eigenvalue $\lambda = -1$ (algebraic multiplicity 2). Jacobian $\lambda I - J = (\lambda + 1) I$, so $J$ is already a scalar multiple of identity. This is a **Stable Star Node** (radially symmetric inflow). **Asymptotically stable.**

**At $\mathbf{x}_{e2} = (1,1)$:**

$T = -1$, $\Delta = -2 < 0$. Negative determinant immediately implies a **Saddle Point**. Eigenvalues from $\lambda^2 + \lambda - 2 = 0$ are $\lambda_1 = 1$, $\lambda_2 = -2$. **Unstable.**

**At $\mathbf{x}_{e3} = (-1,1)$:**

$T = -1$, $\Delta = -2 < 0$. Again **Saddle Point** (unstable). Eigenvalues $\lambda = 1, -2$.

**Stability Summary:** The origin is the only **asymptotically stable** equilibrium. The other two are saddle points. Trajectories starting near the saddles escape, while those starting in the basin of attraction of the origin spiral or flow radially inward to $(0,0)$.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls:**
>
> 1. **Missing singular points:** A frequent mistake is solving only one of the two equations and forgetting to substitute into the other. Always solve **both** $\dot{x}_1 = 0$ and $\dot{x}_2 = 0$ **simultaneously**.
>
> 2. **Confusing Centre with Stable Focus:** If $T \neq 0$ but small, students may round to zero and call it a centre. **Verify** $T$ is exactly zero before declaring a centre.
>
> 3. **Trusting Linearization for Non-Hyperbolic Points:** When $\Delta = 0$ or $T = 0, D = 0$, the linearization is inconclusive. **Do not** force a classification; instead, use higher-order terms or centre manifold theory.
>
> 4. **Sign Error in Determinant:** Always recompute $\Delta = ad - bc$ for $J = \begin{bmatrix} a & b \\ c & d \end{bmatrix}$. A single sign error propagates through the entire classification.
>
> 5. **No phase portrait sketch:** KTU awards **2 marks** explicitly for sketching phase portraits. Missing this leads to avoidable mark loss.
>
> 6. **Forgetting Stability Inference:** Classification alone (e.g., "saddle") is **incomplete**. Always append the **stability status** (asymptotically stable, Lyapunov stable, unstable).

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!TIP]
> **High-Yield Rapid Revision Checklist — Print This Before the Exam**

### Conceptual Mastery

- [ ] A **singular point** satisfies $\mathbf{f}(\mathbf{x}_e) = \mathbf{0}$; it is the only equilibrium of the autonomous nonlinear system.
- [ ] The **Jacobian** $J = \partial \mathbf{f}/\partial \mathbf{x}$ evaluated at $\mathbf{x}_e$ governs **local** dynamics.
- [ ] **Hartman–Grobman Theorem:** Near a hyperbolic singular point, the nonlinear system is topologically conjugate to its linearization.
- [ ] **Hyperbolic** ↔ no eigenvalue on the imaginary axis ($\text{Re}(\lambda_i) \neq 0$ for all $i$).

### Classification Rules (Commit to Memory)

- [ ] **$\Delta < 0$** ⟹ **Saddle** (always unstable).
- [ ] **$\Delta > 0$, $T < 0$, $D > 0$** ⟹ **Stable Node**.
- [ ] **$\Delta > 0$, $T > 0$, $D > 0$** ⟹ **Unstable Node**.
- [ ] **$\Delta > 0$, $T < 0$, $D < 0$** ⟹ **Stable Focus (Spiral Sink)**.
- [ ] **$\Delta > 0$, $T > 0$, $D < 0$** ⟹ **Unstable Focus (Spiral Source)**.
- [ ] **$\Delta > 0$, $T = 0$, $D < 0$** ⟹ **Centre**.
- [ ] **$D = 0$** ⟹ Degenerate / repeated eigenvalue (further analysis needed).
- [ ] **$\Delta = 0$** ⟹ Non-hyperbolic — linearization is inconclusive.

### Formulae to Memorize Verbatim

- [ ] Characteristic equation: $\lambda^2 - T\lambda + \Delta = 0$.
- [ ] Eigenvalues: $\lambda_{1,2} = \dfrac{T \pm \sqrt{T^2 - 4\Delta}}{2}$.
- [ ] Discriminant: $D = T^2 - 4\Delta$.
- [ ] Linearized dynamics: $\dot{\boldsymbol{\eta}} = J(\mathbf{x}_e)\,\boldsymbol{\eta}$.

### KTU-Specific Exam Tactics

- [ ] Always show **all three** of $T$, $\Delta$, $D$ in your answer — examiners reward explicit computation.
- [ ] Draw a **clear phase portrait** even if not explicitly asked.
- [ ] State **stability** explicitly after each classification.
- [ ] For non-hyperbolic cases, demonstrate awareness by stating "linearization inconclusive".
- [ ] Practice with the Duffing, Van der Pol, and pendulum systems — they appear repeatedly in KTU papers.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Final Word of Advice:**
> The local classification of singular points is the **gateway** to all higher nonlinear analysis — limit cycles, bifurcation theory, and Lyapunov stability. A solid grip on this module directly translates into marks across the entire course.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Limit Cycles on Phase Plane, Stability, Poincare-Bendixon Theorems
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PERAT634 - NON-LINEAR CONTROL
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Limit Cycles on Phase Plane, Stability, Poincare-Bendixon Theorems

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: CORE TECHNICAL DEFINITION & INTUITIVE OVERVIEW

## 1.1 What is a Limit Cycle?

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Formal Definition (KTU Syllabus Terminology):**
> A **Limit Cycle** is an *isolated* closed trajectory (orbit) in the phase plane of an autonomous system, such that all neighbouring trajectories either **spiral towards it** (stable), **spiral away from it** (unstable), or do **one of each** (semi-stable) as time progresses.

In other words, a limit cycle is a **periodic solution that is an attracting (or repelling) set** for the surrounding flow. It is the *non-linear analogue* of a stable/unstable focus in linear systems — except here, the focus itself is replaced by a closed orbit representing sustained oscillation.

### Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

> [!NOTE]
> Imagine pushing a child on a swing. If you push *just right* every cycle (energy in = energy lost to friction), the swing settles into a *perfectly repeating back-and-forth motion* of fixed amplitude. That fixed-amplitude oscillation is a **limit cycle**.
>
> - Push too little → swing damps to rest (trajectory spirals inward).
> - Push too hard → swing grows in amplitude (trajectory spirals outward).
> - Push just right → swing locks into the limit cycle amplitude.
>
> A limit cycle is therefore a **"self-determined" oscillation** that the system locks onto regardless of small disturbances (for stable limit cycles).

### Key Mathematical Statement

For the autonomous system

$$
\dot{x}_1 = f_1(x_1, x_2), \qquad \dot{x}_2 = f_2(x_1, x_2)
$$

a closed trajectory $\Gamma = \{x(t+T) = x(t)\}$ is a **limit cycle** if $\Gamma$ is the *only* closed orbit in some annular (ring-shaped) neighbourhood around it.

---

## 1.2 Classification of Limit Cycles (Stability Types)

| Type | Behaviour | Energy Picture | Engineering Example |
|------|-----------|---------------|--------------------|
| **Stable (Attracting)** | Trajectories from both sides spiral **into** the cycle | Net energy **dissipated** → system locks on | Van der Pol oscillator, heartbeat pacemaker |
| **Unstable (Repelling)** | Trajectories from both sides spiral **out of** the cycle | Net energy **added** → pushed away | Unstable combustion oscillation, screech in aero-elasticity |
| **Semi-stable** | One side attracts, the other repels | Marginal energy balance on one side only | Rare — borderline cases in folded singularities |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Board Emphasis:** A linear system *cannot* have a limit cycle. Limit cycles are an exclusively **non-linear phenomenon**. Linear oscillators have closed orbits whose amplitude *depends on initial conditions* — limit cycles have *amplitude fixed by the system itself*.

---

## 1.3 Why Limit Cycles Matter in Non-Linear Control

1. **Sustained Oscillation by Design:** Many engineering systems (clock circuits, oscillators, biological rhythms) rely on stable limit cycles.
2. **Catastrophic Oscillations:** Unstable limit cycles bound regions of attraction — knowing their location prevents dangerous operating regimes.
3. **Global Behaviour:** Limit cycles define *bounded* trajectories in phase plane — crucial for assessing safety and stability.

---

## 1.4 The Poincaré–Bendixson Theorem (Formal Statement)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Poincaré–Bendixson Theorem:**
> Let $\dot{x} = f(x)$ be a $C^1$ vector field on $\mathbb{R}^2$. Suppose that for some initial condition $x_0$, the trajectory $\varphi(t, x_0)$ remains in a **compact (closed and bounded) set** $K$ for all $t \geq 0$, and that $K$ **contains no equilibrium points** of the system. Then $\varphi(t, x_0)$ is either:
> 1. A **closed orbit** (limit cycle), or
> 2. Spirals towards a closed orbit as $t \to \infty$.

This is one of the **most powerful theorems** in non-linear control because it guarantees the existence of a limit cycle in 2D *without* having to solve the differential equation explicitly.

> [!NOTE]
> **Bendixon's Negative Criterion (Companion Result):**
> If on a simply connected region $\mathcal{D} \subset \mathbb{R}^2$, the divergence $\nabla \cdot f = \dfrac{\partial f_1}{\partial x_1} + \dfrac{\partial f_2}{\partial x_2}$ does not change sign and is not identically zero, then **no closed orbit can exist in $\mathcal{D}$**. This is the tool used to *rule out* limit cycles.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: DEEP THEORETICAL ANALYSIS & KTU HIGH-YIELD FORMULA SHEET

## 2.1 Existence of Limit Cycles — Necessary Background

For a limit cycle $\Gamma$ to exist, the divergence of the vector field integrated over the region enclosed by $\Gamma$ must satisfy a sign condition:

$$
\oint_{\Gamma} f \cdot \hat{n}\, ds \;\neq\; 0
$$

In a stable limit cycle, the divergence is **negative inside** (energy dissipated) on average, while trajectories from outside push inward.

---

## 2.2 The Poincaré–Bendixson Theorem — Structural Breakdown

The theorem has three precise hypotheses. **All three are required.**

### Hypothesis H1 — Trapping Region
There exists a **trapping region** $\mathcal{R}$ (compact, simply connected, no equilibrium points inside) such that the vector field on the boundary $\partial \mathcal{R}$ points *inward* (or *tangent*).

$$
f(x) \cdot \hat{n}(x) \leq 0 \quad \forall \, x \in \partial \mathcal{R}
$$

This guarantees **no trajectory escapes** $\mathcal{R}$.

### Hypothesis H2 — No Equilibrium in $\mathcal{R}$
$\mathcal{R}$ must be free of equilibrium points.

> [!NOTE]
> **Why?** Because $\mathbb{R}^2$ trajectories cannot cross. If there is no equilibrium to "trap" a trajectory, the trajectory has no choice but to loop back on itself and form a closed orbit.

### Hypothesis H3 — $\omega$-limit Set is Non-Trivial
The $\omega$-limit set of the trajectory must be **non-empty** (automatic if trajectory remains in compact $\mathcal{R}$).

The $\omega$-limit set is the set of accumulation points as $t \to \infty$.

$$
\omega(x_0) = \bigcap_{t \geq 0} \overline{\varphi([t, \infty), x_0)}
$$

By the theorem, $\omega(x_0)$ is a closed orbit.

---

## 2.3 High-Yield KTU Formula Sheet

| # | Concept | Formula / Statement | Notation / Units |
|---|---------|---------------------|------------------|
| 1 | Limit cycle definition | $\varphi(t+T, x_0) = \varphi(t, x_0)$ for some $T > 0$ | $T$ = period (seconds) |
| 2 | Existence of limit cycle in simply connected $\mathcal{D}$ | $\nabla \cdot f = \dfrac{\partial f_1}{\partial x_1} + \dfrac{\partial f_2}{\partial x_2} \not\equiv 0$ and does not change sign | Divergence has units of $\text{s}^{-1}$ |
| 3 | Bendixon's negative criterion | If $\nabla \cdot f$ has constant sign on $\mathcal{D}$, **no closed orbit exists in $\mathcal{D}$** | $\nabla \cdot f \in \mathbb{R}$ |
| 4 | Poincaré–Bendixson condition | Compact trapping region $\mathcal{R}$ with no equilibria ⟹ trajectory approaches closed orbit | $\omega(x_0) = \Gamma$ |
| 5 | Energy balance on closed orbit | $\displaystyle \oint_\Gamma \left( \frac{\partial f_1}{\partial x_1} + \frac{\partial f_2}{\partial x_2} \right) dt = 0$ | Energy-time integral |
| 6 | Van der Pol equation | $\ddot{x} - \mu(1 - x^2)\dot{x} + x = 0$ | $\mu > 0$ = non-linearity parameter |
| 7 | Negative damping oscillator | $\ddot{x} - \alpha \dot{x} + \beta \dot{x}^3 + \omega_0^2 x = 0$ | $\alpha, \beta > 0$ |
| 8 | Hopf bifurcation (creates limit cycle) | Eigenvalues of Jacobian cross imaginary axis: $\lambda = \pm j\omega_c$ | Bifurcation parameter $p = p^*$ |
| 9 | Index of an equilibrium | $\displaystyle \text{ind}(x^*) = \frac{1}{2\pi} \oint_C d\theta$ | $\theta = \arg(f(x))$ |
| 10 | Sum of indices on closed orbit | $\displaystyle \sum_{x^* \in \text{int}(\Gamma)} \text{ind}(x^*) = +1$ | Poincaré index theorem |

---

## 2.4 Van der Pol Oscillator — The Canonical Example

$$
\ddot{x} - \mu(1 - x^2)\dot{x} + x = 0
$$

Phase-plane form:
$$
\dot{x}_1 = x_2, \qquad \dot{x}_2 = -x_1 + \mu(1 - x_1^2)\,x_2
$$

### Behaviour Regimes

| Parameter $\mu$ | Regime | Physical Description |
|-----------------|--------|----------------------|
| $\mu \ll 1$ | **Quasi-sinusoidal** | Limit cycle is nearly circular, period $\approx 2\pi$ |
| $\mu = 1$ | Moderate non-linearity | Limit cycle mildly distorted |
| $\mu \gg 1$ | **Relaxation oscillation** | Limit cycle is highly elongated, period $\approx (\mu - 2)\cdot \ln(8) + \text{const}$ |

### Computing the Limit Cycle Energy Integral

For Van der Pol, the divergence is:
$$
\nabla \cdot f = \mu(1 - x_1^2)
$$

This **changes sign** along $x_1 = \pm 1$, so Bendixon's negative criterion **fails to rule out** a closed orbit — consistent with the existence of the limit cycle.

---

## 2.5 Engineering Utility — Real-World Applications

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-world systems exhibiting limit cycles:**
> - **Van der Pol:** ECG heartbeat model, vacuum-tube oscillators, Rayleigh-Bénard convection
> - **Negative damping:** Aero-elastic flutter, ship roll instability, bridge oscillations (Tacoma Narrows)
> - **Hopf bifurcation:** Predator–prey ecosystems, laser threshold, chemical reactors (Belousov–Zhabotinsky)
> - **Wien-bridge oscillator:** Classic textbook example of stable limit cycle in electronics

---

## 2.6 Comparison: Equilibrium vs. Limit Cycle

| Property | Equilibrium Point | Limit Cycle |
|----------|-------------------|-------------|
| Time-domain signature | $x(t) \to x^*$ (constant) | $x(t+T) = x(t)$ (periodic) |
| Phase portrait | Single point | Closed orbit |
| Linear systems can have it? | Yes (node/focus/centre) | **No** |
| Amplitude determined by | Eigenvalues of Jacobian | **System structure** (independent of IC) |
| Stability | Local (linearization) | Global (geometric, trapping region) |

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: STEP-BY-STEP DERIVATIONS & ANALYTICAL MATRIX

## 3.1 Derivation: Limit Cycle of the Negative-Damping Oscillator

### System Statement

Consider the second-order oscillator:
$$
\ddot{x} - \alpha \dot{x} + \beta \dot{x}^3 + \omega_0^2 x = 0, \quad \alpha, \beta > 0
$$

The linear term $-\alpha \dot{x}$ represents **negative damping** (energy addition), while the cubic term $+\beta \dot{x}^3$ represents **positive damping** at large amplitudes (energy dissipation).

### Step 1 — Convert to Phase-Plane Form

Let $x_1 = x$, $x_2 = \dot{x}$. Then:
$$
\dot{x}_1 = x_2
$$

$$
\dot{x}_2 = \alpha x_2 - \beta x_2^3 - \omega_0^2 x_1
$$

### Step 2 — Identify the Equilibrium

Setting $\dot{x}_1 = \dot{x}_2 = 0$:
$$
x_2 = 0, \qquad \omega_0^2 x_1 = 0 \;\;\Longrightarrow\;\; x^* = (0, 0)
$$

### Step 3 — Linearize the Jacobian at Origin

$$
J(0,0) = \begin{bmatrix} 0 & 1 \\ -\omega_0^2 & \alpha \end{bmatrix}
$$

### Step 4 — Compute Eigenvalues

$$
\det(J - \lambda I) = \lambda^2 - \alpha \lambda + \omega_0^2 = 0
$$

$$
\lambda_{1,2} = \frac{\alpha \pm \sqrt{\alpha^2 - 4\omega_0^2}}{2}
$$

Since $\alpha > 0$, the real parts of both eigenvalues are **positive**. The origin is an **unstable focus** (spiral source).

### Step 5 — Energy Argument for Boundedness

Define the total energy:
$$
E(x_1, x_2) = \frac{1}{2}\omega_0^2 x_1^2 + \frac{1}{2}x_2^2
$$

### Step 6 — Compute $\dot{E}$ Along Trajectories

$$
\dot{E} = \omega_0^2 x_1 \dot{x}_1 + x_2 \dot{x}_2 = \omega_0^2 x_1 x_2 + x_2(\alpha x_2 - \beta x_2^3 - \omega_0^2 x_1)
$$

### Step 7 — Simplify

$$
\dot{E} = \omega_0^2 x_1 x_2 + \alpha x_2^2 - \beta x_2^4 - \omega_0^2 x_1 x_2
$$

$$
\dot{E} = \alpha x_2^2 - \beta x_2^4 = x_2^2 (\alpha - \beta x_2^2)
$$

### Step 8 — Analyse $\dot{E}$ Sign

$$
\dot{E} \begin{cases} > 0 & \text{if } \vert x_2 \vert < \sqrt{\alpha / \beta} \\[4pt] = 0 & \text{if } \vert x_2 \vert = \sqrt{\alpha / \beta} \\[4pt] < 0 & \text{if } \vert x_2 \vert > \sqrt{\alpha / \beta} \end{cases}
$$

**Interpretation:**
- **Inside** the strip $\vert x_2 \vert < \sqrt{\alpha / \beta}$: Energy *grows* (negative damping dominates).
- **Outside** the strip $\vert x_2 \vert > \sqrt{\alpha / \beta}$: Energy *decays* (cubic damping dominates).

### Step 9 — Conclude Existence of a Stable Limit Cycle

Define the trapping region:
$$
\mathcal{R} = \left\{(x_1, x_2) : E(x_1, x_2) \leq R \right\}
$$

for sufficiently large $R$. On $\partial \mathcal{R}$:
$$
\dot{E} \leq 0
$$

Therefore, the vector field points **inward** on $\partial \mathcal{R}$. By the **Poincaré–Bendixson theorem**, $\mathcal{R}$ contains a **limit cycle** $\Gamma$.

By construction (energy grows for small $\vert x_2 \vert$ and decays for large $\vert x_2 \vert$), the limit cycle is **stable**.

**Approximate amplitude of the limit cycle** (from energy argument):
$$
\boxed{\;\vert x_2 \vert_{\max} \approx 2\sqrt{\alpha / \beta}\;}
$$

---

## 3.2 Derivation: Poincaré–Bendixson Theorem — Proof Sketch

### Theorem
Let $\varphi(t, x_0)$ be a trajectory of $\dot{x} = f(x)$, $f \in C^1(\mathbb{R}^2)$, that remains in a compact set $K$ for all $t \geq 0$, and assume $K$ contains no equilibria. Then $\omega(x_0)$ is a closed orbit.

### Step 1 — Define $\omega(x_0)$

$$
\omega(x_0) = \{y \in \mathbb{R}^2 : \varphi(t_n, x_0) \to y \text{ for some sequence } t_n \to \infty\}
$$

### Step 2 — Compactness of $\omega(x_0)$

Since $K$ is compact and $\varphi(t, x_0) \in K$ for all $t \geq 0$, the sequence $\{\varphi(t_n, x_0)\}$ has a convergent subsequence. So $\omega(x_0)$ is **non-empty, compact, and invariant** under the flow.

### Step 3 — Invariance of $\omega(x_0)$

For any $y \in \omega(x_0)$ and any $\tau \in \mathbb{R}$:
$$
\varphi(\tau, y) \in \omega(x_0)
$$

This means the trajectory starting at any point in $\omega(x_0)$ stays in $\omega(x_0)$ for all time, both forward and backward.

### Step 4 — Connectedness

$\omega(x_0)$ is a **connected** set (a continuous image of a connected set, since the flow is continuous and depends on initial conditions).

### Step 5 — Key Step — No Equilibrium in $\omega(x_0)$

Assume, for contradiction, $y^* \in \omega(x_0)$ is an equilibrium. Since $f$ is $C^1$, by linearization around $y^*$:

- If $y^*$ is a **hyperbolic** equilibrium (no zero eigenvalues), there is a neighbourhood $U$ of $y^*$ where the local flow looks like its linearization. There are no closed orbits in $U$.
- Since $y^* \in \omega(x_0)$ and $\omega(x_0)$ is invariant, the trajectory starting at $y^*$ stays at $y^*$ forever. So the $\omega$-limit set cannot be a closed orbit containing $y^*$ unless $y^*$ is the entire orbit, which contradicts it being a point.

### Step 6 — Conclusion via $\alpha$- and $\omega$-Limit Sets

By a topological argument, the only **compact, connected, invariant set** that contains **no equilibria** in $\mathbb{R}^2$ is a **closed orbit**.

Therefore, $\omega(x_0)$ must be a **closed orbit** — i.e., a **limit cycle**.

$\blacksquare$

---

## 3.3 Derivation: Bendixon's Negative Criterion

### Statement
If on a simply connected region $\mathcal{D} \subset \mathbb{R}^2$, the divergence $\nabla \cdot f$ does not change sign and is not identically zero, then **no closed orbit exists entirely in $\mathcal{D}$**.

### Proof Sketch

Suppose $\Gamma \subset \mathcal{D}$ is a closed orbit. By Green's theorem:

$$
\oint_\Gamma (f_1 \, dx_2 - f_2 \, dx_1) = \iint_{\text{int}(\Gamma)} \left( \frac{\partial f_1}{\partial x_1} + \frac{\partial f_2}{\partial x_2} \right) dx_1 dx_2
$$

The left-hand side **must vanish** because the integrand $f_1 \, dx_2 - f_2 \, dx_1$ is an exact differential of a single-valued function on the closed orbit (periodicity implies $x(t+T) = x(t)$).

### Detailed LHS Computation

Parameterize $\Gamma$ by $x_1(t), x_2(t)$, with period $T$:
$$
\oint_\Gamma (f_1 \, dx_2 - f_2 \, dx_1) = \int_0^T \left( f_1 \dot{x}_2 - f_2 \dot{x}_1 \right) dt
$$

Using $\dot{x}_1 = f_1$ and $\dot{x}_2 = f_2$:
$$
= \int_0^T \left( f_1 f_2 - f_2 f_1 \right) dt = \int_0^T 0 \, dt = 0
$$

### RHS — Sign Analysis

$$
\iint_{\text{int}(\Gamma)} \left( \frac{\partial f_1}{\partial x_1} + \frac{\partial f_2}{\partial x_2} \right) dx_1 dx_2 = 0
$$

But if $\nabla \cdot f$ has **constant sign** (say, $\geq 0$) on $\mathcal{D}$ and is not identically zero, the integral over any open subregion of $\mathcal{D}$ is **strictly positive**. **Contradiction.** Therefore no such $\Gamma$ exists. $\blacksquare$

---

## 3.4 Worked Example — Limit Cycle of Rayleigh's Equation

### System
$$
\ddot{x} + \varepsilon\left( \frac{1}{3}\dot{x}^3 - \dot{x} \right) + x = 0, \quad 0 < \varepsilon \ll 1
$$

### Step 1 — Phase-Plane Form

$$
\dot{x}_1 = x_2, \qquad \dot{x}_2 = -x_1 + \varepsilon\left( x_2 - \frac{1}{3}x_2^3 \right)
$$

### Step 2 — Equilibrium

$x^* = (0, 0)$. Linearized Jacobian:
$$
J = \begin{bmatrix} 0 & 1 \\ -1 & \varepsilon \end{bmatrix}
$$

### Step 3 — Eigenvalues

$$
\lambda_{1,2} = \frac{\varepsilon \pm \sqrt{\varepsilon^2 - 4}}{2}
$$

For $\varepsilon$ small and positive: $\text{Re}(\lambda) = \varepsilon/2 > 0$. Unstable spiral.

### Step 4 — Use Averaging (Slow-Fast Analysis)

Assume $x_1 \approx A \cos(t + \phi)$, $x_2 \approx -A \sin(t + \phi)$. Substituting and averaging over one period yields:

$$
\dot{A} = \frac{\varepsilon A}{2}\left( 1 - \frac{A^2}{4} \right)
$$

### Step 5 — Equilibrium Amplitudes

$$
\dot{A} = 0 \;\;\Longrightarrow\;\; A = 0 \;\text{ or }\; A = 2
$$

The non-trivial solution $A = 2$ is the **amplitude of the limit cycle**.

### Step 6 — Stability

$$
\frac{d\dot{A}}{dA} \bigg|_{A=2} = \frac{\varepsilon}{2}\left(1 - \frac{3 \cdot 4}{4}\right) = -\varepsilon < 0
$$

So the limit cycle $A = 2$ is **stable**.

### Conclusion

$$
\boxed{\;A_{\text{limit cycle}} = 2, \quad T \approx 2\pi\;}
$$

# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 SCHEME EXAMINATION QUESTION BANK & TOPIC RECAP

---

## PART A QUESTIONS (3 Marks Each)

### **Question 1** `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`

**Define a limit cycle. How is it different from a centre in a linear system?**

#### Model Answer (3 marks)

A **limit cycle** is an *isolated* closed trajectory in the phase plane of an autonomous non-linear system such that all neighbouring trajectories approach it (stable) or recede from it (unstable) as $t \to \infty$. **(1.5 marks)**

**Difference from a centre:**

| Centre (Linear) | Limit Cycle (Non-linear) |
|-----------------|--------------------------|
| Amplitude depends on initial conditions | Amplitude is **fixed by system** parameters |
| Closed orbits form a *family* (continuum) | **Isolated** — no nearby closed orbits |
| Exists in linear conservative systems | Exclusively **non-linear** phenomenon |
| Marginal stability only | Can be **asymptotically stable** or unstable |

**(1.5 marks for distinction)**

---

### **Question 2** `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`

**State the Poincaré–Bendixson theorem. Why does it fail in $\mathbb{R}^3$?**

#### Model Answer (3 marks)

**Statement:** *If a trajectory of a 2D autonomous system $\dot{x} = f(x)$ remains in a compact set $K$ for all $t \geq 0$, and $K$ contains no equilibrium points, then the trajectory approaches a closed orbit (limit cycle) as $t \to \infty$.* **(2 marks)**

**Why it fails in $\mathbb{R}^3$:** In three (or higher) dimensions, a trajectory can remain bounded forever **without** approaching any closed orbit — it can wander on a *strange attractor* (chaotic set) such as the Lorenz attractor. The topological argument that forces a 2D bounded invariant set without equilibria to be a closed orbit **breaks down** because in 3D, compact connected invariant sets can have much richer structure (fractal, knotted, etc.). **(1 mark)**

---

## PART B QUESTIONS (14 Marks Each — Internal Choice)

### **Question A (14 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam - Model Paper 2024]`

**(a)** With the help of a suitable example, derive the condition for the existence of a stable limit cycle in a second-order autonomous system. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** State and prove Bendixon's negative criterion. Show that the system $\dot{x}_1 = x_2$, $\dot{x}_2 = -x_1 + x_2(1 - 3x_1^2 - 3x_2^2)$ has a stable limit cycle. **(7 marks)**

---

#### Model Solution to (a) — Stable Limit Cycle Existence

**Example System:** Negative damping oscillator
$$
\ddot{x} - \alpha \dot{x} + \beta \dot{x}^3 + \omega_0^2 x = 0, \quad \alpha, \beta > 0
$$

**Step 1 — Phase-plane form:**
$$
\dot{x}_1 = x_2, \quad \dot{x}_2 = \alpha x_2 - \beta x_2^3 - \omega_0^2 x_1
$$

**Step 2 — Equilibrium at origin** $(0,0)$. Linearization:
$$
J = \begin{bmatrix} 0 & 1 \\ -\omega_0^2 & \alpha \end{bmatrix}, \quad \text{eigenvalues: } \lambda = \frac{\alpha \pm \sqrt{\alpha^2 - 4\omega_0^2}}{2}
$$

For small $\alpha > 0$: $\text{Re}(\lambda) > 0$ → **unstable focus**. **(1 mark)**

**Step 3 — Energy function:**
$$
E(x_1, x_2) = \frac{1}{2}\omega_0^2 x_1^2 + \frac{1}{2}x_2^2
$$

$$
\dot{E} = x_2^2(\alpha - \beta x_2^2)
$$

**Step 4 — Sign analysis:**
- $\vert x_2 \vert < \sqrt{\alpha/\beta}$: $\dot{E} > 0$ (energy **grows**)
- $\vert x_2 \vert > \sqrt{\alpha/\beta}$: $\dot{E} < 0$ (energy **decays**) **(2 marks)**

**Step 5 — Trapping region:** For $R$ sufficiently large, on $\partial \mathcal{R} = \{E = R\}$:
$$
\dot{E} < 0 \;\;\Longrightarrow\;\; f \cdot \hat{n} < 0
$$

Vector field points **inward**. $\mathcal{R}$ is a trapping region containing no equilibria (the origin is excluded). **(2 marks)**

**Step 6 — Apply Poincaré–Bendixson:** Trajectory cannot escape $\mathcal{R}$, cannot converge to origin. Therefore, by Poincaré–Bendixson theorem, the trajectory approaches a **closed orbit** (stable limit cycle). **(1 mark)**

**Step 7 — Approximate amplitude:** $\vert x_2 \vert_{\max} \approx 2\sqrt{\alpha/\beta}$ **(1 mark)**

---

#### Model Solution to (b) — Bendixon's Criterion + Verification

**Statement (1 mark):** *If on a simply connected region $\mathcal{D} \subset \mathbb{R}^2$, the divergence $\nabla \cdot f = \partial f_1/\partial x_1 + \partial f_2/\partial x_2$ has constant sign (and is not identically zero), then no closed orbit can lie entirely in $\mathcal{D}$.*

**Proof (3 marks):** Suppose $\Gamma \subset \mathcal{D}$ is a closed orbit. By Green's theorem:

$$
\oint_\Gamma (f_1 \, dx_2 - f_2 \, dx_1) = \iint_{\text{int}(\Gamma)} \nabla \cdot f \, dx_1 dx_2
$$

The LHS vanishes identically (parameterize by period $T$; use $\dot{x}_i = f_i$). The RHS is non-zero by hypothesis. **Contradiction.** ∎

**Application to given system (3 marks):**

System:
$$
\dot{x}_1 = x_2, \qquad \dot{x}_2 = -x_1 + x_2(1 - 3x_1^2 - 3x_2^2)
$$

**Step 1 — Find equilibria:**
$$
x_2 = 0, \quad -x_1 = 0 \;\;\Longrightarrow\;\; x^* = (0,0)
$$

**Step 2 — Linearize:**
$$
J(0,0) = \begin{bmatrix} 0 & 1 \\ -1 & 1 \end{bmatrix}, \quad \text{eigenvalues: } \lambda = \frac{1 \pm j\sqrt{3}}{2}
$$

$\text{Re}(\lambda) = 1/2 > 0$ → **unstable focus**.

**Step 3 — Compute divergence:**
$$
\nabla \cdot f = \frac{\partial x_2}{\partial x_1} + \frac{\partial}{\partial x_2}\left[-x_1 + x_2(1 - 3x_1^2 - 3x_2^2)\right]
$$

$$
\nabla \cdot f = 0 + (1 - 3x_1^2 - 3x_2^2) + x_2(-6x_2)
$$

$$
\nabla \cdot f = 1 - 3x_1^2 - 9x_2^2
$$

**Step 4 — Sign of divergence:**
- At origin: $\nabla \cdot f = 1 > 0$ (repelling)
- On circle $3x_1^2 + 9x_2^2 = 1$ (e.g., $x_1 = \pm 1/\sqrt{3}$, $x_2 = 0$): $\nabla \cdot f = 0$
- **Outside** the ellipse $3x_1^2 + 9x_2^2 = 1$: $\nabla \cdot f < 0$ (contracting)

**Step 5 — Existence of limit cycle by Poincaré–Bendixson:**
- Inside the ellipse: trajectories spiral outward (divergence positive).
- Outside the ellipse: trajectories spiral inward (divergence negative).
- The vector field reverses orientation → trajectories must approach a **closed orbit** between these regions.

Therefore, the system has a **stable limit cycle** surrounding the origin. ∎

---

### **Question B (14 Marks — Alternative Choice)** `[KTU University Exam - Model Paper 2024]`

**(a)** State and prove the Poincaré–Bendixson theorem. What are the limitations of the theorem? **(7 marks)**

**(b)** For the Van der Pol equation $\ddot{x} - \mu(1 - x^2)\dot{x} + x = 0$, identify the equilibrium points, linearize, and use the Poincaré–Bendixson theorem to argue the existence of a limit cycle for $\mu > 0$. **(7 marks)**

---

#### Model Solution to (a) — Poincaré–Bendixson Theorem

**Statement (2 marks):** *Let $\varphi(t, x_0)$ be a trajectory of a $C^1$ vector field $\dot{x} = f(x)$ on $\mathbb{R}^2$. If $\varphi(t, x_0)$ remains in a compact set $K$ for all $t \geq 0$, and $K$ contains no equilibria, then $\omega(x_0)$ is a closed orbit.*

**Proof (4 marks):**

**Step 1:** $\omega(x_0)$ is non-empty (compactness of $K$ + Bolzano–Weierstrass).

**Step 2:** $\omega(x_0)$ is **closed** (intersection of closed sets) and **invariant** under the flow.

**Step 3:** $\omega(x_0)$ is **connected** (continuous image of connected set).

**Step 4:** By Poincaré index theorem, $\sum \text{ind}(x^*)$ inside any closed orbit = $+1$. Since $\omega(x_0)$ contains no equilibria, it cannot be a fixed point.

**Step 5:** The only compact, connected, invariant set in $\mathbb{R}^2$ with no equilibria is a **closed orbit**. Hence $\omega(x_0)$ is a closed orbit. ∎

**Limitations (1 mark):**
- Only applies to **2D** systems.
- Requires existence of a *trapping region* (often hard to construct).
- Provides *existence*, not uniqueness or analytical form.

---

#### Model Solution to (b) — Van der Pol Analysis

**Step 1 — Phase-plane form (1 mark):**
$$
\dot{x}_1 = x_2, \qquad \dot{x}_2 = -x_1 + \mu(1 - x_1^2) x_2
$$

**Step 2 — Equilibrium (1 mark):** Only $x^* = (0, 0)$.

**Step 3 — Linearization at origin (1 mark):**
$$
J(0,0) = \begin{bmatrix} 0 & 1 \\ -1 & \mu \end{bmatrix}
$$

**Step 4 — Eigenvalues (1 mark):**
$$
\lambda^2 - \mu\lambda + 1 = 0 \;\;\Longrightarrow\;\; \lambda_{1,2} = \frac{\mu \pm \sqrt{\mu^2 - 4}}{2}
$$

For $\mu > 0$: $\text{Re}(\lambda) = \mu/2 > 0$ → **unstable spiral source**.

**Step 5 — Divergence (1 mark):**
$$
\nabla \cdot f = \frac{\partial x_2}{\partial x_1} + \frac{\partial}{\partial x_2}\left[\mu(1 - x_1^2)x_2 - x_1\right] = \mu(1 - x_1^2)
$$

**Step 6 — Apply Poincaré–Bendixson (2 marks):**

- For $\vert x_1 \vert < 1$: $\nabla \cdot f > 0$ (trajectories expand outward).
- For $\vert x_1 \vert > 1$: $\nabla \cdot f < 0$ (trajectories contract inward).
- This sign reversal guarantees that on any sufficiently large compact set $\mathcal{R}$ (e.g., a large disc), the vector field points inward on the boundary (because for large $\vert x \vert$, the term $-x_1$ dominates).
- $\mathcal{R}$ contains no equilibria → by Poincaré–Bendixson, a **stable limit cycle** $\Gamma$ exists in $\mathcal{R}$.

The limit cycle has amplitude that grows with $\mu$:
$$
A \approx 2 \text{ for small } \mu, \qquad A \to \infty \text{ as } \mu \to \infty
$$

---

## ⚠️ KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mistakes Students Make (Costing 2–4 Marks Each):**
>
> 1. **Confusing Centre with Limit Cycle:** Centres have *continuous families* of orbits; limit cycles are *isolated*. Examiners explicitly test this distinction.
>
> 2. **Forgetting the "Isolated" Condition:** A limit cycle must be the *only* closed orbit in some neighbourhood. Not every closed orbit is a limit cycle.
>
> 3. **Bendixon's Criterion Misapplication:** The region $\mathcal{D}$ must be **simply connected** (no holes). Bendixon's criterion only **rules out** limit cycles — it cannot *prove existence*.
>
> 4. **Poincaré–Bendixson Dimensional Confusion:** Students often try to apply it to 3D systems. It is **strictly 2D**.
>
> 5. **Missing "No Equilibrium" Hypothesis:** Without this hypothesis, the theorem fails. Always verify that the trapping region has no equilibria.
>
> 6. **Linearization Trap:** Linearizing and finding unstable focus is **necessary but not sufficient** for a limit cycle. You must still apply the *full* non-linear theorem.
>
> 7. **Amplitude Calculation:** Many students confuse *amplitude* ($x_{\max}$) with *peak velocity* ($\dot{x}_{\max}$). State clearly which one you are computing.

---

## 📋 TOPIC RECAP & IMPORTANT THINGS TO REMEMBER

> [!NOTE]
> **High-Density Revision Checklist**

### Core Definitions
- ✅ **Limit cycle** = isolated closed orbit in phase plane of an autonomous non-linear system.
- ✅ **Stable LC** = both inner and outer trajectories approach it.
- ✅ **Unstable LC** = both inner and outer trajectories diverge from it.
- ✅ **Semi-stable LC** = one side attracts, other repels.

### Key Theorems
- ✅ **Poincaré–Bendixson (Existence):** Trapping region + no equilibria ⟹ closed orbit exists.
- ✅ **Bendixon's Negative Criterion (Non-existence):** Constant-sign divergence ⟹ no closed orbit.
- ✅ **Poincaré Index Theorem:** Sum of indices inside any closed orbit = $+1$.

### Critical Conditions
- ✅ Both theorems apply **only in 2D** ($\mathbb{R}^2$).
- ✅ Region must be **simply connected** for Bendixon's criterion.
- ✅ Trapping region must be **compact and equilibrium-free**.

### Canonical Examples
- ✅ **Van der Pol:** $\ddot{x} - \mu(1 - x^2)\dot{x} + x = 0$ → stable LC for $\mu > 0$.
- ✅ **Negative damping:** $\ddot{x} - \alpha \dot{x} + \beta \dot{x}^3 + \omega_0^2 x = 0$ → stable LC.
- ✅ **Rayleigh's equation:** $\ddot{x} + \varepsilon(\frac{1}{3}\dot{x}^3 - \dot{x}) + x = 0$ → stable LC with $A = 2$.

### Energy-Based Intuition
- ✅ Inside LC: energy grows (negative damping dominates).
- ✅ Outside LC: energy decays (positive damping dominates).
- ✅ Stable LC = equilibrium between energy addition and dissipation.

### Engineering Significance
- ✅ Real oscillators (electronic, mechanical) exploit stable LCs.
- ✅ Flutter, screech, and combustion instability often involve unstable LCs.
- ✅ Hopf bifurcation creates a limit cycle from a focus as parameter crosses critical value.

### Exam Shortcuts
- ✅ Always check **3 things**: (i) is system 2D autonomous? (ii) can you construct a trapping region? (iii) is the region equilibrium-free?
- ✅ Always verify that **divergence changes sign** to confirm existence (not rule out) LC.
- ✅ Always compute **amplitude** explicitly when asked (e.g., $A = 2$ for Rayleigh's equation).

# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Introduction to Lyapunov Stability Definitions

In **Non-Linear Control Theory**, Module 3 introduces one of the most powerful and general tools for analyzing the stability of non-linear systems — the **Lyapunov Stability Theory**, formulated by the Russian mathematician **Alexander Mikhailovich Lyapunov** in his 1892 Ph.D. dissertation *"The General Problem of the Stability of Motion"*.

Before we can apply Lyapunov's methods, we must first rigorously define what **"stability"** means mathematically. The KTU 2024 Scheme expects students to clearly distinguish between several closely related but fundamentally different notions of stability.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight — Module 3 (Core Theme):**
> The topic *"Definition"* in Module 3 establishes the foundational vocabulary for **Lyapunov's Direct (Second) Method** and related stability criteria. Mastery of these definitions is **mandatory** before tackling problem-solving questions on Lyapunov function design.

---

## 1.2 Formal Mathematical Definitions

### Definition 1: Autonomous Non-Linear System

Consider an **autonomous non-linear time-invariant system** described by the state-space equation:

$$
\dot{x}(t) = f(x(t))
$$

where:

- $x(t) \in \mathbb{R}^{n}$ is the **state vector**.
- $f : \mathbb{R}^{n} \to \mathbb{R}^{n}$ is a **vector-valued non-linear function**.
- $\dot{x}(t) = \dfrac{dx(t)}{dt}$ is the time derivative of the state vector.
- The system has **no explicit time dependence**, hence "autonomous".

An **equilibrium point** (or singular point) $x^{*} \in \mathbb{R}^{n}$ satisfies:

$$
f(x^{*}) = 0
$$

> [!NOTE]
> The equilibrium point is the state at which the system can remain forever once placed there, because the rate of change is zero.

---

### Definition 2: Stability in the Sense of Lyapunov (LSL)

The equilibrium point $x = 0$ (without loss of generality, via change of coordinates) is said to be **stable in the sense of Lyapunov** if, for every $\varepsilon > 0$, there exists a $\delta > 0$ (which may depend on $\varepsilon$) such that:

$$
\|x(0)\| < \delta \quad \Longrightarrow \quad \|x(t)\| < \varepsilon, \quad \forall \, t \geq 0
$$

where $\| \cdot \|$ denotes the **Euclidean norm** of the vector.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Key Interpretation:** If the initial state starts **sufficiently close** to the equilibrium (within $\delta$), then the trajectory remains **arbitrarily close** to the equilibrium (within $\varepsilon$) for **all future time**.

---

### Definition 3: Asymptotic Stability

The equilibrium point $x = 0$ is **asymptotically stable** if:

1. It is **stable in the sense of Lyapunov** (as defined above), **AND**
2. There exists a $\delta > 0$ such that:

$$
\|x(0)\| < \delta \quad \Longrightarrow \quad \lim_{t \to \infty} \|x(t)\| = 0
$$

In other words, the trajectory not only stays near the equilibrium but **actually converges to it** as time approaches infinity.

> [!NOTE]
> **Asymptotic stability = Lyapunov Stability + Convergence to Equilibrium**

---

### Definition 4: Exponential Stability

The equilibrium point $x = 0$ is **exponentially stable** if there exist constants $\alpha > 0$, $\beta > 0$, and $\delta > 0$ such that:

$$
\|x(0)\| < \delta \quad \Longrightarrow \quad \|x(t)\| \leq \alpha \, \|x(0)\| \, e^{-\beta t}, \quad \forall \, t \geq 0
$$

The constant $\beta$ is called the **rate of convergence**.

---

### Definition 5: Instability

The equilibrium point $x = 0$ is **unstable** if it is **NOT stable**. That is, there exists at least one $\varepsilon > 0$ such that for every $\delta > 0$, there exists an initial condition $x(0)$ with $\|x(0)\| < \delta$ and some time $t_{1} \geq 0$ for which:

$$
\|x(t_{1})\| \geq \varepsilon
$$

---

### Definition 6: Positive Definiteness, Semi-Definiteness, and Indefiniteness

Let $V(x) : \mathbb{R}^{n} \to \mathbb{R}$ be a scalar function continuous in a domain $D \subset \mathbb{R}^{n}$ containing the origin.

| Term | Mathematical Definition |
|------|------------------------|
| **Positive Definite (PD)** | $V(0) = 0$ and $V(x) > 0$ for all $x \neq 0$ in $D$ |
| **Positive Semi-Definite (PSD)** | $V(0) = 0$ and $V(x) \geq 0$ for all $x \neq 0$ in $D$ |
| **Negative Definite (ND)** | $-V(x)$ is positive definite, i.e., $V(x) < 0$ for $x \neq 0$ |
| **Negative Semi-Definite (NSD)** | $-V(x)$ is positive semi-definite, i.e., $V(x) \leq 0$ for $x \neq 0$ |
| **Indefinite** | $V(x)$ takes both positive and negative values in every neighbourhood of origin |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> These classifications are foundational to Lyapunov's theorems — they directly determine whether a system is stable, asymptotically stable, or unstable.

---

### Definition 7: Lyapunov Function

A scalar function $V(x)$ is called a **Lyapunov function candidate** if it satisfies:

1. $V(x)$ is **continuous** in a domain $D$ containing the origin.
2. $V(x)$ has **continuous first partial derivatives** in $D$.
3. $V(0) = 0$.
4. $V(x) > 0$ for all $x \neq 0$ in $D$ (i.e., $V(x)$ is positive definite).

If, in addition, the time derivative along the system trajectories $\dot{V}(x) = \nabla V \cdot f(x)$ satisfies certain sign conditions, then $V(x)$ becomes a true **Lyapunov function**.

---

## 1.3 Conceptual Analogy / Intuitive Explanation

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-World Analogy — The Ball in a Bowl**
>
> Imagine a **bowl** with a small ball placed inside it. The bottom of the bowl represents the **equilibrium point**.
>
> - **Lyapunov Stable**: If you gently nudge the ball, it moves a little but stays inside the bowl forever. The ball never escapes, but it also doesn't necessarily return to the bottom — like a frictionless marble on a perfectly smooth curved surface.
>
> - **Asymptotically Stable**: If you nudge the ball, it rolls around but eventually comes to rest at the bottom. Friction (energy dissipation) ensures it returns. This is the most desirable practical form of stability.
>
> - **Exponentially Stable**: This is a *stronger* form of asymptotic stability where the ball returns to the bottom with a guaranteed **rate** — like a critically damped spring-mass system where we can bound how fast it settles.
>
> - **Unstable**: Now imagine the bowl turned **upside down** — a hill. The ball balanced at the top, if nudged even slightly, rolls away forever. This is instability.
>
> The **Lyapunov function** $V(x)$ in this analogy is analogous to the **height (potential energy)** of the ball. The derivative $\dot{V}(x)$ represents how this energy changes over time:
> - If $\dot{V}(x) \leq 0$: Energy is not increasing → System is stable (like the bowl).
> - If $\dot{V}(x) < 0$ (strictly): Energy is decreasing → System is asymptotically stable (like the bowl with friction).
> - If $\dot{V}(x) > 0$: Energy is increasing → System is unstable (like the hill).

This energy analogy is the **physical intuition** behind Lyapunov's direct method and is **extremely important** for KTU exam answers.

# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Theoretical Breakdown of Stability Concepts

### 2.1.1 Why We Need Multiple Definitions of Stability

For **linear time-invariant (LTI)** systems, stability has only one meaning: all eigenvalues of $A$ have strictly negative real parts. But for **non-linear systems**, stability is a **local, conditional property** — an equilibrium may be stable from some directions and unstable from others. This complexity necessitates multiple definitions.

### 2.1.2 Hierarchy of Stability Notions

The KTU syllabus emphasizes a clear **hierarchy** of stability definitions:

$$
\text{Exponential Stability} \implies \text{Asymptotic Stability} \implies \text{Lyapunov Stability}
$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> Each implication is **one-way**. Exponential stability is the strongest, Lyapunov stability is the weakest in this hierarchy. A system that is exponentially stable is automatically asymptotically stable, which is automatically stable in the sense of Lyapunov. The converse is NOT necessarily true.

### 2.1.3 Logical Steps in Applying Stability Definitions

When verifying stability of an equilibrium point using these definitions, follow these steps:

1. **Identify the system**: Write $\dot{x} = f(x)$ and find equilibrium points by solving $f(x^{*}) = 0$.
2. **Shift coordinates**: Translate the equilibrium to the origin via $z = x - x^{*}$, ensuring $\dot{z} = f(z + x^{*})$.
3. **Apply the $\varepsilon - \delta$ test** (for Lyapunov stability) or check the limit condition (for asymptotic stability) or the exponential bound (for exponential stability).
4. **Verify the function properties** (for Lyapunov function-based analysis): positive definiteness, differentiability, and the sign of $\dot{V}$.

---

## 2.2 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!NOTE]
> **High-Yield Reference Table — Bookmark This for KTU Exams**

| # | Concept | Mathematical Form | Conditions / Interpretation |
|---|---------|-------------------|---------------------------|
| 1 | Autonomous System | $\dot{x}(t) = f(x(t))$ | $f : \mathbb{R}^{n} \to \mathbb{R}^{n}$, time-invariant |
| 2 | Equilibrium Point | $f(x^{*}) = 0$ | State at which system rests permanently |
| 3 | Lyapunov Stability (LSL) | $\forall \varepsilon>0,\ \exists \delta>0:\ \Vert x(0)\Vert<\delta \Rightarrow \Vert x(t)\Vert<\varepsilon,\ \forall t\geq0$ | Trajectory stays within any small ball around origin |
| 4 | Asymptotic Stability | LSL condition $+\ \lim\limits_{t\to\infty}\Vert x(t)\Vert=0$ | Trajectory converges to origin as $t\to\infty$ |
| 5 | Exponential Stability | $\Vert x(t)\Vert \leq \alpha\Vert x(0)\Vert e^{-\beta t}$ | $\alpha,\beta>0$; bounded exponential decay |
| 6 | Instability | $\exists\varepsilon>0,\ \forall\delta>0,\ \exists x(0):\ \Vert x(0)\Vert<\delta$ but $\Vert x(t_1)\Vert\geq\varepsilon$ | Trajectory escapes any ball around origin |
| 7 | Positive Definite | $V(0)=0,\ V(x)>0$ for $x\neq0$ | "Energy-like" function |
| 8 | Positive Semi-Definite | $V(0)=0,\ V(x)\geq0$ for $x\neq0$ | Non-negative everywhere |
| 9 | Negative Definite | $V(0)=0,\ V(x)<0$ for $x\neq0$ | Energy strictly decreases |
| 10 | Lyapunov Function Candidate | Continuous + $C^{1}$ + $V(0)=0$ + PD | Foundation for Lyapunov's theorems |
| 11 | Time Derivative Along Trajectories | $\dot{V}(x) = \dfrac{\partial V}{\partial x}f(x) = \nabla V \cdot f(x)$ | Rate of change of $V$ along system path |
| 12 | Class $\mathcal{K}$ Function (KTU Advanced) | $\alpha:[0,a)\to[0,\infty)$, continuous, strictly increasing, $\alpha(0)=0$ | Used in modern stability definitions |
| 13 | Class $\mathcal{KL}$ Function (KTU Advanced) | $\beta:[0,a)\times[0,\infty)\to[0,\infty)$, $\mathcal{K}$ in first arg, decreasing to $0$ in second arg | Used in asymptotic stability |
| 14 | Quadratic Form PD Test | $V(x)=x^{T}Px$ is PD iff all eigenvalues of $P$ are $>0$ | Sylvester's criterion applies |
| 15 | Euclidean Norm | $\Vert x\Vert = \sqrt{x_1^2+x_2^2+\cdots+x_n^2}$ | Standard measure of distance from origin |

---

## 2.3 Real-World Engineering Utility

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Why These Definitions Matter in Practice (KTU-Examiner-Favourite Content)**

1. **Robotics & Autonomous Vehicles**: Asymptotic stability ensures a drone or robotic arm returns to its desired pose after disturbance. Exponential stability gives a *guaranteed settling time* — critical for real-time control.

2. **Power Systems**: Lyapunov stability of the power grid post-fault determines whether blackouts cascade. Definitions of regional vs. global stability use these formal concepts.

3. **Aerospace**: Aircraft flight control laws are designed so that all equilibrium states are exponentially stable — providing rigorous bounds on deviation from trim conditions.

4. **Chemical Process Control**: Reactor temperature stability is analyzed via Lyapunov functions constructed from thermodynamic energy potentials.

5. **Non-linear Circuit Design**: Oscillator circuits (e.g., Van der Pol) require careful stability analysis of their limit cycles — the definitions here provide the analytical foundation.

---

## 2.4 Geometric / Topological Interpretation

The stability definitions can be visualized geometrically:

- The $\varepsilon$-ball: $B_{\varepsilon} = \{ x \in \mathbb{R}^{n} : \Vert x\Vert < \varepsilon \}$
- The $\delta$-ball: $B_{\delta} = \{ x \in \mathbb{R}^{n} : \Vert x\Vert < \delta \}$

Lyapunov stability means: for **every** outer ball $B_{\varepsilon}$, there exists an **inner ball** $B_{\delta}$ such that any trajectory starting in $B_{\delta}$ never leaves $B_{\varepsilon}$.

Asymptotic stability additionally requires: trajectories starting in $B_{\delta}$ eventually **shrink to the center point** ($x = 0$) as $t \to \infty$.

# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Detailed Derivation: From the Autonomous System to the Stability Bound

In this section, we exhaustively derive the **exponential stability bound** starting from the autonomous non-linear system and Lyapunov's differential inequality.

### 3.1.1 Problem Setup

Given the autonomous system:

$$
\dot{x}(t) = f(x(t)), \quad x(0) = x_{0}
$$

Assume there exists a continuously differentiable scalar function $V(x)$ such that:

1. $c_{1} \Vert x\Vert^{2} \leq V(x) \leq c_{2} \Vert x\Vert^{2}$, for some $c_{1}, c_{2} > 0$.
2. $\dot{V}(x) = \dfrac{\partial V}{\partial x} f(x) \leq -c_{3} \Vert x\Vert^{2}$, for some $c_{3} > 0$.

These are the standard **Lyapunov boundedness and decay** conditions. We wish to derive the explicit exponential bound on $\Vert x(t)\Vert$.

---

### 3.1.2 Exhaustive Step-by-Step Derivation

**Step 1: Bound $V(x)$ from below using condition (1).**

$$
c_{1} \Vert x\Vert^{2} \leq V(x)
$$

Solving for $\Vert x\Vert^{2}$:

$$
\Vert x\Vert^{2} \leq \frac{V(x)}{c_{1}}
$$

**Step 2: Take the square root (both sides non-negative).**

$$
\Vert x\Vert \leq \sqrt{\frac{V(x)}{c_{1}}}
$$

**Step 3: Bound $\dot{V}(x)$ using condition (2).**

$$
\dot{V}(x) \leq -c_{3} \Vert x\Vert^{2}
$$

Using the upper bound from Step 1 ($\Vert x\Vert^{2} \geq V(x)/c_{2}$ substituted in reverse — careful here):

We can write $\dot{V} \leq -c_{3} \Vert x\Vert^{2}$, and since $V(x) \leq c_{2}\Vert x\Vert^{2}$, we have $\Vert x\Vert^{2} \geq V/c_{2}$. Substituting:

$$
\dot{V} \leq -\frac{c_{3}}{c_{2}} V
$$

**Step 4: Recognize this as a linear scalar differential inequality.**

$$
\dot{V} + \frac{c_{3}}{c_{2}} V \leq 0
$$

**Step 5: Apply the integrating factor $e^{(c_{3}/c_{2})t}$.**

Multiplying both sides:

$$
e^{(c_{3}/c_{2})t} \dot{V} + \frac{c_{3}}{c_{2}} e^{(c_{3}/c_{2})t} V \leq 0
$$

The left-hand side is exactly the derivative of $e^{(c_{3}/c_{2})t} V$:

$$
\frac{d}{dt}\left[e^{(c_{3}/c_{2})t} V(x(t))\right] \leq 0
$$

**Step 6: Integrate both sides from $0$ to $t$.**

$$
e^{(c_{3}/c_{2})t} V(x(t)) - V(x(0)) \leq 0
$$

Rearranging:

$$
V(x(t)) \leq V(x(0)) \, e^{-(c_{3}/c_{2})t}
$$

**Step 7: Apply the upper bound on $V(x(t))$ from condition (1): $V(x(t)) \geq c_{1}\Vert x(t)\Vert^{2}$.**

$$
c_{1} \Vert x(t)\Vert^{2} \leq V(x(0)) \, e^{-(c_{3}/c_{2})t}
$$

**Step 8: Apply the upper bound on $V(x(0))$ from condition (1): $V(x(0)) \leq c_{2}\Vert x(0)\Vert^{2}$.**

$$
c_{1} \Vert x(t)\Vert^{2} \leq c_{2} \Vert x(0)\Vert^{2} \, e^{-(c_{3}/c_{2})t}
$$

**Step 9: Divide by $c_{1}$ and take the square root.**

$$
\Vert x(t)\Vert^{2} \leq \frac{c_{2}}{c_{1}} \Vert x(0)\Vert^{2} \, e^{-(c_{3}/c_{2})t}
$$

$$
\Vert x(t)\Vert \leq \sqrt{\frac{c_{2}}{c_{1}}} \, \Vert x(0)\Vert \, e^{-(c_{3}/2c_{2})t}
$$

**Step 10: Compare with the definition of exponential stability.**

We obtain the canonical exponential stability bound:

$$
\boxed{\Vert x(t)\Vert \leq \alpha \, \Vert x(0)\Vert \, e^{-\beta t}, \quad \alpha = \sqrt{\frac{c_{2}}{c_{1}}}, \quad \beta = \frac{c_{3}}{2c_{2}}}
$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Conclusion**: A system possessing a Lyapunov function satisfying the **bounded-from-below**, **bounded-from-above**, and **strict negative derivative** conditions is **exponentially stable** with explicit decay rate $\beta = c_3 / (2c_2)$.

---

## 3.2 Analytical Matrix: Classification of Functions

| Function $V(x)$ | $V(0)$ | Sign of $V(x)$ for $x \neq 0$ | Classification | $\dot{V}$ along trajectory | Conclusion |
|----------------|---------|------------------------------|----------------|---------------------------|------------|
| $V = x_1^2 + x_2^2$ | $0$ | $>0$ | Positive Definite | $\dot{V} = -2x_1^2 - 2x_2^2 < 0$ | Asymptotically Stable |
| $V = (x_1 + x_2)^2$ | $0$ | $\geq 0$ | Positive Semi-Definite | $\dot{V} = -2(x_1+x_2)^2 \leq 0$ | Stable (not asymptotic) |
| $V = x_1^2$ | $0$ | $\geq 0$ | Positive Semi-Definite | $\dot{V} = -2x_1^2 \leq 0$ | Stable (not asymptotic) |
| $V = -x_1^2 - x_2^2$ | $0$ | $<0$ | Negative Definite | $\dot{V} = +2x_1^2 + 2x_2^2 > 0$ | Unstable |
| $V = x_1 x_2$ | $0$ | Mixed signs | Indefinite | Cannot conclude | Inconclusive |

---

## 3.3 Worked Numerical Example (KTU-Style)

**Problem**: Consider the system $\dot{x} = -x^3$, $x \in \mathbb{R}$. Show that the origin is asymptotically stable.

**Step 1**: Verify $x^{*} = 0$ is an equilibrium: $f(0) = -(0)^3 = 0$. ✓

**Step 2**: Choose Lyapunov function candidate: $V(x) = \dfrac{1}{2} x^2$.

**Step 3**: Check positive definiteness:
- $V(0) = 0$ ✓
- $V(x) = \dfrac{1}{2}x^2 > 0$ for all $x \neq 0$ ✓
- Hence $V(x)$ is **positive definite**.

**Step 4**: Compute the time derivative:

$$
\dot{V}(x) = x \cdot \dot{x} = x \cdot (-x^3) = -x^{4}
$$

**Step 5**: Check sign of $\dot{V}(x)$:

- $\dot{V}(x) = -x^4 \leq 0$ for all $x$.
- $\dot{V}(x) = -x^4 < 0$ for all $x \neq 0$.
- Hence $\dot{V}(x)$ is **negative definite** (not just semi-definite!).

**Step 6**: Apply Lyapunov's theorem:

Since $V(x)$ is positive definite and $\dot{V}(x)$ is negative definite, the equilibrium point $x = 0$ is **asymptotically stable** (in fact, globally asymptotically stable, since the analysis holds for all $x \in \mathbb{R}$).

**Step 7 (Optional — Exponential Bound)**: Using the derivation in §3.1:

Here $V = \tfrac{1}{2}x^2$, so $c_1 = c_2 = \tfrac{1}{2}$. Also $\dot{V} = -x^4 = -2(V^2/(\tfrac{1}{2})^2 \cdot \tfrac{1}{4}) = $ ... a direct application gives:

$$
|x(t)| \leq \frac{|x(0)|}{\sqrt{1 + 2x(0)^2 t}}
$$

which shows $x(t) \to 0$ as $t \to \infty$, confirming asymptotic stability (though not exponential in the strict sense here because the decay is algebraic, not exponential).

# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question A1: `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`

**Q: Define stability in the sense of Lyapunov for an autonomous non-linear system. State the mathematical condition using $\varepsilon - \delta$ notation.**

**Model Answer:**

For the autonomous system $\dot{x}(t) = f(x(t))$ with equilibrium at $x = 0$, the origin is said to be **stable in the sense of Lyapunov** if for every $\varepsilon > 0$, there exists a $\delta(\varepsilon) > 0$ such that:

$$
\|x(0)\| < \delta \quad \Longrightarrow \quad \|x(t)\| < \varepsilon, \quad \forall \, t \geq 0
$$

This means a trajectory starting sufficiently close to the equilibrium (within the $\delta$-ball) remains arbitrarily close (within the $\varepsilon$-ball) for **all future time**, without necessarily converging to the origin.

---

### Question A2: `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`

**Q: Differentiate between Lyapunov stability and asymptotic stability. Define a positive definite function with a suitable example.**

**Model Answer:**

| Aspect | Lyapunov Stable | Asymptotically Stable |
|--------|---------------|----------------------|
| Convergence | Trajectory stays near origin | Trajectory returns to origin |
| Mathematical Condition | $\varepsilon - \delta$ only | $\varepsilon - \delta$ plus $\lim_{t \to \infty} \|x(t)\| = 0$ |
| Strength | Weaker | Stronger (implies Lyapunov stable) |

**Positive Definite Function Example:**

A scalar function $V(x) : \mathbb{R}^n \to \mathbb{R}$ is **positive definite** if $V(0) = 0$ and $V(x) > 0$ for all $x \neq 0$ in some domain $D$ containing the origin.

**Example**: $V(x_1, x_2) = x_1^2 + 2x_2^2$

- $V(0,0) = 0$ ✓
- $V(x_1,x_2) = x_1^2 + 2x_2^2 > 0$ for any $(x_1, x_2) \neq (0,0)$ ✓
- Hence $V$ is positive definite.

---

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each — Module Internal Choice)

### Question A: `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023, Modified for 2024 Scheme]`

**(a) [7 Marks]** Define the following terms with appropriate mathematical expressions:
   (i) Autonomous system
   (ii) Equilibrium point
   (iii) Asymptotic stability
   (iv) Positive definite function

**(b) [7 Marks]** For the system $\dot{x}_1 = -x_1 + x_1 x_2$, $\dot{x}_2 = -x_2$, determine the equilibrium points and analyze the stability of each using the Lyapunov function $V(x_1, x_2) = x_1^2 + x_2^2$.

---

#### Model Solution to Question A

**Part (a) — Definitions:**

**(i) Autonomous System:** A system whose dynamics do not depend explicitly on time is called autonomous. Mathematically:

$$
\dot{x}(t) = f(x(t))
$$

There is no explicit $t$ term on the right-hand side.

**(ii) Equilibrium Point:** A state $x^{*} \in \mathbb{R}^n$ is called an equilibrium point if $f(x^{*}) = 0$, i.e., once the system reaches $x^{*}$, it remains there for all future time.

**(iii) Asymptotic Stability:** The equilibrium point $x = 0$ is asymptotically stable if:

(a) It is stable in the sense of Lyapunov, AND

(b) $\lim\limits_{t \to \infty} \|x(t)\| = 0$ for all $x(0)$ sufficiently close to the origin.

**(iv) Positive Definite Function:** A scalar function $V(x)$ is positive definite if $V(0) = 0$ and $V(x) > 0$ for all $x \neq 0$ in a domain $D$ containing the origin.

---

**Part (b) — Stability Analysis:**

**Step 1: Find equilibrium points.**

Setting $\dot{x}_1 = 0$ and $\dot{x}_2 = 0$:

From $\dot{x}_2 = -x_2 = 0 \Rightarrow x_2 = 0$.

From $\dot{x}_1 = -x_1 + x_1 x_2 = -x_1(1 - x_2) = 0$: with $x_2 = 0$, we get $-x_1 = 0 \Rightarrow x_1 = 0$.

**Unique equilibrium: $(x_1^{*}, x_2^{*}) = (0, 0)$.**

**Step 2: Shift coordinates** (already at origin): $z_1 = x_1$, $z_2 = x_2$.

**Step 3: Verify positive definiteness of $V$:**

$V(0,0) = 0$ ✓

$V(x_1, x_2) = x_1^2 + x_2^2 > 0$ for any $(x_1, x_2) \neq (0,0)$ ✓

Hence $V$ is **positive definite**.

**Step 4: Compute $\dot{V}$ along trajectories:**

$$
\dot{V} = \frac{\partial V}{\partial x_1} \dot{x}_1 + \frac{\partial V}{\partial x_2} \dot{x}_2
$$

$$
\dot{V} = 2x_1(-x_1 + x_1 x_2) + 2x_2(-x_2)
$$

$$
\dot{V} = -2x_1^2 + 2x_1^2 x_2 - 2x_2^2
$$

$$
\dot{V} = -2x_1^2(1 - x_2) - 2x_2^2
$$

**Step 5: Analyze the sign of $\dot{V}$.**

In the domain $|x_2| < 1$, we have $(1 - x_2) > 0$, so:

- $-2x_1^2(1-x_2) \leq 0$ (since $x_1^2 \geq 0$ and $1-x_2 > 0$)
- $-2x_2^2 \leq 0$

Therefore:

$$
\dot{V}(x_1, x_2) = -2x_1^2(1 - x_2) - 2x_2^2 \leq 0 \text{ for } |x_2| < 1
$$

with strict inequality for $(x_1, x_2) \neq (0, 0)$.

**Step 6: Apply Lyapunov's theorem.**

Since $V$ is positive definite and $\dot{V}$ is negative definite (locally for $|x_2| < 1$), the origin is **asymptotically stable** in the region $|x_2| < 1$ (a subset of the state space).

The **region of attraction** includes all states with $|x_2| < 1$.

---

### Question B: `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`

**(a) [7 Marks]** Define the following and give one example each:
   (i) Negative definite function
   (ii) Negative semi-definite function
   (iii) Indefinite function

**(b) [7 Marks]** State and prove Lyapunov's stability theorem for asymptotic stability. Use it to show that the system $\dot{x}_1 = x_2$, $\dot{x}_2 = -x_1 - x_2$ is asymptotically stable by choosing an appropriate Lyapunov function.

---

#### Model Solution to Question B

**Part (a) — Definitions:**

**(i) Negative Definite Function:** $V(x)$ is negative definite if $-V(x)$ is positive definite, i.e., $V(0) = 0$ and $V(x) < 0$ for all $x \neq 0$ in $D$.

**Example**: $V(x_1, x_2) = -x_1^2 - x_2^2$. Then $-V = x_1^2 + x_2^2$ is positive definite, so $V$ is negative definite.

**(ii) Negative Semi-Definite Function:** $V(x)$ is negative semi-definite if $V(0) = 0$ and $V(x) \leq 0$ for all $x \neq 0$ in $D$.

**Example**: $V(x_1, x_2) = -x_1^2$. Then $V \leq 0$ everywhere (with equality on the $x_2$-axis), so $V$ is negative semi-definite.

**(iii) Indefinite Function:** $V(x)$ is indefinite if it takes both positive and negative values in every neighbourhood of the origin.

**Example**: $V(x_1, x_2) = x_1 x_2$. In any neighborhood of the origin, this function is positive (e.g., at $(1,1)$) and negative (e.g., at $(1,-1)$), so $V$ is indefinite.

---

**Part (b) — Lyapunov's Theorem and Application:**

**Theorem (Lyapunov's Theorem for Asymptotic Stability):**

*Let $x = 0$ be an equilibrium point of $\dot{x} = f(x)$. Let $V: D \to \mathbb{R}$ be a continuously differentiable function in a domain $D$ containing the origin, such that:*

*(i) $V(0) = 0$ and $V(x) > 0$ for all $x \neq 0$ in $D$ (V is positive definite).*

*(ii) $\dot{V}(x) = \dfrac{\partial V}{\partial x} f(x) < 0$ for all $x \neq 0$ in $D$ ($\dot{V}$ is negative definite).*

*Then the origin is **asymptotically stable**.*

**Proof Sketch:**

(1) Since $V$ is positive definite and continuous with $V(0)=0$, for any $\varepsilon > 0$ defining the ball $B_\varepsilon$, the minimum of $V$ on the boundary $\partial B_\varepsilon$ is some $m > 0$. Choose $\delta$ such that the ball $B_\delta$ is contained in $\{x : V(x) < m\}$. Then any trajectory starting in $B_\delta$ has $V(x(t)) \leq V(x(0)) < m$, hence $\|x(t)\| < \varepsilon$ for all $t \geq 0$ — establishing **Lyapunov stability**.

(2) Since $\dot{V} < 0$ along trajectories (and bounded away from zero on compact subsets avoiding the origin), $V(x(t))$ is strictly decreasing. Since $V \geq 0$, it must converge to a limit $L \geq 0$. By invariance arguments (using the fact that $\dot{V}$ cannot remain bounded away from zero on any trajectory), one shows $L = 0$, hence $x(t) \to 0$.

**Application to the given system:**

**Step 1:** Choose the Lyapunov function candidate:

$$
V(x_1, x_2) = x_1^2 + x_2^2
$$

**Step 2:** Verify positive definiteness: $V(0,0) = 0$, $V(x_1, x_2) > 0$ for $(x_1, x_2) \neq (0,0)$ ✓

**Step 3:** Compute $\dot{V}$:

$$
\dot{V} = 2x_1 \dot{x}_1 + 2x_2 \dot{x}_2
$$

$$
\dot{V} = 2x_1(x_2) + 2x_2(-x_1 - x_2)
$$

$$
\dot{V} = 2x_1 x_2 - 2x_1 x_2 - 2x_2^2
$$

$$
\dot{V} = -2x_2^2
$$

**Step 4:** Check sign: $\dot{V} = -2x_2^2 \leq 0$ for all $(x_1, x_2)$, with equality on the $x_1$-axis.

So $\dot{V}$ is **negative semi-definite**.

**Step 5:** Apply LaSalle's Invariance Principle (extension of Lyapunov's theorem):

To strengthen the conclusion to asymptotic stability, we use LaSalle's theorem: the largest invariant set contained in $\{x : \dot{V}(x) = 0\} = \{x_2 = 0\}$ is the origin itself (since on $x_2 = 0$, the dynamics reduce to $\dot{x}_1 = 0$, $\dot{x}_2 = 0$, so trajectories starting on the $x_1$-axis remain on it — but they can only remain there if they are already at the origin).

Hence, by **LaSalle's Invariance Principle**, the origin is **asymptotically stable** (in fact globally asymptotically stable, since $V$ is radially unbounded).

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout:**
> 
> ⚠️ **Common Mistake #1**: Students often write $\dot{V} = -2x_2^2 \leq 0$ and **incorrectly** claim this is negative definite. It is **negative semi-definite**, not negative definite! Direct application of standard Lyapunov theorem gives only stability, not asymptotic stability. Students must invoke **LaSalle's Invariance Principle** for asymptotic stability in such cases.
> 
> ⚠️ **Common Mistake #2**: Forgetting to verify $V(0) = 0$ explicitly. Even if $V(x) > 0$ for $x \neq 0$, omitting $V(0)=0$ loses full marks.
> 
> ⚠️ **Common Mistake #3**: Confusing "negative semi-definite" with "negative definite" — this costs 1-2 marks per occurrence.
> 
> ⚠️ **Common Mistake #4**: Not specifying the **region of attraction** (local vs. global) when the Lyapunov function is only locally valid.
> 
> ⚠️ **Common Mistake #5**: In exponential stability derivations, mixing up $c_1, c_2, c_3$ subscripts. Use the mnemonic: **c1 ≤ c2** (lower bound ≤ upper bound), **c3 > 0** is the decay rate coefficient.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!NOTE]
> **High-Density Rapid-Revision Checklist — Definitions Module 3**

### Core Concepts (Must Memorize)

- [x] **Autonomous system**: $\dot{x} = f(x)$, no explicit time dependence.
- [x] **Equilibrium point**: $x^{*}$ satisfying $f(x^{*}) = 0$.
- [x] **Lyapunov stable (LSL)**: $\varepsilon - \delta$ condition; trajectory stays close.
- [x] **Asymptotic stability**: LSL + $\lim_{t \to \infty} \|x(t)\| = 0$.
- [x] **Exponential stability**: $\|x(t)\| \leq \alpha \|x(0)\| e^{-\beta t}$.
- [x] **Instability**: Trajectory escapes any $\varepsilon$-ball.

### Function Classifications

- [x] **PD**: $V(0) = 0$, $V(x) > 0$ for $x \neq 0$.
- [x] **PSD**: $V(0) = 0$, $V(x) \geq 0$ for $x \neq 0$.
- [x] **ND**: $-V$ is PD.
- [x] **NSD**: $-V$ is PSD.
- [x] **Indefinite**: $V$ takes both positive and negative values in any neighborhood.

### Key Lyapunov Theorem (Direct Method)

- [x] **Stability**: $V$ PD + $\dot{V}$ NSD ⟹ Stable in sense of Lyapunov.
- [x] **Asymptotic Stability**: $V$ PD + $\dot{V}$ ND ⟹ Asymptotically stable.
- [x] **Instability (Chetaev's)**: $V$ with indefinite $\dot{V}$ ⟹ Possibly unstable.

### Critical Distinctions

- [x] Exponential ⟹ Asymptotic ⟹ Lyapunov (strict hierarchy, one-way).
- [x] ND vs NSD: ND is strict inequality; NSD allows zeros.
- [x] Local vs Global: depends on domain $D$ and behavior of $V$.

### Exam Quick-Fire Formulas

- [x] $\dot{V} = \nabla V \cdot f(x) = \sum_{i=1}^{n} \dfrac{\partial V}{\partial x_i} f_i(x)$
- [x] Quadratic form PD ⟺ all eigenvalues of $P > 0$ (Sylvester's criterion).
- [x] Exponential bound: $\alpha = \sqrt{c_2/c_1}$, $\beta = c_3/(2c_2)$.

### Common Lyapunov Function Choices

- [x] $V = x^T P x$ for quadratic systems.
- [x] $V = \frac{1}{2}(x_1^2 + x_2^2)$ — energy-like.
- [x] $V = $ sum of squared states — generic candidate.

### Pitfalls to Avoid (Exam Safety)

- [x] Never claim asymptotic stability from semi-definite $\dot{V}$ without LaSalle.
- [x] Always verify $V(0) = 0$ explicitly.
- [x] Always state the region of attraction.
- [x] Distinguish local from global results.
- [x] Check that $V$ is $C^1$ (continuously differentiable) before applying theorems.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Describing Function Analysis: Describing Functions of Typical Nonlinearities: Ideal Relay
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PERAT634 - NON-LINEAR CONTROL
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Describing Function Analysis: Describing Functions of Typical Nonlinearities: Ideal Relay

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1 — Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Describing Function (DF): Formal Definition

> [!NOTE]
> **Describing Function (N):** A quasi-linear approximation in which a sinusoidal input $x(t)=A\sin(\omega t)$ applied to a memoryless (or weakly memory) nonlinear element is replaced by its **fundamental Fourier component** of the output. The describing function is defined as the complex ratio of the fundamental output phasor to the input sinusoid amplitude.

Mathematically, for a nonlinear element $N(\cdot)$ with input $x(t)=A\sin(\omega t)$ and periodic output $y(t)$:

$$N(A,\omega)=\frac{Y_1}{A}\angle \phi_1=\frac{1}{A}\Big[\underbrace{\frac{1}{\pi}\!\int_{0}^{2\pi}\! y(\theta)\cos\theta\, d\theta}_{A_1}+j\underbrace{\frac{1}{\pi}\!\int_{0}^{2\pi}\! y(\theta)\sin\theta\, d\theta}_{B_1}\Big]$$

where $\theta=\omega t$, and $(A_1,B_1)$ are the first-harmonic Fourier coefficients of the output.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight:** The describing function is the *engineer's harmonic balance tool* — it lets us apply Nyquist/Bode ideas to nonlinear systems by replacing the nonlinear block with a *gain* $N(A)$ that depends on the **signal amplitude** $A$.

---

## 1.2 The Ideal Relay — Definition

> [!NOTE]
> **Ideal Relay (Two-Position / On-Off Relay):** A memoryless, single-valued, odd-symmetric nonlinearity whose output instantaneously switches between two constant levels $+M$ and $-M$ the instant the input crosses zero. There is **no dead zone, no hysteresis, and no saturation**.

The input–output law is the **signum** function:

$$y(t)=M\cdot \operatorname{sgn}\{x(t)\}=\begin{cases}+M, & x(t)>0\\[2pt] 0, & x(t)=0\\[2pt] -M, & x(t)<0\end{cases}$$

The magnitude $M$ is the relay output level (a real, positive constant with units of the controlled variable, e.g., **volts** for an electrical relay or **N·m** for a torque relay).

---

## 1.3 Conceptual Analogy — Plain English Intuition

> [!TIP]
> **Light-Switch Analogy:** Think of the ideal relay as a *wall light switch with a spring return*. Push the lever right (input positive) → bulb glows at *full brightness $+M$*. Push the lever left (input negative) → bulb glows at *full reverse brightness $-M$*. The bulb is **never dim, never off**; it is **either fully ON or fully REVERSED**. The moment your finger crosses the centerline, the bulb flips — that is the *zero-crossing switching* behaviour.

For a sinusoidal push (your finger moving back and forth smoothly), the bulb produces a **square wave** of light, because the switch can only occupy two positions. Describing-function analysis says: *"Forget the higher-order wiggles of that square wave; just keep the first sine-shaped piece of it"* — and that single sine is the **fundamental** we use to study the whole system.

---

## 1.4 Why the Ideal Relay Matters in KTU Examinations

> [!WARNING]
> The ideal relay is the **canonical building block** for deriving the describing function of *all* two-position relays. Master the derivation of $N(A)=\dfrac{4M}{\pi A}$, and the other relays (dead-zone, hysteresis, saturation) become routine extensions. KTU examiners routinely test:
> 1. The derivation (7 marks).
> 2. The graphical $-1/N(A)$ locus and prediction of limit cycles (7 marks).
> 3. Comparison of describing functions across nonlinearities (3-mark short notes).

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2 — Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Operational Logic of Describing Function Analysis — Step-by-Step

The harmonic-balance / describing-function method reduces a closed-loop nonlinear system to an equivalent quasi-linear feedback problem. The logic is:

- **Step 1 — Assume a periodic solution.** Postulate that at the relay input, $x(t)=A\sin(\omega t)$ exists as a sustained oscillation.
- **Step 2 — Compute the relay output $y(t)$.** Substitute the assumed sinusoid into the relay law $y=M\,\mathrm{sgn}(x)$ to obtain a square wave of amplitude $\pm M$.
- **Step 3 — Extract the fundamental component.** Use Fourier series; the square wave has $A_1=0$ (odd symmetry) and $B_1=\dfrac{4M}{\pi}$.
- **Step 4 — Form the describing function.** $N(A)=B_1/A=\dfrac{4M}{\pi A}$. This is a **real, amplitude-dependent, frequency-independent** gain.
- **Step 5 — Apply the harmonic-balance (limit-cycle) condition** $1+N(A)G(j\omega)=0$, equivalently $-\dfrac{1}{N(A)}=G(j\omega)$.
- **Step 6 — Solve graphically/analytically** for the intersection of the **critical locus** $G(j\omega)$ (Nyquist of the linear part) with the **relay locus** $-1/N(A)$ on the real axis.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Why "real and frequency-independent"?** Because the ideal relay is **memoryless** (output at any instant depends only on the *current* input, not its history or rate) and **single-valued** (no input ambiguity). Memoryless $\Rightarrow$ no phase lag; single-valued $\Rightarrow$ the imaginary part $A_1$ vanishes, leaving only the real gain $B_1/A$.

---

## 2.2 KTU Formula Sheet — Describing Functions of Common Memoryless Nonlinearities

> [!IMPORTANT]
> Memorize the **column "Describing Function $N(A)$"** and the **"Onset / Domain"** column. KTU problems almost always restrict to $A \ge \Delta$ or $A \ge a$, etc.

| # | Nonlinearity | Input-Output Relation | Describing Function $N(A)$ | Domain of Validity |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | **Ideal Relay** | $y = M\cdot\mathrm{sgn}(x)$ | $N(A) = \dfrac{4M}{\pi A}$ | $A > 0$ |
| 2 | Relay with Dead Zone | $y = M\cdot\mathrm{sgn}(x)$ if $\vert x \vert > \Delta$, else $0$ | $N(A) = \dfrac{4M}{\pi A}\sqrt{1-\left(\dfrac{\Delta}{A}\right)^{2}}$ | $A \ge \Delta$ |
| 3 | Relay with Hysteresis | $y = +M$ for $x \ge h$, $-M$ for $x \le -h$ | $N(A) = \dfrac{4M}{\pi A}\!\left[\sqrt{1-\left(\dfrac{h}{A}\right)^{2}}-j\dfrac{h}{A}\right]$ | $A \ge h$ |
| 4 | Saturation | $y = kx$ for $\vert x \vert \le a$, $= k a\cdot \mathrm{sgn}(x)$ for $\vert x \vert > a$ | $N(A)=\dfrac{2k}{\pi}\!\left[\sin^{-1}\!\dfrac{a}{A}+\dfrac{a}{A}\sqrt{1-\left(\dfrac{a}{A}\right)^{2}}\right]$ | $A \ge a$ |
| 5 | Dead Zone | $y=k(x-\Delta\cdot \mathrm{sgn}(x))$ for $\vert x \vert > \Delta$, else $0$ | $N(A)=k-\dfrac{2k}{\pi}\!\left[\sin^{-1}\!\dfrac{\Delta}{A}+\dfrac{\Delta}{A}\sqrt{1-\left(\dfrac{\Delta}{A}\right)^{2}}\right]$ | $A \ge \Delta$ |
| 6 | Coulomb Friction | $y = b\cdot \mathrm{sgn}(\dot x)$ | $N(A)=\dfrac{4b}{\pi A}$ | $A > 0$ |
| 7 | Quantizer ($q$-step) | Piecewise constant $\pm M_i$ | $N(A)=\dfrac{2}{\pi A}\!\sum_{i} \Delta M_i\cos\theta_i$ | piecewise |

> [!NOTE]
> **Sign convention used by KTU textbook style:** $N(A)$ is plotted on the **negative reciprocal** axis as $-1/N(A)$, a real negative number for the ideal relay. As $A \to 0^{+}$, $N(A) \to +\infty$ and $-1/N(A) \to 0^{-}$; as $A \to \infty$, $N(A) \to 0^{+}$ and $-1/N(A) \to -\infty$.

---

## 2.3 Engineering Real-World Utility

> [!TIP]
> The ideal relay's describing function is the workhorse of **limit-cycle prediction** in:
> - **Servo-hydraulic position control** (bang-bang valves).
> - **Thermostatic on-off temperature regulators** (room ACs, incubators).
> - **Aircraft autopilot bang-bang control surfaces** (early designs).
> - **Power-electronics DC–DC converters** with hysteretic (relay) controllers.
> - **Bang-bang time-optimal control** of robotic joints.

Knowing $N(A)=4M/(\pi A)$ allows a control engineer to **predict** the oscillation amplitude and frequency *before* building the prototype, and to **size $M$** to avoid undesired chattering.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3 — Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Exhaustive Derivation of the Ideal-Relay Describing Function

### 3.1.1 Setup

Let the input be a pure sinusoid:

$$x(t)=A\sin(\omega t),\qquad A>0,\ \omega>0$$

The ideal relay's input-output law is:

$$y(t)=\begin{cases}+M, & 0<\omega t<\pi \quad\Longleftrightarrow\quad x>0\\[3pt] -M, & \pi<\omega t<2\pi \quad\Longleftrightarrow\quad x<0\end{cases}$$

The output $y(t)$ is a **symmetric square wave** of amplitude $M$ and period $T=2\pi/\omega$.

### 3.1.2 Computing the Cosine Coefficient $A_1$

The cosine Fourier coefficient is:

$$A_1=\frac{1}{\pi}\int_{0}^{2\pi}y(\theta)\cos\theta\,d\theta,\qquad \theta=\omega t$$

Substituting the piecewise definition:

$$A_1=\frac{1}{\pi}\!\left[\int_{0}^{\pi}(+M)\cos\theta\,d\theta+\int_{\pi}^{2\pi}(-M)\cos\theta\,d\theta\right]$$

Evaluate the first integral:

$$\int_{0}^{\pi}\cos\theta\,d\theta=\big[\sin\theta\big]_{0}^{\pi}=\sin\pi-\sin 0=0$$

Evaluate the second integral:
