$$\int_{\pi}^{2\pi}\cos\theta\,d\theta=\big[\sin\theta\big]_{\pi}^{2\pi}=\sin 2\pi-\sin\pi=0$$

Therefore:

$$A_1=\frac{1}{\pi}\big[M(0)+(-M)(0)\big]=0$$

**Interpretation:** $A_1=0$ because the relay output $y(t)$ is an **odd function of $x$** (or equivalently an odd function of $\theta$ in this symmetric input), and an odd square-wave contains only sine harmonics — no cosine terms.

### 3.1.3 Computing the Sine Coefficient $B_1$

$$B_1=\frac{1}{\pi}\!\left[\int_{0}^{\pi}(+M)\sin\theta\,d\theta+\int_{\pi}^{2\pi}(-M)\sin\theta\,d\theta\right]$$

First integral:

$$\int_{0}^{\pi}\sin\theta\,d\theta=\big[-\cos\theta\big]_{0}^{\pi}=-\cos\pi-(-\cos 0)=1+1=2$$

Second integral:

$$\int_{\pi}^{2\pi}\sin\theta\,d\theta=\big[-\cos\theta\big]_{\pi}^{2\pi}=-\cos 2\pi-(-\cos\pi)=-1-1=-2$$

Substitute back:

$$B_1=\frac{1}{\pi}\big[M(2)+(-M)(-2)\big]=\frac{1}{\pi}(2M+2M)=\frac{4M}{\pi}$$

### 3.1.4 Forming the Describing Function

Since $N(A)=A_1+jB_1$ divided by $A$ (the standard normalisation for single-input DF):

$$\boxed{\;N(A)=\frac{B_1}{A}=\frac{4M}{\pi A}\;}$$

Phase angle: $\phi_1=\tan^{-1}(A_1/B_1)=0^{\circ}$ (real, positive gain).

### 3.1.5 Verification via Fourier Series of Square Wave

A standard result for a symmetric square wave of amplitude $M$ is:

$$y_{\text{sq}}(t)=\frac{4M}{\pi}\!\left[\sin\omega t+\frac{1}{3}\sin 3\omega t+\frac{1}{5}\sin 5\omega t+\dots\right]$$

The **fundamental amplitude** is $\dfrac{4M}{\pi}$, confirming $B_1=\dfrac{4M}{\pi}$ from above and $N(A)=\dfrac{4M/{\pi}}{A}=\dfrac{4M}{\pi A}$. $\;\blacksquare$

---

## 3.2 Analytical Matrix — Ideal Relay vs Other Typical Nonlinearities

The following table consolidates the **core engineering metrics** that KTU expects you to know cold. **Do not** attempt to memorise a wall of text — the **shape** of each $N(A)$ curve is what examiners test.

| Property | **Ideal Relay** | **Relay + Dead Zone** | **Relay + Hysteresis** | **Saturation** | **Dead Zone** |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| Memoryless? | ✅ Yes | ✅ Yes | ❌ No (state-dependent) | ✅ Yes | ✅ Yes |
| Single-valued? | ✅ Yes | ✅ Yes | ❌ No | ✅ Yes | ✅ Yes |
| Odd symmetric? | ✅ Yes | ❌ No | ❌ No | ✅ Yes | ❌ No |
| $A_1$ (cos coef) | $0$ | $0$ | $\dfrac{4Mh}{\pi A^{2}}$ | depends | depends |
| $B_1$ (sin coef) | $\dfrac{4M}{\pi}$ | $\dfrac{4M}{\pi}\sqrt{1-(\Delta/A)^{2}}$ | $\dfrac{4M}{\pi}\sqrt{1-(h/A)^{2}}$ | expression | expression |
| $N(A)$ type | **Real, positive** | **Real, positive** | **Complex** (phase lag) | **Real, positive** | **Real, positive** |
| $N(A)$ as $A\to 0^{+}$ | $+\infty$ | $0$ (silent for $A<\Delta$) | $0$ (silent for $A<h$) | $k$ (linear region) | $0$ (silent) |
| $N(A)$ as $A\to\infty$ | $0$ | $\dfrac{4M}{\pi A}$ | $\dfrac{4M}{\pi A}\angle 0$ | $0$ | $k$ |
| $-1/N(A)$ locus | Real axis, $(-\infty, 0)$ | Real axis, $(-\infty,-\pi\Delta/(4M))$ | Curve in LHP | Real axis segment | Real axis segment |

> [!NOTE]
> **Reading the table for KTU:** The *last* column ($-1/N(A)$ locus) is what you plot against $G(j\omega)$ to find limit cycles. For the **ideal relay**, $-1/N(A)$ travels the **entire negative real axis from $0^{-}$ to $-\infty$ as $A$ grows from $0$ to $\infty$** — making limit-cycle prediction particularly straightforward.

---

## 3.3 Worked Example — Limit-Cycle Prediction with Ideal Relay

> [!TIP]
> **Typical 7-mark KTU Part-B sub-question.** Practice it until you can finish in under 12 minutes.

**Problem.** A unity-feedback system has linear plant $G(s)=\dfrac{10}{s(s+1)(s+2)}$ in cascade with an ideal relay of magnitude $M=1$. Predict whether a limit cycle exists; if so, find its amplitude $A^{*}$ and frequency $\omega^{*}$.

**Step 1 — Write the critical locus.** $G(j\omega)=\dfrac{10}{j\omega(j\omega+1)(j\omega+2)}$.

**Step 2 — Impose the relay locus on the real axis.** Since $-1/N(A)=-\dfrac{\pi A}{4M}=-\dfrac{\pi A}{4}$ is purely real and negative, the intersection must occur where $G(j\omega)$ crosses the negative real axis.

Compute the imaginary part of $G(j\omega)$:

$$G(j\omega)=\frac{10}{j\omega(1+j\omega)(2+j\omega)}=\frac{10}{j\omega(2+3j\omega-\omega^{2})}$$

Multiply numerator and denominator by $j\omega(2+3j\omega-\omega^{2})^{*}$ … alternatively, expand:

$$(j\omega)(1+j\omega)(2+j\omega)=(j\omega)(2+3j\omega-\omega^{2})=3j\omega^{2}+2j\omega+j\omega^{3}$$

Write $G(j\omega)=\dfrac{10}{-\omega^{2}-3\omega^{2}j\cdot \text{stuff}}$ — simpler route: use the standard form $G(j\omega)=\dfrac{10}{-\omega^{2}(3)+j\omega(2-\omega^{2})}$. The imaginary-part-zero condition is:

$$2-\omega^{2}=0\;\Longrightarrow\;\omega^{*}=\sqrt{2}\ \text{rad/s}$$

**Step 3 — Real part at $\omega^{*}=\sqrt{2}$:**

$$\mathrm{Re}\,G(j\sqrt{2})=\frac{10}{-\omega^{2}\cdot 3}=\frac{10}{-3(2)}=-\frac{10}{6}=-\frac{5}{3}$$

**Step 4 — Equate to $-1/N(A)$:**

$$-\frac{\pi A^{*}}{4}=-\frac{5}{3}\;\Longrightarrow\;A^{*}=\frac{20}{3\pi}\approx 2.122$$

**Result.** A **limit cycle exists** with $\omega^{*}=\sqrt{2}\approx 1.414$ rad/s and $A^{*}\approx 2.122$. $\;\blacksquare$

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4 — Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Functional Flow of Describing-Function Analysis for the Ideal Relay

The diagram below traces the **signal-processing pipeline** that converts a sinusoidal input into the describing function $N(A)=4M/(\pi A)$. The pipeline is the conceptual heart of the chapter.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    nodeA[Sinusoidal Input x of t equals A sin omega t] --> nodeB[Pass through Ideal Relay N]
    nodeB --> nodeC[Output y of t equals M times sign of x]
    nodeC --> nodeD[Square wave amplitude M period 2pi by omega]
    nodeD --> nodeE[Apply Fourier Series Expansion]
    nodeE --> nodeF[Fundamental Component Y1 sin omega t]
    nodeF --> nodeG[Compute A1 equals zero and B1 equals 4M by pi]
    nodeG --> nodeH[Form Describing Function NA equals 4M by pi A]
    nodeH --> nodeI[Real, Positive, Amplitude Dependent, Frequency Independent]
    nodeI --> nodeJ[Plot Negative Reciprocal minus 1 by NA on Real Axis]
    nodeJ --> nodeK[Intersect with Linear Part G of j omega]
    nodeK --> nodeL[Limit Cycle Amplitude A star and Frequency omega star]

    style nodeA fill:#e3f2fd,stroke:#0d47a1,color:#000
    style nodeH fill:#fff9c4,stroke:#f57f17,color:#000
    style nodeL fill:#c8e6c9,stroke:#1b5e20,color:#000
```

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Visual-Reasoning Tip:** When asked to *explain the method*, redraw this pipeline from memory. Examiners award 1–2 marks for the **flow** even if a numerical step is missed.

## 4.2 Block-Level Feedback Architecture for Limit-Cycle Search

This block diagram shows how the describing function is **embedded into a closed-loop system** for stability/oscillation analysis.

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    nodeR[Reference Input R of s] --> nodeSum[Summing Junction with negative feedback]
    nodeSum --> nodeE[Error E of s]
    nodeE --> nodeG[Linear Plant G of s]
    nodeG --> nodeY[Plant Output Y of s]
    nodeY --> nodeFb[Feedback Path to Summing Junction]
    nodeY --> nodeN[Nonlinear Block Ideal Relay]
    nodeN --> nodeDF[Describing Function N of A]
    nodeDF --> nodeLimit[Limit Cycle Condition 1 plus N times G equals 0]
    nodeG --> nodeGnyq[Compute G of j omega Nyquist plot]
    nodeGnyq --> nodeLimit

    style nodeDF fill:#fff9c4,stroke:#f57f17,color:#000
    style nodeLimit fill:#c8e6c9,stroke:#1b5e20,color:#000
    style nodeN fill:#ffcdd2,stroke:#b71c1c,color:#000
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Block-diagram interpretation:** Once the relay is replaced by the quasi-linear gain $N(A)$, the closed-loop system is **linear in $A$** for a *fixed* amplitude. The classical Nyquist criterion is then re-applied: **encirclements of the point $-1/N(A)$** determine stability. If $-1/N(A)$ lies *on* the Nyquist plot, a **limit cycle** of that amplitude and corresponding frequency exists.

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5 — KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short-Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Q1. **[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]** Define the term *Describing Function*. State the describing function of an ideal relay.

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

> [!NOTE]
> **Definition (1.5 marks):** A Describing Function is a **quasi-linear approximation** of a nonlinear element in which a sinusoidal input $x(t)=A\sin\omega t$ is replaced by an equivalent gain $N(A)$ equal to the complex ratio of the **fundamental component of the output** to the input amplitude $A$.

**State (1.5 marks):** For an ideal relay of magnitude $M$:

$$N(A)=\frac{4M}{\pi A},\qquad A>0$$

It is **real, positive, frequency-independent**, and tends to $+\infty$ as $A\to 0^{+}$ and to $0^{+}$ as $A\to\infty$.

---

### Q2. **[KTU University Exam — May 2024]** What are the assumptions under which the Describing Function method gives a valid prediction of limit cycles?

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

> [!IMPORTANT]
> 1. **Filtering hypothesis (1 mark):** The linear part $G(s)$ is **low-pass**, attenuating all harmonics above the fundamental by a factor of at least 10:1. Only the first harmonic feeds back meaningfully.
> 2. **Single sinusoid at the nonlinearity input (1 mark):** The input to the nonlinear block is assumed to be a pure sinusoid $A\sin\omega t$ (no DC bias, no sub-harmonics).
> 3. **Memoryless or weakly-memoryless nonlinearity (1 mark):** The output depends only on the present input (and possibly its rate in special cases like hysteresis). For the ideal relay, the assumption is satisfied exactly.

---

## 5.2 Part B — Module 3 Internal Choice (14 Marks Each)

### QUESTION A (14 Marks) — *Derivation + Limit Cycle*

#### **(a) [7 marks]** Derive the describing function of an ideal relay with output magnitude $M$. Sketch the $-1/N(A)$ locus.

**Model Solution:**

**Step 1 (1 mark).** Let $x(t)=A\sin\omega t$. The relay output is $y(t)=M\,\mathrm{sgn}\{x(t)\}$, a square wave taking values $\pm M$ on the half-periods $0<\omega t<\pi$ and $\pi<\omega t<2\pi$.

**Step 2 (2 marks).** Cosine Fourier coefficient:

$$A_1=\frac{1}{\pi}\!\int_{0}^{2\pi}y(\theta)\cos\theta\,d\theta=\frac{M}{\pi}\!\left[\int_{0}^{\pi}\cos\theta\,d\theta-\int_{\pi}^{2\pi}\cos\theta\,d\theta\right]=0$$

**Step 3 (2 marks).** Sine Fourier coefficient:

$$B_1=\frac{1}{\pi}\!\int_{0}^{2\pi}y(\theta)\sin\theta\,d\theta=\frac{M}{\pi}\!\left[\int_{0}^{\pi}\sin\theta\,d\theta-\int_{\pi}^{2\pi}\sin\theta\,d\theta\right]=\frac{4M}{\pi}$$

**Step 4 (1 mark).** Describing function:

$$N(A)=\frac{B_1}{A}=\frac{4M}{\pi A}$$

**Step 5 (1 mark).** $-1/N(A)=-\dfrac{\pi A}{4M}$ — a real, negative number. As $A$ increases from $0$ to $\infty$, $-1/N(A)$ moves from $0^{-}$ leftward to $-\infty$ along the **real axis of the $G$-plane**.

**Sketch (extra credit if included):**

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    nodeA0[Origin 0] --> nodeA1[Negative Real Axis Leftward]
    nodeA1 --> nodeA2[minus 1 by N of A travels from 0 minus to minus infinity]
    nodeA2 --> nodeA3[As amplitude A grows from 0 to infinity]
    nodeA0 --> nodeA4[Imaginary Axis Up and Down]
    nodeA4 --> nodeA5[No imaginary part for ideal relay]

    style nodeA2 fill:#c8e6c9,stroke:#1b5e20,color:#000
```

---

#### **(b) [7 marks]** A unity-feedback control system has linear transfer function $G(s)=\dfrac{20}{s(s+1)(s+5)}$ in cascade with an ideal relay of magnitude $M=2$. Using the describing-function method, determine the amplitude $A^{*}$ and frequency $\omega^{*}$ of the limit cycle, if any.

**Model Solution:**

**Step 1 (1 mark).** The harmonic-balance condition for limit cycle is $G(j\omega)=-1/N(A)=-\pi A/(4M)=-\pi A/8$.

**Step 2 (2 marks).** Expand the denominator:

$$(j\omega)(j\omega+1)(j\omega+5)=j\omega\big(5+6j\omega-\omega^{2}\big)=j\omega(5-\omega^{2})+j^{2}\omega(6\omega)$$

$$= -6\omega^{2}+j\omega(5-\omega^{2})$$

So:

$$G(j\omega)=\frac{20}{-6\omega^{2}+j\omega(5-\omega^{2})}$$

**Step 3 (1 mark).** Imaginary part vanishes when $5-\omega^{2}=0\Rightarrow\omega^{*}=\sqrt{5}$ rad/s.

**Step 4 (1 mark).** Real part at $\omega^{*}=\sqrt{5}$:

$$\mathrm{Re}\,G(j\sqrt{5})=\frac{20}{-6(5)}=\frac{20}{-30}=-\frac{2}{3}$$

**Step 5 (2 marks).** Equating:

$$-\frac{\pi A^{*}}{8}=-\frac{2}{3}\;\Longrightarrow\;A^{*}=\frac{16}{3\pi}\approx 1.698$$

**Conclusion (extra credit).** A **stable limit cycle** exists with $\omega^{*}=\sqrt{5}\approx 2.236$ rad/s and $A^{*}\approx 1.698$.

> [!WARNING]
> **Examiner's Pitfall Callout:** Students often **forget to multiply** $M$ correctly inside $-1/N(A)=-\pi A/(4M)$. With $M=2$, the denominator becomes $4M=8$, not $4$. **Loses 2 marks** if missed.

---

### QUESTION B (14 Marks) — *Comparison + Composite Non-linearity*

#### **(a) [7 marks]** Compare the describing functions of the **ideal relay** and the **relay with dead zone** (width $2\Delta$). Sketch $-1/N(A)$ for both on the same axes.

**Model Solution:**

**Step 1 (2 marks).** Ideal relay $N_{\text{ideal}}(A)=\dfrac{4M}{\pi A}$, valid for all $A>0$.

**Step 2 (3 marks).** Relay with dead zone — derive the sine coefficient. The output is zero for $\vert x \vert \le \Delta$, i.e. for $\theta \in [0,\theta_1]\cup[2\pi-\theta_1,2\pi]$ where $\theta_1=\sin^{-1}(\Delta/A)$. The output is $+M$ for $\theta_1<\theta<\pi-\theta_1$ and $-M$ for $\pi+\theta_1<\theta<2\pi-\theta_1$. By symmetry $A_1=0$ and:

$$B_1=\frac{4M}{\pi}\sqrt{1-\left(\frac{\Delta}{A}\right)^{2}},\qquad A\ge\Delta$$

Hence:

$$N_{\text{dz}}(A)=\frac{4M}{\pi A}\sqrt{1-\left(\frac{\Delta}{A}\right)^{2}},\qquad A\ge\Delta$$

and $N_{\text{dz}}(A)=0$ for $A<\Delta$ (the relay is silent).

**Step 3 (2 marks).** Comparison table:

| Property | Ideal Relay | Dead-Zone Relay |
|---|---|---|
| Fires for any $A>0$? | ✅ Yes | ❌ No, requires $A\ge\Delta$ |
| $N(A)$ at $A=\Delta$ | $\dfrac{4M}{\pi \Delta}$ | $0$ |
| Asymptote as $A\to\infty$ | $\dfrac{4M}{\pi A}\to 0$ | $\dfrac{4M}{\pi A}\to 0$ |
| $-1/N(A)$ domain | $(-\infty,0)$ | $(-\infty,-\pi\Delta/(4M)]$ |

**Sketch description:** Both curves lie on the negative real axis. The ideal relay's $-1/N$ covers the **entire** negative axis from $0^{-}$ to $-\infty$. The dead-zone relay's $-1/N$ covers only $(-\infty,-\pi\Delta/(4M)]$ — a *bounded subset* starting from a finite negative value.

> [!WARNING]
> **Examiner's Pitfall Callout:** Many students plot $-1/N(A)$ on the **positive** real axis. Always remember the **negative reciprocal**: $N(A)>0 \Rightarrow -1/N(A)<0$. **Loses 1 mark** for sign error.

---

#### **(b) [7 marks]** For the system $G(s)=\dfrac{K}{s(s+1)(s+4)}$ with $K=12$ and an ideal relay $M=1$, find the limit-cycle amplitude and frequency. Comment on stability of the limit cycle.

**Model Solution:**

**Step 1 (2 marks).** Denominator expansion:

$$(j\omega)(j\omega+1)(j\omega+4)=j\omega(4+5j\omega-\omega^{2})=-5\omega^{2}+j\omega(4-\omega^{2})$$

$$G(j\omega)=\frac{12}{-5\omega^{2}+j\omega(4-\omega^{2})}$$

**Step 2 (1 mark).** Imaginary part zero: $4-\omega^{2}=0\Rightarrow\omega^{*}=2$ rad/s.

**Step 3 (1 mark).** Real part: $\dfrac{12}{-5(4)}=\dfrac{12}{-20}=-\dfrac{3}{5}$.

**Step 4 (2 marks).** Equate to $-1/N(A)=-\pi A/4$:

$$-\frac{\pi A^{*}}{4}=-\frac{3}{5}\;\Longrightarrow\;A^{*}=\frac{12}{5\pi}\approx 0.764$$

**Step 5 (1 mark).** **Stability check** — examine $\dfrac{d}{dA}\big[\mathrm{Re}\,G(j\omega^{*})\big]$ vs $\dfrac{d}{dA}\big[-1/N(A)\big]$ at the intersection:
- The Nyquist plot crosses the negative real axis from **above to below** as $\omega$ increases through $2$ rad/s (sign of $\mathrm{Im}\,G$ changes from + to −).
- The locus $-1/N(A)$ moves **leftward** (more negative) as $A$ increases.
- Standard result: an intersection where the Nyquist **moves from above to below** as $\omega$ increases past $\omega^{*}$, while $-1/N(A)$ moves **leftward** as $A$ increases, gives a **stable limit cycle**.

**Conclusion:** A **stable limit cycle** exists at $\omega^{*}=2$ rad/s, $A^{*}\approx 0.764$.

> [!WARNING]
> **Examiner's Pitfall Callout:** Forgetting the **stability test** costs 1–2 marks. The test rule: if the Nyquist plot **encircles** $-1/N(A)$ for $A$ slightly *less* than $A^{*}$, the limit cycle is **unstable**; if it does *not* encircle, the limit cycle is **stable**.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Density Rapid-Revision Checklist (Ideal Relay + DF Module):**
> - ✅ **Describing function** = fundamental-output-phasor ÷ input-amplitude; a *quasi-linear* approximation.
> - ✅ **Ideal relay law:** $y = M\cdot\mathrm{sgn}(x)$; no dead zone, no hysteresis, no saturation.
> - ✅ **Master formula:** $\;N(A)=\dfrac{4M}{\pi A}\;$ — real, positive, frequency-independent.
> - ✅ **Fourier trick:** Square wave of amplitude $M$ has $A_1=0$ (odd symmetry) and $B_1=4M/\pi$.
> - ✅ **$-1/N(A)$ locus:** entire negative real axis $(-\infty, 0)$ as $A$ goes $0\to\infty$.
> - ✅ **Limit-cycle equation:** $G(j\omega)=-1/N(A)$. For real $-1/N$, the intersection must be on the **real axis** of the Nyquist plot.
> - ✅ **Procedure:** (i) Find $\omega^{*}$ from $\mathrm{Im}\,G(j\omega)=0$. (ii) Compute $\mathrm{Re}\,G(j\omega^{*})$. (iii) Solve $A^{*}$ from $-\pi A^{*}/(4M)=\mathrm{Re}\,G$.
> - ✅ **Stability test:** Use $\dfrac{d}{dA}(-1/N)$ vs. direction of Nyquist crossing at the intersection.
> - ✅ **Assumptions:** (a) $G(s)$ low-pass, (b) input is pure sinusoid, (c) nonlinearity memoryless.
> - ✅ **For other nonlinearities**, modify the limits of integration: dead zone $\Rightarrow \theta_1=\sin^{-1}(\Delta/A)$; hysteresis $\Rightarrow$ different upper/lower thresholds $\pm h$.
> - ✅ **Pitfalls to avoid:** sign of $-1/N$ (always negative for ideal relay), missing $M$ in denominator $4M$, forgetting the stability check, plotting $-1/N$ on the wrong axis.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Relay with Deadzone
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PERAT634 - NON-LINEAR CONTROL > - **Module:** Module 3 > - **Topic:** Relay with Deadzone # SECTION 1 — Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition A **Relay with Deadzone** (also called a **Relay with Insensitive Zone** or **Three-Position Relay**) is a non-linear control element whose output assumes **three discrete states** depending on the magnitude of the input error signal relative to a predefined threshold band called the *deadzone* (denoted by the half-width $\Delta$). The defining mathematical characteristic is: $$ y(t) = \begin{cases} +M, & \text{if } e(t) > +\Delta \\ 0, & \text{if } -\Delta \le e(t) \le +\Delta \\ -M, & \text{if } e(t) < -\Delta \end{cases} $$ where: - $e(t)$ = input error signal to the relay - $y(t)$ = output of the relay - $\Delta$ = half-width of the **deadzone** (a **positive real number**, typically $\Delta > 0$) - $M$ = constant **magnitude of the relay output** (saturated positive/negative level) > [!IMPORTANT] > **Syllabus Highlight (KTU 2024 Scheme — Module 3):** Relay with Deadzone is a foundational non-linear element studied under *Describing Function Analysis*. It serves as a critical bridge between ideal relay systems and real-world relays that exhibit a measurable insensitivity around zero input. --- ## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition > [!NOTE] > **Plain-English Analogy: The Old-Style Home Thermostat** > > Imagine a thermostat set to maintain $25\,^{\circ}\text{C}$, but it has a built-in tolerance (deadzone) of $\pm 1\,^{\circ}\text{C}$ to prevent rapid switching: > - If room temperature rises **above $26\,^{\circ}\text{C}$** → Compressor turns **ON** ($+M$). > - If room temperature stays between **$24\,^{\circ}\text{C}$ and $26\,^{\circ}\text{C}$** → Compressor remains **OFF** (output $= 0$). > - If room temperature drops **below $24\,^{\circ}\text{C}$** → Heater turns **ON** ($-M$). > > The temperature range $[24, 26]\,^{\circ}\text{C}$ is the *deadzone* — a region where the controller intentionally **does nothing**. This prevents wear-and-tear from rapid on/off cycling (a phenomenon called *chattering*). **Engineering Reality Check:** Almost no physical relay is ideal. Real relays, solenoid valves, and bang-bang actuators exhibit a small insensitivity near the trip point due to mechanical stiction, friction, contact arcing, or electrical hysteresis. The *Relay with Deadzone* model mathematically captures this essential imperfection. --- ## 1.3 Key Terminology at a Glance | Term | Meaning | Typical Units | |---|---|---| | Deadzone half-width $\Delta$ | Half-thickness of the insensitivity band | Same as input units (V, rad, etc.) | | Output magnitude $M$ | Saturated relay output level | Same as output units | | Insensitive zone | Range $[-\Delta, +\Delta]$ — output stays at 0 | — | | Trip points | $\pm \Delta$ — boundaries where output switches | Same as input units | --- ## 1.4 Relation to Other Non-Linear Elements (KTU Hierarchy) - **Ideal Relay** ($N(E) = \frac{4M}{\pi E}$): $\Delta = 0$ special case. - **Relay with Deadzone**: introduces the *insensitive zone* parameter $\Delta$. - **Relay with Hysteresis**: introduces *memory* (state-dependent switching) — studied as a separate element. > [!TIP] > When $\Delta \to 0$, the Relay with Deadzone **reduces** to an Ideal Relay. Always remember this limiting case — examiners love to test it! # SECTION 2 — Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ## 2.1 Operational Logic — Structured Breakdown The Relay with Deadzone operates on **three discrete logical rules**, evaluated **continuously** in real time: ### Step 1 — Continuous Input Monitoring - The relay samples the error signal $e(t)$ at every instant. - No integration, no differentiation — pure static non-linearity. ### Step 2 — Decision Against Upper Threshold - If $e(t) > +\Delta$ → switch ON positive branch → output $= +M$. - The positive output is **saturated** and **constant**, independent of how large $e(t)$ becomes (as long as it exceeds $+\Delta$). ### Step 3 — Decision Against Lower Threshold - If $e(t) < -\Delta$ → switch ON negative branch → output $= -M$. ### Step 4 — Deadzone (Null Output) Condition - If $-\Delta \le e(t) \le +\Delta$ → output $= 0$. - This is the **distinguishing feature** compared to an ideal relay. ### Step 5 — Memoryless Behavior - The relay's output at any instant depends **only** on the present input — there is **no memory** of past inputs (this distinguishes it from the Relay with Hysteresis). --- ## 2.2 Input-Output Characteristic Curve The static input-output curve consists of **three horizontal line segments**: ``` y(t) | +M ----------●==================● | | | DEADZONE | 0 ===========●==================●====→ e(t) -Δ | | +Δ | | -M ----------●==================● ``` The curve has **two jump discontinuities** at $e = \pm \Delta$ and a **flat zero region** in between. --- ## 2.3 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet > [!IMPORTANT] > Memorize the **Describing Function** formula below — it is the single most exam-relevant result for this topic. | # | Quantity | Formula | Validity / Conditions | |---|---|---|---| | 1 | Describing Function $N(E)$ | $N(E) = \dfrac{4M}{\pi E}\,\sqrt{1 - \left(\dfrac{\Delta}{E}\right)^2}$ | Valid for $E \ge \Delta$ | | 2 | Describing Function (deadzone region) | $N(E) = 0$ | Valid for $E < \Delta$ | | 3 | Equivalent Deadzone Width (full band) | $2\Delta$ | Total width of insensitive region | | 4 | Output Fundamental Amplitude $B_1$ | $B_1 = \dfrac{4M}{\pi}\,\sqrt{1 - \left(\dfrac{\Delta}{E}\right)^2}$ | Fundamental component magnitude | | 5 | Phase of $N(E)$ | $\angle N(E) = 0$ | Purely **real**, no phase shift | | 6 | Ideal Relay Limiting Case | $N(E) \to \dfrac{4M}{\pi E}$ as $\Delta \to 0$ | Recovery of standard result | | 7 | Output RMS in sinusoid | $y_{\text{rms}} = M\,\sqrt{\dfrac{\pi - 2\psi}{\pi}}$ | Where $\psi = \sin^{-1}(\Delta / E)$ | | 8 | Switching Phase Angle | $\psi = \sin^{-1}(\Delta / E)$ | Angle at which relay trips | | 9 | Total conduction angle per quarter | $\dfrac{\pi}{2} - \psi$ | Time relay is ON per quarter cycle | > [!NOTE] > **Critical Property:** The Describing Function $N(E)$ is a **purely real** quantity (phase = $0$ or $\pi$). This means the Relay with Deadzone **does not introduce phase lag** — only amplitude distortion. This is essential for the *Nichols / Nyquist* stability analysis in module-level problems. --- ## 2.4 Why is the Deadzone Important in Real Engineering? | Engineering Field | Practical Use of Deadzone | |---|---| | **Hydraulic Servo Valves** | Prevents valve motion for very small command signals (avoids leakage). | | **Thermostats / HVAC** | Prevents rapid compressor cycling (extends equipment life). | | **Robotic Joint Actuators** | Avoids motor jitter due to sensor noise near zero position. | | **Aircraft Control Surfaces** | Filters out small turbulence-induced commands. | | **Numerical Control (CNC)** | Eliminates chatter due to quantization noise. | --- ## 2.5 Limiting Behavior (Quick Exam Insight) $$ \lim_{\Delta \to 0} N(E) = \frac{4M}{\pi E} \quad \text{(Ideal Relay)} $$ $$ \lim_{E \to \Delta^+} N(E) = \frac{4M}{\pi E}\,\sqrt{1 - 1} = 0 \quad \text{(At threshold, just-emerging fundamental)} $$ $$ \lim_{E \to \infty} N(E) \to 0 \quad \text{(Large-signal: relay becomes negligible gain)} $$ # SECTION 3 — Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ## 3.1 Derivation of the Describing Function $N(E)$ > [!NOTE] > **What we want to find:** When a sinusoidal input $e(t) = E \sin(\omega t)$ is applied, the relay output $y(t)$ is a **rectangular pulse train** (with gaps). We approximate $y(t)$ by its **fundamental Fourier component** and compute the complex gain $N(E) = B_1/E$ — this is the *Describing Function*. --- ### Step 1 — Define the Sinusoidal Input Let the input be: $$ e(t) = E \sin(\omega t), \quad E > 0 $$ --- ### Step 2 — Identify the Switching Angles The relay switches its output when $e(t) = \pm \Delta$. Setting: $$ E \sin(\omega t_1) = \Delta \;\;\Rightarrow\;\; \sin(\omega t_1) = \frac{\Delta}{E} $$ Define the **switching angle**: $$ \psi \equiv \omega t_1 = \sin^{-1}\!\left(\frac{\Delta}{E}\right), \quad 0 \le \psi \le \frac{\pi}{2} $$ By the symmetry of the relay characteristic: - Output switches from $0 \to +M$ at $\omega t = \psi$ - Output switches from $+M \to 0$ at $\omega t = \pi - \psi$ - Output switches from $0 \to -M$ at $\omega t = \pi + \psi$ - Output switches from $-M \to 0$ at $\omega t = 2\pi - \psi$ --- ### Step 3 — Express the Output $y(t)$ Over One Period $$ y(t) = \begin{cases} 0, & 0 \le \omega t \le \psi \\ +M, & \psi \le \omega t \le \pi - \psi \\ 0, & \pi - \psi \le \omega t \le \pi + \psi \\ -M, & \pi + \psi \le \omega t \le 2\pi - \psi \\ 0, & 2\pi - \psi \le \omega t \le 2\pi \end{cases} $$ This is an **odd-symmetric** waveform, so the DC term ($A_0$) and the cosine Fourier coefficients ($A_n$) are **all zero**. Only the sine coefficients $B_n$ survive. --- ### Step 4 — Compute the Fundamental Sine Coefficient $B_1$ $$ B_1 = \frac{1}{\pi} \int_{0}^{2\pi} y(\omega t)\, \sin(\omega t)\, d(\omega t) $$ Using quarter-wave symmetry (the function is odd and symmetric), we simplify: $$ B_1 = \frac{4}{\pi} \int_{0}^{\pi/2} y(\omega t)\, \sin(\omega t)\, d(\omega t) $$ Substituting the piecewise definition (note $y = 0$ on $[0, \psi]$): $$ B_1 = \frac{4}{\pi} \int_{\psi}^{\pi/2} M \sin(\omega t)\, d(\omega t) $$ --- ### Step 5 — Perform the Integration $$ B_1 = \frac{4M}{\pi} \left[ -\cos(\omega t) \right]_{\psi}^{\pi/2} $$ $$ B_1 = \frac{4M}{\pi} \left( -\cos(\frac{\pi}{2}) + \cos(\psi) \right) $$ Since $\cos(\pi/2) = 0$: $$ B_1 = \frac{4M}{\pi}\,\cos(\psi) $$ --- ### Step 6 — Express in Terms of $\Delta$ and $E$ We have $\sin(\psi) = \dfrac{\Delta}{E}$. Using the identity $\cos^2(\psi) + \sin^2(\psi) = 1$: $$ \cos(\psi) = \sqrt{1 - \sin^2(\psi)} = \sqrt{1 - \left(\frac{\Delta}{E}\right)^2} $$ Therefore: $$ B_1 = \frac{4M}{\pi}\,\sqrt{1 - \left(\frac{\Delta}{E}\right)^2} $$ --- ### Step 7 — Form the Describing Function By definition: $$ N(E) = \frac{B_1}{E} $$ $$ \boxed{\,N(E) = \frac{4M}{\pi E}\,\sqrt{1 - \left(\frac{\Delta}{E}\right)^2}, \quad E \ge \Delta\,} $$ For $E < \Delta$: the relay output is identically zero (no switching occurs), so: $$ \boxed{\,N(E) = 0, \quad E < \Delta\,} $$ --- ## 3.2 Analytical Matrix — Parameter Effects on $N(E)$ | Parameter Action | Effect on $N(E)$ | Physical Interpretation | |---|---|---| | $\Delta \uparrow$ (wider deadzone) | $N(E)$ decreases for a given $E$ | Relay needs stronger signal to produce output | | $M \uparrow$ (higher output level) | $N(E)$ scales linearly | Stronger control authority | | $E \uparrow$ (larger input amplitude) | $N(E) \to 0$ asymptotically | Saturated element behaves like low gain | | $E = \Delta$ (at threshold) | $N(E) = 0$ | Fundamental vanishes exactly at threshold | | $\Delta \to 0$ | $N(E) \to 4M/(\pi E)$ | Recovers the **ideal relay** formula | | $E \to \infty$ | $N(E) \to 0$ | Equivalent to "open" for small gains | --- ## 3.3 Worked Numerical Example **Problem:** A Relay with Deadzone has $M = 10\,\text{V}$ and $\Delta = 2\,\text{V}$. A sinusoidal input of amplitude $E = 5\,\text{V}$ is applied. Compute the Describing Function. **Solution:** Step 1: Verify $E \ge \Delta$: $\;\;5 \ge 2 \;\;\checkmark$ Step 2: Compute the ratio: $$ \frac{\Delta}{E} = \frac{2}{5} = 0.4 $$ Step 3: Compute the square-root term: $$ \sqrt{1 - (0.4)^2} = \sqrt{1 - 0.16} = \sqrt{0.84} \approx 0.9165 $$ Step 4: Plug into the formula: $$ N(E) = \frac{4 \times 10}{\pi \times 5}\,\sqrt{0.84} = \frac{40}{5\pi}\,(0.9165) = \frac{8}{\pi}\,(0.9165) $$ $$ N(E) \approx 2.333 \text{ (dimensionless)} $$ **Interpretation:** At this operating point, the relay behaves like an **amplifier with gain $\approx 2.33$** for the fundamental component. --- ## 3.4 Average Output Power / Conduction Angle The total conduction angle per half-cycle is $2 \times \left(\dfrac{\pi}{2} - \psi\right) = \pi - 2\psi$, where $\psi = \sin^{-1}(\Delta/E)$. As $E$ grows large, $\psi \to 0$ and the conduction angle $\to \pi$ (full conduction like a square wave). As $E \to \Delta^+$, $\psi \to \pi/2$ and the conduction angle $\to 0$ (relay barely turns on). # SECTION 4 — Structural Diagrams & Schematics ## 4.1 Block-Level Functional Architecture of a Relay with Deadzone Control Loop The following Mermaid block diagram illustrates how a Relay with Deadzone sits inside a closed-loop non-linear control system, and where the Describing Function approximation is invoked for stability analysis. ```mermaid flowchart LR Rref[Reference Input R s] --> Sum1((Sum Node)) Fbk[Feedback Signal] --> Sum1 Sum1 --> Err[Error Signal e t] Err --> Relay{Relay with
Deadzone NL} Relay -->|y t saturated or zero| Out[Plant Output y t] Out --> Sensor[Sensor / Feedback Gain H s] Sensor --> Fbk Err --> DesFunc[Describing Function
Approximation N E] DesFunc --> Stability[Stability Analysis
GH plane or Nichols Chart] ``` **Reading the diagram:** - **R(s)** is the desired reference (e.g., temperature setpoint). - **Sum Node** computes the error $e(t) = R - \text{feedback}$. - **Relay with Deadzone (NL)** is the **non-linear element** under study. - **Plant** is the linear process being controlled. - The **Describing Function block** replaces the non-linear element by an equivalent linear gain $N(E)$, enabling classical linear stability methods (Bode / Nyquist). --- ## 4.2 State Transition Logic of the Relay ```mermaid stateDiagram-v2 [*] --> Idle Idle --> Positive: e t greater than plus Delta Idle --> Negative: e t less than minus Delta Positive --> Idle: e t falls in negative Delta to plus Delta Negative --> Idle: e t rises in negative Delta to plus Delta Positive --> Negative: e t less than minus Delta Negative --> Positive: e t greater than plus Delta ``` **Interpretation of states:** - **Idle:** Output $= 0$ (relay is in the deadzone). - **Positive:** Output $= +M$ (upper saturation). - **Negative:** Output $= -M$ (lower saturation). --- ## 4.3 Plot of $N(E)$ vs $E$ (Conceptual Trend) ```mermaid graph LR Axis["E axis (input amplitude)"] --> P1["E = Delta : N E = 0"] P1 --> P2["E increases : N E rises to a peak"] P2 --> P3["E very large : N E tends to 0"] ``` **Key Trend:** $N(E)$ starts at zero at $E = \Delta$, rises to a **maximum** at some intermediate $E$, then decays back toward zero as $E \to \infty$. The peak value can be obtained by differentiation: $$ \frac{dN}{dE} = 0 \;\;\Rightarrow\;\; E_{\text{peak}} = \sqrt{2}\,\Delta $$ with peak value: $$ N_{\max} = \frac{2\sqrt{2}\,M}{\pi \Delta} $$ > [!TIP] > This peak value $N_{\max}$ is often required when plotting the **$-1/N(E)$ locus** on the Nyquist plane for limit-cycle prediction. # SECTION 5 — KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap ## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each) ### Question A1 [KTU University Exam - Dec 2023] **Q:** Define the *Relay with Deadzone* non-linearity. Sketch its input-output characteristic and label all parameters. **Model Answer (3 Marks):** A Relay with Deadzone is a non-linear element whose output assumes **three discrete values** based on the input error relative to a deadzone half-width $\Delta$: $$ y = +M \;\text{ if }\; e > \Delta, \quad y = 0 \;\text{ if }\; \vert e \vert \le \Delta, \quad y = -M \;\text{ if }\; e < -\Delta $$ **Sketch:** Horizontal lines at $+M$, $0$, $-M$ with vertical jumps at $e = \pm\Delta$. ``` y +M ──────────●●●●●●●●● ╱ Δ 0 ───────●──────────●──────→ e -Δ Δ -M ──────────●●●●●●●●● ``` **Parameters labeled:** $M$ (output level), $\Delta$ (deadzone half-width). --- ### Question A2 [KTU University Exam - May 2024] **Q:** Write the Describing Function expression for a Relay with Deadzone and state the condition for its validity. **Model Answer (3 Marks):** $$ N(E) = \frac{4M}{\pi E}\,\sqrt{1 - \left(\frac{\Delta}{E}\right)^{2}}, \quad E \ge \Delta $$ with $N(E) = 0$ for $E < \Delta$. The function is **purely real**, so $\angle N(E) = 0$. The validity condition ensures the input sinusoid is large enough to cross the trip threshold $\Delta$. --- ## 5.2 Part B — Full-Length Questions (14 Marks Each, with Internal Choice) ### Question A (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam - Model Paper 2024] **(a)** Derive the Describing Function $N(E)$ for a Relay with Deadzone having output magnitude $M$ and deadzone half-width $\Delta$. **(7 Marks)** **(b)** A Relay with Deadzone with $M = 12$ and $\Delta = 3$ is used in a unity-feedback control loop with linear plant $G(s) = \dfrac{10}{s(s+2)}$. Determine whether a **limit cycle** can exist. If yes, estimate its amplitude $E$ and frequency $\omega$. **(7 Marks)** --- ### Model Solution for Question A #### Part (a) — Derivation (7 Marks) **Step 1:** Assume sinusoidal input $e(t) = E \sin(\omega t)$. **Step 2:** Define switching angle $\psi = \sin^{-1}(\Delta/E)$, with $E \ge \Delta$. **Step 3:** Output $y(t)$ is a square wave with gaps: - $y = +M$ for $\omega t \in [\psi, \pi - \psi]$ - $y = -M$ for $\omega t \in [\pi + \psi, 2\pi - \psi]$ - $y = 0$ elsewhere. **Step 4:** Compute the fundamental sine coefficient: $$ B_1 = \frac{4}{\pi} \int_{\psi}^{\pi/2} M \sin(\omega t)\, d(\omega t) = \frac{4M}{\pi}\cos(\psi) $$ **Step 5:** Substitute $\cos(\psi) = \sqrt{1 - (\Delta/E)^2}$: $$ B_1 = \frac{4M}{\pi}\,\sqrt{1 - \left(\frac{\Delta}{E}\right)^2} $$ **Step 6:** Describing function: $$ \boxed{\,N(E) = \frac{B_1}{E} = \frac{4M}{\pi E}\,\sqrt{1 - \left(\frac{\Delta}{E}\right)^2}\,} $$ **(Marks breakdown:** Setup 1M, Switching angle 1M, Integration 2M, Trigonometric identity 1M, Final formula 2M.) --- #### Part (b) — Limit Cycle Analysis (7 Marks) **Step 1 — Condition for limit cycle:** The describing function locus $-1/N(E)$ must intersect the Nyquist plot of $G(j\omega)$. **Step 2 — Nyquist of $G(j\omega)$:** $$ G(j\omega) = \frac{10}{j\omega(j\omega + 2)} = \frac{10}{-2\omega^2 + j\omega^3 \cdot \text{(correction)}} $$ Let me redo: $$ G(j\omega) = \frac{10}{j\omega(j\omega + 2)} = \frac{10}{j\omega \cdot j\omega + j\omega \cdot 2} = \frac{10}{-\omega^2 + j2\omega} $$ Rationalize: $$ G(j\omega) = \frac{10(-\omega^2 - j2\omega)}{(-\omega^2)^2 + (2\omega)^2} = \frac{10(-\omega^2 - j2\omega)}{\omega^4 + 4\omega^2} = \frac{-10}{\omega^2 + 4} - j\frac{20}{\omega(\omega^2 + 4)} $$ **Step 3 — Describing function locus** $-1/N(E)$: $$ -\frac{1}{N(E)} = -\frac{\pi E}{4M\,\sqrt{1 - (\Delta/E)^2}} = -\frac{\pi E}{48\sqrt{1 - (3/E)^2}} $$ This is a **purely real negative** locus, moving from $-\infty$ at $E = 3^+$ to $-\infty$ as $E \to \infty$ with a **maximum negative real excursion** at $E_{\text{peak}} = \sqrt{2}\Delta = \sqrt{18} \approx 4.24$. Peak value: $$ -\frac{1}{N_{\max}} = -\frac{\pi \cdot 3\sqrt{2}}{48 \cdot (1/\sqrt{2})} = -\frac{\pi \cdot 3\sqrt{2}}{48/\sqrt{2}} = -\frac{6\pi}{48} = -\frac{\pi}{8} \approx -0.393 $$ **Step 4 — Intersection check:** The Nyquist locus $G(j\omega)$ for $\omega \in (0, \infty)$: - As $\omega \to 0^+$: $\text{Re}(G) \to -2.5$, $\text{Im}(G) \to -\infty$ - As $\omega \to \infty$: $G \to 0$ from third quadrant - Crosses negative real axis when $\text{Im}(G) = 0$ → this never happens for finite $\omega > 0$ (always negative imaginary part) **Conclusion:** The Nyquist plot lies entirely in the **third quadrant** with $\text{Re} \in (-2.5, 0)$. The $-1/N(E)$ locus is on the **negative real axis** between $-\infty$ and $-0.393$. **Intersection check:** For $\text{Re}(G) = -0.393$: $$ \frac{-10}{\omega^2 + 4} = -0.393 \;\;\Rightarrow\;\; \omega^2 + 4 = 25.45 \;\;\Rightarrow\;\; \omega \approx 4.63\,\text{rad/s} $$ Then $\text{Im}(G) \approx -20/(4.63 \times 25.45) \approx -0.170$. Since the describing function locus is **purely real**, the intersection is **not exact** — limit cycle is possible only if the real-axis intersection of $G(j\omega)$ falls within the range of $-1/N(E)$. **Approximate intersection (along real axis projection):** $$ -\frac{1}{N(E)} = \text{Re}[G(j\omega)] \;\;\Rightarrow\;\; E \approx \text{solve numerically} $$ Using the crossing at $\omega \approx 4.63$ rad/s with $\text{Re} = -0.393$: $$ \frac{\pi E}{48\sqrt{1 - 9/E^2}} = 0.393 $$ Trial: $E = 6$: $$ \frac{\pi \cdot 6}{48\sqrt{1 - 9/36}} = \frac{18.85}{48 \times 0.866} = \frac{18.85}{41.57} \approx 0.453 \quad \text{(too large)} $$ Trial: $E = 5$: $$ \frac{15.71}{48\sqrt{1 - 9/25}} = \frac{15.71}{48 \times 0.8} = \frac{15.71}{38.4} \approx 0.409 \quad \text{(close)} $$ Trial: $E = 4.7$: $$ \frac{14.76}{48\sqrt{1 - 9/22.09}} = \frac{14.76}{48 \times 0.771} \approx 0.399 \quad \text{(very close)} $$ **Answer:** A limit cycle exists with amplitude **$E \approx 4.7$** and frequency **$\omega \approx 4.63\,\text{rad/s}$**. --- ### Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice [KTU University Exam - Sample QP] **(a)** Explain the concept of Describing Function. Why is it restricted to systems with low-pass linear parts? **(7 Marks)** **(b)** For a Relay with Deadzone with $M = 8$ and $\Delta = 1$, plot (qualitatively) the $-1/N(E)$ locus on the real axis. Indicate the peak and the asymptotic behavior. **(7 Marks)** #### Model Solution Outline for Question B **Part (a):** - Describing Function (DF) is the **first-harmonic equivalent gain** of a non-linear element. - DF approximation: $y(t) \approx N(E) \cdot \sin(\omega t)$, ignoring higher harmonics. - Justification: If the linear part $G(s)$ is **low-pass**, higher harmonics of the output are **attenuated** by $|G(jn\omega)| \to 0$ for $n \ge 2$. Only the fundamental component propagates around the loop. - Mathematical form: $N(E) = \dfrac{B_1 + jA_1}{E}$. **Part (b):** The $-1/N(E)$ locus lies on the **negative real axis** of the $G(j\omega)$ plane: $$ -\frac{1}{N(E)} = -\frac{\pi E}{32\sqrt{1 - 1/E^2}} $$ | $E$ | $-1/N(E)$ | |---|---| | $1.0^+$ | $-\infty$ | | $\sqrt{2} \approx 1.414$ (peak) | $-\pi/(2 \cdot 4) = -\pi/8 \approx -0.393$ | | $2$ | $-0.524$ | | $5$ | $-0.508$ | | $\infty$ | $-\infty$ | The locus starts at $-\infty$, rises to a **maximum** (closest to origin) at $E = \sqrt{2}\Delta$, then recedes to $-\infty$. --- ## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout > [!WARNING] > **Common Mark-Deduction Traps (Real Examiner Pet Peeves):** > > 1. **Forgetting the validity condition $E \ge \Delta$.** Always state the domain of validity of the formula explicitly. *(−1 mark)* > 2. **Sign errors in $\cos(\psi)$.** Many students write $\cos(\psi) = \sqrt{1 + (\Delta/E)^2}$ by mistake. Memorize $\sin^2 + \cos^2 = 1$. > 3. **Confusing phase of $N(E)$.** The phase is **zero** for Relay with Deadzone (purely real). Do not write a non-zero phase. > 4. **Drawing $-1/N(E)$ locus incorrectly.** It lies on the **negative real axis**, not in the complex plane — students often plot it as a curve in 2-D. > 5. **Missing quarter-wave symmetry argument.** When computing $B_1$, exploit the symmetry to write $\frac{4}{\pi}\int_0^{\pi/2}$ instead of evaluating four integrals separately — this saves time and reduces errors. > 6. **Unit confusion.** $N(E)$ is **dimensionless** only if input/output have same units. Otherwise it carries units. --- ## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist) - **Definition:** Relay with Deadzone → output $\in \{-M, 0, +M\}$ based on $e \lessgtr \pm\Delta$. - **Key Parameters:** $M$ (output level), $\Delta$ (deadzone half-width), $E$ (input amplitude). - **Master Formula (DF):** $$ N(E) = \frac{4M}{\pi E}\,\sqrt{1 - \left(\frac{\Delta}{E}\right)^2}, \quad E \ge \Delta; \quad N(E) = 0,\; E < \Delta $$ - **Phase:** $\angle N(E) = 0$ (purely real, no phase lag). - **Limit Case:** $\Delta \to 0 \;\Rightarrow\;$ ideal relay $N(E) = 4M/(\pi E)$. - **Peak of $N(E)$:** occurs at $E_{\text{peak}} = \sqrt{2}\,\Delta$ with $N_{\max} = \dfrac{2\sqrt{2}\,M}{\pi \Delta}$. - **Switching Angle:** $\psi = \sin^{-1}(\Delta / E)$ — half the conduction angle on each side. - **Conduction Per Half-Cycle:** $\pi - 2\psi$ — decreases as $\Delta$ grows. - **Use of Describing Function:** Valid when linear part $G(s)$ is **low-pass** (filters higher harmonics). - **Limit Cycle Condition:** Intersection of $-1/N(E)$ locus with Nyquist plot of $G(j\omega)$. - **Static Characteristic:** Three horizontal segments, jumps at $e = \pm\Delta$, no memory. - **Difference from Relay with Hysteresis:** Deadzone has **no memory**; hysteresis has **state-dependent switching**. - **Engineering Relevance:** Real relays, solenoid valves, thermostats — every practical switching device has deadzone.

topics.title: Simple Deadzone
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PERAT634 - NON-LINEAR CONTROL
> - **Module:** Module Module 3
> - **Topic:** Simple Deadzone

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

> [!IMPORTANT] **KTU 2024 Syllabus Definition**
> A **Simple Deadzone (or Dead-Zone) Non-linearity** is a *memoryless, odd-symmetric, static non-linearity* in which the output remains identically **zero** for all input magnitudes lying within a finite interval around the origin, and exhibits a *piecewise-linear* relationship with the input outside this interval.

Mathematically, for a simple deadzone with **half-width $\Delta$** and **active-region slope $m$**:

$$
y(t) = \begin{cases} m\bigl(u(t) - \Delta\bigr), & u(t) > \Delta \\ 0, & \vert u(t) \vert \le \Delta \\ m\bigl(u(t) + \Delta\bigr), & u(t) < -\Delta \end{cases}
$$

The defining constants are:

| Symbol | Engineering Name | Typical Units |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| $\Delta$ | **Deadzone Width (Half-Width)** | Same as input |
| $m$ | **Active-Region Slope / Gain** | Output / Input (dimensionless if normalized) |

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — Plain-English Intuition

> [!NOTE] **Real-World Analogy: The Loose Steering Wheel**
> Imagine driving a car with a slightly worn steering column. When you rotate the wheel by a **very small angle** (say less than **2 degrees** to either side), the front wheels of the car **do not respond at all** — the car keeps going straight. Only after you exceed this small "play angle" do the wheels actually begin to turn. That invisible region of useless input is exactly a **deadzone**. The "play" in old hydraulic valves, the static friction stiction in DC servomotors, the threshold voltage of a silicon diode, and the backlash in gear trains are all physical instances of the same mathematical beast.

## 1.3 Physical Origin of Deadzone

- **Stiction / Static Friction:** A motor will not move until the driving torque exceeds Coulomb friction.
- **Pneumatic / Hydraulic Valves:** The spool has a small overlap that must be cleared before fluid flows.
- **Electronic Thresholds:** Diodes, transistors, and operational amplifiers require a minimum $V_{BE}$ or offset voltage to conduct.
- **Mechanical Backlash:** Loose meshing between gear teeth creates an "input-null" zone.

> [!TIP] **Key Takeaway for Examiners**
> Always draw the deadzone characteristic with the **flat zero-output plateau** straddling the origin. Examiners instantly recognize a correct deadzone sketch by the presence of two **kinks (corner points)** at $u = \pm \Delta$ where the slope abruptly changes from $0$ to $m$.

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Structural Breakdown of the Deadzone Operator

The deadzone operator $\mathcal{N}(\cdot)$ is completely characterised by *two* scalar parameters $(\Delta, m)$ and satisfies the following engineering-grade properties:

- **Static (Memoryless):** The output at any instant depends *only* on the input at that same instant — no energy storage, no hysteresis, no dynamics.
- **Odd-Symmetric:** $\mathcal{N}(-u) = -\mathcal{N}(u)$. This implies the DC Fourier coefficient $a_0 = 0$ and all cosine harmonics vanish, which dramatically simplifies the describing-function analysis.
- **Single-Valued (Sector-Bounded):** The non-linearity lies inside the sector $[\,0,\; m\,]$ on the $(u,y)$ plane.
- **Sector Condition:** $0 \le u \cdot y \le m\,u^2$ for all $u$.

## 2.2 Operational Logic Step-by-Step

1. **Acquire** the instantaneous input $u(t)$.
2. **Compare** $\vert u(t) \vert$ with the threshold $\Delta$.
3. **Branch into one of three regimes:**
   - *Inner Regime:* If $\vert u \vert \le \Delta \Rightarrow$ output is **identically zero** (no actuation, no effect on the plant).
   - *Positive Outer Regime:* If $u > \Delta \Rightarrow$ output follows the *shifted* line $y = m(u - \Delta)$.
   - *Negative Outer Regime:* If $u < -\Delta \Rightarrow$ output follows $y = m(u + \Delta)$.
4. **Continuity** at the corner points is preserved only if the output is **exactly zero** at $u = \pm \Delta$ (which it is by definition).

## 2.3 The Describing Function (DF) of Simple Deadzone

For a sinusoidal input $u(t) = A \sin(\omega t)$ with $A > \Delta$, the output is a *clipped sinusoid*. The first-harmonic describing function is derived in Section 3. The final closed-form expression is:

$$
\boxed{\;N(A) \;=\; m \;-\; \frac{2m}{\pi}\Biggl[\arcsin\!\Bigl(\tfrac{\Delta}{A}\Bigr) \;+\; \frac{\Delta}{A}\,\sqrt{\,1 - \bigl(\tfrac{\Delta}{A}\bigr)^{2}\,}\,\Biggr]\;}
$$

> [!NOTE] **Why "Describing Function"?**
> Because the output of a non-linearity driven by a sinusoid contains *harmonics* of the input frequency, the system is no longer LTI. The Describing Function (DF) $N(A)$ is a *quasi-linear* approximation that retains **only the first harmonic** of the output, expressed as a complex gain that depends on the **input amplitude $A$** alone. It is the workhorse tool of the *Harmonic Balance / Limit-Cycle Prediction* method.

## 2.4 Properties of the Deadzone Describing Function

- **Purely Real** (no phase shift): the non-linearity does not introduce a phase lag, only a gain reduction.
- **Amplitude-Dependent:** $N(A)$ is a function of $A$, not of $\omega$.
- **Boundary Values:**
  - At $A = \Delta$ (minimum active amplitude): $N(\Delta) = 0$.
  - At $A \to \infty$ (large-signal limit): $N(A) \to m$ (recovers the active-region slope).
- **Monotonic:** $N(A)$ is *monotonically increasing* in $A$.
- **The curve $-1/N(A)$** lives entirely on the **negative real axis**, sweeping from $-\infty$ (as $A \to \Delta^+$) to $-1/m$ (as $A \to \infty$).

## 2.5 KTU Formula Cheat Sheet

| \# | Quantity | Formula / Expression | Key Condition |
| :---: | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| 1 | Deadzone output (positive side) | $y = m(u - \Delta)$ | $u > \Delta$ |
| 2 | Deadzone output (negative side) | $y = m(u + \Delta)$ | $u < -\Delta$ |
| 3 | Deadzone output (silent band) | $y = 0$ | $\vert u \vert \le \Delta$ |
| 4 | Deadzone DF $N(A)$ | $N(A) = m - \tfrac{2m}{\pi}\!\left[\arcsin\!\tfrac{\Delta}{A} + \tfrac{\Delta}{A}\sqrt{1-(\Delta/A)^{2}}\,\right]$ | $A \ge \Delta$ |
| 5 | DF lower limit | $N(\Delta) = 0$ | $A \to \Delta^+$ |
| 6 | DF upper limit | $N(A) \to m$ | $A \to \infty$ |
| 7 | Negative reciprocal locus | $-1/N(A)$ on negative real axis, $-\infty \to -1/m$ | All $A \ge \Delta$ |
| 8 | Harmonic balance equation | $1 + N(A)\,G(j\omega) = 0$ | Limit cycle condition |
| 9 | Crossing angle (sinusoidal input) | $\omega t_{1} = \arcsin(\Delta/A)$ | Boundary of active region |
| 10 | Sector bound | $0 \le u\cdot y \le m\,u^{2}$ | All $u$ |

> [!TIP] **Engineering Utility**
> The deadzone DF is the *cornerstone* of limit-cycle prediction in hydraulic position servos, robot-arm joint controllers (with gear backlash), process-control valves with overlap, and op-amp circuits with input offset. Almost every KTU numerical on non-linear control ultimately reduces to **plotting $-1/N(A)$ on the Nyquist chart of $G(j\omega)$ and finding the intersection.**

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Exhaustive Derivation of the Describing Function of Simple Deadzone

**Given:** Simple deadzone with half-width $\Delta$ and active-region slope $m$.  
**Input:** $u(t) = A \sin(\omega t)$, with $A > \Delta$ (otherwise output is identically zero).  
**Goal:** Compute the describing function $N(A) = \dfrac{B_{1} + j A_{1}}{A}$.

### Step 1 — Identify the Output Waveform in One Period

The input $u = A\sin(\omega t)$ crosses the deadzone boundary $\pm\Delta$ at the angles

$$
\omega t_{1} \;=\; \arcsin\!\Bigl(\frac{\Delta}{A}\Bigr), \qquad \omega t_{2} \;=\; \pi - \omega t_{1}.
$$

The output $y(t)$ over one full period $[0, 2\pi]$ is piecewise:

$$
y(t) = \begin{cases} 0, & 0 \le \omega t \le \omega t_{1} \\[4pt] m\bigl(A\sin(\omega t) - \Delta\bigr), & \omega t_{1} \le \omega t \le \pi - \omega t_{1} \\[4pt] 0, & \pi - \omega t_{1} \le \omega t \le \pi + \omega t_{1} \\[4pt] m\bigl(A\sin(\omega t) + \Delta\bigr), & \pi + \omega t_{1} \le \omega t \le 2\pi - \omega t_{1} \\[4pt] 0, & 2\pi - \omega t_{1} \le \omega t \le 2\pi \end{cases}
$$

> **Reasoning:** When the input lies inside the silent band, the non-linearity emits nothing. Once the input exceeds $\Delta$, the output tracks the input with slope $m$ but is **shifted down by $m\Delta$** (or up by $m\Delta$ on the negative half-cycle) to ensure continuity through the origin.

### Step 2 — Recognise Symmetries to Eliminate Most Fourier Coefficients

- **Odd symmetry** of $y(t)$ (because the deadzone is odd-symmetric in $u$): all *cosine* coefficients $A_n = 0$ for every $n \ge 1$, and the DC term $a_0 = 0$.
- **Half-wave symmetry** $y(\omega t + \pi) = -y(\omega t)$: all *even* sine coefficients $B_{2}, B_{4}, \dots$ are zero.

Therefore the Fourier series reduces to **only odd sine terms**, and the DF is

$$
N(A) \;=\; \frac{B_{1}}{A} \quad \text{where} \quad B_{1} \;=\; \frac{1}{\pi}\int_{0}^{2\pi} y(\tau)\,\sin(\tau)\,d\tau, \quad \tau \equiv \omega t.
$$

### Step 3 — Apply Half-Wave Symmetry to Halve the Integration Range

Because $y(\tau+\pi)\sin(\tau+\pi) = y(\tau)\sin(\tau)$, the integrand is $\pi$-periodic, and

$$
B_{1} \;=\; \frac{2}{\pi}\int_{0}^{\pi} y(\tau)\,\sin(\tau)\,d\tau.
$$

Within $[0,\pi]$, $y(\tau)$ is non-zero only on $[\omega t_{1},\, \pi - \omega t_{1}]$, so

$$
B_{1} \;=\; \frac{2}{\pi}\int_{\omega t_{1}}^{\pi - \omega t_{1}} m\bigl(A\sin\tau - \Delta\bigr)\sin\tau\,d\tau.
$$

Factor out the constant $m$:

$$
B_{1} \;=\; \frac{2m}{\pi}\int_{\omega t_{1}}^{\pi - \omega t_{1}} \Bigl(A\sin^{2}\tau - \Delta\sin\tau\Bigr)\,d\tau.
$$

### Step 4 — Evaluate the First Integral $I_{1} = \displaystyle\int_{\omega t_{1}}^{\pi-\omega t_{1}} A\sin^{2}\tau\,d\tau$

Use the identity $\sin^{2}\tau = \dfrac{1 - \cos 2\tau}{2}$:

$$
I_{1} \;=\; A\int_{\omega t_{1}}^{\pi-\omega t_{1}}\frac{1-\cos 2\tau}{2}\,d\tau \;=\; \frac{A}{2}\Bigl[\tau\Bigr]_{\omega t_{1}}^{\pi-\omega t_{1}} - \frac{A}{4}\Bigl[\sin 2\tau\Bigr]_{\omega t_{1}}^{\pi-\omega t_{1}}.
$$

Compute each piece separately:

- The $\tau$-term: $\dfrac{A}{2}\bigl[(\pi - \omega t_{1}) - \omega t_{1}\bigr] = \dfrac{A}{2}\bigl(\pi - 2\omega t_{1}\bigr)$.
- The $\sin 2\tau$-term: $\sin(2\pi - 2\omega t_{1}) = -\sin(2\omega t_{1})$, so

$$
\frac{A}{4}\bigl[\sin(2\pi - 2\omega t_{1}) - \sin(2\omega t_{1})\bigr] = \frac{A}{4}\bigl[-\sin(2\omega t_{1}) - \sin(2\omega t_{1})\bigr] = -\frac{A}{2}\sin(2\omega t_{1}).
$$

Hence

$$
I_{1} \;=\; \frac{A}{2}\bigl(\pi - 2\omega t_{1}\bigr) + \frac{A}{2}\sin(2\omega t_{1}) \;=\; \frac{A}{2}\bigl[\pi - 2\omega t_{1} + 2\sin(\omega t_{1})\cos(\omega t_{1})\bigr].
$$

> **Conversion logic:** We used the angle-difference identity $\sin(2\pi - x) = -\sin x$ and the double-angle identity $\sin(2\theta) = 2\sin\theta\cos\theta$ to bring the result into a form involving only the *boundary angle* $\omega t_1$.

### Step 5 — Substitute $\sin(\omega t_{1}) = \Delta/A$ and $\cos(\omega t_{1}) = \sqrt{1 - (\Delta/A)^2}$

$$
I_{1} \;=\; \frac{A}{2}\Biggl[\pi - 2\arcsin\!\Bigl(\frac{\Delta}{A}\Bigr) + 2\cdot\frac{\Delta}{A}\sqrt{1 - \bigl(\tfrac{\Delta}{A}\bigr)^{2}}\,\Biggr].
$$

### Step 6 — Evaluate the Second Integral $I_{2} = \displaystyle\int_{\omega t_{1}}^{\pi - \omega t_{1}} \Delta\sin\tau\,d\tau$

$$
I_{2} \;=\; \Delta\Bigl[-\cos\tau\Bigr]_{\omega t_{1}}^{\pi-\omega t_{1}} \;=\; -\Delta\bigl[\cos(\pi - \omega t_{1}) - \cos(\omega t_{1})\bigr].
$$

Since $\cos(\pi - \omega t_{1}) = -\cos(\omega t_{1})$:

$$
I_{2} \;=\; -\Delta\bigl[-\cos(\omega t_{1}) - \cos(\omega t_{1})\bigr] \;=\; 2\Delta\cos(\omega t_{1}) \;=\; 2\Delta\sqrt{1 - \bigl(\tfrac{\Delta}{A}\bigr)^{2}}.
$$

### Step 7 — Combine the Two Integrals

$$
I_{1} - I_{2} \;=\; \frac{A}{2}\Biggl[\pi - 2\arcsin\!\Bigl(\frac{\Delta}{A}\Bigr) + \frac{2\Delta}{A}\sqrt{1 - \bigl(\tfrac{\Delta}{A}\bigr)^{2}}\,\Biggr] - 2\Delta\sqrt{1 - \bigl(\tfrac{\Delta}{A}\bigr)^{2}}.
$$

Pull out a common factor of $A/2$ by writing $2\Delta = A \cdot (2\Delta/A)$:

$$
I_{1} - I_{2} \;=\; \frac{A}{2}\Biggl[\pi - 2\arcsin\!\Bigl(\frac{\Delta}{A}\Bigr) + \frac{2\Delta}{A}\sqrt{1 - \bigl(\tfrac{\Delta}{A}\bigr)^{2}} - \frac{4\Delta}{A}\sqrt{1 - \bigl(\tfrac{\Delta}{A}\bigr)^{2}}\,\Biggr].
$$

Simplify the two radical terms:

$$
\frac{2\Delta}{A} - \frac{4\Delta}{A} \;=\; -\frac{2\Delta}{A},
$$

so

$$
I_{1} - I_{2} \;=\; \frac{A}{2}\Biggl[\pi - 2\arcsin\!\Bigl(\frac{\Delta}{A}\Bigr) - \frac{2\Delta}{A}\sqrt{1 - \bigl(\tfrac{\Delta}{A}\bigr)^{2}}\,\Biggr].
$$

### Step 8 — Assemble $B_{1}$ and Divide by $A$ to Obtain the Describing Function

$$
B_{1} \;=\; \frac{2m}{\pi}\,(I_{1} - I_{2}) \;=\; \frac{mA}{\pi}\Biggl[\pi - 2\arcsin\!\Bigl(\frac{\Delta}{A}\Bigr) - \frac{2\Delta}{A}\sqrt{1 - \bigl(\tfrac{\Delta}{A}\bigr)^{2}}\,\Biggr].
$$

Dividing by $A$:

$$
N(A) \;=\; \frac{B_{1}}{A} \;=\; m - \frac{2m}{\pi}\Biggl[\arcsin\!\Bigl(\frac{\Delta}{A}\Bigr) + \frac{\Delta}{A}\sqrt{1 - \bigl(\tfrac{\Delta}{A}\bigr)^{2}}\,\Biggr].
$$

$$
\boxed{\;N(A) \;=\; m \;-\; \frac{2m}{\pi}\left[\arcsin\!\Bigl(\frac{\Delta}{A}\Bigr) + \frac{\Delta}{A}\sqrt{1 - \bigl(\tfrac{\Delta}{A}\bigr)^{2}}\,\right]\;}
$$

> **Sanity checks built into the formula:**
> - Put $A = \Delta$: $\arcsin(1) = \pi/2$ and $\sqrt{1-1}=0$, so $N(\Delta) = m - \dfrac{2m}{\pi}\cdot\dfrac{\pi}{2} = 0$. ✓
> - Put $A \to \infty$: $\arcsin(0) = 0$ and the radical term $\to 0$, so $N(A) \to m$. ✓

## 3.2 Limit-Cycle Prediction Using the Describing Function

For a unity-feedback closed loop with the deadzone in cascade with a linear plant $G(s)$, the characteristic equation is

$$
1 \;+\; N(A)\,G(j\omega) \;=\; 0 \quad\Longleftrightarrow\quad G(j\omega) \;=\; -\frac{1}{N(A)}.
$$

### Step 1 — Find the Frequency $\omega^{\star}$ from the Linear Part

Since $-1/N(A)$ lies on the **negative real axis** for all $A$, the limit-cycle frequency is determined *solely* by the condition that $G(j\omega)$ crosses the negative real axis:

$$
\operatorname{Im}\bigl\{G(j\omega^{\star})\bigr\} \;=\; 0.
$$

### Step 2 — Find the Amplitude $A^{\star}$ from the Magnitude Condition

$$
\bigl\lvert G(j\omega^{\star})\bigr\rvert \;=\; \frac{1}{N(A^{\star})}.
$$

Equivalently, $A^{\star}$ solves

$$
N(A^{\star}) \;=\; -\frac{1}{G(j\omega^{\star})} \quad \text{(taking the real value of } -1/G \text{ at } \omega^{\star}\text{)}.
$$

Since $G(j\omega^{\star})$ is real and negative at the crossover, $N(A^{\star})$ is real and positive, and $A^{\star}$ is obtained by inverting the DF expression. In practice, students construct a small table of $A$ vs. $N(A)$ or read $A^{\star}$ graphically from the intersection of $G(j\omega)$ and $-1/N(A)$ on the Nyquist diagram.

> [!IMPORTANT] **Stability of the Predicted Limit Cycle**
> If the intersection point is such that *increasing* $A$ moves $-1/N(A)$ into the *unstable* region of the Nyquist plot of $G$, the limit cycle is **stable**. Conversely, if increasing $A$ moves it into the *stable* region, the limit cycle is **unstable** and is therefore not physically observable.

## 3.3 Worked Numerical Illustration (For Examiner Reference)

> **System:** Unity feedback with deadzone $m = 1$, $\Delta = 0.5$, and plant $G(s) = \dfrac{10}{s(s+1)(s+2)}$.

- $G(j\omega) = \dfrac{10}{j\omega\,(1+j\omega)\,(2+j\omega)}$. The imaginary part vanishes when $\omega^{\star} = \sqrt{2}\,\text{rad/s}$. (Verification: setting $\mathrm{Im}\{G\}=0$ gives $\omega^{2} = 2$.)
- $G(j\sqrt{2}) = -\dfrac{10}{(\sqrt{2})(1+2)(3)} \cdot (\text{real}) = -\dfrac{10}{9\sqrt{2}} \approx -0.7857$.
- So $-1/N(A^{\star}) = -0.7857 \Rightarrow N(A^{\star}) = 1.272$.
- Inverting numerically (try $A = 1.2$): $N(1.2) = 1 - \frac{2}{\pi}[\arcsin(0.4167) + 0.4167\sqrt{1-0.1736}] = 1 - \frac{2}{\pi}[0.4300 + 0.3785] = 1 - 0.5141 = 0.486$.
- Try $A = 2.0$: $N(2.0) = 1 - \frac{2}{\pi}[\arcsin(0.25) + 0.25\sqrt{0.9375}] = 1 - \frac{2}{\pi}[0.2527 + 0.2421] = 1 - 0.3149 = 0.685$.
- Try $A = 5.0$: $N(5.0) = 1 - \frac{2}{\pi}[\arcsin(0.1) + 0.1\sqrt{0.99}] = 1 - \frac{2}{\pi}[0.1002 + 0.0995] = 1 - 0.1272 = 0.873$.
- Try $A = 3.0$: $N(3.0) = 1 - \frac{2}{\pi}[\arcsin(1/6) + (1/6)\sqrt{35}/6] \approx 1 - \frac{2}{\pi}[0.1674 + 0.1649] = 1 - 0.2114 = 0.789$.
- Try $A = 2.7$: $N(2.7) \approx 1 - \frac{2}{\pi}[0.1862 + 0.1808] = 1 - 0.2337 = 0.766$.
- The required $N = 1.272$ is **impossible** since $N(A) \le m = 1$ always. **Conclusion:** No intersection exists; the limit cycle does not occur — the closed-loop system reaches steady state without sustained oscillation. (This numerical is for examiner-side reference only and is not part of the question bank.)

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Deadzone Input-Output Characteristic (Block Architecture)

The deadzone characteristic is best represented as a **decision-flow block** showing the three operating regimes. The Mermaid flowchart below uses *purely alphanumeric* node identifiers and contains *no* markdown formatting inside labels, in strict compliance with the rendering safeguards.

```mermaid
graph TD
    IN[Input u] --> CMP1{Absolute value of u greater than Delta}
    CMP1 -- NO --> ZERO[Output y = 0]
    CMP1 -- YES --> CMP2{Is u greater than Delta}
    CMP2 -- YES --> POS[Output y = m times u minus Delta]
    CMP2 -- NO --> NEG[Output y = m times u plus Delta]
    ZERO --> OUT[Final Output y]
    POS --> OUT
    NEG --> OUT
    OUT --> END[Delivered to Plant]
```

> **Reading the diagram:** Start at the input node, branch on the magnitude test, then branch again on the sign of the input. The three operational regimes (silent / positive-active / negative-active) collapse into the single output line.

## 4.2 Closed-Loop Control System with Deadzone (Functional Block Architecture)

The standard KTU architecture for *harmonic-balance analysis* of a deadzone in a unity-feedback loop is shown below.

```mermaid
graph LR
    R[Reference r of t] --> SUMM[Summer Node]
    SUMM -- Error e of t --> NL[Deadzone Nonlinearity N of A]
    NL -- Output y nl --> PLANT[Linear Plant G of s]
    PLANT -- Output c of t --> SUMM
    SUMM --> FBACK[Feedback Path unity gain]
    FBACK --> SUMM
```

> **Block-level interaction summary:**
> 1. The summer produces the error $e(t) = r(t) - c(t)$.
> 2. The deadzone maps the error to a *quasi-linear* first-harmonic gain $N(A)$ where $A = \vert e \vert$ amplitude.
> 3. The linear plant $G(s)$ shapes the frequency response.
> 4. The closed loop is reduced to the equivalent LTI loop $N(A)\cdot G(s)$ in cascade, whose characteristic equation is $1 + N(A)G(j\omega) = 0$.

## 4.3 Negative-Reciprocal Locus $-1/N(A)$ on the Nyquist Plane

```mermaid
graph LR
    AXIS[Nyquist Plane Real Axis Imag Axis] --> LABEL1[At A equals Delta minus reciprocal diverges to negative infinity]
    LABEL1 --> ARROW1[Direction of increasing A]
    ARROW1 --> LABEL2[As A tends to infinity minus reciprocal approaches negative one over m]
```

> **Engineering reading rule:** The locus is a *half-line* on the negative real axis, starting at $-\infty$ (smallest input amplitude $A=\Delta$) and terminating at $-1/m$ (large-signal limit). Any point on this locus that intersects the Nyquist plot of $G(j\omega)$ corresponds to a **limit cycle**, with the **frequency** dictated by $G$ and the **amplitude** dictated by $N$.

## 4.4 Limit-Cycle Stability Logic

```mermaid
graph TD
    INT[Intersection Point of G of j omega and minus one over N of A] --> CHECK{Does minus one over N move outward as A increases}
    CHECK -- YES INTO UNSTABLE --> STABLE[Limit Cycle is STABLE]
    CHECK -- NO INTO STABLE --> UNSTABLE[Limit Cycle is UNSTABLE]
```

> **Kochenburger's stability rule:** Encroachment of the $-1/N$ locus into the *unstable* region of $G$'s Nyquist contour (i.e. the region *not* enclosed by the $-1$ point) as $A$ grows implies a stable limit cycle.

---

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

> [!WARNING] **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Common Pitfalls**
> 1. **Forgetting the silent plateau.** Students frequently plot the deadzone as a *V-shape* (two lines meeting at the origin) instead of a *flat-bottomed trough* with a horizontal segment from $-\Delta$ to $+\Delta$ at $y=0$. The flat plateau is the defining feature; lose 2 marks instantly if it is missing.
> 2. **Sign error in the active region.** The output on the positive side is $m(u-\Delta)$, **not** $m \cdot u$. The shift by $\Delta$ is what guarantees $y=0$ at $u=\Delta$. Marks are deducted if students write $y = mu$.
> 3. **Dropping a factor of 2 in the DF.** The classical error is to forget that $B_1 = (2/\pi)\int_0^{\pi}$, not $(1/\pi)\int_0^{2\pi}$ after applying half-wave symmetry. The factor of 2 propagates and produces a wrong DF.
> 4. **Treating $N(A)$ as a constant.** The DF is *amplitude-dependent*. A student who writes "the equivalent gain is $m$" loses the entire application portion of the problem.
> 5. **Forgetting that the limit cycle may not exist.** A common question demands that you *check* whether $G(j\omega)$ ever crosses the negative real axis inside the interval $[-1/m,\, -\infty)$. If it does not, the limit cycle does not exist. State this explicitly.

---

## 5.1 Part A — Short-Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Q.A.1 `[KTU University Exam — May 2024]`
**Define a simple deadzone non-linearity. Sketch its input-output characteristic and give two practical engineering examples.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

A simple deadzone is a memoryless, static, odd-symmetric non-linearity in which the output is **identically zero** for all input magnitudes within a finite interval $[-\Delta,\,+\Delta]$ around the origin, and is **piecewise-linear** with slope $m$ outside this interval. Mathematically,

$$
y = \begin{cases} m(u - \Delta), & u > \Delta \\ 0, & \vert u \vert \le \Delta \\ m(u + \Delta), & u < -\Delta. \end{cases}
$$

The input-output characteristic is a **flat zero-output plateau** of width $2\Delta$ centred on the origin, flanked by two parallel straight-line segments of slope $m$ that are **shifted** by $\pm\Delta$ along the input axis.

**Engineering examples (any two):** *(i)* Stiction (static friction) in DC servomotors; *(ii)* Threshold voltage of semiconductor diodes; *(iii)* Spool overlap in hydraulic / pneumatic control valves; *(iv)* Gear-train backlash in robotic joint transmissions.

> **Mark split (Examiner pattern):** Definition 1 m, sketch 1 m, two examples 1 m.

---

### Q.A.2 `[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]`
**State the describing function of a simple deadzone and mention its two important boundary values.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

For a simple deadzone of half-width $\Delta$ and active-region slope $m$, the describing function (first-harmonic equivalent gain) is

$$
N(A) \;=\; m - \frac{2m}{\pi}\left[\arcsin\!\Bigl(\tfrac{\Delta}{A}\Bigr) + \frac{\Delta}{A}\sqrt{1 - \bigl(\tfrac{\Delta}{A}\bigr)^{2}}\,\right], \qquad A \ge \Delta.
$$

It is **purely real** (no phase shift) and depends only on the input amplitude $A$.

**Two boundary values (1.5 marks each):**
- At $A = \Delta^{+}$: $N(\Delta) = 0$ — the input is just barely outside the deadzone, the output is an infinitesimally thin pulse, and the first-harmonic amplitude vanishes.
- At $A \to \infty$: $N(A) \to m$ — for very large inputs the deadzone is negligible, and the non-linearity behaves as a pure linear gain $m$.

---

## 5.2 Part B — 14-Mark Questions (Module Internal Choice Pattern)

### Question B.1 `[KTU University Exam — June 2024]` **(14 Marks)**

**B.1(a)** Derive the describing function of a simple deadzone non-linearity. Clearly state the assumptions and the symmetry properties used. **(7 marks)**

**B.1(b)** For a unity-feedback control system with a simple deadzone ($m = 1$, $\Delta = 0.5$) in cascade with a linear plant $G(s) = \dfrac{10}{s(s+1)(s+2)}$, determine whether a limit cycle can exist. If yes, predict its frequency and amplitude. **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution — B.1(a) [7 marks]

1. **[1 mark] Statement of the non-linearity.** Write the three-piece definition of $y$ versus $u$ and the input $u = A\sin\omega t$ with $A > \Delta$.
2. **[1 mark] Output waveform.** Identify the boundary crossing angles $\omega t_1 = \arcsin(\Delta/A)$ and write $y(t)$ in its four-segment piecewise form over one period.
3. **[1 mark] Symmetry exploitation.** State that the non-linearity is *odd-symmetric* (kills all cosine terms and DC) and *half-wave symmetric* (kills all even sine harmonics). Therefore the Fourier series contains *only* odd sine terms and the DF reduces to $N(A) = B_1/A$.
4. **[1 mark] Half-wave simplification.** Reduce $B_1$ to $B_1 = (2/\pi)\int_{\omega t_1}^{\pi - \omega t_1} m(A\sin\tau - \Delta)\sin\tau\,d\tau$ after applying half-wave symmetry.
5. **[1.5 marks] Evaluation of $I_1$.** Carry out $\int \sin^2\tau\,d\tau$ using the identity $\sin^2\tau = (1-\cos 2\tau)/2$ and obtain $I_1 = (A/2)[\pi - 2\omega t_1 + \sin 2\omega t_1]$.
6. **[1.5 marks] Evaluation of $I_2$ and final assembly.** Compute $\int \sin\tau\,d\tau = -\cos\tau$, obtain $I_2 = 2\Delta\cos(\omega t_1)$, combine, substitute $\sin\omega t_1 = \Delta/A$, $\cos\omega t_1 = \sqrt{1 - (\Delta/A)^2}$, and arrive at

$$
N(A) \;=\; m - \frac{2m}{\pi}\left[\arcsin\!\Bigl(\tfrac{\Delta}{A}\Bigr) + \frac{\Delta}{A}\sqrt{1 - \bigl(\tfrac{\Delta}{A}\bigr)^{2}}\,\right].
$$

7. **[1 mark] Boundary check.** Verify $N(\Delta) = 0$ and $N(\infty) = m$ as sanity checks.

#### Model Solution — B.1(b) [7 marks]

1. **[1 mark] Harmonic-balance equation.** $1 + N(A)G(j\omega) = 0 \;\Rightarrow\; G(j\omega) = -1/N(A)$.
2. **[2 marks] Find $\omega^{\star}$.** Substitute $s = j\omega$ into $G(s)$:

$$
G(j\omega) \;=\; \frac{10}{j\omega\,(1+j\omega)\,(2+j\omega)}.
$$

Multiplying numerator and denominator by the conjugate of the denominator and simplifying, the imaginary part vanishes when $\omega^2 = 2$, giving $\omega^{\star} = \sqrt{2} \approx 1.414$ rad/s.

3. **[1 mark] Compute $G(j\omega^{\star})$.**

$$
G(j\sqrt{2}) \;=\; \frac{10}{j\sqrt{2}\,(1+j\sqrt{2})\,(2+j\sqrt{2})} \;=\; \frac{10}{-3\sqrt{2}} \;\approx\; -2.357.
$$

Wait — recompute: the magnitude at this point is

$$
\bigl\lvert G(j\sqrt{2}) \bigr\rvert \;=\; \frac{10}{\sqrt{2}\,\sqrt{1+2}\,\sqrt{4+2}} \;=\; \frac{10}{\sqrt{2}\cdot\sqrt{3}\cdot\sqrt{6}} \;=\; \frac{10}{6} \;=\; 1.667.
$$

Since the phase is $-180°$, the *real value* of $G(j\sqrt{2})$ is $-1.667$.

4. **[2 marks] Magnitude condition.** $-1/N(A^{\star}) = -1.667 \;\Rightarrow\; N(A^{\star}) = 0.6$.
5. **[1 mark] Solve for $A^{\star}$.** Iterate the DF formula with $m=1$, $\Delta=0.5$:
   - $A = 1.0$: $N = 0$.
   - $A = 2.0$: $N \approx 0.685$.
   - $A = 1.5$: $N \approx 0.392$ (compute: $\arcsin(1/3) \approx 0.3398$, $\sqrt{8/9}\approx 0.9428$, so $N \approx 1 - (2/\pi)(0.3398 + 0.3143) \approx 1 - 0.4165 \approx 0.584$).
   - $A = 1.55$: $N \approx 0.610$ (closer match).
   - Therefore $A^{\star} \approx 1.53$ units.
6. **[Optional 1 mark for stability comment.]** Since increasing $A$ moves $-1/N(A)$ *towards* $-1/m = -1$, i.e. *away* from the unstable region of $G$ (in this case the system is stable for small linear gains), the limit cycle is **stable** and physically observable.

> **Result:** $\boxed{\omega^{\star} \approx 1.414 \text{ rad/s}, \quad A^{\star} \approx 1.53 \text{ units (stable limit cycle)}}$.

---

### Question B.2 `[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]` **(14 Marks)** — *Alternative Choice*

**B.2(a)** With the help of a neat sketch, describe the input-output characteristic of a simple deadzone non-linearity. List its key mathematical properties and two physical examples. **(7 marks)**

**B.2(b)** Explain the procedure for predicting the existence, frequency, and amplitude of a limit cycle in a feedback system containing a simple deadzone, using the describing-function method. Illustrate the stability check using Kochenburger's rule. **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution — B.2(a) [7 marks]

1. **[2 marks] Sketch.** Draw a coordinate system $(u, y)$:
   - Horizontal segment from $u = -\Delta$ to $u = +\Delta$ at $y = 0$ (the silent plateau).
   - Right segment: line $y = m(u-\Delta)$ starting at $(+\Delta, 0)$ with slope $m$.
   - Left segment: line $y = m(u+\Delta)$ starting at $(-\Delta, 0)$ with slope $m$.
   - Label the kink points at $(\pm\Delta, 0)$ clearly.
2. **[2 marks] Mathematical properties (any four for full marks):**
   - Memoryless / static.
   - Odd-symmetric.
   - Single-valued (no multi-valued branches, no hysteresis).
   - Sector-bounded: $0 \le u y \le m u^2$.
   - DF is purely real, lies in $[0, m]$.
3. **[2 marks] Two physical examples:**
   - Static friction (stiction) in DC motors.
   - Threshold voltage of diodes / op-amp offsets.
   - (Any two of: hydraulic valve overlap, gear backlash, pneumatic actuator deadband.)
4. **[1 mark] Equation** — write the three-piece definition with $m$ and $\Delta$.

#### Model Solution — B.2(b) [7 marks]

1. **[1.5 marks] Limit-cycle concept.** Define a limit cycle as a *sustained periodic oscillation of fixed amplitude and frequency* that arises in non-linear systems without external periodic excitation.
2. **[1.5 marks] Harmonic-balance assumption.** Assume the loop is "low-pass" so that the higher harmonics generated by the non-linearity are filtered out by $G(s)$. Then the closed loop reduces to an *equivalent LTI loop* with gain $N(A)$, and the characteristic equation becomes $1 + N(A)G(j\omega) = 0$.
3. **[1.5 marks] Two-step procedure.**
   - *Step 1 (Frequency):* Find $\omega^{\star}$ from $\mathrm{Im}\{G(j\omega^{\star})\} = 0$, because $-1/N(A)$ is purely real.
   - *Step 2 (Amplitude):* Set $N(A^{\star}) = -1/G(j\omega^{\star})$ and solve for $A^{\star}$ from the inverse of the DF formula.
4. **[1 mark] Existence criterion.** A limit cycle exists **iff** the Nyquist plot of $G(j\omega)$ crosses the negative real axis *inside* the interval $[-1/m,\, -\infty)$ that is swept by $-1/N(A)$. If the crossing falls *outside* this interval, no intersection exists and the system reaches a stable equilibrium.
5. **[1.5 marks] Kochenburger's stability rule.** Examine the *direction* of motion of $-1/N(A)$ on the negative real axis as $A$ increases. The locus moves *from* $-\infty$ (small $A$) *towards* $-1/m$ (large $A$). If the locus *encroaches* into the **unstable region** of $G$'s Nyquist plot as $A$ increases, the limit cycle is **stable**. If it encroaches into the **stable region** of $G$'s Nyquist plot, the limit cycle is **unstable** and is physically unobservable.

> **Examiner remark:** Always conclude with a clear statement of the *physical* interpretation — a stable limit cycle is a self-sustained oscillation that the engineer can see on an oscilloscope; an unstable one is a theoretical curiosity that cannot be triggered by any physical initial condition.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!TIP] **Rapid-Revision Checklist — Print This Before the Exam**

- **Definition:** A deadzone is a *flat zero-output* zone of width $2\Delta$ around the origin, with linear segments of slope $m$ on either side. It is *odd-symmetric* and *memoryless*.
- **Three-piece equation:**

$$
y = \begin{cases} m(u - \Delta), & u > \Delta \\ 0, & \vert u \vert \le \Delta \\ m(u + \Delta), & u < -\Delta. \end{cases}
$$

- **DF formula (memorise verbatim):**

$$
N(A) \;=\; m - \frac{2m}{\pi}\!\left[\arcsin\!\Bigl(\tfrac{\Delta}{A}\Bigr) + \frac{\Delta}{A}\sqrt{1 - \bigl(\tfrac{\Delta}{A}\bigr)^{2}}\,\right], \quad A \ge \Delta.
$$

- **Two extreme values:** $N(\Delta) = 0$ and $N(\infty) = m$.
- **Phase = 0:** $N(A)$ is purely real; the non-linearity introduces **no phase shift**, only a gain reduction.
- **$-1/N(A)$ locus:** Half-line on the negative real axis from $-\infty$ (at $A = \Delta$) to $-1/m$ (at $A \to \infty$).
- **Harmonic balance equation:** $1 + N(A)G(j\omega) = 0$.
- **Limit cycle frequency** comes from $\mathrm{Im}\{G(j\omega^{\star})\} = 0$.
- **Limit cycle amplitude** comes from $N(A^{\star}) = -1/G(j\omega^{\star})$.
- **Existence test:** Does $G(j\omega)$ cross the negative real axis inside $[-1/m,\, -\infty)$? If *no*, no limit cycle.
- **Stability test (Kochenburger):** As $A$ grows, $-1/N$ moves *towards* $-1/m$. If this direction is *into* the unstable region of $G$'s Nyquist, the limit cycle is **stable**.
- **Practical causes:** stiction, valve overlap, diode threshold, gear backlash, op-amp offset.
- **Distinguish from saturation:** deadzone = silent middle; saturation = silent ends. They are mirror-image non-linearities on the $(u,y)$ plane.
- **Distinguish from relay (on-off):** relay output is binary $\pm M$; deadzone output is a smooth function of $u$ outside the silent band.
- **Units check:** $m$ has units of *output / input*; $\Delta$ has units of *input*; $A$ has units of *input*; $N(A)$ is dimensionless.
- **Default KTU assumption:** $m = 1$ unless stated otherwise — always confirm from the problem statement.

> **One-Line Mantra:** *"Deadzone kills small signals, lets large ones through with reduced effective gain — its DF is a real-valued function of amplitude only, and its negative reciprocal is a half-line on the negative real axis."*

---

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Saturation or Limiter, Closed Loop Stability Using Describing Function, Stability of Limit Cycles
topics.content:
<!-- SECTION_1_START -->

> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PERAT634 - NON-LINEAR CONTROL
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Saturation or Limiter, Closed Loop Stability Using Describing Function, Stability of Limit Cycles

# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Saturation Nonlinearity (The Limiter)

### Formal Definition
**Saturation** (also called a **Limiter**) is a memoryless, static, single-valued nonlinear element in which the output increases linearly with the input over a finite range, and beyond that range the output remains **constant** at a saturation level (the **ceiling**) regardless of further increases in input amplitude. Mathematically, it is a piecewise-linear function whose slope in the active region is the **gain k**, while outside the linear zone the slope drops to **zero**.

The standard input-output relationship of an ideal saturation element with linear gain **k** and saturation level **M** is:

$$
y(t) = \begin{cases} k \cdot M & \text{if } e(t) > +M \\ k \cdot e(t) & \text{if } -M \le e(t) \le +M \\ -k \cdot M & \text{if } e(t) < -M \end{cases}
$$

where **e(t)** is the input error signal, **y(t)** is the output, **k > 0** is the linear-region slope, and **M > 0** is the saturation (break-point) level.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight:** In KTU examination questions, the saturation nonlinearity is virtually always specified by its slope **k** and the breakpoint **a** (or **M**). Students are expected to derive its **Describing Function (DF)** explicitly, then use it to find **limit cycle parameters** — a classic 14-mark Part B question.

### Conceptual Analogy — The Water Tap
Imagine a domestic water tap connected to a pump. When you open the tap slightly, water flow increases proportionally with how much you turn the handle. That proportional regime is the **linear region**. But once the tap is fully open, no matter how hard you crank the handle, the flow rate cannot exceed the pipe's maximum capacity. The flow **saturates** — it flattens out at a ceiling. Saturation in control systems behaves exactly the same way: the actuator (motor, valve, amplifier) has a physical limit beyond which additional input produces no extra output.

### Real-World Engineering Examples
- **DC motor speed** limited by armature voltage saturation.
- **Hydraulic valve** flow limited by maximum port opening.
- **Operational amplifier** output clamped at ±V_sat (≈ ±13 V on a ±15 V supply).
- **Control surface deflection** of an aircraft limited by structural stops.

> [!NOTE]
> **Single-valued vs. Multi-valued:** Saturation is **single-valued** (for every input there is exactly one output). This single-valued property is precisely what permits the use of the **Describing Function method**, since the output contains no input-frequency-dependent phase shift — only amplitude-dependent gain compression.

---

## 1.2 Closed-Loop Stability Using Describing Function

### Formal Definition
The **Describing Function (DF)** of a nonlinear element is the **quasi-linear gain** that approximates the nonlinear element's response to a sinusoidal input by retaining only the **fundamental Fourier component** of the output and discarding all higher harmonics. For a memoryless nonlinearity NL with input **e(t) = A sin(ωt)** and output **y(t)**, the DF is defined as the complex ratio:

$$
N(A) \;=\; \frac{Y_1}{A}\,\angle\, 0 \;=\; \frac{B_1 + j\,C_1}{A}
$$

where **B₁** and **C₁** are the fundamental Fourier coefficients of the periodic output y(t). Because saturation is an **odd, single-valued, memoryless** nonlinearity, **C₁ = 0** and the DF reduces to a **real, amplitude-dependent gain**:

$$
N(A) \;=\; \frac{B_1(A)}{A}
$$

### Closed-Loop Stability Criterion (Kochenburger's Extension of Nyquist)
