# Section 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Sign Definiteness — Quadratic Form Analysis (Most Exam-Relevant)

In KTU examinations, the scalar function is almost always a **quadratic form**:

$$V(\mathbf{x}) = \mathbf{x}^{T}\mathbf{P}\mathbf{x}$$

where $\mathbf{P}$ is a real symmetric $n \times n$ matrix. The sign definiteness of $V$ is **completely determined by the eigenvalues of $\mathbf{P}$**, known as **Sylvester's Criterion**.

### 2.1.1 Sylvester's Criterion (For Positive Definiteness)

A real symmetric matrix $\mathbf{P}$ is positive definite if and only if **all its leading principal minors are strictly positive**:

$$p_1 = p_{11} > 0, \quad p_2 = \begin{vmatrix} p_{11} & p_{12} \\ p_{21} & p_{22} \end{vmatrix} > 0, \quad \ldots, \quad p_n = \det(\mathbf{P}) > 0$$

For **negative definiteness**, alternate signs appear:

$$p_1 < 0, \quad p_2 > 0, \quad p_3 < 0, \ldots$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> This is the **most heavily tested criterion** in KTU boards. Master the determinant calculations of $2 \times 2$ and $3 \times 3$ matrices.

---

## 2.2 The Time Derivative $\dot{V}(\mathbf{x})$ — The Key to Stability

Along a system trajectory $\mathbf{x}(t)$ satisfying $\dot{\mathbf{x}} = \mathbf{f}(\mathbf{x})$, the total derivative is:

$$\dot{V}(\mathbf{x}) = \frac{dV}{dt} = \nabla V(\mathbf{x}) \cdot \mathbf{f}(\mathbf{x}) = \sum_{i=1}^{n} \frac{\partial V}{\partial x_i} \dot{x}_i$$

For a quadratic form $V(\mathbf{x}) = \mathbf{x}^{T}\mathbf{P}\mathbf{x}$:

$$\dot{V}(\mathbf{x}) = \dot{\mathbf{x}}^{T}\mathbf{P}\mathbf{x} + \mathbf{x}^{T}\mathbf{P}\dot{\mathbf{x}} = \mathbf{x}^{T}(\mathbf{P}\mathbf{A} + \mathbf{A}^{T}\mathbf{P})\mathbf{x} = -\mathbf{x}^{T}\mathbf{Q}\mathbf{x}$$

where $\mathbf{A}$ is the system Jacobian and $\mathbf{Q} = -(\mathbf{P}\mathbf{A} + \mathbf{A}^{T}\mathbf{P})$ is symmetric.

> [!NOTE]
> The matrix equation $\mathbf{P}\mathbf{A} + \mathbf{A}^{T}\mathbf{P} = -\mathbf{Q}$ is called the **Lyapunov Equation**. For a given stable $\mathbf{A}$, we choose any $\mathbf{Q} > 0$ and solve for $\mathbf{P} > 0$.

---

## 2.3 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet (Cheat Sheet)

| Theorem | Condition on $V(\mathbf{x})$ | Condition on $\dot{V}(\mathbf{x})$ | Conclusion |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Lyapunov's Stability Theorem (Theorem 1)** | $V(\mathbf{x})$ is **PD** in a region $B_r$ | $\dot{V}(\mathbf{x}) \leq 0$ (NSD) in $B_r$ | The origin is **stable** |
| **Lyapunov's Asymptotic Stability Theorem (Theorem 2)** | $V(\mathbf{x})$ is **PD** in a region $B_r$ | $\dot{V}(\mathbf{x}) < 0$ (ND) in $B_r$ | The origin is **asymptotically stable** |
| **Lyapunov's Instability Theorem (Theorem 3)** | $V(\mathbf{0}) = 0$ and $V(\mathbf{x})$ has both positive and negative values arbitrarily close to origin | $\dot{V}(\mathbf{x})$ is **PD** (Chetaev's variant) | The origin is **unstable** |
| **Barbashin-Krasovskii Theorem** | $V(\mathbf{x})$ is **PD** in **entire** $\mathbb{R}^n$ | $\dot{V}(\mathbf{x}) < 0$ for all $\mathbf{x} \neq \mathbf{0}$ | The origin is **globally asymptotically stable (GAS)** |

### 2.3.1 Key Definitions (Must Memorize)

| Term | Mathematical Statement | Physical Meaning |
|---|---|---|
| **Stability (Lyapunov sense)** | $\forall \, \epsilon > 0, \; \exists \, \delta > 0 : \Vert \mathbf{x}(0) \Vert < \delta \Rightarrow \Vert \mathbf{x}(t) \Vert < \epsilon \, \forall t \geq 0$ | Trajectory stays close to origin if started close |
| **Asymptotic Stability** | Stable **and** $\lim_{t \to \infty} \Vert \mathbf{x}(t) \Vert = 0$ | Trajectory **converges** to origin |
| **Global Asymptotic Stability (GAS)** | Asymptotically stable for **all** $\mathbf{x}(0) \in \mathbb{R}^n$ | Stability holds everywhere, not just locally |
| **Exponential Stability** | $\Vert \mathbf{x}(t) \Vert \leq k \Vert \mathbf{x}(0) \Vert e^{-\alpha t}$ for some $k, \alpha > 0$ | Convergence is bounded by exponential decay |

### 2.3.2 Sign Definiteness of Common Scalar Functions

| $V(\mathbf{x})$ | Sign Definiteness | Reasoning |
|---|---|---|
| $V = x_1^2 + 2x_2^2$ | **PD** | Sum of squared terms with positive coefficients |
| $V = (x_1 + x_2)^2$ | **PSD** | Zero along line $x_1 = -x_2$ |
| $V = x_1^2 - x_2^2$ | **Indefinite** | $V(1,0) = 1 > 0$ and $V(0,1) = -1 < 0$ |
| $V = -x_1^2 - x_2^2$ | **ND** | Negative for all non-zero $\mathbf{x}$ |
| $V = \sin^2(x_1) + x_2^2$ | **PD** (local) | $\sin^2(x_1) > 0$ for $x_1 \neq k\pi$, $k \in \mathbb{Z}$ |

---

## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

### 2.4.1 Power System Stability

A **swing equation** of a synchronous generator is highly non-linear:

$$M\ddot{\delta} + D\dot{\delta} + P_{\max}\sin(\delta) = P_m$$

The energy function $V(\delta, \dot{\delta}) = \frac{1}{2}M\dot{\delta}^2 - P_m\delta - P_{\max}\cos(\delta)$ provides **transient energy margin** — engineers use this to compute the **critical clearing time (CCT)** before system collapse.

### 2.4.2 Robot Manipulator Control

The total energy of an $n$-joint robot:

$$V(\mathbf{q}, \dot{\mathbf{q}}) = \frac{1}{2}\dot{\mathbf{q}}^{T}\mathbf{M}(\mathbf{q})\dot{\mathbf{q}} + U(\mathbf{q})$$

where $\mathbf{M}(\mathbf{q})$ is the inertia matrix (PD) and $U(\mathbf{q})$ is potential energy. The **Computed Torque Control** and **PD+ control** use this $V$ with $\dot{V} = -\mathbf{K}_D \dot{\mathbf{q}}^{T}\dot{\mathbf{q}} < 0$ to prove asymptotic stability.

### 2.4.3 Adaptive Control (Lyaspunov-Based Design)

For a system with unknown parameter $\theta$, define the parameter error $\tilde{\theta} = \theta - \hat{\theta}$. A Lyapunov function:

$$V = \mathbf{x}^{T}\mathbf{P}\mathbf{x} + \gamma^{-1}\tilde{\theta}^{2}, \quad \gamma > 0$$

yields an **adaptive law** by forcing $\dot{V} \leq 0$. This is the basis of **MIT rule** and **MRAC (Model Reference Adaptive Control)**.

# Section 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: Sign Definiteness of a 2×2 Quadratic Form

Consider the scalar function:

$$V(\mathbf{x}) = \mathbf{x}^{T}\mathbf{P}\mathbf{x} = \begin{bmatrix} x_1 & x_2 \end{bmatrix} \begin{bmatrix} p_{11} & p_{12} \\ p_{12} & p_{22} \end{bmatrix} \begin{bmatrix} x_1 \\ x_2 \end{bmatrix}$$

### Step 1: Expand the Quadratic Form

Multiply the matrices:

$$V(\mathbf{x}) = \begin{bmatrix} x_1 & x_2 \end{bmatrix} \begin{bmatrix} p_{11}x_1 + p_{12}x_2 \\ p_{12}x_1 + p_{22}x_2 \end{bmatrix}$$

$$V(\mathbf{x}) = x_1(p_{11}x_1 + p_{12}x_2) + x_2(p_{12}x_1 + p_{22}x_2)$$

$$V(\mathbf{x}) = p_{11}x_1^2 + 2p_{12}x_1 x_2 + p_{22}x_2^2$$

**Conversion Logic:** The off-diagonal term appears twice because $\mathbf{P}$ is symmetric, hence $p_{12} = p_{21}$ contributes $x_1 p_{12} x_2$ and $x_2 p_{21} x_1$, summing to $2p_{12}x_1 x_2$.

### Step 2: Apply Sylvester's Criterion

For $V(\mathbf{x}) > 0$ for all $\mathbf{x} \neq \mathbf{0}$ (positive definiteness), we need:

$$\Delta_1 = p_{11} > 0$$

$$\Delta_2 = \begin{vmatrix} p_{11} & p_{12} \\ p_{12} & p_{22} \end{vmatrix} = p_{11}p_{22} - p_{12}^2 > 0$$

If both $\Delta_1 > 0$ and $\Delta_2 > 0$, then $V(\mathbf{x})$ is **positive definite**.

> [!NOTE]
> **Conversion Logic:** $\Delta_2 > 0$ ensures the "discriminant" of the quadratic form is negative (the function doesn't change sign), and $\Delta_1 > 0$ ensures the leading coefficient is positive (the bowl opens upward).

---

## 3.2 Worked Example — Sign Definiteness Classification

**Problem:** Classify the sign definiteness of $V(\mathbf{x}) = 3x_1^2 + 2x_1 x_2 + x_2^2$.

### Step 1: Identify the $\mathbf{P}$ Matrix

$$V(\mathbf{x}) = \mathbf{x}^{T}\begin{bmatrix} 3 & 1 \\ 1 & 1 \end{bmatrix}\mathbf{x}$$

**Reasoning:** Comparing with $V = p_{11}x_1^2 + 2p_{12}x_1 x_2 + p_{22}x_2^2$, we get $p_{11} = 3$, $p_{12} = 1$ (since $2p_{12} = 2$), $p_{22} = 1$.

### Step 2: Apply Sylvester's Criterion

$$\Delta_1 = 3 > 0 \quad \checkmark$$

$$\Delta_2 = (3)(1) - (1)^2 = 3 - 1 = 2 > 0 \quad \checkmark$$

### Step 3: Conclusion

Both leading principal minors are positive, so $\mathbf{P} > 0$ and $V(\mathbf{x})$ is **Positive Definite (PD)**.

---

## 3.3 Derivation: Lyapunov's Stability Theorem (Theorem 1)

### Statement

> If a scalar function $V(\mathbf{x})$ exists in a region $B_r$ such that:
> 1. $V(\mathbf{x})$ is **continuous**, with continuous partial derivatives
> 2. $V(\mathbf{0}) = 0$
> 3. $V(\mathbf{x}) > 0$ for all $\mathbf{x} \in B_r - \{\mathbf{0}\}$ (PD)
> 4. $\dot{V}(\mathbf{x}) \leq 0$ (NSD) in $B_r$
>
> Then the equilibrium $\mathbf{x} = \mathbf{0}$ is **stable** in the sense of Lyapunov.

### Proof Sketch

#### Step 1: Setup the Problem

Let $\epsilon > 0$ be given (arbitrary). We need to find $\delta > 0$ such that:

$$\Vert \mathbf{x}(0) \Vert < \delta \quad \Rightarrow \quad \Vert \mathbf{x}(t) \Vert < \epsilon, \quad \forall \, t \geq 0$$

#### Step 2: Define a Level Surface

Let $\Omega_\alpha = \{ \mathbf{x} \in B_\epsilon : V(\mathbf{x}) \leq \alpha \}$ for some small $\alpha > 0$.

Since $V(\mathbf{0}) = 0$ and $V$ is continuous and positive definite, we can choose $\alpha$ small enough that $\Omega_\alpha$ is a non-empty closed set contained within $B_\epsilon$.

**Conversion Logic:** "Contained within $B_\epsilon$" means we have bounded the level set inside the $\epsilon$-ball — the geometric "energy bowl" fits inside the allowed region.

#### Step 3: Use the Hypothesis $\dot{V} \leq 0$

Since $\dot{V}(\mathbf{x}) \leq 0$, the function $V(\mathbf{x}(t))$ is non-increasing along trajectories:

$$V(\mathbf{x}(t)) \leq V(\mathbf{x}(0)) \quad \forall \, t \geq 0$$

If we start on the level surface $\Omega_\alpha$ (i.e., $V(\mathbf{x}(0)) \leq \alpha$), then $V(\mathbf{x}(t)) \leq \alpha$ for all $t$, hence $\mathbf{x}(t) \in \Omega_\alpha \subset B_\epsilon$.

#### Step 4: Find $\delta$

Since $V(\mathbf{x})$ is continuous and $V(\mathbf{0}) = 0$, define:

$$\delta = \min_{\Vert \mathbf{x} \Vert = \epsilon} V(\mathbf{x})$$

(Actually, choose $\delta$ such that $\{ \Vert \mathbf{x} \Vert \leq \delta \} \subset \Omega_\alpha$.)

Then $\Vert \mathbf{x}(0) \Vert < \delta$ implies $V(\mathbf{x}(0)) \leq \alpha$, and by Step 3, $\mathbf{x}(t)$ stays in $B_\epsilon$.

#### Step 5: Conclusion

This $\delta$ satisfies the Lyapunov stability definition, hence the origin is **stable**.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Key Insight:** The condition $\dot{V} \leq 0$ is critical. If $\dot{V}$ were positive somewhere, the trajectory could "climb" the level surfaces outward, potentially escaping $B_\epsilon$.

---

## 3.4 Derivation: Lyapunov's Asymptotic Stability Theorem (Theorem 2)

### Statement

> If in addition to the conditions of Theorem 1, $\dot{V}(\mathbf{x}) < 0$ (Negative Definite) for all $\mathbf{x} \in B_r - \{\mathbf{0}\}$, then the origin is **asymptotically stable**.

### Proof Sketch

#### Step 1: Stability Holds

By Theorem 1, the origin is stable. So given $\epsilon$, we can find $\delta_1$ such that $\Vert \mathbf{x}(0) \Vert < \delta_1$ implies $\Vert \mathbf{x}(t) \Vert < \epsilon$ for all $t \geq 0$.

#### Step 2: Convergence Argument

Since $\dot{V}(\mathbf{x}) < 0$ for $\mathbf{x} \neq \mathbf{0}$:

- $V(\mathbf{x}(t))$ is **strictly decreasing** along any non-trivial trajectory
- $V(\mathbf{x}(t)) \geq 0$ always (since $V$ is PD)
- Therefore $V(\mathbf{x}(t))$ is bounded below and decreasing, so it converges to a limit $V_\infty \geq 0$.

**Conversion Logic:** A bounded monotone sequence converges. Here, $V(\mathbf{x}(t))$ is a continuous analog of such a sequence.

#### Step 3: Show the Limit is Zero

Suppose $V_\infty > 0$. Then $\mathbf{x}(t)$ stays in the set $L = \{ \mathbf{x} \in B_\epsilon : V(\mathbf{x}) \geq V_\infty \}$ which is bounded away from origin.

Since $\dot{V}$ is continuous and strictly negative on the closed set $L$ (compact), it attains a maximum value $-k < 0$:

$$\dot{V}(\mathbf{x}) \leq -k < 0 \quad \forall \, \mathbf{x} \in L$$

Integrating:

$$V(\mathbf{x}(t)) = V(\mathbf{x}(0)) + \int_{0}^{t} \dot{V}(\mathbf{x}(\tau)) \, d\tau \leq V(\mathbf{x}(0)) - kt$$

As $t \to \infty$, the right side goes to $-\infty$, contradicting $V(\mathbf{x}(t)) \geq 0$.

Hence $V_\infty = 0$, and since $V$ is PD, $\mathbf{x}(t) \to \mathbf{0}$.

> [!NOTE]
> This is the famous "**LaSalle's Invariance Principle**" argument generalized — the actual LaSalle's theorem relaxes the strict negativity to merely $\dot{V} \leq 0$ with no trajectory staying in $\dot{V} = 0$ except at the origin.

---

## 3.5 Derivation: Chetaev's Instability Theorem (Theorem 3)

### Statement

> If there exists a function $V(\mathbf{x})$ with $V(\mathbf{0}) = 0$ and a region $B_r$ such that:
> 1. $V(\mathbf{x})$ takes positive values arbitrarily close to origin (i.e., in every neighborhood there exists $\mathbf{x}$ with $V(\mathbf{x}) > 0$)
> 2. $\dot{V}(\mathbf{x})$ is **positive definite** in the region where $V(\mathbf{x}) > 0$
>
> Then the origin is **unstable**.

### Proof Sketch

#### Step 1: Construct the Region

Let $\mathcal{U} = \{ \mathbf{x} \in B_r : V(\mathbf{x}) > 0 \}$. This is non-empty by hypothesis 1 and open by continuity.

#### Step 2: Trajectories Move Outward

For $\mathbf{x} \in \mathcal{U}$, $\dot{V} > 0$, so $V$ increases along trajectories. This means trajectories move to higher level surfaces — away from origin in the $V$ sense.

#### Step 3: Boundedness Argument

Since $\dot{V}$ is continuous and PD on a closed subset of $\mathcal{U}$ (with $V \geq \alpha > 0$), we have $\dot{V} \geq \beta > 0$ there. Then $V(\mathbf{x}(t)) \geq \alpha + \beta t$ grows without bound, forcing $\mathbf{x}(t)$ to escape any bounded region.

#### Step 4: Conclusion

The trajectory escapes $B_r$ in finite time, violating the stability condition. Hence the origin is **unstable**.

---

## 3.6 Comprehensive Worked Example: Stability Analysis Using Lyapunov

**System:** $\dot{x}_1 = x_2, \quad \dot{x}_2 = -x_1 - x_2$

**Candidate Lyapunov Function:** $V(\mathbf{x}) = x_1^2 + x_2^2$ (Energy function)

### Step 1: Verify $V$ is Positive Definite

$V(\mathbf{x}) = x_1^2 + x_2^2 = \begin{bmatrix} x_1 & x_2 \end{bmatrix} \begin{bmatrix} 1 & 0 \\ 0 & 1 \end{bmatrix} \begin{bmatrix} x_1 \\ x_2 \end{bmatrix}$

The matrix is $\mathbf{P} = \mathbf{I}$ with eigenvalues $1, 1 > 0$, so $V$ is **PD** (and radially unbounded — for global results).

### Step 2: Compute $\dot{V}(\mathbf{x})$

$$\dot{V} = \frac{\partial V}{\partial x_1}\dot{x}_1 + \frac{\partial V}{\partial x_2}\dot{x}_2$$

Compute partial derivatives:

$$\frac{\partial V}{\partial x_1} = 2x_1, \quad \frac{\partial V}{\partial x_2} = 2x_2$$

Substitute the system dynamics:

$$\dot{V} = 2x_1(\dot{x}_1) + 2x_2(\dot{x}_2) = 2x_1(x_2) + 2x_2(-x_1 - x_2)$$

$$\dot{V} = 2x_1 x_2 - 2x_1 x_2 - 2x_2^2$$

$$\dot{V} = -2x_2^2$$

### Step 3: Classify $\dot{V}$

$\dot{V} = -2x_2^2 \leq 0$ for all $\mathbf{x}$, and $\dot{V} = 0$ not only at $\mathbf{x} = \mathbf{0}$ but also on the line $x_2 = 0$.

So $\dot{V}$ is **Negative Semi-Definite (NSD)**.

### Step 4: Apply Theorem 1

By Theorem 1: $V$ is PD, $\dot{V}$ is NSD $\Rightarrow$ origin is **stable**.

### Step 5: Apply LaSalle's Invariance Principle

The set where $\dot{V} = 0$ is $\mathcal{S} = \{ \mathbf{x} : x_2 = 0 \}$. For $\mathbf{x} \in \mathcal{S}$ with $\mathbf{x} \neq \mathbf{0}$:

$$\dot{x}_2 = -x_1 - x_2 = -x_1 \neq 0 \quad (\text{since } x_1 \neq 0)$$

So $\mathbf{x}$ does not stay in $\mathcal{S}$ — it leaves $\mathcal{S}$ immediately. The largest invariant set in $\mathcal{S}$ is just the origin $\{\mathbf{0}\}$.

By LaSalle: origin is **asymptotically stable**. Since $V$ is radially unbounded, it is also **globally asymptotically stable (GAS)**.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Conversion Logic Summary:** The energy $V$ is dissipated only through the $x_2$ coordinate (velocity), but the system coupling forces $x_1$ to change, ensuring complete convergence.

# Section 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Sign Definiteness Classification Tree

The following Mermaid diagram maps the complete classification logic for sign definiteness of scalar functions:

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A["Scalar Function V of x"] --> B{"Is V of 0 equal to 0?"}
    B -- "No" --> C["Not a Lyapunov Candidate"]
    B -- "Yes" --> D{"Does V have a uniform sign near origin?"}
    
    D -- "V is strictly positive" --> E{"Is V greater than 0 only at origin?"}
    E -- "Yes, V greater than 0 for all x not equal to 0" --> F["Positive Definite PD"]
    E -- "No, V equals 0 on a non trivial set" --> G["Positive Semi Definite PSD"]
    
    D -- "V is strictly negative" --> H{"Is V less than 0 only at origin?"}
    H -- "Yes, V less than 0 for all x not equal to 0" --> I["Negative Definite ND"]
    H -- "No, V equals 0 on a non trivial set" --> J["Negative Semi Definite NSD"]
    
    D -- "V takes both positive and negative values" --> K["Indefinite"]
    
    F --> L["Use Theorem 1 if V dot is NSD: Stable"]
    F --> M["Use Theorem 2 if V dot is ND: Asymptotic Stability"]
    
    G --> N["Use Theorem 1 if V dot is NSD: Stable only"]
    I --> O["Use Theorem 2: Asymptotic Stability"]
    J --> P["Use LaSalle Invariance Principle"]
    K --> Q["Use Chetaev Theorem for Instability"]
    
    style F fill:#90EE90
    style I fill:#FFB6C1
    style K fill:#FFD700
    style L fill:#87CEEB
    style M fill:#87CEEB
    style Q fill:#FF6347
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Reading the Diagram:** Start at node A, follow the decision branches to determine the classification. The colored nodes (PD, ND, Indefinite) are the three primary categories, with the action nodes (L, M, Q) on the right showing which Lyapunov theorem applies.

---

## 4.2 Lyapunov Stability Analysis — Block-Level Functional Architecture

The following Mermaid block diagram illustrates the procedural flow of a typical Lyapunov stability analysis:

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A["Step 1: Identify System Dynamics x dot equals f of x"] --> B["Step 2: Verify Origin is Equilibrium: f of 0 equals 0"]
    B --> C["Step 3: Propose Candidate V of x"]
    C --> D{"Step 4: Is V Positive Definite?"}
    
    D -- "No" --> E["Step 4a: Try New V or Check Chetaev Instability"]
    D -- "Yes" --> F["Step 5: Compute V dot along trajectories"]
    
    F --> G{"Step 6: Sign of V dot?"}
    
    G -- "V dot less than 0 ND" --> H["Step 7a: Apply Theorem 2: Asymptotic Stability"]
    G -- "V dot less than or equal to 0 NSD" --> I["Step 7b: Apply Theorem 1: Stability"]
    G -- "V dot greater than 0" --> J["Step 7c: Try Chetaev Instability"]
    G -- "V dot Indefinite" --> K["Step 7d: Inconclusive, try different V"]
    
    H --> L{"Step 8: Is V radially unbounded?"}
    L -- "Yes" --> M["Conclusion: Globally Asymptotically Stable GAS"]
    L -- "No" --> N["Conclusion: Locally Asymptotically Stable"]
    
    I --> O{"Step 9: Apply LaSalle Invariance Principle?"}
    O -- "Invariant set is origin only" --> H
    O -- "Larger invariant set" --> P["Conclusion: Stable but not Asymptotic"]
    
    J --> Q["Conclusion: Origin is Unstable"]
    
    style H fill:#90EE90
    style I fill:#87CEEB
    style M fill:#32CD32
    style N fill:#98FB98
    style Q fill:#FF6347
    style P fill:#FFA500
```

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Block Diagram Insight:** This is the canonical "decision flow" used by control engineers when applying Lyapunov's method. In KTU exams, presenting a similar flowchart in your answer earns extra presentation marks.

---

## 4.3 Stability Theorem Relationship Diagram

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A["Lyapunov Stability Theorems"] --> B["Theorem 1: Stability"]
    A --> C["Theorem 2: Asymptotic Stability"]
    A --> D["Theorem 3: Instability via Chetaev"]
    
    B --> B1["Requires: V PD and V dot NSD"]
    B1 --> B2["Conclusion: Stable in Lyapunov Sense"]
    
    C --> C1["Requires: V PD and V dot ND"]
    C1 --> C2["Asymptotic Stability"]
    C1 --> C3["Add: V radially unbounded gives GAS"]
    
    D --> D1["Requires: V takes both signs and V dot PD on positive V region"]
    D1 --> D2["Conclusion: Unstable Origin"]
    
    C2 --> E["Stronger Conclusion: Use LaSalle Extension"]
    E --> E1["V dot NSD but only origin in invariant set implies Asymptotic Stability"]
    
    style B2 fill:#87CEEB
    style C2 fill:#90EE90
    style D2 fill:#FF6347
    style E1 fill:#FFD700
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering Interpretation:** Theorem 1 is the weakest guarantee (trajectory stays bounded near origin). Theorem 2 is stronger (trajectory actually converges). Theorem 3 (Chetaev) is the impossibility result (no convergence possible).
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# Section 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1 [KTU University Exam - May 2024]

**Define the following with respect to scalar function $V(\mathbf{x})$: (i) Positive definite, (ii) Positive semi-definite, (iii) Negative definite. Give one example for each.**

#### Model Answer

**(i) Positive Definite (PD):** A scalar function $V(\mathbf{x})$ is said to be positive definite in a region $B_r = \{ \mathbf{x} : \Vert \mathbf{x} \Vert < r \}$ if:
- $V(\mathbf{0}) = 0$
- $V(\mathbf{x}) > 0$ for all $\mathbf{x} \in B_r, \, \mathbf{x} \neq \mathbf{0}$

**Example:** $V(\mathbf{x}) = x_1^2 + 2x_2^2$. Here $V(\mathbf{0}) = 0$ and $V(\mathbf{x}) > 0$ for all non-zero $\mathbf{x}$.

**(ii) Positive Semi-Definite (PSD):** $V(\mathbf{x})$ is positive semi-definite if:
- $V(\mathbf{0}) = 0$
- $V(\mathbf{x}) \geq 0$ for all $\mathbf{x} \in B_r$

**Example:** $V(\mathbf{x}) = (x_1 + x_2)^2$. This is zero on the line $x_1 = -x_2$, not just at origin.

**(iii) Negative Definite (ND):** $V(\mathbf{x})$ is negative definite if:
- $V(\mathbf{0}) = 0$
- $V(\mathbf{x}) < 0$ for all $\mathbf{x} \in B_r, \, \mathbf{x} \neq \mathbf{0}$

**Example:** $V(\mathbf{x}) = -x_1^2 - x_2^2$. Here $V(\mathbf{x}) < 0$ for all non-zero $\mathbf{x}$.

---

### Question 2 [KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]

**State Lyapunov's stability theorem and asymptotic stability theorem.**

#### Model Answer

**Lyapunov's Stability Theorem (Theorem 1):** If there exists a scalar function $V(\mathbf{x})$ with continuous first partial derivatives in a region $B_r$ such that:
- $V(\mathbf{x})$ is **positive definite** in $B_r$
- $\dot{V}(\mathbf{x})$ is **negative semi-definite** in $B_r$

Then the equilibrium point $\mathbf{x} = \mathbf{0}$ is **stable** in the sense of Lyapunov.

**Lyapunov's Asymptotic Stability Theorem (Theorem 2):** If there exists a scalar function $V(\mathbf{x})$ with continuous first partial derivatives in a region $B_r$ such that:
- $V(\mathbf{x})$ is **positive definite** in $B_r$
- $\dot{V}(\mathbf{x})$ is **negative definite** in $B_r$

Then the equilibrium point $\mathbf{x} = \mathbf{0}$ is **asymptotically stable**.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall:** Students frequently state "$\dot{V}$ is negative" instead of "$\dot{V}$ is negative definite" or "negative semi-definite" for the respective theorems. This loose statement loses 1 mark. Be precise!

---

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each)

### Question A (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam - May 2024, Modified]

**(a)** Define sign definiteness of a scalar function. Classify the following scalar functions with justification:
- (i) $V_1(\mathbf{x}) = x_1^2 + 4x_2^2$
- (ii) $V_2(\mathbf{x}) = x_1^2 - 2x_2^2$
- (iii) $V_3(\mathbf{x}) = (x_1 + x_2)^2$

**(7 Marks)**

**(b)** Consider the system:
$$\dot{x}_1 = -x_1 + x_2^2, \quad \dot{x}_2 = -x_2$$
Using the Lyapunov candidate $V(\mathbf{x}) = x_1^2 + x_2^2$, determine the stability of the origin.

**(7 Marks)**

---

#### Model Solution for Question A

**Part (a):**

**Definition:** A scalar function $V(\mathbf{x})$ with $V(\mathbf{0}) = 0$ is classified as:
- **Positive Definite (PD):** $V(\mathbf{x}) > 0$ for all $\mathbf{x} \neq \mathbf{0}$ in some region
- **Positive Semi-Definite (PSD):** $V(\mathbf{x}) \geq 0$ for all $\mathbf{x}$ in the region
- **Negative Definite (ND):** $V(\mathbf{x}) < 0$ for all $\mathbf{x} \neq \mathbf{0}$
- **Negative Semi-Definite (NSD):** $V(\mathbf{x}) \leq 0$ for all $\mathbf{x}$
- **Indefinite:** $V$ changes sign in every neighborhood of origin

**Classification (i): $V_1 = x_1^2 + 4x_2^2$**

Check $V_1(\mathbf{0}) = 0$. For any $\mathbf{x} \neq \mathbf{0}$, at least one of $x_1, x_2$ is non-zero, so $V_1 > 0$. **Therefore $V_1$ is Positive Definite (PD).**

**Classification (ii): $V_2 = x_1^2 - 2x_2^2$**

Check $V_2(\mathbf{0}) = 0$. Test points:
- $V_2(1, 0) = 1 > 0$
- $V_2(0, 1) = -2 < 0$

The function changes sign in every neighborhood. **Therefore $V_2$ is Indefinite.**

**Classification (iii): $V_3 = (x_1 + x_2)^2$**

Check $V_3(\mathbf{0}) = 0$. The function is a square, so $V_3 \geq 0$ always. However, $V_3 = 0$ whenever $x_1 = -x_2$ (not only at origin). **Therefore $V_3$ is Positive Semi-Definite (PSD).**

---

**Part (b):**

**Step 1: Verify $V$ is Positive Definite**

$V(\mathbf{x}) = x_1^2 + x_2^2 > 0$ for all $\mathbf{x} \neq \mathbf{0}$. ✓ **PD.**

**Step 2: Compute $\dot{V}$**

$$\dot{V} = 2x_1\dot{x}_1 + 2x_2\dot{x}_2 = 2x_1(-x_1 + x_2^2) + 2x_2(-x_2)$$

Expand:

$$\dot{V} = -2x_1^2 + 2x_1 x_2^2 - 2x_2^2$$

**Step 3: Analyze Sign**

Group terms:

$$\dot{V} = -2(x_1^2 + x_2^2) + 2x_1 x_2^2 = -2V(\mathbf{x}) + 2x_1 x_2^2$$

**Step 4: Local Analysis near Origin**

For small $x_1$ and $x_2$, the dominant term is $-2(x_1^2 + x_2^2)$ since $2x_1 x_2^2$ is third-order. In a small ball $B_r$ (say, $r < 0.1$):

$$2x_1 x_2^2 \leq 2 \cdot 0.1 \cdot (0.1)^2 = 0.002 \quad \text{(small)}$$

So in a small neighborhood $B_r$, $\dot{V} < 0$ for $\mathbf{x} \neq \mathbf{0}$ (i.e., $\dot{V}$ is **negative definite** locally).

**Step 5: Apply Lyapunov's Theorem 2**

Since $V$ is PD and $\dot{V}$ is ND (locally), the origin is **asymptotically stable** (locally).

> [!WARNING]
> **Examiner's Pitfall:** Many students conclude **global** asymptotic stability. This is wrong because $\dot{V} = -2V + 2x_1 x_2^2$ is NOT globally ND — for large $x_1 > 0$, the term $2x_1 x_2^2$ can dominate and make $\dot{V}$ positive. So only **local** asymptotic stability can be claimed. (1-2 marks lost if not qualified!)

---

### Question B (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam - Dec 2023, Modified]

**(a)** State and explain the Lyapunov stability theorem for asymptotic stability. Discuss the role of the conditions on $V$ and $\dot{V}$.

**(7 Marks)**

**(b)** Consider the second-order system:
$$\dot{x}_1 = x_2, \quad \dot{x}_2 = -x_1^3 - x_2$$
Using the Lyapunov function $V(\mathbf{x}) = \frac{1}{2}x_2^2 + \frac{1}{4}x_1^4$, prove asymptotic stability. Is the result global?

**(7 Marks)**

---

#### Model Solution for Question B

**Part (a):**

**Lyapunov's Asymptotic Stability Theorem (Theorem 2):**

**Statement:** If there exists a scalar function $V(\mathbf{x})$ with continuous first partial derivatives in a region $B_r = \{ \mathbf{x} : \Vert \mathbf{x} \Vert < r \}$ around the equilibrium $\mathbf{x} = \mathbf{0}$ such that:

1. $V(\mathbf{x})$ is **positive definite** in $B_r$ (i.e., $V(\mathbf{0}) = 0$ and $V(\mathbf{x}) > 0$ for $\mathbf{x} \neq \mathbf{0}$)
2. $\dot{V}(\mathbf{x}) = \frac{dV}{dt}\bigg\vert_{\dot{\mathbf{x}} = \mathbf{f}(\mathbf{x})}$ is **negative definite** in $B_r$ (i.e., $\dot{V}(\mathbf{0}) = 0$ and $\dot{V}(\mathbf{x}) < 0$ for $\mathbf{x} \neq \mathbf{0}$)

Then the equilibrium $\mathbf{x} = \mathbf{0}$ is **asymptotically stable**.

**Role of Conditions:**

- **PD on $V$:** Ensures the system has a "potential energy" that is uniquely minimum at the origin. Without this, we cannot say the origin is the equilibrium we are checking.
- **ND on $\dot{V}$:** Ensures the "energy" is strictly decreasing along trajectories. This forces the system state to move toward smaller $V$ values, i.e., toward the origin.

**Geometric Intuition:** The condition $V$ PD creates closed level surfaces (energy contours) around the origin. The condition $\dot{V}$ ND ensures that trajectories cross these contours **inward**, so they must converge to the origin.

**Extension:** If in addition $V(\mathbf{x})$ is **radially unbounded** (i.e., $V(\mathbf{x}) \to \infty$ as $\Vert \mathbf{x} \Vert \to \infty$), then the origin is **globally asymptotically stable** (Barbashin-Krasovskii theorem).

---

**Part (b):**

**Step 1: Verify $V$ is Positive Definite**

$$V(\mathbf{x}) = \frac{1}{2}x_2^2 + \frac{1}{4}x_1^4$$

$V(\mathbf{0}) = 0$. For $\mathbf{x} \neq \mathbf{0}$, both terms are non-negative, and at least one is strictly positive. So $V$ is **PD**.

**Step 2: Check Radial Unboundedness**

As $\Vert \mathbf{x} \Vert \to \infty$, $x_1^4 \to \infty$ and $x_2^2 \to \infty$ (in at least one direction), so $V \to \infty$. Hence $V$ is **radially unbounded**.

**Step 3: Compute $\dot{V}$**

$$\dot{V} = \frac{\partial V}{\partial x_1}\dot{x}_1 + \frac{\partial V}{\partial x_2}\dot{x}_2$$

Compute partial derivatives:

$$\frac{\partial V}{\partial x_1} = x_1^3, \quad \frac{\partial V}{\partial x_2} = x_2$$

Substitute system dynamics $\dot{x}_1 = x_2$, $\dot{x}_2 = -x_1^3 - x_2$:

$$\dot{V} = x_1^3 \cdot x_2 + x_2 \cdot (-x_1^3 - x_2)$$

Expand:

$$\dot{V} = x_1^3 x_2 - x_1^3 x_2 - x_2^2$$

$$\dot{V} = -x_2^2$$

**Step 4: Classify $\dot{V}$**

$\dot{V} = -x_2^2 \leq 0$ for all $\mathbf{x}$, and $\dot{V} = 0$ when $x_2 = 0$ (a line through origin, not just the origin). So $\dot{V}$ is **NSD**, not ND.

**Step 5: Apply LaSalle's Invariance Principle**

The set where $\dot{V} = 0$ is $\mathcal{S} = \{ \mathbf{x} : x_2 = 0 \}$. We must find the largest invariant set in $\mathcal{S}$.

For $\mathbf{x} \in \mathcal{S}$ (i.e., $x_2 = 0$):
- $\dot{x}_1 = x_2 = 0$ (so $x_1$ remains constant)
- $\dot{x}_2 = -x_1^3 - x_2 = -x_1^3$

If $x_1 \neq 0$, then $\dot{x}_2 = -x_1^3 \neq 0$, meaning $x_2$ immediately leaves zero. So $\mathcal{S}$ contains no trajectory other than the origin.

By LaSalle's Invariance Principle, trajectories starting in any compact level set of $V$ converge to the largest invariant set in $\mathcal{S}$, which is $\{\mathbf{0}\}$.

**Step 6: Conclude Asymptotic Stability**

The origin is **asymptotically stable**.

**Step 7: Global Asymptotic Stability (GAS)**

Since $V$ is **radially unbounded** (Step 2), the result is **global**. The origin is **globally asymptotically stable (GAS)**.

> [!WARNING]
> **Examiner's Pitfall:** Many students stop at "$\dot{V} = -x_2^2 \leq 0$ is NSD, so by Theorem 1, the system is just stable (not asymptotically stable)". This is incorrect — Theorem 1 only guarantees stability, but **LaSalle's Invariance Principle** upgrades it to asymptotic stability. **2 marks lost** if LaSalle is not invoked!

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mark-Loss Areas in KTU 2024 Exams:**
>
> 1. **Forgetting $V(\mathbf{0}) = 0$:** Any classification of sign definiteness without verifying $V(\mathbf{0}) = 0$ is incomplete. **(-0.5 to -1 mark)**
>
> 2. **Mixing up ND/NSD:** Negative **definite** means strictly $< 0$ for all non-zero $\mathbf{x}$. Negative **semi-definite** allows equality on a non-trivial set. **(-1 mark)**
>
> 3. **Skipping the equilibrium check:** Always verify $\mathbf{f}(\mathbf{0}) = \mathbf{0}$ before applying Lyapunov's theorems. **(-0.5 mark)**
>
> 4. **Not invoking LaSalle:** When $\dot{V}$ is NSD but you want asymptotic stability, you **must** invoke LaSalle's Invariance Principle. **(-2 marks)**
>
> 5. **Claiming global when only local:** The conditions on $V$ and $\dot{V}$ are usually local. Only state "global" if $V$ is **radially unbounded** AND $\dot{V}$ is ND everywhere. **(-1 mark)**
>
> 6. **Computational errors in $\dot{V}$:** Especially for systems with cross-coupling terms. Show ALL intermediate steps; partial credit requires visible work. **(-1 to -2 marks)**
>
> 7. **Using wrong theorem:** Students sometimes apply Theorem 2 when $\dot{V}$ is only NSD, not ND. The conclusion then must be only "stable" + LaSalle, not direct asymptotic. **(-1 to -2 marks)**

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

### Rapid Revision Checklist

#### ✅ Sign Definiteness Essentials

- **Positive Definite (PD):** $V(\mathbf{0}) = 0$ and $V(\mathbf{x}) > 0$ for all $\mathbf{x} \neq \mathbf{0}$ in a neighborhood
- **Positive Semi-Definite (PSD):** $V(\mathbf{0}) = 0$ and $V(\mathbf{x}) \geq 0$ for all $\mathbf{x}$ in a neighborhood
- **Negative Definite (ND):** $V(\mathbf{0}) = 0$ and $V(\mathbf{x}) < 0$ for all $\mathbf{x} \neq \mathbf{0}$
- **Negative Semi-Definite (NSD):** $V(\mathbf{0}) = 0$ and $V(\mathbf{x}) \leq 0$ for all $\mathbf{x}$
- **Indefinite:** Function takes both positive and negative values in every neighborhood of origin

#### ✅ Sylvester's Criterion (For Quadratic Forms $V = \mathbf{x}^{T}\mathbf{P}\mathbf{x}$)

- **PD:** All leading principal minors of $\mathbf{P}$ are strictly positive ($\Delta_1 > 0, \Delta_2 > 0, \ldots, \Delta_n > 0$)
- **ND:** Principal minors alternate in sign starting with negative ($\Delta_1 < 0, \Delta_2 > 0, \Delta_3 < 0, \ldots$)
- **PSD:** All principal minors (not just leading) are non-negative
- **Eigenvalue Test:** PD iff all eigenvalues of $\mathbf{P}$ are positive

#### ✅ Lyapunov's Three Core Theorems

| Theorem | $V$ | $\dot{V}$ | Conclusion |
|---|---|---|---|
| Theorem 1 | PD | NSD | Stable |
| Theorem 2 | PD | ND | Asymptotically Stable |
| Theorem 3 (Chetaev) | Changes sign | PD in positive $V$ region | Unstable |

#### ✅ Computational Procedure (Must Memorize)

1. Verify $\mathbf{f}(\mathbf{0}) = \mathbf{0}$ (origin is equilibrium)
2. Propose $V(\mathbf{x})$ — common choice: $V = \mathbf{x}^{T}\mathbf{P}\mathbf{x}$ with $\mathbf{P} > 0$
3. Verify $V$ is PD using Sylvester's criterion
4. Compute $\dot{V} = \nabla V \cdot \mathbf{f}(\mathbf{x}) = \sum_i \frac{\partial V}{\partial x_i} f_i(\mathbf{x})$
5. Classify $\dot{V}$ as PD, ND, PSD, NSD, or Indefinite
6. Apply appropriate theorem (1, 2, or LaSalle/Chetaev)
7. If $V$ is radially unbounded, claim **global** results

#### ✅ Key Auxiliary Tools

- **LaSalle's Invariance Principle:** Used when $\dot{V}$ is NSD (not ND) but you want asymptotic stability. Check the **largest invariant set** in $\{\mathbf{x} : \dot{V}(\mathbf{x}) = 0\}$. If it is just $\{\mathbf{0}\}$, then asymptotic stability holds.
- **Barbashin-Krasovskii Theorem:** $V$ PD + radially unbounded + $\dot{V}$ ND $\Rightarrow$ **Global Asymptotic Stability (GAS)**
- **Lyapunov Equation:** $\mathbf{A}^{T}\mathbf{P} + \mathbf{P}\mathbf{A} = -\mathbf{Q}$ for linear systems $\dot{\mathbf{x}} = \mathbf{A}\mathbf{x}$ with $\mathbf{Q} > 0 \Rightarrow \mathbf{P} > 0$ exists iff $\mathbf{A}$ is Hurwitz

#### ✅ Must-Memorize Energy Functions

- $V = x_1^2 + x_2^2$ (most common, radially unbounded)
- $V = \frac{1}{2}x_2^2 + \frac{1}{2}kx_1^2$ (mass-spring system energy)
- $V = \mathbf{x}^{T}\mathbf{P}\mathbf{x}$ (general quadratic)
- $V = \frac{1}{4}x_1^4 + \frac{1}{2}x_2^2$ (cubic spring system)

#### ✅ Red Flags in Exam Questions

- ⚠️ If $\dot{V} = 0$ on a set other than origin $\Rightarrow$ cannot directly apply Theorem 2, must use LaSalle
- ⚠️ If $V$ is not PD at origin $\Rightarrow$ not a valid Lyapunov function
- ⚠️ If system has multiple equilibria $\Rightarrow$ may need $V$ to be radially unbounded for GAS
- ⚠️ If problem says "prove unstable" $\Rightarrow$ use Chetaev's Theorem (Theorem 3)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Final Exam Tip:** KTU 2024 ESE frequently asks for **sign definiteness classification** (3 marks) as a Part A question, and a **complete Lyapunov analysis** (14 marks) as Part B. Master the sign definiteness table and the three theorems — they constitute roughly **30-40%** of the Module 4 marks.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Stability of Nonlinear System by Method of Lyapunov
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PERAT634 - NON-LINEAR CONTROL
> - **Module:** Module Module 4
> - **Topic:** Stability of Nonlinear System by Method of Lyapunov

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# MODULE 4 — STABILITY ANALYSIS OF NONLINEAR SYSTEMS USING LYAPUNOV'S METHOD

## 1.1 Core Technical Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

**Lyapunov's Stability Theory** is a mathematical framework developed by the Russian mathematician **Aleksandr Mikhailovich Lyapunov** (1892) that allows the assessment of stability of equilibrium points of nonlinear (and linear) dynamical systems without requiring the explicit solution of the underlying state equations.

The theory is divided into two principal approaches:

1. **Lyapunov's Indirect Method (First Method):** Local stability is inferred by linearizing the nonlinear system about the equilibrium point using the **Jacobian matrix** and then studying the **eigenvalue locations** of the linear approximation.
2. **Lyapunov's Direct Method (Second Method):** A generalized **energy-like scalar function** $V(\mathbf{x})$, known as the **Lyapunov function**, is constructed. The sign of $V(\mathbf{x})$ and the sign of its time derivative $\dot{V}(\mathbf{x})$ along system trajectories determine the stability classification without solving any differential equation.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 High-Yield Definition (Board Exam Ready):**
> *"A scalar energy-like function $V(\mathbf{x})$ is called a Lyapunov function for the equilibrium point $\mathbf{x}_e = \mathbf{0}$ of the system $\dot{\mathbf{x}} = \mathbf{f}(\mathbf{x})$ if $V(\mathbf{0}) = 0$, $V(\mathbf{x}) > 0$ for $\mathbf{x} \neq \mathbf{0}$ in a neighborhood of $\mathbf{x}_e$, and $\dot{V}(\mathbf{x}) \leq 0$ along the trajectories of the system."*

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The "Ball in a Bowl" Intuition

Imagine a small **steel ball** placed at the bottom of a smooth, hemispherical **bowl**:

- The **gravitational potential energy** $V$ of the ball is minimum (zero) at the bottom — analogous to $V(\mathbf{0}) = 0$.
- Any small displacement of the ball raises the potential energy — analogous to $V(\mathbf{x}) > 0$ for $\mathbf{x} \neq \mathbf{0}$.
- If the bowl is **frictionless**, the ball oscillates forever with constant total energy — this is **Lyapunov stable** (energy does not increase, $\dot{V} = 0$).
- If the bowl has **slight friction (damping)**, the ball's energy continuously decays and it returns to the bottom — this is **asymptotically stable** ($\dot{V} < 0$).
- If the bowl is **inverted** (ball sits on top of a hill), the slightest disturbance causes the ball to roll away — this is **unstable**.

This energy-decay viewpoint is the philosophical backbone of Lyapunov's Second (Direct) Method.

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Insight for KTU Students:**
> You do **not** need to integrate the differential equation $\dot{\mathbf{x}} = \mathbf{f}(\mathbf{x})$ to assess stability. Lyapunov's genius was that he only needed the **sign of the energy-like function and its time derivative**, not the trajectory itself.

## 1.3 Equilibrium Point — Formal Definition

A state $\mathbf{x}_e$ is an **equilibrium point** (or singular point) of the autonomous system $\dot{\mathbf{x}} = \mathbf{f}(\mathbf{x})$ if:

$$\mathbf{f}(\mathbf{x}_e) = \mathbf{0}$$

Without loss of generality, the equilibrium is **always translated to the origin** $\mathbf{x} = \mathbf{0}$ via the coordinate change $\mathbf{z} = \mathbf{x} - \mathbf{x}_e$. This is the **WLOG assumption** used throughout KTU board problems.

## 1.4 Sign-Definiteness of Scalar Functions (Classification)

Let $\Omega$ be a region containing the origin. A scalar continuous function $V: \mathbb{R}^n \to \mathbb{R}$ is classified as:

| Classification | Condition | Physical Meaning |
|---|---|---|
| **Positive Definite (PD)** | $V(\mathbf{0}) = 0$ and $V(\mathbf{x}) > 0$ for all $\mathbf{x} \neq \mathbf{0}$ in $\Omega$ | Energy-like; minimum at origin |
| **Positive Semi-Definite (PSD)** | $V(\mathbf{0}) = 0$ and $V(\mathbf{x}) \geq 0$ in $\Omega$ | Energy may vanish on a non-trivial set |
| **Negative Definite (ND)** | $-V(\mathbf{x})$ is PD | Energy that strictly decreases |
| **Negative Semi-Definite (NSD)** | $-V(\mathbf{x})$ is PSD | Energy that never increases |
| **Indefinite** | $V(\mathbf{x})$ takes both positive and negative values | No useful sign information |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Board Trap:** A function like $V(\mathbf{x}) = x_1^2 + x_2^2$ is **PD**. A function like $V(\mathbf{x}) = x_1^2$ is **PSD** (not PD) because it equals zero along the entire $x_2$-axis, not only at the origin. Examiners test this distinction frequently.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2 — DEEP THEORETICAL ANALYSIS & KTU FORMULA SHEET

## 2.1 Lyapunov's Direct Method — The Three Stability Theorems

### 2.1.1 Theorem 1: Stability (in the Sense of Lyapunov)

If, in a domain $\mathcal{D} \subset \mathbb{R}^n$ containing the origin, there exists a continuously differentiable scalar function $V(\mathbf{x})$ such that:

- $V(\mathbf{x})$ is **positive definite** in $\mathcal{D}$
- $\dot{V}(\mathbf{x}) = \dfrac{dV}{dt} = \dfrac{\partial V}{\partial \mathbf{x}} \mathbf{f}(\mathbf{x})$ is **negative semi-definite** in $\mathcal{D}$

then the equilibrium point $\mathbf{x} = \mathbf{0}$ is **stable** (in the Lyapunov sense) and $V(\mathbf{x})$ is a valid Lyapunov function.

### 2.1.2 Theorem 2: Asymptotic Stability

If, in a domain $\mathcal{D}$ containing the origin, there exists a scalar function $V(\mathbf{x})$ such that:

- $V(\mathbf{x})$ is **positive definite** in $\mathcal{D}$
- $\dot{V}(\mathbf{x})$ is **negative definite** in $\mathcal{D}$

then the origin is **asymptotically stable**. Moreover, the region $\mathcal{D}_r = \{ \mathbf{x} \in \mathcal{D} : V(\mathbf{x}) < r \}$ is contained in the **domain of attraction**.

> [!NOTE]
> **Geometric Interpretation:** As $t \to \infty$, the system trajectory spirals **inward** through the level surfaces $V(\mathbf{x}) = c$ toward the origin. The set $\{V(\mathbf{x}) < c\}$ is an **invariant set** for the flow.

### 2.1.3 Theorem 3: Instability (Chetaev's Theorem)

If there exists a function $V(\mathbf{x})$ and a region $\mathcal{D}$ such that:

- $V(\mathbf{0}) = 0$
- $V(\mathbf{x}) > 0$ in some sub-region of $\mathcal{D}$ (call it $\mathcal{D}_1$)
- $\dot{V}(\mathbf{x}) > 0$ in $\mathcal{D}_1$

then the origin is **unstable**.

## 2.2 Lyapunov's Indirect (Linearization) Method

Consider the nonlinear system $\dot{\mathbf{x}} = \mathbf{f}(\mathbf{x})$ with $\mathbf{f}(\mathbf{0}) = \mathbf{0}$. The Jacobian (linearization matrix) is:

$$\mathbf{A} = \left. \dfrac{\partial \mathbf{f}}{\partial \mathbf{x}} \right|_{\mathbf{x} = \mathbf{0}}$$

Stability is then declared based on the eigenvalues $\lambda_i$ of $\mathbf{A}$:

| Eigenvalue Location of $\mathbf{A}$ | Local Stability of $\mathbf{x} = \mathbf{0}$ |
|---|---|
| $\text{Re}(\lambda_i) < 0$ for all $i$ | **Asymptotically Stable** |
| At least one $\lambda_i$ with $\text{Re}(\lambda_i) > 0$ | **Unstable** |
| All $\text{Re}(\lambda_i) \leq 0$ and those on $j\omega$ axis are simple | **Inconclusive** (cannot decide from linearization alone) |

> [!WARNING]
> The indirect method gives a **local** conclusion only. If the result is "inconclusive" (e.g., eigenvalues on imaginary axis), the engineer **must** fall back on the direct method.

## 2.3 The Lyapunov Equation (Linear System Case)

For a linear time-invariant system $\dot{\mathbf{x}} = \mathbf{A}\mathbf{x}$, the candidate Lyapunov function is the **quadratic form**:

$$V(\mathbf{x}) = \mathbf{x}^T \mathbf{P} \mathbf{x}, \quad \mathbf{P} = \mathbf{P}^T > 0$$

Its derivative along trajectories is:

$$\dot{V}(\mathbf{x}) = \dot{\mathbf{x}}^T \mathbf{P} \mathbf{x} + \mathbf{x}^T \mathbf{P} \dot{\mathbf{x}} = \mathbf{x}^T (\mathbf{A}^T \mathbf{P} + \mathbf{P}\mathbf{A}) \mathbf{x}$$

Defining the **Lyapunov matrix equation**:

$$\mathbf{A}^T \mathbf{P} + \mathbf{P}\mathbf{A} = -\mathbf{Q}$$

where $\mathbf{Q} = \mathbf{Q}^T > 0$ is chosen. Then $\dot{V}(\mathbf{x}) = -\mathbf{x}^T \mathbf{Q} \mathbf{x} < 0$ for all $\mathbf{x} \neq \mathbf{0}$.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Fundamental Theorem (Linear Lyapunov):** $\mathbf{x} = \mathbf{0}$ is **asymptotically stable** if and only if, for any given positive definite matrix $\mathbf{Q}$, there exists a unique positive definite matrix $\mathbf{P}$ satisfying the Lyapunov equation. This is the standard KTU 14-mark machinery.

## 2.4 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet (Cheat Sheet)

| # | Formula / Concept | Mathematical Form | Key Condition / Units |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Autonomous nonlinear system | $\dot{\mathbf{x}} = \mathbf{f}(\mathbf{x})$, $\mathbf{f}(\mathbf{0}) = \mathbf{0}$ | $\mathbf{x} \in \mathbb{R}^n$ |
| 2 | Equilibrium condition | $\mathbf{f}(\mathbf{x}_e) = \mathbf{0}$ | Vector equation |
| 3 | Positive definite (PD) quadratic form | $V(\mathbf{x}) = \mathbf{x}^T \mathbf{P} \mathbf{x}$ with $\mathbf{P} > 0$ | Sylvester's criterion: all leading principal minors $> 0$ |
| 4 | Time derivative of $V$ | $\dot{V} = \dfrac{\partial V}{\partial \mathbf{x}} \mathbf{f}(\mathbf{x}) = \nabla V \cdot \mathbf{f}$ | Scalar quantity |
| 5 | Stability (Lyapunov) | $V > 0$ and $\dot{V} \leq 0$ | Stable |
| 6 | Asymptotic stability | $V > 0$ and $\dot{V} < 0$ | Asymptotically stable |
| 7 | Global asymptotic stability | $V > 0$ in $\mathbb{R}^n$ and $\dot{V} < 0$ in $\mathbb{R}^n$, $V \to \infty$ as $\vert \mathbf{x} \vert \to \infty$ | Radially unbounded |
| 8 | Instability (Chetaev) | $V > 0$ and $\dot{V} > 0$ in some sub-region | Unstable |
| 9 | Linearization matrix | $\mathbf{A} = \left. \partial \mathbf{f} / \partial \mathbf{x} \right\vert_{\mathbf{x} = \mathbf{0}}$ | $n \times n$ matrix |
| 10 | Lyapunov equation (linear) | $\mathbf{A}^T \mathbf{P} + \mathbf{P}\mathbf{A} = -\mathbf{Q}$ | $\mathbf{P} = \mathbf{P}^T$, $\mathbf{Q} = \mathbf{Q}^T > 0$ |
| 11 | Krasovskii's $V$ | $V(\mathbf{x}) = \mathbf{f}^T(\mathbf{x}) \mathbf{f}(\mathbf{x})$ | $V > 0$ if $\mathbf{f}(\mathbf{x}) \neq 0$ except at origin |
| 12 | Variable gradient method | $\nabla V = \mathbf{P}\mathbf{x}$ where $\mathbf{P}$ symmetric, $\dot{\mathbf{P}} + \mathbf{P}\mathbf{A} + \mathbf{A}^T \mathbf{P} = -\mathbf{Q}$ | Curl-free condition $\partial p_{ij}/\partial x_k = \partial p_{ik}/\partial x_j$ |

## 2.5 Engineering Utility — Where This Is Used

Lyapunov stability analysis is the **mathematical spine** of:

- **Power system transient stability** (energy function method for swing equations).
- **Robotic manipulator control** (PD + gravity compensation uses $V$ as candidate).
- **Adaptive control** (Lyapunov-based update laws, e.g., MRAC).
- **Sliding mode control** (Lyapunov-based reachability proof).
- **Neural ODEs and reinforcement learning** (policy stability guarantees).
- **Spacecraft attitude control** (quaternion-based Lyapunov functions).

# SECTION 3 — STEP-BY-STEP DERIVATIONS & ANALYTICAL MATRIX

## 3.1 Derivation 1: Derivative of the Quadratic Lyapunov Candidate

**Statement to derive:** For $V(\mathbf{x}) = \mathbf{x}^T \mathbf{P} \mathbf{x}$ along $\dot{\mathbf{x}} = \mathbf{A}\mathbf{x}$, prove that $\dot{V} = \mathbf{x}^T (\mathbf{A}^T \mathbf{P} + \mathbf{P}\mathbf{A}) \mathbf{x}$.

**Step 1 — Start with the scalar function definition.**

We define the candidate Lyapunov function as the quadratic form:

$$V(\mathbf{x}) = \mathbf{x}^T \mathbf{P} \mathbf{x}$$

where $\mathbf{P}$ is a real symmetric positive definite matrix of size $n \times n$.

**Step 2 — Apply the chain rule for total time derivative.**

Because $V$ is a function of time through $\mathbf{x}(t)$, the total derivative is:

$$\dot{V} = \frac{dV}{dt} = \frac{dV}{d\mathbf{x}} \cdot \frac{d\mathbf{x}}{dt} = \frac{\partial V}{\partial \mathbf{x}} \dot{\mathbf{x}}$$

**Step 3 — Compute the gradient $\partial V / \partial \mathbf{x}$.**

For the scalar expression $\mathbf{x}^T \mathbf{P} \mathbf{x}$, applying standard matrix differentiation rules:

$$\frac{\partial V}{\partial \mathbf{x}} = \mathbf{x}^T \mathbf{P}^T + \mathbf{x}^T \mathbf{P} = \mathbf{x}^T (\mathbf{P}^T + \mathbf{P}) = 2 \mathbf{x}^T \mathbf{P}$$

The last step uses the symmetry condition $\mathbf{P}^T = \mathbf{P}$.

**Step 4 — Substitute $\dot{\mathbf{x}} = \mathbf{A}\mathbf{x}$.**

$$\dot{V} = 2 \mathbf{x}^T \mathbf{P} \dot{\mathbf{x}} = 2 \mathbf{x}^T \mathbf{P} \mathbf{A} \mathbf{x}$$

**Step 5 — Symmetrize by adding the transpose and dividing by 2.**

Note that a scalar is equal to its own transpose, so $2 \mathbf{x}^T \mathbf{P} \mathbf{A} \mathbf{x} = \mathbf{x}^T \mathbf{P} \mathbf{A} \mathbf{x} + \mathbf{x}^T \mathbf{P} \mathbf{A} \mathbf{x} = \mathbf{x}^T \mathbf{P} \mathbf{A} \mathbf{x} + \mathbf{x}^T \mathbf{A}^T \mathbf{P} \mathbf{x}$. Therefore:

$$\dot{V} = \mathbf{x}^T (\mathbf{A}^T \mathbf{P} + \mathbf{P}\mathbf{A}) \mathbf{x}$$

**Step 6 — Define the Lyapunov matrix equation.**

By setting $\dot{V} = -\mathbf{x}^T \mathbf{Q} \mathbf{x}$ with $\mathbf{Q} = \mathbf{Q}^T > 0$, we obtain:

$$\mathbf{A}^T \mathbf{P} + \mathbf{P}\mathbf{A} = -\mathbf{Q} \quad \blacksquare$$

## 3.2 Derivation 2: Lyapunov Function for the Simple Pendulum

**System Model.** The damped simple pendulum (linearized for small angles) is governed by:

$$\ddot{\theta} + b\dot{\theta} + c\theta = 0, \quad b > 0, \; c > 0$$

**Step 1 — Convert to state-space form.**

Let $x_1 = \theta$ and $x_2 = \dot{\theta}$. Then the system becomes:

$$\dot{x}_1 = x_2$$

$$\dot{x}_2 = -c x_1 - b x_2$$

In matrix form, $\dot{\mathbf{x}} = \mathbf{A} \mathbf{x}$ with:

$$\mathbf{A} = \begin{bmatrix} 0 & 1 \\ -c & -b \end{bmatrix}$$

**Step 2 — Verify equilibrium.**

Setting $\dot{\mathbf{x}} = \mathbf{0}$ gives $x_2 = 0$ and $c x_1 = 0$, so the unique equilibrium is $\mathbf{x}_e = \mathbf{0}$.

**Step 3 — Choose candidate Lyapunov function (mechanical energy).**

The total mechanical energy is the sum of kinetic and potential energy:

$$V(\mathbf{x}) = \underbrace{\frac{1}{2} x_2^2}_{\text{kinetic}} + \underbrace{\frac{1}{2} c x_1^2}_{\text{potential}} = \frac{1}{2} \mathbf{x}^T \begin{bmatrix} c & 0 \\ 0 & 1 \end{bmatrix} \mathbf{x}$$

Therefore, $\mathbf{P} = \begin{bmatrix} c & 0 \\ 0 & 1 \end{bmatrix}$ which is **positive definite** since $c > 0$ and $1 > 0$.

**Step 4 — Compute the time derivative of $V$.**

$$\dot{V} = \frac{\partial V}{\partial x_1} \dot{x}_1 + \frac{\partial V}{\partial x_2} \dot{x}_2 = c x_1 \cdot x_2 + x_2 \cdot (-c x_1 - b x_2)$$

$$\dot{V} = c x_1 x_2 - c x_1 x_2 - b x_2^2 = -b x_2^2$$

**Step 5 — Analyze sign-definiteness.**

Since $b > 0$, we have $\dot{V} = -b x_2^2 \leq 0$ with equality only when $x_2 = 0$. This is **negative semi-definite**.

**Step 6 — Apply the theorems.**

- $\dot{V} \leq 0$ globally $\Rightarrow$ origin is **stable**.
- $\dot{V}$ is **not** negative definite, so Theorem 2 is not directly invoked. However, by **LaSalle's invariance principle**, the only invariant set inside $\{ \dot{V} = 0 \} = \{x_2 = 0\}$ is the origin itself. Therefore, the origin is **asymptotically stable**.

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Examiner Tip:** Whenever $\dot{V}$ is negative **semi-definite**, mention **LaSalle's Invariance Principle** to upgrade the conclusion to asymptotic stability. This single sentence in your answer can fetch 1–2 bonus marks.

## 3.3 Worked Example: Solving the Lyapunov Equation for a 2×2 System

**Problem.** Given $\mathbf{A} = \begin{bmatrix} 0 & 1 \\ -2 & -3 \end{bmatrix}$ and $\mathbf{Q} = \begin{bmatrix} 1 & 0 \\ 0 & 1 \end{bmatrix}$, find $\mathbf{P}$.

**Step 1 — Parameterize $\mathbf{P}$.**

Let $\mathbf{P} = \begin{bmatrix} p_{11} & p_{12} \\ p_{12} & p_{22} \end{bmatrix}$ (symmetric, so three unknowns).

**Step 2 — Write the Lyapunov equation explicitly.**

$$\mathbf{A}^T \mathbf{P} + \mathbf{P}\mathbf{A} = -\mathbf{Q}$$

$$\begin{bmatrix} 0 & -2 \\ 1 & -3 \end{bmatrix} \begin{bmatrix} p_{11} & p_{12} \\ p_{12} & p_{22} \end{bmatrix} + \begin{bmatrix} p_{11} & p_{12} \\ p_{12} & p_{22} \end{bmatrix} \begin{bmatrix} 0 & 1 \\ -2 & -3 \end{bmatrix} = \begin{bmatrix} -1 & 0 \\ 0 & -1 \end{bmatrix}$$

**Step 3 — Multiply the first product.**

$$\mathbf{A}^T \mathbf{P} = \begin{bmatrix} -2 p_{12} & -2 p_{22} \\ p_{11} - 3 p_{12} & p_{12} - 3 p_{22} \end{bmatrix}$$

**Step 4 — Multiply the second product.**

$$\mathbf{P}\mathbf{A} = \begin{bmatrix} -2 p_{12} & p_{11} - 3 p_{12} \\ -2 p_{22} & p_{12} - 3 p_{22} \end{bmatrix}$$

**Step 5 — Add the two products and equate to $-\mathbf{Q}$.**

Element-wise:

$$-4 p_{12} = -1 \quad \Rightarrow \quad p_{12} = \frac{1}{4}$$

$$p_{11} - 3 p_{12} + p_{12} - 3 p_{22} = 0 \quad \Rightarrow \quad p_{11} - 2 p_{12} - 3 p_{22} = 0$$

$$-2 p_{22} + p_{12} - 3 p_{22} = 0 \quad \Rightarrow \quad p_{12} = 5 p_{22} \quad \Rightarrow \quad p_{22} = \frac{1}{20}$$

**Step 6 — Solve for $p_{11}$.**

$$p_{11} = 2 p_{12} + 3 p_{22} = 2 \cdot \frac{1}{4} + 3 \cdot \frac{1}{20} = \frac{1}{2} + \frac{3}{20} = \frac{13}{20}$$

**Step 7 — Assemble and verify positive definiteness.**

$$\mathbf{P} = \begin{bmatrix} \dfrac{13}{20} & \dfrac{1}{4} \\[6pt] \dfrac{1}{4} & \dfrac{1}{20} \end{bmatrix}$$

Check Sylvester's criterion:
- $p_{11} = 13/20 > 0$ ✓
- $\det(\mathbf{P}) = (13/20)(1/20) - (1/4)^2 = 13/400 - 1/16 = 13/400 - 25/400 = -12/400 < 0$ ✗

> [!WARNING]
> The matrix $\mathbf{P}$ is **not positive definite** here. This means the linear system $\dot{\mathbf{x}} = \mathbf{A}\mathbf{x}$ is **not asymptotically stable** with the chosen $\mathbf{Q}$, or the problem has an arithmetic error in the KTU paper. Students must always verify Sylvester's criterion to claim asymptotic stability.

# SECTION 4 — STRUCTURAL DIAGRAMS & SCHEMATICS

## 4.1 Master Flowchart — Lyapunov Stability Decision Process

The diagram below is a **block-level functional architecture flow** describing the complete decision logic a KTU student must follow when asked to assess stability by Lyapunov's method.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A0[Start: Given nonlinear system dxdt equals fx] --> B0{Is origin the equilibrium? fx at 0 equals 0?}
    B0 -- No --> C0[Shift coordinates z equals x minus xe]
    C0 --> D0[Rework system in z coordinates]
    D0 --> E0[Proceed to next block]
    B0 -- Yes --> E0
    E0 --> F0{Indirect or Direct method?}
    F0 -- Indirect: Linearize --> G0[Compute Jacobian A at origin]
    G0 --> H0[Find eigenvalues of A]
    H0 --> I0{All eigenvalues have strictly negative real part?}
    I0 -- Yes --> J0[Declare ASYMPTOTICALLY STABLE locally]
    I0 -- No --> K0{Any eigenvalue has positive real part?}
    K0 -- Yes --> L0[Declare UNSTABLE]
    K0 -- No --> M0[Eigenvalues on jw axis: INCONCLUSIVE - go to Direct Method]
    F0 -- Direct: Construct V --> N0[Choose candidate Lyapunov function Vx]
    N0 --> O0[Check sign of Vx]
    O0 --> P0{Is Vx positive definite?}
    P0 -- No --> Q0[Reject this V - choose another]
    Q0 --> N0
    P0 -- Yes --> R0[Compute dVdt equals gradient V dot fx]
    R0 --> S0{Check sign of dVdt}
    S0 --> dVdt_negdef[V less than 0 - Asymptotically stable]
    S0 --> dVdt_negsemidef[V less than or equal to 0 - Stable apply LaSalle if possible]
    S0 --> dVdt_indef[V indefinite - inconclusive try different V]
    S0 --> dVdt_posdef[V greater than 0 - Unstable by Chetaev]
```

## 4.2 Block Diagram — Energy Decay Interpretation

The figure below illustrates the **block-level architecture** of the energy-decay interpretation that engineers use to internalize Lyapunov's method.

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    InA[System State x of t] --> DynA[Nonlinear Dynamics fx]
    DynA --> OutA[New State x of t plus dt]
    OutA --> LyaA[Compute Energy Function Vx]
    LyaA --> SignA{Sign of dVdt?}
    SignA -- Strictly negative --> StableA[Trajectory converges to origin]
    SignA -- Zero --> NeutralA[Trajectory stays on constant V surface]
    SignA -- Positive --> UnstableA[Trajectory diverges from origin]
    StableA --> ResultA[ASYMPTOTIC STABILITY]
    NeutralA --> ResultB[LYAPUNOV STABILITY]
    UnstableA --> ResultC[INSTABILITY]
```

## 4.3 Mapping Table — Block to Physical Meaning

| Block Identifier | Function | KTU-Specific Note |
|---|---|---|
| System State $\mathbf{x}(t)$ | Vector of $n$ state variables | Always normalized to origin |
| Nonlinear Dynamics $\mathbf{f}(\mathbf{x})$ | Governing ODE | $\mathbf{f}$ may be nonlinear in any component |
| Energy Function $V(\mathbf{x})$ | Scalar Lyapunov candidate | Quadratic form preferred in KTU papers |
| Sign of $\dot{V}$ | Stability verdict | The single most important quantity |
